*' Taping  shoos." — ^In  an  old  oak  parish 
■chest,  possessed  of  three  locks,  but  now 
minus  any  keys,  in  the  fifteenth-century 
parish  church  of  St.  Stephen  at  Treleigh 
(Cornwall),  is  a  document  containing  a  list 
of  pauper  expenses  for  1709.  The  entry  for 
'•  taping  shoos  "  occurs  frequently.  Taping 
means  solein^;,  and  is  a  word  still  in  very 
general  use  m  the  West  country.  Indeed 
in  the  ordinary  spheres  of  life  eight  out  of 
ten  people  would  say,  *'  Those  boots  must 
be  tapped"  rather  than  that  they  should  be 
**  soled."  In  broad  Devon  the  rendering 
would  be  :  "  Ef  zo  be  yii  taps  thew  biites, 
they'll  least  awl  drii  tha  zummer."     This 

2 notation  I  give  from  the  late  Mrs.  Sarah 
[ewett's  '  Peasant  Speech  of  Devon  '  (1892). 

Harry  Hems. 
Fair  Park,  Exeter. 

"  Precket  "  :  "  Cageful  of  tkkth." — 
A  doctor  in  North  Devon,  on  viuiting  a 
woman,  found  her  scolding  her  child — a  girl 
of  twelve — with  niucli  severity.  The  child 
left  the  room,  and  the  doctor  asked  what  was 
tlie  matter.  '*  Well,"  said  her  mother, 
"  slie  's  so  prerket  "  ;  and  then,  to  explain 
preckety  added.  "  There  's  too  much  forraaUty 
about  )ier  ;  it 's  as  if  she  wanted  to  reign 
over  us  all."  The  idea  of  attributing  the 
formality  which  surrounds  the  throne  to 
the  naughtiness  of  a  ohihl  of  twelve  strikes 
me  as  very  ludicrous. 

|||In  North  Devon  they  also  say  of  a  mouth 
full  of  teetli,  "'  So-and-so  lias  a  good  cageful 
of  teeth."  T.  M.  W.

Mb.  CHAMBEBLAIZr  AND  ROBGBT  BUBTON. 

— With  reference  to  Mr.  Joseph  Chamber- 
lain's often  quoted  phrase,  '*  What  1  have 
said,  I  }iave  said,"  has  it  been  noticed  that 
Burton  in  his  '  Anatomy  of  Melancholy ' 
writes  as  follows  ? — 

**  As  the  barking  of  the  dog  I  securely  contemn 
those  malioious  and  sciirrile  oblotiuies,  iioutA,  and 
calumnies  of  railerH  and  detractors ;  I  scorn  the 
rest.  What  therefore  I  have  said,  pro  tenuitate 
mei,  I  have  said."—'  Democritus  to  the  Reader,' 
p.  10,  W.  Tegg's  edition,  1866. 

And  on  p.  50  in  the  same  work  Burton,  in 
referring  to  the  Utopia  that  he  would 
establisli,  says  as  follows  : — 

"As  of  sucli  wares  as  are  tranajtorted  or  brought 
in,  if  they  be  necessary,  commodious,  and  such  as 
nearly  concern  man's  life,  as  com,  wood,  coal,  &e., 
and  sucli  jtrovision  we  cannot  want,  I  will  have 
little  or  no  custom  paid,  no  taxes :  but  for  such 
things  as  are  for  pleasure,  delight,  or  ornament,  as 
wine,  spice,  tobacco,  silk,  velvet,  cloth  of  gold,  lace, 
jewels,  &c.,  a  greater  imitost." 

Had  Mr.  Chamberlain  been  studying 
Burton  before  he  started  his  Tariff  Reform 
campaign  ?  Walteb  L.  Jode.

Cromwell  anv  CuAi^fourr  St.  Giles  : 
Pikes  for  ab>[Iho  the  People.— I  should 
be  glad  of  any  information  on  the  following 
two  points.  There  is  a  tradition  in  the 
village  of  Chalfont  St.  Giles,  Bucks,  that 
*'  after  the  battle  of  Aylesbury  the  army  of 
Cromwell  encamped  in  the  Silsden  meadows, 
close  to  the  village  ;  that  Cromwell  slept 
at  the  old  Stone  House,  since  pulled  dowm  ; 
and  that  his  guns  fired  at  the  Church," 
When  the  church  was  restored,  a  small 
round-sliot  was  found  in  one  of  the  mullions 
of  the  east  window,  which  would  have  been 
the  nearest  target,  and  another,  a  little 
larger,  was  found  in  the  garden  of  the 
Rectory,  on  higher  ground,  which  would 
have  been  in  the  line  of  fire.  The  village  lie« 
about  seventeen  miles  south  from  Aylesbury 
and  eight  north  from  Uxbridge,  in  the 
Mlaboume  valley,  which  cuts  through  the 
Chilterns  between  those  two  towns. 

In  the  tower  of  the  church  used  to  be  a 
number  of  pike*,  rather  short,  the  heads 
of  which  are  said  to  have  been  made  from 
files,  and  which  were  *'  intended  for  the 
arming  of  the  people."  The  few  now  re- 
maining are  in  the  Milton  Cottage  (where 
Milton  wrote  'Paradise  Regained^)  in  the 
viUage.  When  could  it  have  been  intended 
to  arm  the  people  T  and  what  truth  can 
there  he  in  the  Cromwell  story  ? 
R.  W.  Pmpps, 

Co),  late  Royal  Artillery.

The  inditer  of  this  rather  turgid  and 
pedantic  code  may  have  been  the  son  of 
Francis  Vargas-Macciucca,  Marquis  of  Vatolla 
(1699-1785),  celebrated  for  his  classical  and 
linguistic  attainments.

A Paddeln),  the  phrase  "  De  Hase  brouet  " 

is  still  current  in  Low  German,  in  the  very 
same   sense   as   quoted   from    the    Bremen 

*  Worterbuch.'  But  there  is,  likewise,  an 
ancient  and  metaphorical  folk-lore  expression 
which  still  survives  in  modem  High 
German  poetical  and  proverbial  language, 
regarding  a  low  mist  or  cloud  closely  pressing 
upon  the  mountain  or  meadow.  People 
say,  that  subterranean  goblins  are  brewmg 
when  such  clouds  or  vapours  rise,  so  to 
speak,  out  of  their  kitchen.  And  again, 
*^The  hare  has  brewed  "  (•*  Siehe  da  brauet 
•der  has  im  weisslichen  Dampf  auf  der 
Wiese,"  Voas's  *  IdyU,*   quoted  in  Grimm's 

*  Worterbuch.'  ii.  322).  H.  Kbebs.

Sassen '  proves  that  some  twenty-five  years 
ago  the  phrase  '*  De  Hase  brouet  "  was  still

down,  obscuring  everything,  one  might 
have  tliought  that  Nature  lived  hard  by, 
and  was  brewing  on  a  large  scale." 

The  hare  plays  more  than  one  part  in  the 
imagination  of  the  people  of  Northern 
Germany  ;   why  not  that  of  a  brewer  ?

xise."    Now    if    this    is    the    meaning    of

A  IMIKLIS   MAKS  A   MICKLE  "  (10  S. 

vii.    II,   112). — In  connexion 

baa  bot*n  itatd  on  thin  8ubject.  it 

►  •muw  to  mention  the  occurrence 

jrut-  f^'i{  fi|.  ,.r»Tv'  varinnt.      In 

i   hy  a  lady, 

'.    I.,      j.i.it.i.     uf    February, 

h«<i  oocaMJoiv  to  state  the  difii- 

detective  over  a  very  intricate 


and   puzzUng  case.     The  suspicions   of  the 
expert  have  fallen  upon  a  young  lady,  and 
at  a  critical  stage  of  his  investigations  he 
pauses    to    consider    the   position    in    all    its 
bearings.     He    is    represented    as    running 
over  in  his  mind  all   that  can  possibly  be 
advanced  agaLn>)t  the  su.^pect ;    and  finding 
that  in  the  aggregate  it  does  not  amount  to 
muclu  he  is  fain   to  solace    himself  with   a 
philoHophical  summary.     "  Even  if  she  did 
all  these  tilings,"   he  sententiously  reflects, 
"  it  might  not  ajnount  to  much,  but  it  is  the 
miokle  tliat  makes  the  little,  and  the  little 
the    lot."     It    would    be    curious   to    know 
what    meaning    in    atta(;hed    to    the    term 
'*  mickle "    by    the    writer    of    this    cryptic 
intimation.     Apparently,  tlie  belief  is  that 
tlie  signification  is  akin  to  that  of  the  Latin 
hilum,    out    of    which    came    nihitum    and 
nifnl,  and  which  one  school  of  etymologiata 
uaod  to  define  as  "  the  black  spot  on  a  bean.*' 
The  statement  as  it  stands  affords  a  striking 
illustration    of   how    a    faintly    remenibered 
proverb  may  be  completely  misrepresented.

HoRNSEY  Wood  House  :  Harringay 
House  (10  S.  vii.  106,  167).— If  I  may  be 
permitted  to  say  so.  Prof.  Skeat's  com- 
munication on  this  subject  is  not  quite  so 
clear  as  usual.  Hering  is  either  a  personal 
or  a  tribal  name.  From  the  passage  in  the 
*A.-S.  Chronicle,*  avib  anno  603,  it  would 
seem  to  be  a  i>er8onal  name,  possibly  of 
Scandina\*ian  origin,  and  allied  to  Hsering, 
which  is  found  in  runes  on  a  comb  in  the 
Copenliagen  Museimi.  But  if  a  personal 
name,  the  -inga  would  not  be  a  genitive 
plural.  Tlie  genitive  singular  -es  is,  how- 
ever, constantly  slurred  over  in  place-names. 
We  find  a  similar  instance  in  the  neighbour- 
ing parish  of  Hackney,  the  eg  of  Hacun  or 
Hakon.  It  may  be  noted  that  in  two  fines, 
dated  respectively  1350  and  1357,  the  word 
is  spelt  Haryngeseye,  from  wliich  the  modem 
Hornsoy  is  of  course  derived  (Middlesex 
'  Feet  of  Fines,'  ed.  Hardy  and  Page,  i.  145, 
152). 

There    is   a    pretty    accurate   account   of 
Hornsey   Wood   House   in   '  Old   and  New 
London,'  v.  430,  431.     The  map  on  p.  432 
shows  that  "  Haringay  "  House  and  Hornsey  ' 
Wood  House  were  quite  distinct,  and  situ-  , 
ated  at  some  distance  from  eacli  other.  | 

W.  F.  Prideaux.  j 
I  knew  Hornsey  Wood  House  (or  Tavern) 
very  well  when  I  was  a  lad  in  the  early  fifties 
of  the  last  century.  It  was  pleasantly 
situated  on  rising  ground  which  afforded 
a  fine  view  of  the  surroimding  coimtry,  and 
was  a  favourite  resort  for  schoolchildren 
coming  out  in  summer-time  in  vans,  &c., 
from  London,  or  robust  pedestrians  taking 
footpatlis  through  what  were  called  "  South- 
gate  fields,"  and  crossing  the  (then)  rural 
track  known  as  the  Seven  Sisters  Road. 
The  wood  was  a  small  one,  and  composed 
(if  I  rightly  remember)  of  white-barked 
birch  trees. 

Beyond  the  wood,  to  the  north,  the  clear 
and  gentle  New  River  wound  its  course 


through  green  meadows.     It  abw 
crayfish,  hving  in  holes  in  the  ban 
the  surface  of  the  water.     Theee  w 
catch  by  means  of  a  baited  fish-b 
to  the  end  of  a  stout  bit  of  wire,  f 
inches    long,    the    other    end    of 
fastened  to  a  short  stick  of  eighte 
or  so.     The  modus  operandi  was 
the  bank,  find  a  hole,  and  insert  t 
wire,  moving  it  gently  about.     Th 
(crablike)  would  seize  the  bait  with 
and  hold  on  tenaciously  enough 
it  to  be  drawn  out  of  its  hole  ai 
safely  on  the  bank. 

In  recent  years  I  have  tried  ii 
locate  the  old  place — miles  of  ho 
supplanted  what  were  once  mile 
and  open  country.

Thibkeli.  Family  (10  S.  vi.  229).— Your 
correspondent  will  find  references  to  pedi- 
grees of  this  family  in  Marshall's  '  Genea- 
logist's Guide/  1903,  p.  769,  sub  Thirkeld 
and  Thirkell. 

The  name  Threlkeld  occurs  in  the  registers 
of  Hebbum,  Northumberland,  from  1732  to 
1768,  and  relates  to  those  of  Morpeth  and 
Tritlington. 

The  following  notes  of  the  name  may  be 
of  interest : — 

1.  A  Chas.  Thirldll  witnessed  a  nuurriage 
at  Ryton,  Durham,  in  1757. 

2.  Elizabeth  Thirkill,  of  Hampsthwaite, 
Yorks,  married  Wm.  Colbeck,  of  Ripley,  by 
banns,  at  the  former  place,  20  Aug.,  1729. 

3.  Thomas  ThirkhUl  was  buried  irom  the 
workhouse  in  Kippax  Churchyard,  Yorks, 
7  June,  1810. 

The  name  "  Tliirkyld  "  appears  on  p.  189, 
vol.  xi.  of  The  Ancestor.

Spelling  Changes  (10  S.  vi.  403,  450, 
493  ;    vii.  51,  171).— Mr.  T.  Wilson  seems 
to  be  unaware  that  the  pressure  of  public 
opinion  forced  President  Roosevelt  to  with- 
draw his  proposals  for  spelling   "  reform," 
and  they  have  now  been  relegated  to  the 
"  mi^ht-have-beens."     The  truth  is  that  the 
English,  both  in  this  country  and  America, 
are  temperamentally  Tories,  and  averse  to 
change     on     merely     theoretical     grounds. 
Tliey  will  not  be  dictated  to  by  philologists, 
even  if  backed  by  the  whole  strength  of  the 
British  Academy  and  the  sella  curulis.     No 
one  denies  that  there  is  some  need  of  reform, 
but  it  will  have  to  be  worked  out  gradually 
by    a    natural    process    of    evolution.     Our 
great-grandfathers    wrote    physick,    musick, 
chuse^  and  so  on ;   and  I  dare  say  our  great- 
grandchildren    will     write     meter^     specter, 
theater,   which   after   all    have   Elizabethan 
sanction,  as  well  as  honor,  favor,  and  others 
of  that  kind.     But  I  should  be  sorry  to  lose 
*'  our  Iving  and  Govemour." 

It  lias  often  been  iu*ged  that  our  variegated 
system  of  spelling  renders  the  task  both  of 
teacher  and  pupil  more  difficult,  and  prolongs 
the  rudimentary  period  of  learning.  But 
wliy  teach  English  spelling  at  all  ?  Bojrs 
learn  French  and  German  without  the  aid 


of  a  spelling-book.  Spelliii|;  is 
matter  of  memory,  and  aa  mtellif 
learns  to  spell  words  from  the  booke 
Some  people,  like  Robert  Louis  S 
can  never  learn  to  spell  properly, 
always  shaky  about  the  words  c 
ie  and  ei ,  to  say  nothing  of  great* 
But  what  matter  ?  Such  words  < 
rally  be  slurred  over  in  ordinary  co 
ence  ;  while  if  the  writer  is  a  conti 
the  press,  which  will  probably  be 
in  rune  instances  out  of  ten,  the 
reader  can  always  set  matters  righi 
sonally  think  we  may  safely  1* 
spelling  question  to  take  care  of  itsc 
W.  F.  Pbij

PcrnyUinm  Veneru.   Latine  inoerto  auctorisGrtece 

Hugonis  H.  Johnson.  (Oxford,  Blackwell.) 
This  little  pamphlet  contains  the  remarkable  poem 
concerning  the  coming  of  spring  which  has  been 
.ascribed  to  Hadrian's  time,  and  a  (^reek  version  in 
the  same  metre  by  Mr.  Johnson,  an  Oxford  scholar 
HOW  resident  in  France,  we  believe.  Mr,  Johnson  has 
not  succeeded  in  producing  anything  satisfactory  for 
the  famous  refrain  of  the  iK)em,  and  he  allows  him- 
:flelf  certain  liberties  of  style  and  metre.  But  his 
version  as  a  whole  is  distinguished  for  taste  and 
resource.  The  repetition  of  the  verb  "tacere"  at 
the  end  is  no  doubt  tedious,  but  we  do  not  think 
it  well  to  introduce  variety  by  such  a  phrase  an 
Movtrav  tlKov  tveroikiioaQ,  No  one  without  the 
Latin  would  take  this  to  mean  "perdidi  Musam 
taceudo."  We  doubt  tlXov  altogether,  and  should 
At  any  rate  read  Siv  dvavioc  instead  of  t^trrofAriffae. 

To  "The  New  Universal  Library"  of  Messrs. 
Routledge  &  Sons  has  been  made  an  all-imixirtant 
-contribution  in  the  sha)>e  of  numerous  volumes  of 
-John  Buskin's  works,  now  first  free,  as  roearde 
many  of  them,  from  copyright  restrictions.  Even 
yet  a  portion  only  of  them  has  ainieared  iu  the 
shilling  form  in  which  they  are  all  before  long  to 
ai>i>eal  to  the  general  public.  Those  now  issued 
are,  however,  in  themselves  a  treasury,  and  con- 
(stitute  the  foundation  of  a  reputation  for  English 
i)ro80  such  as  no  previous  writer  has  enjoyed.  First 
in  the  number  comes  Modern  Paiutcm,  appearing, 
.as  hitherto,  in  five  volumes,  with  315  illustrations 
and  plates,  and  one  coloured  plate.  With  this 
-work  began  what  was  most  representative  in  the 
niuclcrn  teaching  of  Ruskin  and  his  succesHora. 
Next,  in  three  volumes  with  173  illustrations  and 
7 (lath's,  and  seven  coloure<l  plates,  is  the  Sfotien  of 
Venice.,  with  its  warm  defence  of  the  Pre-Raphael- 
ites.  Earlier  than  this  came  The  Seven  Lamps  of 
Architecture,  which  did  so  much  to  encourage  the 
(iothic  revival  of  the  middle  of  the  last  century. 
Lectures  on  Architecture  and  Painting  has  23  illus- 
trations, and  EfementH  of  DrauHuy  48.  Unto  this 
LdJit  and  The  Two  Patlis  are  among  the  less  ambi- 
tious, hut  the  more  characteristic  of  the  works  now 
repriTited.  'The  Political  Economy  of  Art,'  sub- 
sequently called  A  Jou  for  Ernr,  comes  i)enultiniate 
in  the  list  of  republications:  while  the  last  (long 
•  «)ne  of  the  scarcest  of  his  works  and  the  most  in 
demand)  is  the  Selections  from  Ruskin's  Writings.

L  Albert  II.  founded 


tlie  order  in  that  year,  wliieli  is  manifestly 
wi'ong,  as  tlie  emperor  died  in  1439.  Some 
of  the  authors,  however,  have  noticed  the 
glaring  anaclu*om.sm,  and  get  over  it,  like 
F.  C.  Woodhouse,  £.</..  by  making  the  founder 
Albert  111.,  who  is  unknown  to  history,  or 
Maximilian  11.,  who  appeared  too  late  on 
the  scene. 

The  '  Uiccionario  Universal,'  by  Fran- 
cisco de  Paula  Mellado  (Madrid,  1848), 
describes  the  order  as  follows  : — 

**  y»M//i.— Ordeiuk'ettbfl.Ueria  creado  en  Aleumnia 
«'ii  l.'»*i2  M  auU'H,  laieeto  que  cotimta  ijue  en  ««stc  ano 
t^l  fniiK.'riulor  AlWrto  11.  la  diti  a  Diego  de  Vftleriu" 

But  IJiego  was  bom  about  1420,  and  the 
emiKTor  fUed  in  1 439,  as  already  stated ; 
and  all  that  1  can  find  about  the  former  is 
that  he  was  at  Prague  in  1437,  when 
Albert  II.,  King  of  the  Romans,  made 
se%'eral  barons  in  his  presence.  This  is  his 
own  statement  in  the  '  Tratado  de  los 
Hieptos  '  (on  the  penultimate  page),  and  no 
mention  whatever  iti  made  of  any  order 
liaving  been  conferred  on  himself.  It  is 
another  author,  Franci^cuj*  Mennenius,  who 
is  to  be  held  responsible  for  dtsseminating 
the  error.  He  in  hia  turn  quotes  Hierony- 
muis  Ronianua  as  Ids  authority  in  the  follow- 
ing passage  : — 

'•Reftfrt  ot  Hienmynnifi  Roiimrms  ex  hist^ri* 
hisimniea  Regis  Joaniiis,  tPiiifjurt*  .SiifiHinuiidi  et 
Alltcrti  Imi)p.  fioiuisse  in  tierumiiirt  tree  iiiai(OieK 
iirdTticH  ei|«estrcs,  net  iion  iJoynem  [*«V.']  I>ida«vini 
de  Val»*ra  Hispaiiuni,  ]>rolwita'  iVutitiifliuiH  e.jiiitem 
all  eo  Alberto,  tribvis  luilititf  ineij^nibus  fiijNge  con- 
rlec-oratum ;  Dracoiiieo  iiem]»e,  tAmqnani  u  Rfge 
Huiigaria-  ;  Tiiwiiiio  ut  a  iwe  Bohemia*,  et  oollari 
l)irt^M|ili)iftnim  aqiiik  t'uridiria  (quj*:-  et  F«»lninjnim 
Fieiium  iji  CaTii|K>  ndwo  tensera  vM)  exontnto.  ut  n 
Once  AiiKtrise.'  'Deliciiv*  E^jut'strium  Onliuura,' 
Antwcrji,  ItiJIi,  p.  156. 

Either  Hioronymua  Romanus  or  his 
authority  was  probably  mistaken  about  the 
individual  upon  whom  the  three  decorations 
were  conferred  by  Albert  II.,  because 
another  Spanish  hidalgo  and  traveller,  Pero 
Tafur,  records  in  his  '  Andances  e  Viajes ' 
tiiat  lie  w^as  presented,  in  1438,  to  Albert  II. 
at  Breslau,  on  which  occasion  the  emperor 
conferred  on  him  the  identical  tluree  orders 
of  cliivalry,  viz.,  the  Hungarian  Order  of 
the  Dragon,  the  Austrian  Order  of  the  Eagle* 
and  "  el  Tu.senique,  que  quiere  dezir  tovaja 
que  es  de  Bohemia,"  that  is,  the  Bohemian 
Order  of  the  Towel,  which  was  no  doubt  the 
Tusin. 

Tafur'a  modem  editor,  M.  Jimenez  de  la 
EHX>ada,  explains  in  a  note  that  the  Tuaen- 
ique  was  the  "  6rden  de  la  Toalla  6  Banda,'* 
and  that  its  device  was  "  ima  cruz  verde  de 
la  milsma  forma  que  La  Montesa  en  cam^o 


222 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES,  [los.  vn  MABcaa^im 


rojo,"  which  agrees  with  Mellado's  Btate« 
ment  that  the  device  of  the  Tasin  was 
"  sobre  Tin  manto  encamado,  una  cruz  liana 
de  verde."  The  cross  of  the  Order  of  Our 
Lady  of  Montesa  is  depicted  in  H.  Schulze's 
large  work  on  *  Military  Orders/  and  is  of 
the  same  shape  as  the  red  cross  in  the  arms 
of  Switzerland. 

Another  modem  author,  A.  M.  Perrot  in 
his  *Ordres  de  Chevalerie '  (Paris,  1820), 
repeats  the  statement  that  the  cross  of  the 
Order  of  the  Tusin  was  green,  and  gives 
an  Ulustration  of  it ;  but  1  have  been  un- 
able to  verify  his  description  of  the  colour 
and  shape  of  the  device  from  any  contem- 
porary source. 

With  regard  to  the  name  of  the  order,  in 
modem  Bohemian  a  towel  is  called  rtUshnik 
— I  spell  the  word  phonetically  to  avoid 
the  use  of  Slavonic  characters — which  is 
similar  to  the  German  Handtuch,  i.e.,  a 
"  hand-cloth  "  ;  but  I  am  open  to  correction. 

The  Order  of  the  Tusin — if  that  is  the 
correct  name — is,  as  will  be  seen,  not  a 
mythical  one,  as  W.  Maigne  avers,  but,  on 
the  other  hand,  very  little  is  known  about  it. 
"  We  do  not  know  when  and  by  whom  it  was 
founded,"  writes  Biedenfeld  ;  "  we  do  not 
know  its  rules  ;  the  origin  and  meaning  of 
its  name  are  also  imknown ;  but  the  exist- 
ence of  the  order  in  past  times  can  be  proved 
by  documentaiy  evidence."  Unfortunately 
ho  gives  no  reference  to  the  documents  in 
question.  L.  L.  K. 

LONGFELLOW. 

(See  ante,  p.  201.) 
*  Hyperion  '  was  written  while  Long- 
fellow was  still  xmder  the  shadow  of  a  ^at 
grief;  and  to  understand  it  aright  it  is 
requisite  to  remember  this.  In  September, 
1831,  he  was  married  to  Mary  Storer  Potter. 
Her  character  and  person  are  described  as 
being  alike  lovely  :  she  had  dark  hair,  with 
eyes  of  deep  blue  which  lighted  a  coimten- 
ance  "  singularly  attractive  with  the  ex- 
I)ression  of  a  gentle  and  affectionate  disposi- 
tion." Husband  and  \viie  were  devoted  to 
each  other  ;  never  was  a  home  more  happy 
than  theirs.  But  the  sweet  companionship 
was  to  last  only  four  brief  years.  From 
Rotterdam,  on  the  28th  of  November,  1835, 
Longfellow  wrote  to  his  father  that  his  wife 
"  had  again  fallen  ill,  and  that  his  anxiety 
was  very  great."  On  the  following  day  she 
died,  "  closing  her  life  by  a  still  more 
peaceful  death;  and  though  called  away 
when  life  was  brightest,  yet  going  without  a 
murmur  and  in  perfect  wimngness  to  the 


bosom  of  her  God."  In  the  londy  hm 
which  followed,  the  bereaved  hnitii 
would  repeat  the  hymns  which  had  sootM 
her  last  hours  and  dwell  upon  her  pnmiH 
"  I  will  be  with  you  and  watch  over  yon.' 
Less  than  a  month  after  her  death  aaDtha 
sorrow  came  to  him  by  the  death  of  hi 
brother-in-law  and  deaffeat  friend,  Geng 
W.  Pierce,  of  whom  he  wrote,  after  tirent; 
years  had  passed,  **  I  have  never  ceased  t 
feel  that  in  liis  death  something  was  take 
from  my  life  which  could  never  be  restond. 
His  poem  *The  Footsteps  of  Angeb'  i 
consecrated  to  the  memories  of  his  wife  0 
his  friend,  and  the  remembrance  that  th^ 
*'  had  lived  and  died  "  consoled  him  in  h 
loneliness.

The  clever  dwarf  of  tin-  mnrionett^  tlieatre 
of  Liege  a  generation  ago  retained  the  cha- 
racteristics of  Pacolet  the  magician.  But  he 
was  generally  identified  with  the  little  devil 
— diablotin — in  the  service  of  a  sorcerer. 

Pacolet  is  a  French  word  not  admitted  to 
the  *  Dictionnaire '  of  the  Academy,  but 
synonymous  with  cheville.  Hence*  it  is^ 
suggest<>d  that  cheval  d  pacolet  has  been 
transformed  into  ch£val  de  pacolet,  and  thence 
into  cheval  de  Pacolet,  thus  giving  the  magic 
horse  a  magician  rider. 

Prof.  Chau\'in  thinks  that  the  storj'  of 
Pacolet  may  be  linked  by  the  '  Cl<k)mades  ' 
of  Adenet-le-Roi  and  the  '  MeUacin '  of 
Girard  d' Amiens,  probably  through  a  Spanish 
source,  with  the  episode  of  the  magic  horse 
to  he  found  in  n^ore  than  one  form  in  different 
editions  of  *  Tlie  Thousand  and  One  Nights.' 

KWdently  Steele's  *'  Mr.  Pacolet "  can 
claim  a  pedigree  as  long,  and  perhaps  a.s 
veracious,  as  some  of  those  to  be  found  in 
Burke.  William  E.  A.  Axon.

Berwick  Law. — Recent  historians  of 
Scottish  literature,  fired  with  zeal  for  a 
ready-made  theory,  have  striven  to  show 
that*  the  record  of  their  country's  poetr\' 
ends  with  the  work  of  Robert  Burns.  The 
impartial  student  may,  howe\'er,  find  pro- 
ducts of  a  somewhat  later  date  not  unworthy 
of  consideration.  There  is,  for  instance,  a 
notable  achievement  to  be  set  to  the  credit 
of  Robert  Tannahill  (1774-1810),  a  hand- 
loom  weaver,  whose  mastery  of  lyrical  form 
is  at  once  genuine  and  distiiictive.  Four  or 
five  of  his  songs — some  of  them,  i>erhaps,. 
indebted  to  their  musical  setting  by  the 
author's  friend  R.  A.  Smith — have  liad  a 
steady  popularity  in  Scotland,  and  tli^' 
fully  deserve  a  wider  recognition.  As  it  is 
possible  that  the  Englt.sh  reader  may  come 
uiion  the  standard  edition  of  Taimahill's 
poems,  edited  by  David  Seniple.  it  may  be 
well  to  draw  attention  to  a  note  on  the  song 
'  \A'reck  on  GliK»my  Isle  of  May.'  which 
occiu^  at  p.  278  of  "the  vohmie.  The  poet 
rej)resents  a  forlorn  damsel  giving  voice  to 
her  sorrow  over  the  sViipwreck  by  which  she 
has  been  for  ever  bereft  M  her  lover.  The 
picture  is  in  some  respect-s  the  converse  of  that 
%vhich  is  given  in  Biu*ns's  "  Go,  fetch  to  me  a 
)nnt  of  wine,"  and  the  Firth  of  Forth,  with 
Berwick  Law  overlooking  it.  is  the  scene  of 
both  lyri«».  Tannahill's  desolate  maiden 
thus  opens  her  lament : —

Garrick  '  (1868*  i.  126)  gives  an  incorrect

Tate   Wilkinson   paid   an   unprofessional 
visit  to  the  Jubilee  in  August  or  September, 
1762,   but  was   disappointed  witli   his  ex- 
perience.    "At  Preston,"  he  writes  in  his 
Memoirs,* 

"we  found  very  Iwwl  accommodation,  very  dear, 
very  dirty,  and  much  crowded.  The  rirocessitm 
was  tolerable,  but  not  worth  the  trouble  or  expence 
of  a  journey  to  Bee  it ;  indeed,  I  was  verj-  glad  on 
the  second  day  to  i)er8uade  Mr.  SoM-don  to  quit 
Preston  for  Chester,  for  it  was  all  confusion  and 
mire,  excei>t  the  main  street,  which  I  recollect  is 
ai>aciou8  and  handsome,  but  it  was  the  crowd  and 
inconvenienoy  that  made  us  glad  to  dejwirt." 
Wilkinson  also  tells  us  that  during  the 
festival  of  1762  Hull  and  Younger's  players 
came  from  Birmingham  to  support  the 
London  performers  who  had  been  engaged.

These  marches  to  Baums  or  Balmes  were 
formerly  known  as  '*  General  Days,"  of 
which  there  were  three  (one  in  May,  June, 
and  August),  and  had  been  regularly  held 
since  the  Kestoration.

'  VirrLE"=ViCTx:AL  (10  8.  vii.  188). — 
It  is  worth  while  to  remind  your  readers 
that  the  original  spelling  was  vitaiWc,  a 
spelling  which  occurs  more  than  a  dozen 
times  in  Chaucer,  and  lasted  down  to  1530, 
when  Palsgrave  gave  us  the  equivalent  form 
vytaile.  But  witli  the  revival  of  learning, 
as  it  was  called,  the  day  came  when  the 
English  people  awoke  to  the  amazing  dis- 
covery that  the  Old  Frendi  vitaiUes  repre- 
sented a  Latin  victualia  :  and  they  were  so 
intoxicateti  thereby  that  they  celebrated 
it  by  the  idiotic  insinuation  of  a  c  before  the 
/,  in  order  that  this  wonderful  fact  might 
never  bo  lost,  and  under  the  delusion  that 
etymological  spelling  means  a  worship 
of  the  letter  without  any  regard  to  the  sound. 
And  now  we  all  liave  to  insert  this  idiotic  c  ; 
for  such  is  its  right  epithet. 

Walter  W.  Skeat. 
Scottish  writers  use  vittaly  vittel,  and 
viUU.  In  the  *  Scottish  Dictionary  '  Jamie- 
son,  aays,  **  Bucfuin-vittal  is  apphed  to  meal 
of  which  the  *  twa  part  is  aits,  and  the  third 
bear  '  "  ;  and  he  adds  that  it  is  said  of  an 
untrustworthy  person,  *'  He's  Buchan-vittal 
that."

NOTES  AND  QUERIES,  [lo  s.  vil  mabch« 


regarding  the  Irish  Rebellion  of  1798,  in 
consequence  of  baseless  or  grossly  exag- 
gerated charges  brought  against  offidMB 
who  lived  in  those  very  difficult  times,  and 
because  (quoting  a  letter  from  Mr.  Lecky 
in  my  possession)  "  a  thick  cloud  of  mis- 
representation or  positive  mendacity  hangs 
over  most  of  those  who  played  a  leading 
part  in  them.** 

The  book  illustrated  by  Cruikshank 
referred  to  by  O.  is  one  of  the  misleading 
works,  for  I  presume  that  Maxwell's  '  History 
of  the  Irish  Kebellion  of  1798  *  is  pointed  to. 
Not  less  misleading  is  *The  Lives  of  the 
United  Irishmen.*  Mr.  Lecky  refers  to 
Madden,  the  author,  as  a  **  furious  partisan.*' 
Fitzpatrick,  author  of  *The  Sham  Squire,* 
*  Secret  Service  under  Pitt,'  &c.,  is  also 
misleading,  and  we  certainly  ought  to  be 
reassured  as  to  the  accuracy  of  excerpts  in 
his  books :  his  reputation  for  contributions 
to  serious  history  depends  much  upon  this. 

I  think  your  correspondent  would  accept 
the  authority  of  Mr.  Lecky,  whose  honesty 
in  dealing  vnth  the  subject  of  the  rebellion 
must  be  manifest,  and  I  briefly  quote  from 
his  *  History  of  the  Eighteenth  Century  *  to 
«lucidate  the  statement  which  has  been 
questioned. 

After  recording  in  vol.  viii.  the  arrest  in 
Dublin  of  the  General  Committee  of  the 
United  Irishmen,  Mr.  Lecky  reviews  the 
state  of  affairs  elsewhere  in  Ireland,  and 
proceeds  : — 

"  Some  soldiers  of  the  North  Cork  Militia  are 
said  to  have  invented  the  pitched  cap  of  linen  or 
thick  brown  jjaper,  which  was  fastened  with  burn- 
ing pitch  to  the  victim's  head,  and  could  not  be 
torn  off  without  tearing  out  tlie  hair  or  lacerating 
the  skin.  One  soldier  obtained  a  si)ecial  reputation 
by  varying  this  torture.  He  was  accustomed  to  cut 
tne  hair  of  the  victims  still  shorter,  to  rub  into  it 
moistened  guniwwder,  and  then  to  set  it  on  tire."— 
P.  18. 

*' Young  subalterns,  sergeants  of  militia,  common 
^soldiers,  ordered  and  iieri)etrated  these  things,  and 
it  is  but  too  probable  that  they  often  acted  on  the 
whisiKjred  suggestion  of  a  j)rivate  enemy." — P.  19. 

"The  torture  of  the  pitched  cap,  which  never 
before  appears  to  have  l)een  known  in  Ireland,  was 
now  introduced  by  the  North  Cork  Militia,  and 

excited  fierce  terror  and  resentment It  was  in 

the  week  ]»reviou8  to  the  rebellion  that  these 
excesses  reached  their  height."— P.  77. 

Concerning  Joseph  Holt,  the  capable  rebel 
leader,  who  lurked  in  the  Wicklow  Hills, 
Mr.  Lecky  states:  "The  picture  he  gives 
of  the  barbarities  on  both  sides  is  probably 
drawn  with  no  unfaithful  touch.'*  He  then 
quotes  from  the  memoirs  of  Holt,  e.g. : — 

"Many  of  the  cruelties  of  the  rebels  were  in 
retaliation  of  the  previous  enormities  committed 
wpon  them  by  the  yeomanry,  who  in  their  turn 


revenged  themselves  with  increased  serin 
thus  all  the  kindlier  and  best  feelings  of  1 

were    eradicated The    rebels   were 

atrocious  or  refined  in  their  cruelties,  bati 
excited  by  the  heads  and  hands  over  tha 

considered  their  acts   meritorious Ea 

accused  the  other  of  cruelty  and  barb 
humanity,  and  the  aocusation  on  both  i 
just"— Pi..  237-8.

and  uneducated  middlemen,  in 
where  there  were  few  resident  gent 
often  made  justices  of  peace  and  fl 
manry  and  militia  officers  ;  that  dv 
rebellion  they  rendered  real  service,  1 
worst  qualities  aj^peared  in  the 
military  Ucence  which  followed  (pp.

Cathay  (10  S.  vii.  168).— The  modem 
pronunciation  i»  stated  in  the  editorial 
note,  but  there  can  be  no  doubt  our  fore- 
fathers called  it  Cat-hay,  or  sounded  the  th 
as  simple  L  Shakespeare  writes  for  the 
adjective  either  Cataian  or  Catayan.  Th© 
spelUng  with  th  seems  confined  to  modern 
English.  The  Persians  write  Ivhatay,  the 
Russians  and  Bohemians  Kitay,  the  Poles 
Kitaj,  the  Roumanians  Chitai.  In  the 
Slavonic  and  Roumanian  tongues  it  is  used, 
not  only  as  a  name  for  Cliina,  but  also  to 
denote  '  a  kind  of  cotton  stuff  (English 
nankeen).  In  Moscow  the  whole  commercial 
quarter  is  comprehensively  styled  Kitay- 
Oorod,  "  Cathay-Town.'' 

J  AS.  Platt,  Jun. 

Khitan,  or  Khatai,  is  the  collective  najno 
of  several  Mongolian  tribes  who  in  the  tenth 
century  inhabited  Eastern  Manchuria,  and, 
conquering  the  northern  provinces  of  China, 
maintained  their  rvde  there  for  200  years 
(till     1123).     Their     name,     m    W\q     Wwaa 


236 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES,  [lo  s.  f  il  m«ch 


Khitai,  Khata,  Cathay,  &c.,  thus  came  to  be 
used  for  nearly  a  thousand  years  as  the 
name  of  China  among  the  people  of  Central 
Asia  and  those  European  nations  which 
became  acquainted  with  China  through  that 
cliannel.  Alfred  Sydney  Lewis. 

Library,  Constitutional  Club. 

Napoleon's  Ca»rla.oe  (10  S.  vii.  170). — 
When  the  carriage  came  into  the  posses- 
sion of  William  Bullock,  and  was  exhibited 
at  the  London  Museum  (afterwards  the 
Egyptian  Hall),  a  20-page  illustrated  *  De- 
scription and  History '  was  on  sale.  The 
several  statements  that  it  contains  relative 
to  the  capture  of  the  carriage  agree  with 
Blucher's  letter  and  the  affidavit  of  Jean 
Homn,  the  coachman,  that  Major  von 
Keller  had  a  mixed  force  of  two  battalions 
of  Fusiliers  from  the  4th  Corps  of  the 
Prussian  army,  some  Tyrolese,  and  a  few 
other  Prussian  light  infantry. 

Aleck  Abrahams. 

al9,  Hillmarton  Road,  N. 

Sibome  says : — 

"  The  moHt  valuable  and  meet  interesting  object 
oonriated  of  Napoleon's  travelling  carriage,  which 
with  all  its  contents  fell  into  the  hands  of  the 
Fnsilier-battalion  of  the  15th  Regiment  (Prussian)." 

W.  P.  M. 

Napoleon's  travelling  carriage  was  cap- 
tured by  the  Fusilier  battalion  of  the  15th 
Regiment  of  Prussian  Infantry  at  Genappe, 
three  or  four  miles  away  from  the  battle- 
field of  Waterloo,  about  an  hour  after  the 
battle  was  over.  Some  English  painters 
have  represented  Napoleon  escaping  from 
the  carriage.  This  is  a  mistake.  Napoleon 
had  not  been  in  the  carriage  during  any  part 
of  that  day.  Wlien  not  seated  on  a  chau*  at 
a  table,  or  walking  about  with  his  hands 
behind  him,  he  was  on  horseback,  although 
suffering  from  a  complaint  which  made 
it  painful  for  him  to  ride. 

Waterlooensis.

(10  S.  vii.  168). — Lely's  portrait  of  Lady

should  be  no  difficulty  in  tracing  this  portrait, 
of  which  Miss  Wotton  could  probably 
obtain  (or  see)  a  photograph  at  the  photo- 
grapher's stall  at  the  Victoria  and  Albert 
Museum.  W.  Roberts.

The  shilling  of  1504  gives  tlie  first  genuine 
portrait ;  but  I  s^uppose  the  heads  of  the 
kings  on  the  coinage  before  the  assumption 
by  Edward  I,  of  the  conventional  Gothic 
full -face  were  generally  intended  to  be 
portraits  more  or  leas — probably  rather  less 
than  more.  Compare  the  Conqueror  on 
his  pennies  witli  his  likeness  on  the  Bayeux 
Tapeetry,  A.  R.  Bavlev. 

The  text  *'  Jesua  autem  transiens  per 
medium  illorum  ibat  '*  was,  I  believe, 
regarded  as  a  protection  against  robbers 
when  carried  about  in  the  pocket  inscribed 
on  coin  or  Tnedal.  Was  it  in  concession  to 
that  belief  that  our  kings  adopted  this 
legend,  thus  giving  a  kind  of  Government 
security — not '  worth  very  much — to  the 
holders  of  their  gold  pieces  ?

The  Chiltern  Hundreds  (10  S.  ii.  441, 
516  ;  iii.  18,  114).— At  the  third  of  the  above 
references  Mr.  Francis  G.  Hai^y  gives  as 
the  name  of  an  authoritative  work  on  the 
subject  *The  Stewardship  of  the  CJhiltem 
Hundreds,*  by  F.  S.  Parry,  C.B.,  published 
by  Eyre  &  Spottiswoode  in  1893.  I  have 
failed  to  find  this  book  at  either  the  British 
Museum  or  the  Guildhall ;  it  does  not  appear 
in  Forteecue'fl  catalogue  ;  and  in  reply  to  an 
inquiry  Messrs.  Eyre  &  Spottiswoode  state 
that  they  do  not  publish  it.  I  shall  therefore 
be  grateful  for  further  information  as  to  this 
book. 

The  interesting  article  on  the  word 
"Chiltem"  in  the  *  N.E.D.*  gives  no  ex- 
planation of  the  origin  of  the  word  ;  cuid  as 
its  list  of  early  references  is  not  exhaustive, 
I  venture  to  set  down  here  all  those  known 
to  me,  in  the  hope  that  I  may  be  referred 
to  others,  and  that  possibly  some  explanation 
of  the  name  may  be  forthcoming. 

The  earliest  use  of  the  word  seems  to  be 
in  the  ancient  document  conveniently  named 
by  the  late  Prof.  Maitland  *The  Tribal 
mdage,*  for  the  original  of  which  Mr.  Corbett 
has  on  good  grounds  claimed  a  seventh- 
century  date.  This  document  attribute 
4,000  hides  to  the  "  Ciltemsoetna.'* 

Two  references  to  the  Chiltern  Forest  are 
found  in  Kemble's  *  Codex  Diplomaticus.' 
The  first  dates  from  1006,  but  is  asterisked 
as  a  possible  forgery  :  it  refers  to  Risborough 
as  "  Hrisebeorgam  margine  luci  Ciltemi 
villula  sBcclesia;  Christe  rite  pertinens — 
Hrisebyrgan  be  Ciltemes  efese  to  Cristes 
cyrceantun  rihte  t6gelicgende."  The  later 
one  is  of  Edward  the  Confessor's  reign,  and 
speaks  of  the  same  place  as  "  innon  Buc- 
cmghamscire  be  Ciltemes  efese." 

The  '  Anglo-Saxon  Chronicle,'  under  date 
1009,  speaks  of  the  Danes  as  going  through 
"  Catem." 

The  chronicles  of  St.  Alban's  Abbey 
record  that  Abbot  Leofstan,  contemporary' 
to  Edward  the  Confessor,  cleared  away  the 
trees  on  either  side  of  Watling  Street,  which 
ran  through  dark  woods  "  a  limbo  Ciltrise 
usque  fere  Londoniam." 

The  name  Chiltern  does  not  appear  any- 
where in  the   Buckinghamshire  section   of 
Domesday  Book.        A.  Morley  Davies. 
Winchmore  Hill,  Amersham. 

*The  Kingdom's  Intelligencer,'  1660- 
1663  (10  S.  vii.  148).— WUl  the  following 
answer  W.  J.  C.'s  inquiry  with  reference  to 
why  The  Kingdom's  Intelligencer  was  dis- 
continued in  1663  ? 

On  31  Aug.,  1663,  Roger  L'Estrange  was 


appointed  "Survwor  of  the  Printing^ 
and  "  Licenser  of  the  Press.'*  Tm 
of  the  press  was  virtually  destn 
Roger's  appointment,  and  no  ne 
could  appear  without  a  licence.  In . 
1664,  L^Estrange  started  a  pape 
was  published  twice  a  week.  The 
edition  was  called  The  InUUigencer, 
Thursday  edition  was  named  Th 
This  paper  was  published  "  with  privi 
but  towards  the  close  of  1665  Roger 
of  favour ;  he  lost  his  appointoi 
The  London  Oazette  took  Uie  plac 
paper.  John  Peth]

LONGFELLOW. 


(See  anU,  pp.  201,  222.) 

On  the  26th  of  February,  1859,  we  find 
in  the  poet's  journal : — 

"  The  thought  struck  me  this  momins,  that  a  very 
good  poem  might  be  written  on  the  Saga  of  King 
Olaf,  who  converted  the  North  to  Christianity. 
Read  the  old  Saga  in  the  *  Heimskrinfda,'  Laing's 
translation.  It  is  very  curious.  *  The  Challenge  of 
Thor'  will  serve  as  a  prelude." 

But  it  was  not  until  November,  1860,  that 
he  took  up  the  task  in  earnest,  when  he 
wrote  fifteen  o|  the  lyrics  in  as  many  days  ; 
and  a  few  days  afterwards  he  completed 
the  whole  of  the  Saga. 

The  framework  of  *The  Wayside  Lin* 
was  determined  later :  the  11th  of  October, 
1862,  is  the  first  indication  we  have  in  his 
diary  ;  and  on  the  31st,  on  *'  a  delicious 
Indian-summer  day,"  he  "  drives  with 
Fields  to  the  old  '  Red  Horse '  Tavern  in 
Sudbury,"  which  used  to  be  a  house  of  call 
for  all  travellers  from  Boston  westward. 
The  title  he  intended  to  give  the  book  was 
*  The  Sudbmy  Tales '  ;  but  when  he  saw 
it  announced  he  disliked  it.  Sumner  cried 
out  against  it,  and  persuaded  him  to  come 
back  to  the  title  of  '  The  Wayside  Lin.' 
All  the    characters  in   it  are  real.     It  was 

Eublished  on  tlie  25th  of  November,  1863. 
y  Ticknor  &  Fields  with  a  first  edition  of 
fifteen  thousand  copies. 

At  this  point  of  my  notes  it  will  be  inter- 
esting to  record  the  reception  given  to  Long- 
fellow's works  in  the  pages  of  The  Athenceum. 

As  far  back  as  the  13th  of  June,  1840, 
that  journal,  in  its  review  of  '  Voices  of  the 
Night,'  had  pointed  out  that  there  was 
"  rising  up  in  America  a  generation  of  poets 
and  scholars  nourished  by  the  old  world, 
but  not  scornful  of  the  new" — travellers, 
who  have  visited 

"  the  galleries  of  Italy  and  the  libraries  of  (Germany, 
and  have  drawn  thence  a  refined  spirit  of  api)re- 
ciation  and  a  fund  of  poetical  associations  which 
ctwt  a  mellower  beauty  uiion  all  the  objects  with 
which  nature  has  glorined  their  home  empire." 

For  a  long  time  The  Athenceum  had  been 
urging  "  the  poetical  doctrine  of  America 


for  the  Americans,"  and  while  tl 
that  country  had  been  **numj 
Marathon  and  the  Seven  HiUs,  t 
and  the  Black  Forest,  in  search 
ore,"  The  Athenceum  had  point 
them  the  rich  lodes  of  fancy  tying 
and  virgin  at  their  feet : — 

"Buried  cities,— vanishing  races,— f( 
mountains,  and  waterfalls,— «11  the  n 
pictorial  elements  in  which  inuginati 
work,— are  there,  in  their  own  great 
we  have  said  aeain  and  aaun,  waiting 
eye  to  see  their  beauty,  and  the  singei 
give  them  voice." 

The  Athenceum  considered  it  to  b 
impeachment  of  the  national  genr 
American  poets  had  neglected*'  t 
tender  chords  of  Indian  8tor\%"  *' 
features  of  the  Red  man,"  *^the 
white    man    in    America,"    in 
"legends   of  European   goblins, 
cities,    and   European   literary   \ 
and  in  reviewing  *  Hiawatha  '  on 
November,  1866,  it  rejoices  that  '. 
"  has  removed  this  literary  reprc 
that  in  *  Hiawatha  *  we  have  **  at 
American  song  by  an  American  si 
"  The  tale  itself  is  beautiful,  fancif 

the  measure  is  novel  as  well  as  the 

is  a  rhymeless  verse,  with  somethii 
music  in  its  rise  and  falL  lu  it  we  heai 
the  swaying  of  trees,  the  whirr  of 
pattermg  of  leaves,  the  trickling  of  wai 
The  Athenceum  hopes  to  find  Long 
"on  a  future  day  still  working  at  this  i 
America  has  foiuid  a  Pactolus  within 
why  Hhould  not  her  itoets  endow  her 
Parnassus  t " 

A  controversy  speedily  arose  on  tl 
of  the  poem,  as  to  whether  it  wa 
Finns  or  Spaniards ;  and  on  tl; 
November  William  Howitt  writes 

"The  measure  which  he[Longfellow]l 
and  which  you  so  justly  praise,  is  the 
nietre  of  Finland.  Almost  the  whole 
Ittudic  jKjetry  is  written  in  it.  It  is  t 
the  •Kalevala,'  the  great  national  e

period  of  the  story  is  when  we  were  at  war 
with  the  United  States.  The  speaker  ia 
the  second  mate  of  an  American  privateer, 
the  True-blooded  Yankee.  He  is  described 
as  "  sour,  ferocious,  quarrelsome."  In  the 
same  chapter  the  American  captain,  who  is 
not  a  blackguard,  speaking  courteously  to 
Lieut.  Frank  Mildmay,  says  *'  You  English- 
men,'* Ac.  Also  a  renegade  sailor,  who  for 
misconduct  is  ordered  to  be  flogged  by  the 
American  captain,  claiming  his  original 
nationaUty  in  the  presence  of  the  English 
lieutenant,  cries  out,  "  I  am  an  Englishman. 
...  .1  am  a  true-bom  Briton."  The  word 
Englishman  occurs  four  times  in  this  ono 
chapter.  The  second  mate  calls  a  British 
war  ship,  which  is  gaining  on  the  privateer, 
"  one  of  John  Bull's  bellowing  calves  of  the 
ocean."     One  wonders  whether  this  phrase, 

Sut  into  the  mouth  of  the  ruffian  mate  by 
[arry^at,  is  now  to  be  accepted. 
"  Britisher  "  appears  in  Farmer's  *  Ame- 
ricansims.  Old  and  New,'  where  may  be 
found  "  blarsted  Britishers,"  auoted  from 
Proctor.  I  have  heard  the  word  used  some- 
tim^i  by  Englishmen  of  little  education^ 
and  am  aware  that  Mr.  Chamberlain  has 
used  the  word  in  speeches. 

A  few  years  ago  in  the  House  of  Commons 
Mr.  Balfour,  referring  to  the  nationaUty  of 
Sir  Robert  Hart  (an  Irishman),  Inspector- 
General  of  Chinese  Maritime  Customs  at 
Peking,  spoke  of  him  as  an  Englishman. 
This  was  met  with  a  remonstrance  from 
some  of  the  Irish  Nationalist  members. 
Mr.  Balfour  excused  himself  for  describing 
Sir  Robert  as  an  Englishman  on  the  ground 
that  there  was  no  other  word  in  the  lan- 
guage which  would  express  his  meaning. 
Robert  Pierpoint. 

The  Last  Will  of  a  Presbytery. — In  his 
'Sketch  of  the  Denominations  of  the  Chris- 
tian World,'  fourteenth  ed.,  1821,  dedicated 
to  Lord  Erskine,  Dr.  John  Evans  quotes 
Benedict's  '  History  of  the  Baptist  Deno- 
mination '  for  the  following  curious  incident. 
About  1801,  in  consequence  of  the  "  great 
revival  "  in  Kentucky,  a  number  of  ministers 


yeans  became  Shaking  Quakers.  The  Springfield 
Presbytery  was  formed  by  ti\^e  ministers,  who 
separated  from  the  Kentucky  Synod,  and  renounced 
the  jurisdiction  of  the  Presbyterian  church.  They 
made  innovations  upon  almost  every  part  of 
Presbyterianism,  but  yet  retained  something  of 
its  form.  But  at  length  they  resolved  to  renounce 
everything  belonging  to  it,  and  made  its  Last  Will 
and  Testament,  as  follows  :— 
"  *The  Presbytery  of  Springfield,  sittuig  at  Cane 


Ridge  in  the  County  of  Bourbon,  hm 
gracious  ProWdenoe,  in  in<»«  than  or 
health,  growing  in  strength  and  sIm 

Iterfeot  soundness  and  composure  o 
:nowing  that  it  is  a])pointed  for  dd 
once  to  die,  and  considering  that  tlM 
such  body  is  verv  uncertain,  do  mak 
this  our  fast  Will  and  Testament,  in 
form  following,  viz. : 

"  *  Imprimi».  we  tcUl  that  this  bod 
solved  and  sink  into  union  with  the  b 

at  lai^e We  ivUl  that  our  u&me  o 

with  Its  Jtti trend  title,  be  forgottei 
that  the  people  henceforth  take  the 
only  sure  guide  to  heaven;  and  as 
offended  with  other  books,  which  st 
I)etition  with  it,  may  cast  them  in  th< 
choose ;  for  it  is  better  to  enter  into  U 
book,  than  having  many  to  be  cast 
Finally,  w«  tnUl  that  all  our  sister  bod 
Bible  carefully,  that  they  may  see  th 
determined,  and  prepare  for  death  bei 
late.  Springfield  Prasbytery,  June  28

In  York  there  was  an  "  old  inliabitant 
familiar  to  me  from  early  days  :  the  figui'«- 
of  Napoleon  1.,  standing  upright,  about  life

Micklegate.  This  was  supposed  to  repre- 
sent him  when  going  to  fight  the  battle  of

prized  lares  et  penate^  of  the  proprietor.

tion, which  represented,  I  suppose,  some 
local  celebrity  or  benefactor.

Brothers  bearing  the  sa&ie  Christian 
Name. — '  N.  &  Q.'  has  recorded  a  number 
of  instances  of  two  brothers  bearing  the  same 
■Christian  name;  butthewillof  Nicholas  Brent, 
of  Stowe  upon  the  Olde,  in  the  diocese  of 
Gloucester,  dated  22  Oct.,  1582  (from  P.C.C. 
Rowe  8),  deserves  a  note,  as  it  mentions 
three  brothers  with  the  same  name,  and  all 
living  at  the  same  time : — 

"  I  give  to  the  poor  people  of  Stowe  1(>».  To  the

'Goldsmith's  Elegy  on  the  Death  of  a 
JVIad  Dog. — When  the  godly  man  of  Isling- 
ton in  this  elegy  was  bitten  by  the  dog,  the 
neighbours  assumed  that  tlie  cur  was  mad, 
and  that  the  man  would  die. 

But  soon  a  wonder  came  to  light 

That  show'd  the  rogues  they  lied — 
The  man  recover'd  of  the  bite, 
The  dog  it  was  that  died.

"  Hammals." — A  witness  from  North- 
umberland stated  in  the  Divorce  Court  the 
other  day  that  this  was  the  name  of  a  gift 
which  a  Northern  mother  expected  those 
she  met  to  present  to  her  new-born  child. 
Can  any  one  give  details  as  to  the  origin 
and  nature  of  this  gift  ?  What  is  the  ety- 
mology of  *'  hammals  '*  ? 

,  J.    HOLDEN   MacMiCHAEL. 

HURSTMONCEAUX  :      ITS     PRONUNCIATION* 

— What  is  the  correct  pronunciation  of  the 
name  Hurstmonceaux,  Sussex  ?  I  have 
heard  it  called  *'  Herst-mon-sew,"  with 
stress  pretty  evenly  divided  between  the 
first  and  last  syllable.  A  writer  in  M.A.P.^ 
however,  recently  stated  that  *'  Hurstmon- 
ceaux should  be  shortened  into  *  Her-so.*  " 
Further,  Hope's  *  Glossary  of  Dialectal  Place 
Nomenclatiu^  *  gives  the  forms  "  Hurst- 
mounceys,"  "  Hossmoimcies,"  and  "  Hars- 
mouncy."  Are  th»ie  purely  rustic,  or  are 
they  used  by  the  educated  ? 

Jas.  Platt,  Jun. 

Curious  Parish  Document. — Among  the 
fragmentary  sixteenth-century  records  of 
the  parish  of  SS.  Anne  and  Agnes,  Alders- 
gate,  are  four  pages  of  a  document  in  Dutch, 
"  translated  from  the  French  language,"  of 
the  first  page  of  which  the  following  is  a 
literal  (modem)  translation  : — 

"  To  all  those  who  shall  see  these  presents,  Louis 
le  Beauolere,  Advocate  of  the  King  in  his  Council 
of  State,  President,  general  Judge  of  the  Justice 
of  Calais,  and  the  reconquered  lands,  and  the  great 
Bailliou  of  the  Island,  (>reetings,  hereby  intorms

or  therealx)uts,  provided  with  sixteen  pieces  of

twenty-eight  men,  the  afore- mentioned  Captain 
included,  the  same  ship  well  victualled,  munitioned,

her  anchors,  cables,  and  fit  sails,  the  which  from 
his  own  free  will  has  acknowledged,  and  left,  sold, 
ceded,  quitted,  transferred,  and  left,  according  to 
the  contents  and  the  delivery  of  these  presents, 
promising  and  promises  to  keep  free  from  all 
troubles,  free  of  navigation,  letters  of  bedom- 
morie  [?]  debts,  mortgages,  obligations,  and  other

places  in  which  the  same  may  rvnam,  a

KICHARD    n. :    HIS    ARMS. 

(10  S,  vii,  188.) 

This  king's  favourite  device  was  the  white 
hart,  lodged,  ducally  gorged  and  chained  or, 
which  he  is  supposed  to  nave  adopted  from 
his  mother  Joan,  '*  the  Fair  Maid  of  Kent,'* 
daughter  of  Edmund  of  Woodstock.  Earl 
of  Kent  (younger  son  of  Edward  I.  by  hi* 
second  wife),  whose  cognizance  was  a  white 
hind.  Richard's  badge  forms  a  conspicuous 
decoration  of  the  string  moulding  which 
connects  the  trusses  in  We^stmLnster  Hall, 
being  sculptured  thereon  no  fewer  than 
eighty-three  times,  alternating  with  the  royal 
crest. 

The  white  hart  also  occurs  in  the  portrait 
of  Richard,  wlien  about  thirteen  years  of 
age,  at  Wilton  House.  This  picture  repre- 
sents the  young  King  kneeling  in  adoration 
before  the  Madonna  and  Child,  who  are 
attended  by  angels.  Richard,  introduced 
by  his  three  patron  saints — St.  John  Baptist, 
Edward  the  Confessor  (holding  the  fisher- 
man's ring),  and  St.  Edmund  the  Martyr 
(delicately  fingering  one  of  the  arrown  which 
slew  him) — is  dressed  in  a  splendid  crimson 
robe,  which  is  dia|)ered  with  this  badge  in 
gold.     Ho  also  wears  a  jewel  repreeenting 


250 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES,  uos.  vil  iiA«ai«,» 


the  white  hart ;  and  every  one  of  the  eleven 
angels  wears  a  similar  jewel  in  honour  of 
the  King.  This  badge  occurs  in  addition 
on  the  panel  at  the  back  of  the  painting, 
which  would  be  shown  when  the  picture  was 
closed. 

The  white  hart  may  further  be  seen  on  the 
beautiful  frame  of  Richard's  great  portrait 
in  Westminster  Abbey ;  on  the  robes  (copied 
in  his  lifetime  from  his  actual  garments)  worn 
by  his  effigy  on  his  tomb  in  the  same  church  ; 
and  on  a  fragment  of  the  royal  dress  at 
South  Kensington  Museum,  which  also  shows 
his  grandfather's  cognizance  (the  sunburst 
of  Edward  III.)  and  the  portrait  of  his  dog 
Math.  A.  R.  Bayley. 

In  Boutell's  *  English  Heraldry,'  second 
ed.,  published  by  Cassell,  Fetter  &  Galpin, 
p.  27,  it  is  stated  that 

"in  Westminster  Abbey  the  favourite  badge  of 
Richard  II.,  a  white  hart,  chained,  and  in  an  atti- 
tude of  rest,  is  rei)eated  as  many  as  eighty-three 
times,  and  all  are  equally  consistent  with  heraldic 
truth,  without  any  one  of  them  being  an  exact 
counteri>art  of  the  other." 

Millington,  at  p.  303  of  '  Heraldry  in  His- 
tory, Poetry,  and  Romance,'  remarks  that 
Richard  II.  derived  from  his  mother,  the 
Fair  Maid  of  Kent, 

"  the  liadge  preserved  in  Westminster  Hall,  and  in 
thechapelof  St.  Minliael  in  Canterbury  Cathedral, 
a  white  hart,  coucluuit  on  a  mount,  under  a  tree 
l)ro|»er,  gorged  with  a  crown,  and  chained;  and 
that  this  \m\ae  iHicame  the  title  of  a  pursuivant, 
and  is  alluded  to  as  the  *  Kintt' «  liverie  in  an  old 
chronicle  entitled  'How  England  was  first  called 
Albyon.'"

According  to  Brewer,  tlie  white  hart,  or 
hind,  was  the  badge  of  Ricliard  II.,  and  was 
worn  by  all  his  courtiers  and  adherents. 
When  the  unsuccessful  rising  on  his  belialf 
took  place,  his  child  wife,  Isabel  of  France, 
tore  off  the  Lancastrian  badges — I  believe 
swans  —  from  her  liveries,  and  replaced 
them,  with  delight,  by  the  white  hart.

Marlv  Horses  (10  S.  vii.  190,  211).— 
Tlure  are  four  groups  called  Marly's  Horsee. 
Besides  the  well-known  pair  decorating  the 
Charaps-filysees.  another  pair  is  to  be  seen  on 
the  other  suie  of  the  Place  de  la  Concorde, 
at  the  entrance  of  the  Tuileries  garden.  The 
latter  are  by  Coysevox,  and  were  originally 
erected  at  Marly  in  the  time  of  Louis  XIV . 
(1702);  whereas  the  other  groups,  by 
Couston,  were  erected  much  later,  in  1745. 

All  informatjon  about  Marly  is  to  bo  found 
in  the  valuable  book  of  Camille  Piton, 
'  Marly-le-Hoi '  (Paris,  Joanin  &  €ie.,  1904).

"  Grind Y  "  (10  S.  vii.  209).— The  '  Eng. 
Dial.  Diet.*  gives  grim,  adj.,  griiny,  and 
(fritnt,  pp.,  begrimed.  It  is  most  likely 
that  the  pp.  grim't  was  turned  into  gritnd 
and  yrlnd  (with  short  i),  with  the  sense 
begrimed  or  grimy  ;  from  wliieh  a  new 
adj.  grind-y  could  easily  be  formed.  The 
short  i  also  occurs  in  grimhh,  to  begrime, 
and  occasions  no  difficulty.

Thibkell  Family  (10  S.  vi.  229;  vii. 
218).— The  Rev.  Thomas  Threlkeld  (173»- 
IHOO)  was  minist«-r  (1778-1806)  of  the 
Unitarian  congregation  worshipping  in  Black- 
water  Street  Cliurch,  Roclidale.  Born  at 
Halifax,  Yorks ;  educated  at  Daventry 
Academy  and  Warrington  Academy,  1758- 
1762  (Dr.  Prietitley  being  tutor  there  diwing 
his  last  year),  he 'became  minister  at  Rialey 
in  1 762,' remaining  there  until  his  removal 
to  Rochdale.     He  married  Martlia  Wright, 


252 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES,  [lo  s.  vil  mabcr  a^m 


of  Risley,  and  Ih  buried  in  Blackwater  Street 
Church.  His  father  was  the  Rev.  Samuel 
Threlkeld,  minister  of  the  Presbyterian 
congregation  at  Halifax.  When  Dr.  Priestley 
went,  m  1762,  to  be  married  at  Wrexham 
to  Mias  Wilkinson,  whose  father  was  an  iron- 
master near  that  town,  Thomas  Threlkeld 
accompanied  him  as  groomsman.  It  had 
been  settled  that  in  the  performance  of  the 
marriage  service  he  should  give  the  bride 
away.  Unfortunately,  u]K)n  entering  the 
church,  Thelkeld,  delighted  with  the  idea  of 
being  now  able,  for  the  first  time,  to  gratifv 
his  passion  for  the  Welsh  language — of  which 
he  was  master — at  the  fountain  head,  had 
buried  himself  in  a  large  and  lofty  pew, 
where  he  had  found  a  Bible  in  the  vernacular. 
He  was  deeply  engaged  in  studying  it  when 
wanted  in  tlie  chancel.  The  service  was  at 
a  standstill :  the  father  did  not  appear  to 
give  away  the  bride  ;  a  hue  and  cry  was  set 
up  for  the  groomsman,  and  at  length  he 
was  discovered  in  his  hiding-place,  ignorant 
of  what  was  ])a8sing,  and  unconscious  of 
anything  but  the  pleasure  of  reading  his 
favourite  language.  His  memory  for  facts, 
figures,  and  quotations  was  prodigious. 
See  Monthly  Kepwiitory,  1807,  p.  172  ;  also 
'  Record  of  Provincial  Assembly  of  Lanca- 
shire and  Cheshire,'  1896,  p.  162.  Is  any 
portrait  of  him  known  ? 

Geo.  Eyre  Evans.

D.  D.'s  recollections  of  how  crayfish,  which 
abounded  in  the  New  River  (especially  near 
the  Sluice  House),  were  caught,  do  not  quite 
coincide  with  my  own.  They  are  not  so 
plentiful  in  the  mud  banks  as  they  were 
amongst  the  piles  abutting  on  the  bridges, 
•especially  the  wooden  one  at  the  Sluice  House, 
which  spanned  the  river  opposite  to  tJiat 
rural  retreat,  and  led  to  the  fields  beyond 
(this  bridge,  by  the  by,  had  a  locked  gate 
upon  it).  The  bait  always  used  was  a  lump 
of  boiled  lights  affixed  to  a  stone  with  a  hole 
through  it.  A  yard  of  string  was  tied  to 
the  stone,  and  the  tempting  morsel  was  let 
carefully  down  between  the  timbers,  for 
it  was  there^ibouts  the  crustaceous  little 
things  lived  by  the  hundred.  It  was  a  poor 
fiaturday  afternoon's  holiday  work  if  half  a 
dozen,  or  even  double  that  number,  were  not 
caught.  A  public-house,  in  the  midst  of  a 
wilderness  of  closely  built  streets,  now 
stands  upon  the  site  of  the  old  Sluice  House. 

Harry  Hems. 

Fair  Park,  Exeter. 

"  Brumby  "  (10  S.  vi.  430,  476).— When 
preparing  his  '  Austral  English,'  the  late 
Prof.  Morris  made  many  inquires  about  the 
origin  of  this  word  ;  but,  as  Prof.  Strong 
has  pointed  out,  no  certain  answer  could  be 
obtained.  To  me  the  native  origin  seems 
most  probable  :  the  other  story  lacks  con- 
firmation. Has  any  one  found  Lieut. 
Brumby  in  tlie  records  ?  Is  there  any  point 
in  the  imj)ortation,  or  in  the  goodness  of  his 
horses,  or  in  anything  but  their  >vildnes8  ? 
And  other  horses  would  go  wild  as  readily 
as  his.     Morris  says  : — 

"  Iioo)-amh}/ 18  Kiven  [in  Cun-'a  'Australian  Race'] 
AS  meaning  '  wild '  on  the  VVarrcKO  in  Queensland. 
The  use  of  tlie  word  Heems  to  liave  siiread  from  the 
Wan-ego  and  the  Balowne  al)out  1804.     Before  that 

<late wild   horses   were   called    rUar  •  xkint  or 

.'<cruhhtrH."

vol.  iv.)  to  James  Brumby,  a  private  soldier 
in  the  N.S.W.  Corps,  along  with  some  com- 
rades, in  May,  1797.  Tliis  James,  or  one 
of  his  descendants,  mav  be  the  origin  of  the 
lieutenant  story.  And  in  this  connexion, 
})erhaps,  I  may  repeat  to  Mr.  MacMichael

to  be  careful  about  mentioning  Botimy  Bay  ; 
especially  as  his  note  ends  with  what  is  in 
form  a  positive  assertion,  though  doubtless 
meant  for  a  probability.     My  own  family,

"  O    DEAR,    WHAT   CAN    THE    MATTER   BE  ?  " 

(10  S.  vi.  29,  57,  73,  92,  116,  152.  198.  454. 
515.) — Some  thirty  years  ago  a  nonsensical 
parody  of  the  above  appeared  in  one  of  the 
society  journals.  T  am  unable  to  give  either 
name  or  date,  but  I  have  ever  since  retained 
the  words  in  my  memory.  It  represent* 
one  of  those  jingles  which,  without  any 
effort  or  desire  on  the  part  of  the  victim, 
haunt  him  through  life.     It  runs  as  follows  : 

O  de<ir,  what  ciui  the  nmttt*r  Ik;? 

Srtlliifit  inuHt  svircly  a»  mail  om  a  hatter  he. 

(irowlina  away  like  a.  liear. 

He  i«  ltuiviii;{  Madrid  at  the  nei^ht  of  the  seiison. 

Without  even  f^iving  his  servants  a  re4i,«ujn  : 

It  sruackg  very  struiiKly  of  murder  or  trejvson, 
liut  timt  isn't  our  atl'iiir. 

Every  time  I  hear  played  tlie  air  of  *'  O 
dear,  what  can  the  matter  be  ?  "  my  memory 
harks  back  to  a  certain  evening  when  I 
formed  one  of  an  audience  asseinbletl  to 
hear  a  lecture  ilKwtrated  by  limelight  views. 
Somehow  or  other  the  pictures  got  out  of 
order,  and  perBist«*ntly  illustrattsil  anything 
except  the  point  on  which  the  lecrtiirer  was 
dilating.  There  was  a  musical  accompani- 
ment as  part  of  the  programme,  and  after 
a  time  the  orchestra  entered  lieartily  into 
the  spirit  of  the  fun  by  Ktrildng  up  "  O  dear, 
what  can  the  matter  be  ?  "  and  thus  filling 
up  the  interludes  during  the  rectification  of 
the  errors.  It  waa  an  amusing  exi>erience 
for  the  audience,  and  one  I  can  never  forget ; 
but  the  feelings  of  the  y.»oor  lecturer  can  be 
better  imagined  than  described. 

John  T.  Pacb. 

Long  Itohinji^on,  VN'arwicksliire.

m\i»ic  than  any  other  person  I  have  met,  and 
she  always  called  '*  O  dear,  what  can  the 
matter   be  ?  "    a    "  modern    Scotch    sotv^.'*

as  the  author  of  sixty-five  translations ; 
G.  B.  of  only  three. 

However,  in  *  Anthologia  Polyglotta,*  by 
Henry  WeUesley,  D.D.,  1849,  the  initials 
G.  B.  mean  George  Booth.  In  this  collection 
George  Butler  does  not  appear.  Booth 
gives  fifty-nine  Latin  and  fifteen  English 
translations.  In  the  preface  Wellesley  offers 
his  **  grateful  acknowledgements  "  to  "  those 
distinguished  members  of  the  University, 
the  Rev.  G.  Booth,  the  Rev.  J.  W.  Burgon, 
the  Rev.  G.  F.  De-Teissier,  the  Rev.  E. 
Stokes,   the  Rev.   G.   C.   Swaine,   Goldwin 

Smith,  Esq.,  and ....  to  a  foreigner Count 

Mortara,  now  residing  among  us." 

It  is  evident,  as  regards  these  two  books, 
that  where  both  George  Booth  and  George 
Butler  appear  the  former  becomes  B.  and 
the  latter  G.  B. ;  but  that  where  the  latter 
is  absent  George  Booth  has  his  two  intials,

Watts  and  the  Rose  (10  S.  vii.  105). — 
Mb.  Lynn  will  scarcely  need  reminding  that 
Watts  wrote  under  the  Old  Style :  his 
April  and  May  were  later  than  ours.  Even 
so,  April  is  certainly  too  early  a  date  for  the 
rose  m  our  climate ;  but  we  must  not  be 
hard  on  a  poet  who  wishes  to  fill  his  line 
euphoniously.  Lyte  says  roses  are  in  flower 
in  May  and  June  ;  Gerard,  "  from  May  till 
the  end  of  August  "  ;  so  we  may  allow  May 
to  consider  them  her  "  glory."  Has  Mb. 
Lynn  forgotten  Laertes's 

O  rose  of  May, 
Dear  maid,  kind  Ki8ter,  sweet  Ophelia  ! 

or  Olivia's  "  roses  of  the  spring  "  ;  or  that 
in  tlie  bridal  song  in  '  The  Two  Noble  Kins- 
men '  tliey  are  named  together  with  prim- 
roses and  oxlips  ;  or  Keats's 
And  mid-May's  eldest  child,  the  coming  musk-rose  ? 
Here  is  warrant  enough  for  Watts,  as 
regards  the  later  montli  at  any  rate. 

C.  C.  B.

Camel-bells  or  caravan  bells  are  mentioned 
several  times  in  Sven  Hedin's '  Tlirough  Asia,' 
1898,  In  describing  a  sandstorm  on  p.  555 
of  the  first  volume  the  author  obser\*es  : — 

"  The  deafening  roar  ot  the  hurricane  overpowers 
every  other  sound.  If  you  do  get  separated  from 
them  fyour  comitanionsj,  you  are  bound  to  wander 
ostrav ;  and  so  become  irretrievably  lost.  All  that 
I  could  see  was  the  camel  immediately  in  front  of  me. 


Everything  else  was  swallowed  up  in  theti

'<u.  and  tliis  vras  about  t}ie 

SH'  ^VJ^.  ri  u  .i. -appeared  ;  for  Dr.  Wliitaker 


says  in  his  preface  to  the   *  Ducatus  Leodi- 
ensis  '  (published  in  1816),  p.  ix,  that  having 

Eerused  the  MS.  about  tlurty  yeai-s  ago,  he 
ad  since  in  vain  endeavoured  to  retrieve  it. 
The  discovery  came  about  in  this  way- 
Last  year  1  edited  for  the  (?un^berland  and 
Westmorland  Antiquarian  and  Arclueo- 
logical  Society  (Tract  Series,  No.  7)  'The 
Beethain  Hepositorj','  a  MS.  historj'  of  the 
j)arish  of  Beetliarn,  in  Westinorland.  com- 
piled by  the  Rev.  Wm.  Hutton,  vicar  from 
1762  to  1811.  Mr.  Hutton  drew  largely 
from  Lucas  for  his  pedigree  of  the  Middle- 
tons  of  Leighton  Hall,  and  it  seemed  appro- 
priate that  I  sliould  collect  in  a  note  all  that 
was  known  of  Lucas  and  his  *  History.' 
Tliis  tract  was  reviewed  in  The  Yorkshire 
Post,  and  Mr.  Wilson,  reading  the  review, 
at  once  told  me  the  MS.  was  in  liis  librarj'. 
He  has  since  kindly  lent  me  the  work,  and 
it  may  not  be  out  of  place  to  describe  it 
shortly.  It  is  in  two  folio  volumes  with  a 
continuous  pagination.  The  first  volume 
contains  44  unnumbered  pages  (occupied  by 
the  title,  the  dedication  to  Ralph  Thoresby, 
the  Introduction,  and  a  catalogue  of  treatises 
quoted)  and  426  pages  of  tejrt,  and  com- 
prises Warton,  Lindeth,  and  Silverdale. 
The  second  volume  contains  pp.  427  to  906 
of  text,  and  30  pages  of  Index,  and  com- 
prises Yealand,  Leighton,  Hutton,  Berwick, 
and  Carnforth-  The  title-page  eorresponda 
with  that  of  the  incomplete  copy  at  Warton 
Church,  except  that  the  last  line  should  lie 
"  Begun  about  mdccx.,  and  finished  in 
MDCC.KLiv./'  and  not  as  printed  in  '  Tho 
Beetbam  Repositorj-/  p.  181.

LONGFELLOW. 

(See  ante,  pp.  201,  222,  242.) 

Like  many  Americans  coming  over  in 
.lune.  Longfellow  went  directly  to  the 
English  Lakes.  In  bis  modesty  he  hod  no 
conce|)tion  of  the  affection  with  which  his 
writings  had  cau.««ed  him  to  be  regorde*!  by 
the  English  people.  The  Daihf  ^.eu's  truly 
gave  eoqpressjon  to  the  popular  voice  when 
it  said  : — 

*'  He  is  the  familiar  friend  who  h*H  sung  to  nrvry 
hou.st-hold,  and  «et  to  miinic  their  ni)]iir«tionA  Aiid 
their  AlTectiaaa." 

The  first  intimation  he  received  of  this  w^as 
his  reception  at  Carlisle,  where,  in  reply 
to  an  oddreaa,  he  said  : — 

"Coming  here  m  a  «tr»nKpr,  this  weltHjinc  makrs 
me  feel  that  I  am  not  a  etmut^r,  (or  huw  ean  a  mun

be  a  stranger  in  a  country  where  he  finds  all  doors 
and  all  hearts  open  to  him  ?  Besides,  I  myself  am 
a  Cumberland  man — for  I  was  bom  in  the  county 
of  Cumberland,  in  the  State  of  Maine,  three 
thousand  miles  from  here." 

On  the  16th  of  June»  1868,  in  the  Senate 
House  at  Cambridge,  he  was  publicly 
admitted  to  the  honorary  degree  of  Doctor 
of  Laws.  When  the  scarlet  gown  was  put 
upon  him,  the  students  shouted,  "Three 
cheers  for  the  red  man  of  the  West !  " 

On  the  26th  of  the  same  montli,  when  he 
arrived  in  London,  a  flood  of  hospitaUty 
flowed  in  upon  him:  the  Queen  received 
Iiim  at  Windsor ;  and  his  countryman  Mr. 
Bierstadt,  the  landscape  painter,  gave  a 
dinner  in  his  honour,  at  which  himdreds 
of  celebrities  in  literature,  science,  and  art 
were  present.  With  his  daughters  he  spent 
a  Sunday  at  Gadshill.  Dickens  had  a  great 
affection  for  him,  and  Forster,  referring  to  a 
former  visit,  speeJcs  of  him  as 

"  our  attached  friend,  who  possesses  all  the  quali- 
ties of  delightful  oomi)anion8hii),  the  culture  and 
the  charm,  which  have  no  higher  tn>e  or  example 
than  the  accomplished  and  genial  American." 

After  a  fortnight  in  London,  Longfellow 
and  his  party  went  to  the  Isle  of  Wight, 
and  spent  two  days  with  Tennyson,  who  in 
one  of  his  letters  to  the  American  poet  in 
the  previous  yecu:  had  written  : — 

"We  Knplish  and  Americaiw  should  all  be 
brothers  as  none  other  among  the  Nations  can  Ik;  ; 
and  some  of  lis,  come  what  may,  will  always  be  so, 
I  trust." 

On  the  15th  of  July  Mrs.  Tennyson  enters 
in  her  diary  : — 

"Mr.  Lonijfellow  arrived  with  a  jtarty  of  ten. 
Very  English  he  is,  wc  thouglit.  A.  considered 
his  'Hiawatha'  his  moat  original  iKX»ni,  and  he 
quoted  his  translation,  'Though  the  mills  of  (4od 
grind  slowly,  vet  they  grind  exceeding  small.'  Both 
poets  admire<l  Platen's  '  In  dor  Nacht.'"— ' Life  of 
Tennyson,'  by  his  Son. 

There  were  forty  or  fifty  guests  invited  to 
tea,     and     Longfellow    spoke    kindly     and 
graciously  to  each  guest.     Altliough  Tenny- 
son and  Longfellow  never  met  again,  tlie 
friendsliip  was  continued  by  letters  on  both 
sides,  and  in  1877  the  American  poet  sent 
to    lus    Enghsh    brother,    as    a    Christmas 
greeting,  liis  beautiful   sonnet  *  WapentaJce 
to    ^fred    Tennyson,'    in    wliich    he    does 
homage  to  the  mastery  in  English  song  of 
the  *'  sweet  historian  of  the  heart  "  : — 
Therefore  to  thee  the  laurel  leaves  belong, 
To  thee  our  love  and  our  allegiance. 
For  thy  allegiance  to  the  i>oet's  art. 

Tennj-son  in  reply  wrote  that  the  Christmas 
greetmg  was  a  very  perfect  flower  from 
your  own  spacious  garden." 


After  a  stay  on  the  Continent  LoDgleAv 
returned  for  a  few  days  to  London,  A» 
went  to  Oxford  to  receive  the  de^  d 
D.C.L.,  and  thence  to  Scotland.  After* 
crowded  eighteen  months  of  travel  hf 
reached  his  home  again  at  Craigie  HooMea 
the  Ist  of  September,  1869,  as  the  sonvw 
setting,  "  and  found  Cambridge  in  all  il» 
beauty  ;  not  a  leaf  faded."  '*  How  gild 
I  am  to  be  at  home  I  "  he  writes  the  8an» 
night  to  his  lifelong  friend  Greene. 

"  There  is  not  a  droi>  of  ink  in  my  inkstand,  aoi 
no  bottle  can  be  found.  Still,  I  most  write  yoa  ov 
word  to  say  we  are  all  safe  again  at  home.  Bat 
strange  ana  how  familiar  it  all  seems !  aod  bof 
thankful  I  am  to  have  brought  my  little  flock  bick 
to  the  fold!  The  young  voices  and  Uttle  feet  ait 
musical  overhead ;  and  the  year  of  travel  floitl 
away,  and  dissolves  like  a  Fata  Morgana.'^ 

In  the  interesting  biography  of  LongfdliCNi 
by  his  brother  Samuel,  to  which  1  ban

shades  of  evening  seem  to  have  been  alreadl

breaking  in  hecdth,  one  day  came  in,  sayiof 
"  I  cannot  work,"  put  his  face  in  his  haiidli 
and  wept,  and  in  a  year,  he  too  was  gooaj 
Sunmer  in  1874  suddenly  cUed,  and  « 
unshadowed  intercourse  of  forty  years  wH 
ended  ;  Lowell  went  abroad,  and  was  atm 
no  more  by  his  friend  and  neighbour.  "So 
the  loneliness  grew  deeper  in  the  study  flt 
Craigie  House."  Yet  there  were  some  choit* 
friends  still  remaining  :  Greene,  his  eariie^

DR.    HALLEY'S    PEDIGREE. 

Occasionally  a  difficult  point  in  genea- 
logy is  settled  by  means  of  an  obsciu^  foot- 
note, or,  jjorchance,  one  important  word ; 
the  desired  clue  may  rest  even  in  a  single 
letter  of  a  name.  More  than  five  years  ago 
t!ie     writer     became     impressed     with     the 


mond  mstead  of  Hidmwnd.  Tliis  was  thougliv 
to  imply  the  possible  derivation  of  his  eur- 
nan^e  from  tbe  French  Halle  or  Hall^ — 
a  supposition  which  appeared  to  receive 
partial  confirmation  from  the  discover^' 
of  actual  examples  of  the  spellirig  Ilalley 
in  Normandy.  On  the  contrary,  ev^denc"© 
recently  obtained  strongly  indicates  that 
Br.  Halley's  siu'uame  is  traceable  to  the 
family  of  Hawley  of  Northamptonshire, 
indifferently  spelt  Hawley  or  Halley.  A 
complete-  pedigree  showing  Dr.  Halley's 
diretrt  male  descent  may  ultimately  be  found 
in  the  Bodleian  Libraiy  as  intimated  at 
10  S.  vi.  408  under  the  bending  "  Dr. 
Arthur  Charlett.'  The  true  explanation, 
in  tliis  connexion,  of  the  Clmatian  name 
Edmond,  appears  to  lie  in  tlie  recorded 
|iedigree  of  Mewce  of  Holdenby,  which 
jjcgins  with  one  John  Mewce  of  Calais,  who 
married  and  had  issue  one  son,  Nicholas 
Mewce,  of  Hedgman'a,  Essex.  The  latter 
married  Elizabeth,  dau.  of  Edmond  Morant, 
of  London,  and  had  issue  Francis,  Edmond 
Christoj)her,  AHce,  Lttcie,  Maline,  and 
Katherm  Mewce  (cf.  '  Visitations  of  North- 
amptonshire, 1504  and  1018-19,'  ed.  bv 
W.  C.  Metcalfe,  London,  1887,  p.  114). 

The  eldest  son,  Francis  Me  wee,  of  Holdenby , 
CO.  Northampton  (hx-ing  It)  18),  married 
Elizabeth  Washington,  dau.  of  Lawrence 
Wasliington,  of  Sulgrave,  co.  Northampton, 
the  ceremony  being  perfotnied  26  May, 
161.5,  according  to  a  Washington  family 
chart  inserted  between  pp.  394-7  of  Waters 's 
'  Genealogical  Gleanings  in  England,'  Boston, 
1901.     This  same  Lawrence  Waaliington  of 


•264 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES,     uo  s.  vn,  afriHW 


Sulgrave  was,  as  is  well  known,  a  direct 
ancestor  of  George  Wasliington,  the  first 
president  of  the  United  States. 

Alice  Mewce  married  Richard  Ellis  or 
EUies,  of  London,  whose  will  (quoted  in 
Waters's  '  Gleanings,'  p.  383)  describes  him 
as  "citizen  and  haberdasher  of  London." 
The  will,  dated  15  Aug.,  1625,  proved 
26  Aug.,  1625  (P.C.C.  Clarke,  86)  names 
several  members  of  the  family,  including 
"Sister  Hallye "  and  "Brother  Himifrey 
Hally."  The  latter's  name  is  spelt  "Hawley" 
in  the  'Visitations.'  These  two,  Himifrey 
Hally  or  Hawley  and  Katherin  Mewce, 
his  wife,  were  probably  the  paternal  grand- 
parents of  Dr.  E.  Halley.  If  so,  it  is  easy 
to  explain  the  existence  of  the  names 
Edmond,  Francis,  and  Elizabeth  (and,  later, 
Katherine)  in  the  Halley  family.  Note 
the  extract  following,  from  the  will  of 
Himiphrey  Halley  the  elder : — 

"  Formerly  of  the  city  of  London,  vintner,  now 
•  of  Aloonbury,  in  the  county  of  Huntingdon,  Sons 
William  and  Humphrey,  and  daughter  Elizabeth 
■Cawthome,  widow,  who  is  sole  executrix.  In  a 
codicil  mentions  son  Edmond.  Will  gives  names 
of  places  in  London  where  i»roi)erty  was  situated. 
Testator  describes  himself  as  'very  aged '  (Register 
Eure,  1674)." 

This  Humphrey  Halley  the  elder  (probably 
identical  with  the  husband  of  Katherin 
Mewce)  may  perhaj^s  have  been  a  son  of  the 
William  Hawley  (fi.  1599)  mentioned  next 
below  : — 

"  William  Hawley,  salt^^r,  and  ElizalKjth  Hye,  of 
St.  Stephen's,  Coleman  St..  London,  widow  of 
Robert  I5ye,  late  of  sjinie,  'itannariiis*;  at  St.  Mary 
Woolchufch,  London.''— Cf.  Harl.  Soc.  Pub.,  xxv, 
2(il,  'Marriage  Licences  granted  by  the  Bishop  of 
London,'  12  Aj.ril,  ]'m. 

"Will  of  William  Halley,  of  the  city  of  Peter- 
borough. To  son  Francis  Halley  brick-built  houses 
in  Fenchurch  Street.  Testator  mentions  his  'loving 
wife'  Ann  Halley;  brothers  Humphrey  and  Edmond 
Hallev.  Will  dated  Jan.  .30,  1673 ;  proved  by  Ann 
Halley.  March  6,  1(57')  (P.C.C.  Dycer,  146)." 

"Will  of  Humtrey  Halley  the  younger,  of  London, 
Gent.  Mentions  two  kinsmen,  Ldmund  Hallev  and 
Humf rey  Halley ;  kinswomen  Catherine  Cawthorno 
and  Anii  Cawthome:  kinsman  Humf  rey  Cawthome. 
Residuary  legatee,  'loving and  well-l)eIovod  brother 
Edmond  Halley,  citizen  and  .salt^r,  of  London.' 
Among  witnesses  was  Thomas  Mewce  (P.C.C. 
Bence.  fo.  66  ;  Feb.  23, 1674/5)." 

"Administration  of  the  personal  goods  of  Hum- 
frey  Halley  [No.  3],  lately  deceased  in  the  iwrts 
beyond  the  seas  or  on  the  high  seas,  was  granted 
April  10,  1684,  to  the  brother  Edmund  Halley 
(P.CC.  Admon.  Act  Book,  1684)." 

The  last  item  disproves  the  assertion  tliat 
Dr.  E.  Halley  was  the  only  child  of  his 
father  (cf.  '  Biog.  Brit,,'  iv.,  2494). 

'•  Will  of  Francis  Halley,  of  London,  gent.  To 
Sonne  Francis  Halley  two  messuages  or  residences 


in  Mincin  Lane  (and)  Fenchuroh  Street  SiK 
Mary  Ward,  wife  of  John  Ward.  XichoUsW^l 
of  the  parish  of  St.  Giles,  Cripplegate.  Om

daughters.  Margaret  and  Katherine.  Fatherut 
mother  [in-law]  Richard  Pyke  and  Eleanor  Fm

Edward  Day.  Sisters  Jane  Day  and  Susan  Pjfte 
'Cosen'  Edmond  Halley  and  Richard  PykesHf 
dians  of  my  said  son  Francis  Halley  until  he  ittsi

The  above  Richard  Pyke  (doubtlea 
identical  with  Richard  IVke  mentiooed  it

The  Taxameter  Cab. — ^The  large  numbdf 
of  taxameter  cabs  which  in  the  course  of  • 
few  weeks  are  to  be  put  on  t^e  Londoa 
streets  represent  a  more  extensive  experi- 
ment of  a  facility  enjoyed  by  Berlin  and  other 
continental  cities  since  1894.  On  15  March, 
1899  {vide  Moore*s  '  Omnibuses  and  Cabs,' 
p.  273),  six  velucles  so  fitted  were  tried  ia 
London ;  but  the  opposition  of  the  Cab- 
men's Union  caused  their  withdrawal  aftff 
a  brief  and  turbulent  existence.  Some  saeh 
fate  must  have  befallen  a  still  earlier  attempt 
to  popularize  a  mechanical  fare-register. 
Tlie  Practical  Mechanic* a  Journal  of  1  June, 
1853,  advertises  an  epitomized  prospectoi 
of  "The  Six  Days'  Cab  Company."  Th» 
principal  advantages  this  was  to  afford  w««

carriages,  the  latter  to  be  provided  with  imi>roved 
indicators  by  which  it  will  be  at  once  seen  the 
distance  travelletl. 

"  To  abolish  the  insults  and  extortions  now  W 
prevalent,  by  employing  men  of  known  res[T^ 
ability  of  character,  who  will  be  provided  wi* 
livery  coats  and  hats,  and  iMid  a  regular  weeHf 
salary.  , 

"I'o  afford  their  servants  the  opportonity*' 
moral  and  religious  instruction,  by  entirely? aboor 
ing  all  Sviiflat/  tcork\  thereby  constituting  tJM 
what  the  title  imports,  viz.,  a  Six-D«iy  CanTBjM* 
Company.
