Zephyb. 

"  Mabu." — Virtually  all  Japanese  mer- 
chant vessels  suffix  "  maru  '*  to  their  names  ; 
thus  America  Maru,  Inaba  Maru,  Wakasa 
Maru.  A  glance  at  the  list  of  Japanese 
vessels  in  *  Lloyd's  Register  '  indicates  that 
99  per  cent,  of  the  vessels  are  "  maru." 
A  year  ago  I  asked  a  Japanese  Admiralty 
official  what  was  the  explanation.  He  repUed 
that  this  waa  very  difficult  ta  explain ; 
that  the  meaning  of  the  word  "  maru " 
was  not  capable  of  exact  rendering  into 
EngUsh,  but  that  in  ancient  days  the  daimios 
or  scunurai  (I  think  he  said)  regarded  their 
sword  with  a  sort  of  reverential  affection : 
it  was  "  maru  "  to  them.  Later,  some  great 
man  called  his  ships  *'  maru,"  more  or  less 
out  of  the  same  sentiment.  The  other  day, 
to  another  Japanese  gentleman  dining  with 
me,  and  engaged  in  the  Government  trans- 
port or  transit  service  at  Tokio,  I  put  the 
same  question.  He  confessed  to  the  same 
difficulty  in  answering  it,  but  confirmed  the 
explanation  of  his  colleague  so  far  as  it  went, 
adding,  "  You  may  call  your  dog  or  your  cat 
'  maru.' "  Further,  he  stated  that  some 
merchant  ships  having  been  called  "  maru," 
tlie  Government  issued  an  instruction  that 
all  Japanese  merchant  vessels  should  be  so 
called,  on  the  ground  that  there  were  cases 
in  wliich  the  same  name  was  common  to  a 
private  and  to  a  public  vessel,  and  that  tliere 
ought  to  be  no  confusion  affecting  the 
national  property. 

Happening  to  mention  this  to  a  friend 
interested  in  sliipping,  I  was  told  by  him 
that  his  o\\Ti  information  differed  entirely 
from  mine.  Several  Japanese,  he  said,  to 
whom  he  had  put  the  question,  wliilst  find- 
ing it  full  of  difficulty,  had  agreed  that,  so 
far  as  the  word  can  be  interpreted  into 
English  at  all,  it  has  the  sense  of  "  going," 
"  moving  onwards."  Is  any  reader  cf 
'  N.  &  Q.'  qualified  to  decide  which,  if  eitlier, 
of  these  explanations  is  correct  ?  and  if 
neitlier  is  so,  then  what  is  the  proper  liistory 
or  meaning  of  the  word  ?  Perhaps  your 
correspondent  Mr.  Kumagusu  Minakata 
may  be  able  to  clear  up  the  matter. 

Douglas  Owen. 

Savile  Club.

Ar.ms  in  Congregational  Chapel  at 
Chertsey. — The  following  arms  in  stained 
glass  are  in  one  of  the  windows  of  an  old 
disused  Congregational  chapel  here,  whicli 
was  built  in  1710,  viz.,  France  and  England 
quarterly,  impaling  a  coat  of  six  quarters : 
1,  Or,  on  a  pile  gidcs  between  six  fleurs-de-lis 
azure,  three  lions  passant  guardant  of  the 
first  (the  augmentation  granted  to  the  Sey- 
mour family  by  Henry  Vlll.  on  liis  marriage 
with  Jane  Se>Tnour)  ;  2,  Gules,  two  wings 
eoiijoined  in  lure  or  (Seymour)  ;  3,  V^air, 
argent  and  azure  (Beauchamp)  ;  4,  Argent. 
two  bars  wavy  gules  ;  5,  Party  per  bend 
argent  and  gules,  three  roses  in  bend  counter- 
changed,  seeded  or  ;  6,  Argent,  a  bend  gides 
charged  with  three  bezants  or.  There  has 
e\idently  been  a  centre  crest  or  crowTi ; 
this  has  gone,  but  on  each  side  is  a  horse's 
head  sable,  in  arniour  azure,  bridled  or,  on 
the  head  a  plume  of  tliree  feathers,  or, 
argent,  and  azure.  Under  the  one  on  the 
sinister  side  are  the  letters  "  T.  H."  On  the 
dexter  side  is  cut  horizontally  with  a  dia- 
mond *'  Robert  Burroye  for  Burage]  1725." 
Locally  the  arms  are  ascribed  to  a  Marquis 
of  Hertford  ;  but  they  are  undoubtedly 
those  of  Henr>'  \Tll.  and  Jane  Sey- 
mour, though  the  crest  puzzles  me.  Did 
Hcnrj-  "V^Hl.  ever  use  .such  a  crest  ?  and 
what*  do  the  initials  '*  T.  H,"  stand  for? 
Can  any  of  your  correspondents  help,  or 
suggest  how  they  came  to  be  in  a  Noncon- 
fomniat  chapel  ?

The  inscription  on  the  pewter  tankard 
belonging  to  C.  V.  H.  S.  clearly  refers  to  the 
"  Beggar's  Bush  "  Inn,  Gravel  Lane,  South- 
wark.  This  ancient  hostelry  is  mentioned 
by  W.  uT.  Meymott  in  his  '  Histor>'  of  the 
Manor  of  Old  Paris  Garden,*  printed  in  1881 
for  private  circulation.  Youi*  correspondent 
may  call  to  mind  that  Beaumont  and  Fletcher, 
who  wrote  the  play  called  '  Tlie  Beggars' 
Buah,'  lived  together  on  the  Bankside, 
hard  by.  Philip  Nok.m.\n. 

Undoubtedly  the  "'  Beggar's  Bush  "  is 
intended,  and  "  Grauel  "  =  Gravel  (Lane). 
Beggar's  Bush  Yard  is  described  in  Dodsley's 
'  London  and  its  Enx'irons,*  1761,  as  being 
in  Gravel  Lane ;  but  %vhether  Gravel  Lane 
near  the  Falcon  Stairs,  Scuthwark.  or  the 
street  of  the  same  name  in  Hovmdsditch, 
be  meant,  one  cannot  say.  It  would  hardly 
be  Old  Gravel  Lane,  in  Ratcliff  Highway, 
since  tliat  thoroughfare,  tlirough  which  the 
sand-taallast  carriers  conveyed  gravel  from 
the  neighbouring  fields  to  the  ships  in  the 
Thames,  is  distinctively  described  as  Old 
Gravel  Lane.  So  that  Beggar's  Bush  Yard 
in  (travel  Lane  was  probably  a  yard  apper- 
taining to  the  *'  Bagers  Bash  "  of'C.  V.  H.  S.'s 
quer>%  J.  Hold  en  MacAIichael.

CocoA-NuTS  (10  S.  vi.  209,  256,  312).—

'*Aooiido,  che  a6  si  tiran  pietre  alii  detti  ^atti

NOTES  AND  QUERIES,     [lo  s.  vil  AnEiV 


bocoa  4  6  5  denti.  II  qual  Francesco  io  lo  oonosoo, 
o  lu  viddi  avanti,  che  1  gat  to  gli  dcsse  la  sassata 
con  gli  suoi  denti,  e  dapoi  niolte  Hate  lo  viddi 
ancora  senza  essi,  perche  gli  perse,  come  i  detto." 

In  my  childhood  I  often  heard  old  folks 
in  tliis  paxt  say  that  if  one  picked  up  stones 
from  the  ground  and  threw  them  at  a  monkey 
on  a  moimtain,  the  monkey  would  do  the 
same  to  the  man  ;  but  that  if  the  stones  were 
taken  out  of  a  pocket  and  thrown,  the  animal 
would  pluck  hairs  off  his  own  body  and  strive 
to  hurl  them  against  the  man.  That  the 
Chinese  of  the  fifteenth  century  or  there- 
abouts entertained  a  similar  belief  is  mani- 
fested in  their  popular  romance  '  Si-yu-ki,' 
wherein  the  monkey -hero  Sun  Wu-Kung  (see 
9  S.  xi.  490)  is  made  repeatedly  to  dismay 
liis  numberless  foes — not  only  men  and 
demons,  but  also  gods — ^by  letting  fly  his 
own  hairs,  each  single  hair  being  instantly 
turned  by  magic  into  a  fighting  duplicate 
of  himself. 

Still  more  marvellous,  perhaps,  is  what 
Sie  Chung-Chi  relates  in  sober  faith  in  his 
'  Wu-tsah-tsu  '  (written  1610,  Japanese  ed., 
1661,  tom.  ix.,  fol.  15).  The  story  is  briefly 
that  Mount  Shi-Chu  in  Fu-Tsing  swarmed 
with  monkeys,  which  General  Tseh  Ki-Kwang 
(sixteenth  century)  captured  and  trained 
well  in  using  fire-arms,  and  through  their 
super-simian  feats  defeated  and  annihilated 
a  band  of  predatory  Japanese. 

Further,  tlie  following  account  in  Twan 
Ching-Sliih's  '  Yu-yang-tsah-tsu  '  (\vTitten 
in  the  nintli  century,  Japanese  ed.,  1697, 
torn.  iv.  fol.  3b)  would  seem  to  imply  an 
older  Chinese  belief  in  the  capability  of 
monkeys  to  throw  missiles  : — 

"The  coxxutry  of  Po-niilan 1ms  in  its  wc«t 

a  very  i)recipitou«,  crngj^y  mountain,  on  which 
alMjund  gigantic  monkeys,  iMldictud  t<»  devastating 
Held  orojiH.  Every  year  there  are  two  to  three 
hundre<i  thousand  of  them  ;  so  after  the  arrival  of 
spring  the  people  collect  armed  soldiers  and  join  in 
ItaHle  irith  the  moukeijH,  of  which  they  slaughter

Tliere  is  an  interesting  article  on  thia  old 
mansion  at  5  S.  xii.  122,  written  by  Lady 
Alwyne  Compton  (signed  Florence  Comp- 
TON).  Some  notes  upon  the  house  may  be 
found  in  Murray '.s  '  Handbook  for  North- 
amptonshire,'  p.'  193.  It  was  built  by  Sir 
Christopher  Hatton  temp.  Elizabeth,  and  is 
now  in  a  woeful  state  of  disrepair.  It  is  in 
the  parish  of  Deene.

Abraham  Lincoln  and  European  Poli- 
ticians (10  S.  vii.  165).— That  Lincoln*s 
later  addresses  "  are  very  far  from  being  the 
utterances  of  a  mere  *  petty  practitioner  ' 
or  '  village  lawyer  '  "  will  hardly  be  disputed

KoBBiNs.  Lincoln's  letter  to  J.  F.  Speed

coln said : —

When  the  Marquis  of  Hartington,  now 
the  Duke  of  Devonshire,  was  introduced  to 
Lincoln,  the  great  President  said,  "  Har- 
tington, Hcurtington,  why,  that  rimes  with 
Partington  ;  any  relation  ?  "  These  words 
could  never  have  been  uttered  by  a  political 
genius  like  Walpole.  Lincoln  did  not  put 
m  practice  the  maxim  that  a  drop  of  honey 
catches  more  flies  than  a  gallon  of  gall.

Santa  ¥± :  Amebican  Place  -  Names 
(10  S.  vi.  310,  353,  394,  452  ;  vii.  17).— I 
would  like  to  remedy  an  injustice  which  I 
unintentionally  did  to  the  Century  Company 
at  the  last  reference,  and  by  so  doing  add 
an  excellent  exemplification  of  the  process 
there  shown  to  be  at  work.  They  inform 
me  that  the  second  edition  of  the  \er\  useful 
*  Dictionary  of-  Names  '  did  give  the  pro- 
nunciation "  Squimo  "  for  Esquimalt,  but 
that,  according  to  the  Mayor  of  Victoria, 
the  local  pronunciation  is  now  *'  Squimalt  " 
or  "  Esquimau."  But  it  certainly  was 
"  Squimo  "  twenty  years  ago,  wlien  I  was 
there,  as  I  lieardit  from  both  naval  men 
and  civilians  ;  and  it  was  evidently  taken 
from  the  traditional  (and  therefore  correct) 
pronunciation  of  tlie  early  colonists  and 
habitants.  If  it  has  since  changed,  that  is 
because  the  influx  of  settlers  who  know  not 
Joseph,  and  insist  on  pronouncing  in  English 
fashion  whatever  letters  they  find  in  a  word, 
has  swamped  tlie  native  and  traditional 
element.  The  result  is  tlie  reverse  of  that 
in  *'  Norwich "  :  the  latter  restores  an 
etymological  form,  or  nearly  so  ;  the  former 
crystallizes  a  blundering  guess  by  pronounc- 
ing letters  never  meant  to  be  sounded.

The  Preston  Jubilee  (10  S.  x-ii.  227). — 
The  Preston  Guild,  or  Guilda  Mercatoria, 
a  jubilee  celebrated  every'  twentieth 
year,  is  the  tenure  by  which  the  freemen 
retain  their  privileges :  it  was  originally 
granted  by  Henry  II.,  and  confirmed  by  the 
charters  of  Charles  II.  It  commences  in  the 
last  week  of  August,  and  is  proclaimed  to 
continue  twenty-eight  days  ;   the  festivities. 


however,  have  been  limited  to  a  fc

and  three  aldermen,  appointed  as  s 
who,  with  the  other  onicers  of  the 
tion,  dressed  in  their  robes  of  o 
and  preceded  by  their  insignia  of  o 
tlie  regalia  of  the  borough,  walk  : 
Guildhall,  attended  by  the  several 
companies  and  their  banners  an 
of  music,  and  by  the  principa 
resident  in  the  neighbourhood,  to  th 
cross  or  obelisk  in  the  niarket-plac< 
a  proclamation  is  read,  calling  up< 
burgesses,  resident  and  non-resi 
appear  before  the  stewards  of  1 
and  three  senior  aldermen  in  op 
and  renew  their  freedomi.  Dui 
festival  various  processions  of  the  : 
bodies  take  place  ;  balls,  concerts, 
representations,  pubUc  banquets,  i 
species  of  amusement,  are  provi 
attract  an  immense  concourse 
surrounding  districts,  to  assist  at 
mony,  and  share  in  the  festivities.

It  is  noted  at  the  end  that  50,  *  Hubert 
and  Arthur* ;  *Dog*  (under  one  number: 
50  and  52  ?) ;  63,  '  Girl  and  Cat  * ;    and  54, 

*  Woman  and  Child,*   were    new  in   1813 ; 
24,  *  Litter    of   Foxes,'    in    1815;   and   49, 

*  Napoleon  Bonaparte,'  in  1816. 

Most  of  the  titles  are  followed  by  poetical 
extracts.  That  given  to  *  Lodona  *  is  of 
thirty-four  lines  ;  that  of  *  Jephtha's  Rash 
Vow  *  is  nearly  six  verses  from  Judses. 

Doubtless  there  were  further  additions  to 
the  gallery  from  time  to  time.  Among  the 
few  pictures  mentioned  by  Lei^h  in  1823 
are  the  following,  which  are  not  in  the  1816 
catalogue  :  '  Moonlight  *  by  Rubens,  two 
landscapes  by  Fruicisco  Mola,  and  a  portrait 
of  Miss  Linwood.  Among  the  pictures 
mentioned  by  Leigh,  "  *  Children  in  a 
Cottage,'  by  Gainsborough,"  probably  means 
'  Children  at  the  Fire '  (62),  under  the 
heading  "  Cottage  "  ;  and  *'  Dead  Birds  and 
Shell-Fish  by  Haughton  "  probably  refers  to 
'  Woodcocks  and  Kingfisher  '  (22)  or  *  Part- 
ridges '  (23),  or  both,  and  *  Oysters '  (8). 
Perhaps  "  Rubens "  stands  for  Joseph 
Wright  (39). 

In  the  1816  catalogue  "Cottage,"  "Grove," 
"  Ruins,"  and  "  Dens  "  mean,  apparently, 
parts  or  offshoots  of  the  "  Gothic  Room." 

If  Timbs  is  correct  in  liis  dates,  the  dura- 
tion of  this  exliibition  was  extraordinary. 
It  would  be  interesting  to  know  whether 
these  worsted  pictures  were  dispersed,  also 
what  was  the  style  of  the  needlework. 

Robert  Pierpoint. 
[Other  references  to  Miss  Linwood's  needlework 

i>ictures  will  be  found  at  8  S.  i.  13 ;  xii.  4^,  517 ; 
•  S>.  i.  314  ;  ii.  275,  512  ;  iii.  72.] 


LONGFELLOW. 

(See  ante,  pp.  201,  222,  224,  261.) 

The  title  'Ultima  Thule '  proved  signi- 
ficant, for  it  was  the  last  work  published 
under  Longfellow's  own  eye.  His  brother 
well  speaks  of  the  eighteen  poems  as  "  con- 
taining the  sweetness  of  ripened  grain " ; 
they  show  that  the  fountain  of  youth  was 


within  hiih,  and  that  in  age 
of  the  poet  may  "  bloom  into  soi 
gorse  blossoms  in  autumn  Mid 
Amons  the  poems  is  the  one  de< 
the  cnildren  of  Cambridge,  *] 
Ajnn-chair.'  The  chair  was  pre 
him  on  his  seventy-second  birtii 
was  made  from  the  wood  of  t] 
blacksmith's  chestnut  tree.  Sevei

Longfellow  was  of  medium  heiglit,  and 
Ixis  face  is  familiar  to  us  from  many  delightful, 
portraits.  In  later  years  his  silvery  hair 
was  carelessly  thrown  back  from  his  forehead ; 
a  full  beard*  and  moustache  partially  con- 
cealed the  pleasant  mouth  :  but  liis  mild 
blue  eyes  expressed  the  kindUness  of  liis 
heart  and  his  quick  reading  of  the  hearts  of 
otliers.  All  who  had  the  privilege  of  being 
received  in  his  home  tell  us  of  his  exquisite 
.simplicity  of  manners,  and  his  soft,  sweet, 
mib^ieal  voice,  which,  like  his  face,  had  the 
innate  charm  of  tranquilUty ;  he  had 
what  the  French  aptly  call  the  "  politeness 
of  tlie  heart,"  and  had  a  magnetism  which 
drew  all  hearts  towards  him,  Mrs.  Carlyle 
remembered  his  visit  to  them  at  Craigen- 
puttock  as  "  the  visit  of  an  angel  "  ;  and 
William  Winter,  who  had  been  greeteti  by 
him  as  a  yoimg  aspirant  in  literature,  would 
walk  miles  to  Longfellow's  hoiwe  only  to 
]mt  his  hand  upon  the  latch  of  tlie  gate 
wliich  the  poet  himself  had  touched. 

Wliittier  wrote  to  Aldrich  a  few  days  after 
Longfellow's  death  :  — 

"  It  at?(Mna  an  if  I  oimld  never  write  again.  A 
keeling  of  uriuttt?rahle  sorrow  and  lonoliiiesa 
uitiircases  nie" ; 

and  in  a  letter  to  liis  niece,  Mrs.  Pickard, 
he  said  : — 

'*  He  haa  hwri  an  influenue  for  ffood ;  all  the 
Ckriatian  virtueH  his  verwe  and  his  lite  exemplified. 
Pure,  kindly,  and  courteous,  simple,  yet  aoholarly, 
he  wft8  never  otherwise  than  a  gentleman.  There 
is  no  blot  on  the  crystal  imrity  of  hia  writings." 

W'hile  America  was  full  of  grief  for  her 
son,  England  mourned  for  liim  as  for  a 
brother.  The  British  pre.'js  was  as  one  with 
the  American  in  its  chorus  of  praise,  and 
The  Atheno'um  pronounced  him  to  be  "  the 
most  popular  of  English-speaking  poets." 
In  the  same  number  appeared  tributes  from 
Mr.  Hall  Caine  and  Mr.  Austin  Dobson.  I 
quote  the  closing  lines  of  the  latter : — 
Lie  cftlnj,  O  white  and  l»ureAtt>  hend  ! 
Lie  enlni,  O  Dead,  that  art  not  dettd, 

fSinoe  fnini  the  voiccleH'*  i^mve 
Thy  voice  shall  sJ^ook  to  old  and  vounj? 
While  Honc  yet  tiiwaks  an  En^hHh  tonKue 
By  Chailos'  or  riiamia'  wave  ! 
It  is  pleasant  to  record  that  Mr.  Dobaon'a 
verses  are    preserved    in  a   volume  on  the 
hbrarj*  table  at  Craigie  House.

rightly  rest  in  the  land  of  his  birtli,  we 
in  England  desired  to  have  him  associated 
with  our  own  VaUialla,  and  my  t»ld  friend  tlie 
late    Dr.  W.  C.  Bennett,  well   remom.bet«rfL 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES.    (i«a  vn.  Arwi  is.  UK. 


*  for  his  *  Songs  for  Sailors,'  jjjave  voieo  to  the 
universal  feeling  that  a  bust  of  him  sliould 
be  placed  in  our  own  Poets'  Comer.     Very 

■soon  a  powerful  committee  was  formed  by 
Dr.  Bennett,  with  the  Prince  of  Wales  as 
chairman,  and  Francis  Bennoch  as  treasurer. 
The  result  was  the  marble  bust  by  Thomas 
Brock,  A.R.A.  It  was  admitt<*d  to  its 
present  place  in  tlie  Abbey  on  March  2nd, 
1884.  by  Dean  Bradley,  and  is  the  first 
monument  of  an  American  autlior  placed 
there.  Dr.  Bennett  presented  the  album 
containing  the  five  hundred  autographs*  of 
the  subscribers  to  the  American  Longfellow 
Memorial  Committee.

Glencoe  Massacre,  (See  '2  S.  i.  32.) — 
The  Daily  Oraphic  of  28  January  last  con- 
tained a  facsmiile  of  the  order  for  the 
massacre  of  Glencoe,  1692,  and  on  16  Feb- 
ruary it  was  pointed  out  that  a  copy  of  the 
warrant  published  in  1711  differs  in  several 
respects  from  the  document  now  put  forward, 
one  material  difference  being  that  the  1711 
copy  reads,  "  This  you  are  to  put  in  execu- 
tion at  five  o'clock  in  the  raormng  precisely ' '  ; 
whereas  tlie  present  facsimile  has  '*  fivu 
o'clock  precisely,"  morning  being  omitted. 
At  the  above  reference  in  '  N.  &  Q.,'  the  order 
is  given  also  without  the  words  "  in  the 
morning  "  ;  but  I  find  another  variation 
in  the  notes  to  Talfourd's  '  Glencoe  ;  or, 
the  Fatei  of  the  Macdonalds,'  wherein  it  is 
stated,  "^  Til  is  you  are  to  put  in  execution  at 
iour  in  the  morning,  precisely "  ;  and 
further  on,  "If  I  do  not  come  to  ycu  at 
four."  R.  J.  F^-NMORE.

"  CrBBAKDY." — This  is  a  word  which  is 
used  to  denote  anything  old,  fusty,  musty, 
or  rusty,  bearing  the  smell  of  long-keeping. 
Articles  of  food  kept  in  a  cupboard  or  pantry 
till  they  are  stale,  tliough  not  pa«t  use,  are 
'*  cubbardy."  It  is  an  expressive  bit  of 
folk-speech,  and  covers  a  good  deal  of  ground 
and  many  subjects.  1  have  known  it  all 
my  hfe,  but  only  occasionally  hear  it  used 
nuw.  Tmos.  Katclitfe.

A  F.\CE  UPON  Conscience. — When  Bal- 
thazar, in  *  Much  Ado  about  Nothing/ 
II.  hi.  47.  has  asked  to  be  excused  from 
repeating  his  song,  Don  Pedro  handsomely 
observes : — 

It  18  the  witneaa  utill  of  excellency 

To  put  a  fltrange  face  on  his  own  j»erfeetion. 

Manifestly  in  the  spirit  of  tliis  happy  cora- 
pUtnent,  Sir  Walter  Scott,  in  a  letter  to 
Cadell  of  12  Dee.,  1830,  refers  to  the  gently 
depreciatory  criticism  of  his  frienda  on 
'  Count  Robert  of  Paris.'  After  alluding 
to  the  unsatisfactory  state  of  lii»  health, 
and  the  difliculties  under  wliich  he  labours 
at  his  inevitable  task,  he  proceeds  thujs : — 

"One  night  last  month,  when  1  had  a  friend  with 
me,  I  had  a  flight  vertigo  when  ^toing  to  l>e<i,  and 
foil  down  ill  my  diVK«ijin-nM.>ni,  though  but  for  one 
instant.  Upon  thin  I  wrf)te  to  Dr.  Alu'ieroniby, 
and  in  {■oiiHt^iuence  of  his  advice,  I  have  restricte<l 
niysvlf  yet  f>\rthor,  and  have  cut  off  tJie  cigar.  uikI 
almost  half  of  the  moiintain-dew.  Now,  in  the 
tid^t  of  all  tht«,  I  Jtegan  my  work  with  a^  nuich 
Attention  &»  I  eoidd  ;  and  liavin^  taken  itainn  with 
niv  nt*iry,  I  find  it  i«  not  relished,  nor  indeed 
toleratecl  by  thorns  who  have  no  interewt  in  <«nii. 
(lemninti;  it,  but  a  strong  iutereut  in  putting  *jven  a 
fafB  upon  their  c-on.^ciencc»," 

Quoting  this  passage  on  p.  157  of  his 
monograpli  on  Scott,  in  '*  English  Men  of 
Letters,"  the  late  Mr.  K.  H.  Hutton  queries 
the  use  of  "  face  "  in  the  closing  phra.se, 
and  suggests  "  force  "  as  the  word  ]>robably 
intended  by  the  \\Titer.  When  proposing 
this  emendation,  tlie  critic  may  have  beeti 
stimulated  by  the  recollection  of  Milton's 
sonnet  *  On  the  New  Forcers  of  Conscience,' 
and  if  bo  he  would  nattu'ally  fail  to  detect 
the  distinctive  shade  of  meaning  in  the  state- 
ment as  it  stands.  TJiere  is  no  forcing  in 
the  attitude  presented  in  Scott's  metaphor ; 
it  is  rather  a  niinble  and  gracious  iniper- 
sonation  that  is  suggested  than  an  imperious 
compulsion.  Like  Hosahnd's  damaeb,  the 
critics  of  the  new  book  might,  presmuably, 
decline  to  make  full  revelation  of  what 
they  firmly  beUeved.  That,  says  the  '*  saucy 
lackey,"  gathering  the  argument  to  a  con- 
clusion, "  is  one  of  the  poijjts  in  the  wliich 
w^oinen  still  give  the  he  to  their  con-sciences." 
Similarly,  it  is  a  feature  in  the  generous 
treatment  of  a  friend  when  failure  within  his 

eial  sphere  comes  up  for  C4>nwideration.

"  Fires  '*  for  "  Cy.mbals."— In  the  1598 
English  translation  of  Linschoten's  'Voyagie' 
there  is,  in  two  places,  an  extraordinarj' 
and  (to  me)  inexplicable  misrendering  of  the 
original  Dutch-  The  tirst  passage  occurs 
in  chap,  xxxiii..  where  we  are  told  of  "  the 
heathens,  Indians,  and  other  strangers 
dwelling  in  Goa/'  that 

*'  when  they  will  nmke  a  voyage  to  Sen.  they  use  at 
least  lourtetne  dayen  l>efofe  fthey  eiit«r  into  their 
«<hi]»»,  to  niftke  wj  great  a  uoyse  with]  stmnding  of 
Trumi>et»,  anri  to  make  fiers,  that  it  may  be  heard 
and  seone  both  by  night  and  day."* 

Here  the  words  in  brackets  are  an  inter- 
polation of  the  translator's,  and  the  last 
clause  should  reeui  "  that  it  may  not  be 
lieard  nor  seene  by  night  and  day  "  ;  while 
"  to  make  fiers "  should  be  "  to  beat 
cymbals  "    (Dutch    '*  Beckens     te    slaen  "  ; 

Latin  of   1599,  *•  clan^ori patenarum**). 

Again,  in  chap,  sxscviii.  we  are  told  regard- 
ing *'  the  C'anaras  and  Decani jns,"  that 
"  when  they  are  to  be  mariod,  they  begin 
fourteene  dayes  before  [to  make]  a  great 
soiuid  with  trumpets,  drummes  and  fires,'* 
this  last  word  bemg  once  more  a  substitu- 
tion for  '*  cymbals  *'  ("  Becken  "). 

Can  any  reader  explain  how  the  translator 
came  to  misunderstand  the  original,  or 
dehberately  to  substitute  the  making  of 
fires  for  the  beating  of  cymbals  ¥ 

Donald  FsBOUaoN.

Fair  in  East  Anglia  t  The  name  MaeMichael

"Forwhy"  (10  S.  vii.  185,  237).— 
*'  Forwhy "  was  one  of  E.  A.  Freeman's 
pet  archaisms.  I  think  I  heard  him  use  it, 
and  it  occurs  frequently  in  his  letters  ;  e.g., 
in  one  to  Boyd  Dawkins,  dated  1  Sept.,  1878 
('  Life  and  Letters,'  vol.  ii.  p.  166) : — 

"  We  spake  lately  of  lyons ;  have  you  any  view 
about  libbards  ?  Did  they  retreat  also  ?  Have  they 
left  any  signs  by  the  banks  of  either  Axios,  Wookey 
Hole,  or  Macedonia?  Forwhy  there  are  some 
casual  allusions  to  them  in  Homer,  and  one— I 
think  only  one— long  simile." 
Other  instances  occur  on  pp.  261,  256,  430, 
456  of  the  same  volimie,  and  careful  search 
would  no  doubt  result  in  the  jneld  of  many 
more  examples.  St.  Swithin.

nietliod  utterly  absurd  and  impossible ;

nounced  in  the  English  fashion  or  I 
precisely  as  in  the  case  of  those  from  R« 
or  other  sources.  People  who  sound  jDrttl 
mcuaage,  fracas,  tiara,  armada,  as  if  « 
were  foreign  words  certainly  have  no 
to  cavil  at  plus,  minus,  vid,  bona  ,

Roman  maimer.

NOTES  AND  QUERIES,    [lo  s.  vn.  afril  «. 


all  its  subtlety  when  ambassadors  are  said 
to  be  men  sent  **  abroad  to  lie/'  instead  of 
men  sent  "  to  lie  abroad/'  for  the  benefit 
of  their  country,  as  there  is  a  double  mean- 
ing in  Ue  which  seems  to  be  dispelled  by  the 
transposition.  To  lie  signifies  not  only  to 
tell  falsehoods,  but  to  reside  or  to  stay. 
The  'N.E.D.'  has  the  following  example 
among  several  others :  "  1632,  Lithgow, 
*Trav.'  iv.  141  [He]  kept  a  better  house, 
than  any  Ambassadour  did  that  ever  lay 
at  Constantinople." 

Izaak  Walton's  account  of  Sir  Henry 
Wotton's  skit  at  his  profession  will  bear 
repetition  (*  Lives,'  pp.  128-9,  Zouch's 
edition) : — 

'*  At  his  first  Koiug  ambassador  into  Italy,  as  he 
Xtfissed  through  Germany,  he  stajred  some  days  at 
Augusta,  where  having  been  iu  his  former  travels, 
well  known  by  many  of  the  best  note  for  learning 
and  in^niousness  (those  that  are  esteemed  the 
virtuosi  of  that  nation),  with  whom  he,  iMkssiug  an 
evening  in  merriments,  was  requested  by  Cnris- 
topher  Flecamore  to  write  some  sentence  in  his 
AlDO  (a  book  of  white  paper  which  the  German 
gentry  uauallv  Oftrry  about  with  them  for  that 
puniose) ;  ana  Sir  Henry  Wotton  consenting  to  the 
motion,  took  an  occasion  from  some  accidental 
discourse  of  the  x>resent  coniiMny  to  write  a 
pleasant  definition  of  an  ambassador,  in  these  very 
words: 

*'  *  Legatus  est  vir  bonus  peregr^  missus  ad  men- 
tiendum  reipubliose  oaus&.' 

"Which  Sir  Henry  Wotton  could  have  been 
content  shonld  have  been  thus  Englished  : 

*"An  ambatisador  is  an  honest  man,  sent  to  lie 
abroad  for  the  good  of  his  country.' 

*'  But  the  woi-d  for  lie,  being  the  hinge  ui>on 
which  the  conceit  was  to  turn,  was  not  so  expressed 
in  Latin  as  would  admit  (in  the  hands  of  an  enemy 
eaiHJcially)  so  fair  a  construction  as  Sir  Henry 
thought  in  English.  Yet  as  it  was,  it  slept  quietly 
among  other  sentences  in  this  Albo,  almost  eight 
years,  till  by  accident  it  fell  into  the  hands  oi 
Jasper  Seioppius,  a  Romanist,  and  a  man  of  a  rest- 
less spirit  and  a  malicious  ]>en ;  who,  with  bonks 
against  King  James.  ]irints  this  as  a  principle  of 
that  religion  professed  by  the  King,  and  his 
Ambassador  Sir  Henry  Wotton,  then  at  Venice: 
and  in  Venice  it  was  presently  after  written  m 
several  glass-windows  and  sjiitefully  declared  to  be 
Sir  Henry  Wotton's." 

It  is  interesting  to  note  how  the  "  Albo  "  of 
the  seventeenth  century  developed  into  the 
fashionable  album  of  the  eighteenth  and 
early  nineteenth,  which  was  chiefly  charac- 
terized by  its  vari-coloured  pages.

gram on  Fr^ron  : — 

L'rtiitre  jour,  411  fond  d'un  \iLlloiif

According  to  M.  6doiiard  Fournier, 
'  L' Esprit  des  Autres'  (sixth  ed.,  1881, 
p.  288),  the  quatrain  is  simply  the  reatijust- 
ment  of  a  Latin  distich  in  the  '  Epigram- 
matuni  Delectus,'  1659.  But  the  idea 
originated  in  a  couplet  from  the  '  Greek 
Antholog>%'  ed.  Jacobs,  1813-17,  ii.  387. 
Another  Greek  version  on  tlie  same  aubjtct 
will  be  found  at  2  S.  iv.  SOO.

°*  stiffish  but  imposing  in  brown  satin  and 
fine  lace  ** ;  "  claw-foot  chairs  and  black 
mahogany  tables  ** ;  "  a  set  of  Hogarth's 
origii]^  plates;  Pope,  original  edition, 
15  volumes,  London,  1717  "  ;  and  books 
— '"  Above  all  things,  as  a  child  he  should 
have  tumbled  about  in  a  library"  and 
should  be  at  home  "whenever  he  smells 
the  invigorating  fragrance  of  Russia  leather," 
All  these  things  and  more  go  to  the  moulding 
of  Oliver  Wendell  Holmes's  man  of  family, 
and  he  makes  the  bold  and  i^parently, 
though  not  really,  anti-repubUcan  declara- 
tion :  "  No,  my  friends,  I  go  (always, 
other  things  being  equal)  for  th^  man  that 
inherits  family  traditions  and  the  cumulative 
humanities  of  at  least  four  or  five  genera- 
tions "  At  the  eleventh  breakfast,  shortly 
after  telling  about  "  the  wonderful  one-hoss- 
shay,"  he  retvims  to  the  subject,  and  glances 
at  another  phase  of  it.  This,  too,  should 
be  read  ;  but  what  the  "  Autocrat "  says 
at  tlie  first  breakfast  is,  I  think,  the  thing 
sought  for.  John  Oxbebry.

The  Ell 
Low, 

From  100,  Southwark  Street^  8. EL,  appears  the 
■seventieth  yearly  issue  of  this  important  Inblio- 
graphical  work,  one  of  the  most  trustworthy  and 
indispensable  guides  to  the  collector  of  books  and 
the  dealer  therein.  In  this  the  titles  and  index 
.  are  in  one  alphabet.  The  value  of  the  work,  proven 
by  long  experience,  is  once  more  a])parent.  The 
names  and  addresses  of  publishers,  together  with 
those  of  the  principal  publishers  in  the  United 
States  and  Canada,  are  supplied. 

The  Scott^^<h  Historical   Jferiew.    January — April, 

1007.  (Glasgow,  MacLehose  k  Sons.) 
Much  has  been  written  on  what  is  known  as  the 
-  Great  Schism  of  the  Roman  Church  —  an  event 
which  largely  modified  the  hold  which  the  Papacy 
had  on  the  nations  of  Euroiw,  and  was  the  lore- 
rrunner,  though  but  in  a  slight  degree  the  cause,  of 
the  Reformation.  No  history,  however,  has  yet 
been  written  of  this  important  contest  of  sufficient 
•flcope  to  satisfy  the  reasonable  desires  of  either  the 
historian  or  the  student  of  theology.  The  subject 
is  in  many  respects  the  most  difiicult  in  mediaeval 
history,  for  it  must  never  be  forgotten  that  a  large 
number  of  the  most  devout  and  oonscientious 
Catholics  of  the  time— St  Vincent  Ferrer  among 
others— took  the  side  of  him  who  is  now  generally 
regarded  as  the  anti-Pope.  To  the  future  historiui, 
when  he  arises,  Mr.  A.  Francis  Steuart's  '  Scotland 
and  the  Papacy  during  the  Great  Schism '  will  be  a 
valuable  directory  of  local  knowledge.  Scotland, 
as  a  kingdom,  took  the  side  of  the  anti-Poj^es.  Why 
this  was  so  it  is  hard  to  say  ;  perhafM  the  fact  that 
England  adhered  to  the  Roman  Pontiff,  and  France 
to  the  dweller  at  Avigiiou,  may  have  had  something 
to  do  with  it,  hut  it  is  not  easy  to  believe  that  a 
geographical  reason  of  this  kind  can  have  had  much 
weight ;  for  although  the  State  held  with  what  is 
called  the  schismatic  j»arty,  the  Roman  Pontiff  had 
a  by  no  means  desincable  following  in  Scotland, 
which  was  at  times  the  cause  of  dispute  and 
turmoil.  On  these  matters  Mr.  Steuart  has  collected 
many  useful  facts,  and,  so  far  as  we  can  see,  hixa 
stated  the  case  with  admirable  fairness. 

Mr.  J.  L.  Morison's  i)ai>er  on  'Ancient  Legend 
and  Modern  Poetry  in  Ireland'  indicates  a  i)ro- 
found  knowledge  of  the  oldest  thoughts  and 
imaginings  of  the  Irish  people.  With  all  he  has 
said  some  people  may  not  agree,  but  no  one  can 
master  what  is  given  without  an  increase  in  breadth 
of  view.  Mr.  Morison  feels  deeply  the  intrinsic 
power  of  those  forms  of  knowledge  or  feeling  which 
are  embodied  in  Irish  legend.  In  such  cases  neither 
knowledge  nor  feeling  has  to  him  anything  beyond 
symbolic  significance,  for  he  says,  in  words  with 
which  many  readers  will  be  in  full  agreement,  that 
*'  The  mood  which  finds  satisfaction  in  old  tales  is 
that  which  recognizee  how  things  which  do  appear 
s constitute  only  a  fringe  of  reality,  and  how  beyond 
are  truths  whose  imiiort  may  bow  the  mind  with 
awe."

more  the  vexed  question  of  the  Papal  dispensation 


for  the  marriage  of  Mary,  Queen  oj

The  accomit  of  this  family  eiven  in  *Tk 
Priory  of  Penwortham*  (Chetnam  Sodetj 
O.S.,  vol.  xzx.),  contains  some  errors. 

Sir  William  Fleetwood,  father  of  S 
Miles  Fleetwood,  Receiver  of  the  Court  < 
Wards,  is  also  said  to  have  been  the  fathi 
of  Qeorge,  the  Swedish  general.  Sir  Williai 
had  a  son  George  (baptized  at  St.  Maifi 
Ealing,  3  Aug.,  1586),  who  was  executor  t 
his  mother  in  1625.  The  Swedish  genen 
was  son,  not  brother,  of  Sir  Miles.  Tb 
same  confusion  occurs  with  regard  to  WilliiiB 
son  of  Sir  William,  and  Sir  William  of  Aid 
wincle  and  Woodstock  Park  (buried  i 
Aldwincle  12  Feb.,  1673/4),  son  of  Sir  Mil« 

The  account  znakes  Col.  George  Fleel 
wood  the  Regicide  and  General  Charles  Flefll 
wood  brothers,  and  sons  of  the  second  9 
William  Fleetwood.  The  Re^cide*s  pareOl 
age  has  been  correctly  given  m  10  S.  i.  42t 
while  General  Charles  was  son  of  Sir  Mik^ 
Receiver  of  the  Court  of  Wards,  and  theft 
fore  brother  to  the  Swedish  general. 

Recorder  Fleetwood  was  not  knighted 
The  remark  "  stated  by  some  authorital 
to  have  been  illegitimate  "  is  not  borne  od 
by  the  context  of  his  father's  will,  ad 
appecurs  to  have  eurisen  from  a  misunderstand 
lag  regarding  his  coat  of  arms,  which  I 
explained  in  '  Lancashire  Funeral  Certil 
cates ' ;  moreover,  the  heralds  style  hi 
son  and  heir  of  Robert  Fleetwood  (Chethai 
Society,  O.S.,  Ixxv.  28-9,  and  10  S.  vi.  26( 
436). 

The  Recorder  had  six  sons  and  M 
daughters.  As  Robert  (the  second  of 
two  sons  assigned  to  him  in  *  The  Pri< 
Penwortham  ^)  was  dead  in  1593,  aeci 
to  the  funeral  certificate  above  menti< 
it  is  obvious  that  he  could  not  have  been 
knight  who  was  Attorney-General  to  Ftial 
Henry,  son  of  James  J. 

Thomas  Talbot  (p.  ha)  is  said  to  hai 
been  baptized  at  Penwortham,  15  Feb.,  1(2 
This  is  a  misprint ;  the  year  ^ould  be  1611 

Mb.  Pink's  interesting  note  on  the  dl 
volution  of  the  Penwortham  estates  cs&  I 
amplified  from  other  sources,  as  I  ducovert 
Arthur  Fleetwood's  parentage  some  yc 
ago  from  his  will.     The  foUowing  pMif 
gives  a  good  deal  of  information  in  •  oon 
form : — 


*  Love's  Laboub'b  Lost,'  V.  ii.  750-53  :— 
The  extreme  parts  of  time  extremely  forms 
All  causes  to  the  purpose  of  his  speed, 

And  often  at  his  very  loose  decides 
That  which  long  process  could  not  arbitrate. 
In  "extremely  forms"  the  idea  of  com- 
pleteness, finality,  seems  prominent.  The 
extreme  of  the  formative  process  is  suddenly 
reached — an  ultimate  stage  of  "  All  causes,'' 
corresponding  to  "  the  extreme  parts  of 
time,'  €md  adapted  to  **  the  purpose  of  his 
speed."  It  is  as  though  the  intermediate 
formative  steps  of  "causes"  had  been 
skipped  with  the  abrupt  termination  of  given 

Eenods  of  time.  This  adjustment  is  rather 
appy  and  free  than  otherwise,  imd  without 
opposition  such  as  is  implied  in  "  unflinch- 
ingly, inexorably,  severely,  relentlessly," 
suggested  by  Furness  as  the  meaning  of 
"  extremely.  I  beUeve  we  should  retain 
the  correspondence  between  "  extreme " 
and  "  extremely,"  the  extreme  parts  of 
time  being  matched  by  causes  which  are 
formed  extremely,  or  which  take  on  extreme 
forms.  E.  Mbbton  Dey. 

*  Henby  IV.,'  Past  I.,  II.  iv.  134 :  "  Pm- 

FCX-HEABTED    TiTAN,    THAT      MELTED  "     (10 

S.  vi.  504 ;  vii.  145). — Theobald's  emenda- 
tion, which  receives  the  support  of  Mb. 
Davey,  has  been  adopted  by  several  modem 
editors.  The  first  edition  which  I  consulted 
— Charles  Knight's — follows  this  reading, 
and  it  is  difficult  to  see  how  sense  can  be 
made  out  of  the  passage  otherwise.  I 
cannot  understand  Mr.  Forrest  Morgan's 
difficulties.  Ho  asks,  "  Who  is  Titan,  and 
why  should  he  be  dragged  in  by  the  heels  ?  " 
Helios,  the  Sun-god,  was  son  of  the  Titan 
Hyperion,  and  the  poets  applied  the  name 
Titan  to  him,  as  being  the  offspring  of 
Titans.  What  the  Prince  means  is  that 
Falstaff,  rushing  into  the  tavern  after  his 
flight   from     Gadshill,    and    dripping    with 

Cerspiration,  reminded  liim  of  a  dish  of 
utter  exposed  to  the  too-melting  rays  of  the 
sun.  Neither  the  waxen  wings  of  Icarus 
nor  the  chariot  of  Phaethon,  who  was  burnt 
up,  not  by  a  sun-stroke,  but  by  the  lightning 
of  Zeus,  have  any  relevance  to  the  Prince's 
rather  ill-natured  chaff.  Those  who  are 
acquainted  with  the  mythology  of  HeUos 
Mali  recognize  the  force  of  the  epithet 
"  pitiful-hearted."  Aglaus. 

There  seems  to  me  no  room  for  doubt  that 
Theobald's  emendation  is  right.  The  pre- 
terit "  melted  "  is  quite  suitable,  as  it  refers 
to  the  special  case  witnessed  in  the  past ; 
Titan  is  euphuistic  for  the  sun,  and  the 
latter  comes  in  naturally,  as  a  face  running

Burke  says  Barbara  Legge  was  sole  heiress 
of  her  father. 

Bishop  James  Fleetwood  also  had  four 
daughters : — 

1.  *  married  John  Hicks  (who  died 

about  1662).  They  had  two  daughters, 
Martha  and  Honora,  living  1682  (Metcalfe's 
*Vis.  of  Worcestershire  1682-3/  p.  66). 
Arthur  Fleetwood  mentions  these  nieces 
.in  his  wiU. 

2.  Bridget,  married Meale. 

3.  Mary,  married Allison. 

4.  Elizabeth,  married Webster. 

Henry  Fleetwood,  who  succeeded  to  the 

Penwortham  estates,  received  an  ensigncy 
in  the  7th  or  Royal  Regiment  of  Fusiliers, 
12  June,  1685 ;  was  a  second  lieutenant  in 
1687;  became  lieutenant  2  Oct.,  and  first 
lieutenant  of  his  uncle  Lord  Dartmouth's 
company,  3  Oct.,  1688.  This  was  one  of  the 
regiments  raised  by  James  II.  in  the  sununer 
of  1685,  Lord  Dartmouth's  commission  as 
colonel  being  dated  Whitehall,  11  June  in 
that  year. 

It  will  be  seen  from  the  above  pedigree 
that  Honora,  daughter  of  Arthur  Fleetwood, 
was  unmarried ;  it  is  probable,  therefore, 
that  it  was  her  sister  Jane  who  married 

Hinton.     Arthur  appears  to  have  had 

another  daughter,  named  Katherine,  buried 
at  St.  Michael's,  Cambridge,  9  June,  1669 
('Registers  of  St.  Michael's,  Cambridge, 
1538-1837  '). 

Clutterbuck's  '  Herts,'  ii.  533-4,  contains 
some  biograpliical  details  of  the  Rev,  John 
Fleetwood,  Archdeacon  of  Worcester  ;  and 
there  is  a  reference  to  him  in  Cussans's 
'  Herts,'  ii.  35,  Hundred  of  Broadwater. 

William  Beckford  of  Ashtead  and  William 
Beckford,  the  author  of  '  Vatliek,'  were 
descended  from  a  common  ancestor. 

R.  W.  B.

MAGDALEN   COLLEGE    SCHOOL  ASD 

THE    *D.N.B.* 
(See  10  S.  iv.  21,  101,  182,  244,  364 ;  v.  22, 

122,  284,  362  ;    vi.  2,  104,  203 ;    vii.  63, 

142.) 

Magdalen  is  the  only  college  in  Oxford  or 
Cambridge  which  has  a  full  choral  service 
twice  daOy.  Peter  Heylyn  (see  10  S.  iv.  21) 
in  his  *  Memorial  of  Waynflete  '  rimes  u 
follows : — 


The  Quire  consists  of  twontv-nine ;  wherein 
There  are  four  Chaplains,  who  by  turns  do  say 
The  Clergy-prayers,  and  more  eight  Clerks  there 

been. 
And  sixteen  Choristers,  o'er  whom  bears  sway 
One,  who  doth  teaoh  them  how  to  sing  with  ease, 
Whose  nimble  fingers  on  the  oraans  play 
Gravely  oomnosedChuroh-mnsio :  and  all  these 

With  different  notes,  which  sweetly  do  accord. 

Sing  Allelujah  to  the  living  Lord. 
And  the  Kev.  James  Elwin  Millard,  himself 
a   former   Head    Master   and   ex-chorister, 
says  in  his  '  Historical  Notices  of  the  Office 
of  Choristers'  (1848):— 

"Bishop  Waynflete did  not  look   upon  hii 

Choristers  as  mere  necessary  appendagM  for  the 
due  i)erformanoe  of  churoh  omces.  They  were 
considered  as  much  a  part  of  the  bodyoorponte 
as  the  Fellows,  Demies,  and  other  memhers  of  the 
foundation.  The  founder  ordered  that  in  case  of 
great  scarcity  or  dearth,  and  the  failure  of  the 
College  rents,  the  number  of  Choristers  should  be 
reduced  only^  from  sixteen  to  eight,  whereas,  if  tie 
scarcity  continue,  he  wills  the  U^mies  of  his  college 
to  be  *  totally  suijpressed,'  and  afterwards  even  the 
numl)er  of  Fellows  to  be  reduced,  rather  than  the 
boys  of  his  choir  entirely  abandoned." 

The  '  New  English  Dictionary '  notei 
that  "  since  the  close  of  the  seventeenth 
century  quire  lias  been  fictitiously  spelt 
cftoir  ;  but  the  spelling  quire  has  never  been 
altered  in  the  EngUsh  Prayer  Book."  At 
Winchester  College  they  still  speak  correctly 
of  their  quiriatera.  Among  Magdalen  { 
choristers,  besides  those  already  noticedt 
may  be  mentioned  Henry  Robert  Bennett 
(1825-8),  who  succeeded  his  father  »» 
organist  of  Chichester  Cathedral;  Charles 
Lockey  (1828-36)  a  native  of  Oxford, 
gentleman  of  the  Chapels  Royal,  and  principv 
tenor  at  the  production  of  Mendelssohn'* 
*  Elijah  '  ;  and  Richard  Redhead  (1828-37). 
also  a  native  of  Oxford,  organist  of  MargaW* 
Chapel,  Marylebone.

tr      ,  1 H  .  I  to  live  at  peace.     The 


French  governor,  De  Marbeuf,  allowed  thera 
to  build  the  village  of  Cargese,  and  to  erect 
a  church  in  1774.  At  that  time  tlie  colony 
coneasted  of  110  famiUea,  who  spoke  only 
Greek,  and  who  preserved  their  national 
dress  and  religion.     By  degrees  the  Corsicans 

'  began  to  settle  in  Carg^,  and  the  ftision 
of  the  two  races  commenced.     At  the  present 

I  time  the  inliabitants  of  Cargese  either  speak 

j  French  or  the  Corsican  dialect,  and  the 
Greek  language  is  very  rarely  heard.     They 

]  have,  however,  adhered  to  their  rehgion, 
and  the  Greek  church,  wiiich  was  rebuilt 
in   1868,  stands    on  a  low  hill  which   foces 

1  another  slight  elevation,  on  which  a  Catholic 
church  has  been  built.  The  Greek  ''  pope  *' 
has  Telinquished  his  Eastern  costume,  and 
is  garbed  Uko  his  Romanist  confrere,  wliilo 
hist  services  are  generally  conducted  in 
French.  The  fact  that  Greek  is  taught  in 
the  eoiuimmal  school  prevents  any  educa- 
tional difficulty  arising.  The  women  uf 
Cargese  Btill  preserve  the  Greek  type  of 
beauty,  and,  unlike  the  Corsicans  of  the 
western  coast,  the  population  is  a  clean, 
hard-working  race  of  peasants,  who  enjoy 
great  material  prosperitv. 

W.  F.  Prideaux.

Wadswo&th  as  a  Yorkshibe  Name. —

**  The  Wadswwtha,  Longfellow's  anoestora  on  the

lK>ssible  oonnection  with  another  famous  poet."

St.  George  :  Georoe  as  a  Christian 
Name. — Many  parochial  guilds  bore  his 
name,  as  well  as  chantries  in  parish  churches  ; 
his  day  was  observed,  and  his  effigy  was 
carried  in  procession  ;  his  image  was  carved 
in  stone  and  in  wood,  painted  on  walls  and 
on  panels,  embroidered  on  banners  and  on 
tapestry,  and  represented  in  stained  glass. 
But,  although  163  churches  in  England  were 
dedicated  in  his  honour,  his  name  remeuns 
on  few  old  bells. 

Before  1700  George  is  not  at  all  a  common 
Christian  name,  and  scarcely  any  distin- 
guished man  seems  to  have  liad  it.  Have 
any  of  your  readers  noticed  this,  and  can 
they  suggest  an  exi)lanation  ?      W.  C.  B. 

"  FiBE  "  :  '*  Fire  out." — How  early  is 
the  slang  use  of  fire — now  niore  commonly 
fire  out — in  the  sense  of  turn  out  of  a  place 
or  situation  ?  I  find  it  in  a  quotation  in 
Dilke's  '  Papers  of  a  Critic  '  (ii.  298),  in  a 
letter  describing  the  very  complete  clearance 
meule  by  Lord  Bute,  in  the  winter  of  1762, 
of  those  who  had  been  appointed  by  the 
old  Whig  ministers  under  George  II.  The 
Duke  of  Newcastle  observes  to  the  Earl  of 
Hardwicke  : — 

"  I  send  your  Lonlship  the  most  criiel  and  in- 
human list  that  ever  was  seen,  not  only  in  a  free 
country,  nor  even  in  any  civilized  nation.  This  list, 
as  I  understand,  was  sent  to  the  Custom  House  on 
Saturday  last,  and  yet,  cruel  as  it  is,  mc  are  told  it 
is  only  their  Jirttt  Jive,  and  that  we  are  to  have  a 

ttexoiHl. There  is  not  one  single  man  tumetlout 

against  whom  the  slightest  complaint  can  be  made 
in  the  execution  of  their  office.'^

and  of  the  exciting  elections  of  the  times. 
He  was  further  connected  with  the  Brock- 
ford  branch  of  the  Revetts  by  the  marriage 
of  his  grandfather  with  the  daughter  of  liis 
couain  Robert  Rovott,  of  Brockford.  In 
fact,  in  old  days,  when  the  means  of  com- 
munication were  limited,  young  squires 
and  others  could  not  go  very  far  afield  for 
their  wives,  and  married  among  theii* 
neighbours  or  cousins  whom  they  had 
opportunities  of  meeting.

The  Revett  family  of  Suffolk — which,  as 
the  British  Museum  MS.  Department  indi- 
cates, was  a  very  extensive  one  some  hundred 
years  ago — is  now  extinct  in  the  male  line 
in  all  ita  branches,  save  in  that  of  wliich 
I  am  a  cadet,  and  wliich  is  now  repre^ 
sented  by  my  covLsin  Sir  James  Rivett- 
Carnac,  Bt.  Our  grandfather,  having  taken 
the  name  of  Carnac  by  sign-manual  in  1800, 
for  some  inexplicable  reason  changed  the 
spelling  of  the  original  family  name  from 
Rfvott  to  Rtvett.  I  may  add  that  much 
furtlier  information  regarding  Chippeiiliam, 
the  Revetts,  and  the  Russells  will  bo  found 
in  the  Rev.  R.  Barber's  *  An  East  Anglian 
Village.'  J.  H.  Ri\-ett-Cabkac.

St.  Agnes*  Eve  (10  S.  iv.  449).— Nearly 
eighteen    months   ago   I   asked   a  queetioa  , 
upon  the  subject  of   Keata's   poem  of  *  QU  | 
Agnes*  Eve.*     The  lover  is  there  described 
on  St.  Agnes'  Eve  as  heaping  a  table  by  the 
bedside  of  his  lady  with  vanoua  **  CAtoa  e.n<i 


312 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES,    [los.  vn.  April  «.w. 


dainties.'*  This  was  apparently  an  allusion 
to  some  old  custom,  and  I  had  long  been 
curious  to  hear  what  it  could  be.  As  no 
notice  was  taken  of  my  question  by  any  of 
your  readers,  I  concluded  that  all  were  as 
Ignorant  upon  the  matter  as  I  was  myself. 
It  may  therefore  be  interesting  to  admirers 
of  Keats's  poems  to  hear  that  St.  Augustine 
in  his  'Confessions'  (Book  \T:.  chap,  ii.) 
aJUudes  to  a  custom,  which  prevailed  m  the 
times  of  the  early  Fathers,  of  bringing  offer- 
ings on  saints'  days  of  cakes,  fruit,  bread, 
and  wine  in  memory  of  the  saint.  These 
offerings  were  first  tasted  by  the  devotee 
and  then  distributed.  The  custom  was  at 
length  forbidden  by  St.  Ambrose,  on  the 
ground  that  it  gave  occasion  to  excess,  and 
savoured  too  much  of  the  rites  of  ancestor 
worship  as  practised  by  the  heathen. 

Eustace  Smith,  M.D. 

Stubmy  or  Esturmy  Family  (10  S.  vii. 
209). — In  a  foot-note  on  p.  293  of  *  Forty

from,  as  is  popularly  beUeved,  the  shelter  it 
gave  in  stress  of  weather  to  Iving  Henry  VIII.,

please,  if  it  do  not  profit,  your  pedigree-hunt-

Ely  Housk  or  Albemarle  HorsE  (10 
S.  vii.  208). — According  to  Peter  Cunning- 
ham's '  Hand-book  of  London,'  1850, 
No.  37,  Dover  StrcK»t,  is  (1850)  Ely  House, 
the  London  KcHidence  of  the  Bisliops  of 
Ely  since  1772  (p.  160).  Monk,  first  Duke 
of  Albemarle,  lived,  from  a  little  before  the 
Restoration  to  hiw  death  (1060/70),  at  the 
Cockpit  at  Whitehall  (p.  133). 

Clarendon  House  was  begun  in  1664.  It 
stood  between  Berkeley  Street  and  Bond 
Street.  It  was  inhabited  by  the  Earl  of 
Clarendon,  his  eldest  son  Lord  Cornbury, 
and  the  great  Duke  of  Onnond.  Lord 
Chancellor  Clarendon  died  in  1674,  and  in 
1675  his  sons  sold  the  house  to  Christopher 
Monk,  second  Duke  of  Albemarle,  when  it 
became  Albemarle  House.  Tlio  Duke  sold 
it  to  Sir  Thomas  Bond,  who  pulled  it  down, 
and  raised  Bond  Street  and  Albemarle 
Buildings.  The  house  appears  to  have  had 
24  acres  of  land  attached  to  it  (pp.  123-4). 
Robert  Pierpoint. 

No.  37,  Dover  Street,  was  the  town  resi-  i 
dence  of  the  Bishop  of  Ely.     It  was  pur-  | 


chased  or  built  in  1772,  out  of  the  prooeedb 
of  the  sale  of  the  ancient  palaoe  of  the 
Bishops  of  Ely  in  Ely  Place,  Holbonu

topher Monck  or  Monk,  second  Dnke  of 
Albemarle  (1653-88),  who  purchased  th» 
mansion  of  the  Earl  of  Clarendon  whidi 
stood  partly  on  its  site.  The  street  im-- 
built  towards  the  close  of  the  seventeentii 
or  beginning  of  the  eighteenth  centniy  taf.

part  of  the  grounds  of  Clarendon  HoiMi 
"  in  order  to  build  a  street  of  tenementB  In

Duke  of  Kent's  Childben  (10  S.  vii. 
48,  115,  172,  235).— When  Mb.  Peet  speaks 
of  exceptional  treatment  of  Constance  Kent, 
does  he  mean  exceptionally  lenient  ?  For 
a  murder  which  was  committed  at  sixteen, 
and  which  she  voluntamly  confessed  in  order 
to  clear  her  father  long  after  all  search  had 
been  given  up,  she  was,  by  order  of  the 
Home  Office,  kept  in  prison  for  twenty 
^ears.  This  is  certainly  exceptional,  but 
It  is  an  odd  reason  for  supposing  her  to  have 
been  related  to  the  royeJ  feunily.  B. 

"Fmeze":  its  Pbonunciation  (10  S. 
vii.  245). — ^I  have  frequently  deprecated  the 
consideration  of  questions  of  pronunciation 
because  the  simplest  elements  of  it  remain 
still  wholly  unknown  to  most  readers.  All 
who  really  have  worked  at  the  subject  come, 
sooner  or  later,  to  appreciate  and  understand 
the  great  and  violent  changes  that  have  taken 
place  during  the  last  three  or  four  centuries. 

The  Unes  about  the  cloths  of  gold  and  of 
frieze  go  back  to  the  time  of  Henry  \1II., 
when  the  i  in  despise  retained  the  Norman 
sound,  like  the  French  i  in  the  modem  words 
machine  and  pique. 

It  is  the  word  frieze  that  is  exceptional ; 
it  was  felt  to  be  a  foreign  word,  and  so 
remained  unchanged.     The  conclusion  drawn 
is,  in  feust,  exactly  contrary  to  the  truth. 
Walteb  W.  Skeat. 

I  remember  a  tailor  who  spoke  to  me  of 
**  frize "  some  five-and-twenty  years  ago» 
and  I  have  an  impression  that  the  word  is 
often  so  rendered  in  Ireland,  and  other  parts 
of  the  United  Kingdom,  stiU.  The  lines 
quoted  by  your  correspondent  were  either 
made  up  or  much  quoted  when  Mary, 
daughter  of  Henry  VII.  and  widow  of 
Lotus  XII.,  married  Charles  Brandon,  Duke 
of  Suffolk.  St.  Swithik. 

The  rime  despite  :  frieze  in  an  old  poem 
might  prove  either  that  despise  was  for- 
merly pronounced  Uke  the  modem  word 
displectse,  or  that  frieze  was  pronounced  lik*" 
the  modern  tliird  person  singular  of  fry. 
As  a  matter  of  fact,  it  is  the  word  despise 
that  has  changed  its  pronimciation.  I  do 
not  know  from  what  source  the  lines  are 


quoted,  but  the  use  of  dwpwe=& 
the  reflexive  use  of  match  point  to 
bethan  period.  In  Shakespeaie' 
(10  S.  vi.  281)  the  word  despise  ft 
old  sound.  tJiough  the  modem 
tion  was  already  becoming  currei 
tradition  might  tolerate  the  < 
even  after  the  modem  pronunc 
triumphed.  L.  R.  M.  Si 

Heidelberg,  Germany.

Abraham  Lincoln  and  Eltiopean  Poli- 
ticians (10  S.  vii.  166,  275).— Mr.  Albert 
Matthews  has  mi.staken  my  point :  the 
emphasis  was  intended  to  be  on  the  word 
"  un-Wiiig,"  not  on  the  idea  **  ^liig." 
Tlio  American  party  nomenclature  of  the 
moment  referred  to  in  my  previous  com- 
munication is  well  known  to  me ;  but, 
unless  it  can  be  shown  that  the  use  of  the 
word  "  un-Whig "  in  the  sense  Lincoln 
employed  it  was  frequent,  I  must  adliere  to 
my  opinion  that  it  was  a  recollection  of 
the  younger  Pitt's  invention  of  that  vorj' 
uncommon  term.

As  the  years  went  on  the  system  .steadily 
spread,  and  it  was  obviously  in  full  swing  in 
1717,  for  I  find  in  The  Daihj  CouratU  for 
4  April  of  tliat  year  two  advertisements —

the  Benefit  of  Mr.  Penkethman,  By  His 
Majesty's  Company  of  Comedians  "  ;  and 
the  other  at  ''  the  Theatre  in  Lincoln's-Inn- 
Fielda,  For  the  Benefit  of  Mr.  Leveridge, 
By  the  Company  of  Comedians "  ;  while 
there  were  foiu"  such  advertisements  two 
days  later,  two  of  them  being  benefits  given 
''  at  the  Desire  [in  one  case,  "  the  par- 
ticular desire  "]  of  several  Ladies  of  Quahty,*'

Benefit  of  8ignor  Cavaliero  Nicolino  Orini- 
aldi,  at  the  King's  Theatre  in  the  Hay- 
Market." 

To  the  first  named  of  these  benefits  a 
special  interest  njay  be  held  to  attach,  for 
it  is  to  be  found  that  they  did  not  always 
realize  the  promise  of  their  narae.  The 
advertisement  which  appeared  on  the  day 
of  the  Drury  Lane  performance  on  4  April, 
1717,  announced  that  there  woidd  be 

•*  presented  a  Coraedv  call'd  Cireen  wick -Park.  The 
nnrt  of  Sir  Thomas  Reveller  bv  Mr.  Penkethmiwi.

THE    PAGE    FAMILY    AND    THEIR 
MIDDLESEX    ESTATES. 

The  name  of  Page  is  by  no  means  the 
oldest  of  Middlesex  surnames,  but  for  600 
years  it  is  to  be  traced  in  the  county  records. 
It  was  not  until  the  close  of  the  fifteenth 
century  that  Paiges  acquired  real  estate  of 
any  size,  and  it  is  in  1409  that  we  first  find 
a  Page  holding  land  in  Willesden.  At  that 
date  the  names  of  John  and  Richard  Page 
occur  in  the  Feet  of  Fines  as  having  parted 
with  their  holding  to  William  and  Mar- 
garet Page,  with  a  warranty  against  the 
Abbot  of  Westminster.  As  Willesden 
chiefly  belonged  to  the  Prebendaries  of  St. 
Paul's  at  that  date,  this  indication  of  the 
source  of  the  Pages*  first  introduction  to  the 
parish  is  interesting,  for  the  patronage 
would  be  more  directl;^  attributable  to  Grown 
influence,  the  Court  being  chiefly  in  the  City 
of  I  Westminster.  Indeed,  for  the  first  half 
of  the  sixteenth  century  and  earlier  the 
kings  and  the  Abbot  of  Westminster  greatly 
influenced  the  population  of  Hendon,  Hamp- 
stead,  and  some  portions  of  Willesden  ;  and 
friends  at  Court  introduced  their  needy 
cousins  and  kindred  to  the  church  pro- 
I)erties  of  tliis  neighbourhood  as  well  as 
otliers.  Tliat  the  name  of  Page  originated, 
as  many  other  surnames  have  done,  from 
the  oc('U]>ation  of  the  original  holder  there 
(^aii  ha  httk>  doubt ;  but  the  name  is  so 
much  more  ancient  tlian  1499  that  the  Pages 
of  that  date  arc  unlikely  to  have  been  actu- 
ally i)agcs  of  the  royal  family,  though  one 
.rohn  Page  of  this  fanxily  was  in  tlie  house- 
hold of  Queen  Elizabethr 

In  1500  tlie  fii-st  ^^'ilI  of  a  Page  of  Willes- 
den was  proved  in  tlio  i*rerogative  Court  of 
Canterbury.  This  testator,  William  Page. 
is  described  as  a  husbandmcui  of  *'  Our 
l^lessed  Lady  of  WillcHdon."  He  was  of 
Hufticient  substance  to  direct  tliat  his  body 
;  hould  be  buried  within  the  parish  church, 
and  he  mentions  for  charitable  bequests  the 
high  altar,  St.  Katherine's  I^ght,  Our  Lady 
of  Pity  Light,  St.  Christopher's  Light,  and 
the  Rood  Light,  all  witliin  the  parisli 
church  of  Willesden.  He  also  bequeathed 
an  altar-cloth  to  cost  26*.  8d.,  and  left  the 
largo  sum  (for  those  days)  of  10/.  for  the 
repair  of  the  lugliway  in  the  parish.  He 
mentions  as  relatives  his  wife  Margaret,  his 
son  John  Page,  also  Henry  and  Thomas  Page, 
and  William  Pago  the  younger.  In  addition 
to  Willesden  lands  (probably  held  on  lease), 
he  had  estate  in  Surrey  and  Middlesex, 
which  he  left  to  liis  wife  for  life  with  re- 
mainder to  his  son. 


Thomas  Pac^  of  Harrow,  in  his  wiUoMl  , 
in  1512  (P.C.C.  8  Fetyplace),  menti«l»  ' 
wife  Joan  and  his  sons  Thomas,  BiM» 
and  Henry,  and  wished  his  wife  to  "mf" 
with  his  son  Richard  in  his  *'  hiM  tf 
Sudburj',  late  Richard  Frankyng's  bm*

Page  of  Upper  Norton  Street,  St.  Maryle- 
bone,  dated  15  Nov.,  1825,  proved  1829.

Richard  Franklin  sen.  and  Richard  Franklin 
]un.»  botli  of  the  Mint,  are  legatees.  It 
is  at  least  clear  that  this  Henrj'  Page  held 
the  Manor  of  Harrow  (or  Wembley)  and  other 
]>roperty  ;  but  the  settlement  is  not  filed 
nor  available  for  public  research,  and  present 
claimants  will  have  considerable  difficulty 
in  tracing  the  lands  held.  It  is  certain  that 
the  estate  is  fai*  smaller  than  has  been  repre- 
sented by  the  press.

"  Amel  of  Ujda," — A  Renter  telegram 
dated  29  March  tells  us  that  "the  Amel 
(Governor)  of  Ujda  "  offered  no  resistance 
to  the  French  expeditionary  forc^.  This. 
i»  the  first  time  that  I  have  noticed  the 
occurrence  of  the  word  *■  Aniel  "  in  an 
English  newspaper.  It  is  an  Arabic  word 
of  genuine  Semitic  origin,  the  root  'owiaf 
occurring  both  in  Hebrew  and  Arabic.  TJie 
radical  notion  of  the  verb  is  "to  work, 
labour,  take  pains."  In  the  tenth  form  of 
the  Arabic  verb  we  find  the  sense  "to  make 
one  a  governor,  an  adniimstrator."  The 
word  'dmdi  often  occurs  in  the  Hebrew 
Fualnis  in  the  sense  of  trouble  (toil  or  sorrow )► 
A.  L.  Mayhew. 

Oxford. 

IXTLTLXOCHITL  AND  OTHER  AzTEC  NaMES. 

— Aft  far  back  at»  4  S.  v.  174  a  question  was 
asked  as  to  the  pronunciation  of  this  name, 
but  it  was  never  answered.  I  waa  recently 
consulted  on  the  same  point,  and  I  found 
that  English  dictionaries  do  not  agree  about 
it.  For  instance,  in  Smith's  '  Cyclopwdia  of 
Names  '  tliis  famous  Aztec  historian  appears 
as  Ixtlilxochitl,  with  final  stress  ;  but  in 
the  appendix  to  Webster's  '  Dictionary  '  he 
appears  a»  IxtHlx6chitl,  with  stress  upon  the 
penultimate.  From  my  knowledge  of  how 
Mexicans  pronoimce  I  should  say  that 
Webster  is  right. 

As  there  are  so  many  readers  of  Prescott 
interested  in  knowing  how  to  pronounce 
the  long  Aztec  naines  wliich  decorate  his 
pages,  I  may  here  cou\'euiently  direct 
attention  to  a  Spanish  poem  called  *  Andhuac,* 
published  at  Mexico  in  1853.  The  author. 
J.  M.  Rodriguez  y  Cos,  tells  tlie  story  of 
the  conquest  of  Mexico  in  verse,  and  as  he 
took  great  pains  to  find  out  how  each  Aztec 
name  should  be  sounded,  his  poem  forms 
a  trustworthy  guide  to  Ai.tec  orthoepy. 
He  frequently  mentions  the  name  Ixtlil- 
xochitl, and  invariably  accents  it  upon  the 
penultimate.  Similarly  lie  has  Popoeat^- 
petl,  Xicotencatl,  and  other  lofty  appella- 
tions ending  in  -//,  all  stressed  upon  the  last 
syllable  but  one.  Jas.  Platt,  Jmi.

royal  Infantas,"  and  adds  that  a  girdle  of

In  pre-Reformation  times  this  devotion 
was  not  unknown  in  England.  In  Hard- 
ham  Church,  near  Pulborough,  is  a  wall- 
painting,  before  1100,  of  the  Annunciation, 
with  the  inscription  "  +  Virgo  salutatur 
sterilis  fecunda  probatur '*  {Antiquary, 
xxxvii.  57). 

At  Westminster  there  was  "  Our  Ladies 
girdell  which  weomen  with  cliield  were  wont 
to  girde  with,"  which  was  removed  in  1535 
(Wriothesley's  *  Chronicle,'  i.  31). 

In  1508  Robert  Lascelles.  Esq.,  of  Braken- 
burgh,  CO,  York,  by  his  will  gave  to  his  son 
Roger 

"one  small  girdill  henieat  w*  sylver  and  gilt,  y* 
which  is  ono  heyrelome,  callyd  our  Lady's  girdill, 
for  seik  women  w*  ohyled,  to  remayn  as  one  heyre- 
lome."—'  Test.  Ebor.,'  iv.  271. 

The  veneration  was  founded  on  the  belief 
that  the  B.V.M.  did  not  suffer  the  usucd 
great  pain  and  peril  at  childbirth.

Voltaire  and  Rousseau. — These  two 
great  originators  of  the  French  Revolution 
axe  strikingly  contrasted  in  what  is  pro- 
bably an  almost  forgotten  work,  and  v'ol- 
taire's  audacious  plagiarism  is  well  exem- 
plified. Madame  Tussaud,  when  young, 
lived  with  her  uncle  at  the  Hotel  d'AJlegre, 
Rue  St.  Honor6,  Paris,  in  Louis  XVI. 's 
rei.n.  M.  Curtius's  rooms  were  the  resort 
of  the  most  talented  men  in  France,  as 
Voltaire,  Rousseau,  Franklin,  Mirabeau, 
La  Fayette,  &c.  ;  and  tlie  yoimg  Marie 
Grosholtz  (afterwards  Tussaud)  was  in  the 
habit  of  liearing  their  conversation. 

"Full  well  she  remembers  the  literary  discus- 
sions, which  were  sometimes  conducted  with  much 
bitterness  by  the  oi)jK)sinK  partisans  of  the  favourite 
authors  of  the  day ;  observing  that  she  never  could 
forget  the  acrimony  displayed  J)etween  Voltaire 
and  Rousseau  in  tneir  dis^^iutes  in  the  sujijiort, 
})erha]is,   of  some  metapliysical  tlieory,   in  whicli  i 

themselves  alone  could  feel  interested One  grand  I 

source  of  complaint,  which  was  jireferred  against 
Voltaire  by  Rousseau,  was  that  he  had  often 
advanced  different  ideas,  which  were  purely 
original,  at  M.  Curtius's  table,  and  w^hich  were 
int^jnded  to  form  the  foundation  of  a  future  work, 
RousseAU  ever  si>ecifying  that  such  was  his  object ; 
yet  hacl  he  the  mortification  to  find  that  Voltaii-e 
would  forestall  him,  by  bringing  out  a  volume  con- 
taining those  very  oj)inions  whieli  his  rival  had 
^xi»resse<l;  and,  in  tact,  the  very  thoughts  and 
subjects  on  which  ho  had  dilated,  and  designed  as 
the  outlines  and  substance  of  his  next  ]>rouuction. 
Voltaire,  perhajw,  scarcely  apjwirently  listening  to 
what  was  said,  or  taking  up  the  opi)osite  side  of 
the  (juestion,  would  argue  with  vehemence  against 
the  very  doctrine  which  he  would  soon  after  publish 


to  the  world  as  his  own.    Thus,  whilst 


cotem]»orary  was  digestiiiK .. 

and,  as  quiok  in  exocation  as  the  former  iaaifP' 
nation,  he  turned  the  fertility  of  his  rival's  kui 
to  his  own  advantage. 

"  Bitter,  indeed,  was  then  the  venom  whiolivii 
emitted  by  those  two  celebrated  authors  at  en 
other ;  most  ranoorous  were  the  reproaches  vlaeh 
Rousseau  would  launch  forth  against  Voltun^ 
whilst  hin  replies  were  not  less  [nc]  delioiait  in  ther 

{)ortion  of  gall.  The  latter  was  far  more  biting  ii 
lis  sarcasms  than  his  oomnetitor,  who  sometinMi 
felt  so  irritated  that,  losinic  nis  seu-possessioo,  tki 
lK>int  of  his  satire  often  lost  its  keenness.  Vdtainb 
also,  was  ever  gay,  whilst  Rousseau  was  genenUf ' 
the  reverse,  and  rather  misanthropic. 

"When  Voltaire  retired,  then  would  RooanW; 
give  free  vent  to  all  his  rage  against  his  arch  ri' 
till  he  would  exhaust  all  the  abusive  vooaboL 
of  the  French  language  in  expressing  his  wrsU^

rascal ! '  until  he  was  fatigued  with  the  fury  of 
own  elo(]uence.    He  was  younger  than  Voltaire 
sixteen  years,  but  they  Ixith  died  in  the  same  yew:,

Court  Leet:  Manor  Court. — In  the 
Middle  Ages  every  part  of  England  was 
within  the  jiu-isdiction  of  a  Court  Leet,  or 
of  a  court  called  by  some  other  name,  but 
exercising  the  powers  of  a  Court  Leet. 
Some  of  these  courts  were  manorial,  others 
municipal,  others  connected  with  the 
hundred,  viz.,  the  Sheriff's  Toiu*n.  Most  of 
these  old  courts  have  died  out,  but  a  few 
still  remain.  1  am  anxious  to  obtain  aa 
complete  a  list  as  possible  of  these  survivals, 
and  to  find  out  what  powers  they  still  exer- 
cise. I  shall  be  greatly  obliged  if  your 
readers  can  give  mo  any  information  on 
these  points.  It  will  be  a  convenience  if 
they  will  write  direct  to  me  at  the  Hartley 
University  College,  Southampton. 

F.  J.  C.  Heabnshaw. 

[The  articles  in  the  Nhith  iSericH  on  Manor  Court 
Rolls  will  proViably  be  of  service  to  Prof.  Hkak>'- 
siXAW.  Useful  inlormation  may  also  l>e  found  under 
the  heading  •Manorial  CusUims '  in  the  General 
Indexes  of  *  N.  &  Q.'J.

with  ltd  indispensable  fhnt  and  steel,  will 
state  whether  in  any,  and  what,  part  of  the 
country  persons  striking  a  hght  held  the 
flint  in  the  left  hand  and  hit  it  with  the  steel. 
I  mean,  of  course,  the  ordinary  domestic 
practice.  In  my  experience,  during  the 
"  hungrj'  forties,"  it  was  the  steel,  held 
firmly  by  its  handle,  that  occupied  the  left 
hand,  wliile  the  right  hand,  holcung  the  flint, 
struck  the  forceful  sliding  blows  that  sent 
the  shower  of  sparks  into  the  tinder-box. 
In  other  words,  it  was  the  flint  that  (as  also 
in  the  firearms  of  that  time)  was  always 
the  moving  body.  In  the  '  Eneyclopa?dic 
Dictionary,^  g.v.  '  Steel.'  is  a  quotation  from 
Knox's  '  Essays '  which  confirms  my  ex- 
perience, as  follows  : — 

"The  steel  TtitiM  l>e  struck  in  a  pro|wr  manr)t;r 
una  with  propGr  matcriftis  l>efore  the  latent  Mpark 
can  he  elJL-ittjd."' 

Yet  I  aiti  told  that  in  a  book  on  '  Bygone 
Englatid,'  by  Mr.  W.  Andrews,  it  is  stated 
that  the  steel  was  the  rao\'lng  body,  and  the 
flint  received  the  impact.

Virginia  and  the  Eastern  CotmriES. — 
I  am  anxious  to  learn  whether  there  was  any 
emigration  to  Virginia  from  the  Eastern 
Counties  in  the  seventeenth  and  eighteenth 
centuries.  So  many  place-names  as  well  as 
surnames  in  Virginia  are  identical  witli 
those  in  the  East  Anglia  of  that  time  that 
one  would  suppose  there  must  have  been 
a  certain  amount  of  emigration  from  Nor- 
folk, Suffolk,  &c,.  to  "  the  Old  Dominion," 
aa  Virginia  loved  to  be  called.  The  import- 
ant city  of  Norfolk  on  the  coast,  now  a  naval 
depot  of  the  U.S.A.,  ])robably  owes  its  name 
to  some  settler  from  the  county  of  the  same 
name  in  the  Mother  Countrj",  as  also,  no 
doubt,  does  the  county  of  Suffolk,  Virginia. 
I  have  come  across  surnames  in  the  eastern 
part  of  the  State  quite  peculiar  to  the 
Eastern  Counties,  especially  Norfolk  and 
Suffolk,  which  leads  me  to  think  that  at 
least  some  of  the  early  settlers  in  Virginia 
came  from  East  Anglia.  I  should  much 
like  to  have  some  corroborative  evidence  in 
favour  of  tliis  suggestion. 

Frederick  T.  HiBOAxre.

Col.  RiCHABD  Norton  belonged  to  the 
Southwick  brancli  of  the  Norton  family  of 
Hampshire.  Mrs.  Suckling  will  find  a

sliire  CJenealogies,'  but  it  is  based  on  the 
Visitation  of  1622,  and  gives  no  information 
subsequent  to  that  date.  The  following 
notes  relating  to  the  Southwick  branch  will 
prhaps  furnish  Mrs.  Suckling  witli  the 
information  she  desires,  and  at  the  same 
time  be  useful  to  other  readers  of  *  N.  &  Q.'

Southwick  (see  10  S.  vi.  83).  Col.  Richard 
Norton  was  the  second,  but  eldest  surviving,

the  Duke  of  York  as  Governor  of  Ports- 
mouth, and  was  afterwards  created  Baron 
Dartmouth. 

Col.  Richard  Norton  was  twice  married. 
His  first  wife  was  Anne,  daughter  of  the 
Parliamentary  colonel  Sir  Walter  Erie,  of 
Charborough,  co.  Dorset,  by  whom  he  had 
issue  a  daughter  Sarah,  who  married  Henry 
Wliitehead^  son  of  Col.  Richard  Whitehead, 
of  Tytherley,  Hants  {Mary,  daughter  of 
their  eldest  son  Richard,  married  iUexander 
Thiatlethwaite,  ancestor  of  the  present 
owner  of  Southwick  Park),  and  a  son 
Daniel  Norton,  who  died  in  1686,  dtuing 
the  lifetime  of  his  father,  leaving  by  his 
wife  Isabel,  daughter  and  coheiress  of 
Admiral  Sir  Jolm  Lawson  (she  afterwards 
married  Admiral  Sir  John  Chioheley,  and 
died  29  Nov.,  1709),  an  only  son,  Richard 
Norton,  of  whom  hereafter. 

The  second  wife  of  Col.  Norton  was  Eliza- 
beth, second  daughter  of  WiJham  Fiennes, 
\'iscount  Say  and  Sele,  and  by  lier  he  had 
issue  three  sons  and  two  daughters  : —

Butler,  of  Amberley  Castle,  Sussex,  and 
died  in  1709.

Hutcliins,  in  his  Introduction  to  the  *  History 
of  Dorset  '  (3rd  ed.),  vol.  i.  p.  Ixiii,  gives  the

formerly  at  the  old  Manor  House  at  Wick- 
hani,  but  now  to  be  seen  on  the  north  wall

bound  to  say  that  there  were  other  monu- 
jnents  to  the  same  family  showing  no 
supporters  in  their  armorial  insignia. 

Notwithstanding  the  general  statements 
collected  from  tiie  above  heraldic  authorities.

not  contrive  to  do  so,  out  of  deference,

rank  liigher  than  their  own.

"Wax  and  curnels  "  (10  S.  vii.  267).— 
It  is  many  a  long  day  since  I  heard  any  one 
speak  of  this  painful  malady,  and,  until 
now,  I  think  I  nave  never  met  its  name  in 
print.  I  should  have  set  it  down  "waxen 
kernels."  I  believe  the  lumps  were  due  to 
the  effect  of  cold  on  the  glands.  The 
*  E.D.D.'  has  "  wax-kernel.**  and  defines  it 
"  a  glandular  swelling,  esp.  used  of  the 
glands  of  the  neck.**  As  the  affection  is 
bupposed  to  be  connected  with  growth, 
perhaps  the  spelling  "  waxing  '*  would  be 
better  than  that  which  I  imagined  for 
myself  in  "  waxen,"  or  that  which  Mr. 
TH03IAS  Hatcliffe  renders  by  "  wax  and." 

St.  Swithin. 

This  comi)laint  in  known  under  the  name 
of  "  waxen  kernels  "  in  both  Northampton- 
shire and  Warwickshire,  and  refers  to  a 
swelling  of  tlie  glands  of  the  neck.  I  ob- 
serve that  Wright  ('  Dictionary'  of  Obsolete 
and  Provincial  Eiiglisli,'  1857)  duly  records 
tlie  word  :  "  Waxen-kernel,  s.  An  enlarged 
gland  in  the  neck.     Palsgr," 

I  infer  from  Mr.  Katcliffe's  note  that 

in  the   locaUty   iroin  whicli    he   writes   the 

expression  indicates  two  distinct  complaints, 

which  may  or  may  not  occur  sinmltaneously.

He  was  canonized  by  Celestin  111.  in  1192. 
H©  probably  gave  his  name  to  the  villages 
of  St.  Thibault  (Aisne)  and  St.  Thi^baud 
(Jura). 

2.  St.  Theobald  of  Proving,  bom  in  1017, 
is  generally  said  to  have  become  a  Camal- 
dolese  monk  of  the  Abbey  of  Vangadisiza, 
near  V^erona.  Some  authorities,  however, 
differentiate  St.  Theobald  of  I*rovins  from 
the  Camaldolese  St.  Theobald,  and  assert 
that  the  latter  was  an  Abbot  of  Van- 
gatUzza  who  died  in  1050.  However  this 
may  be,  St.  Theobald  of  Provins  lived 
for  many  years  the  Ufe  of  a  hermit  in  the 
diocese  of  Vicenza,  and  probably  also  Hved 
in  the  diocese  of  Adria,  ui  Venetia,  as  he  ia 
saiil  to  have  been  specially  honoured  at 
Badia  Polwine,  in  the  latter  diocese.  Ho 
died  on  30  June  in  or  aboiit  the  year  1066, 
but  liis  feast  was  variouBly  kept  on  the  1st 
or  4th  of  July.  He  was  canonized  by 
Alexander  111,  in  1175.  His  relics,  or  most 
of  tliem,  were  at  a  very  early  date  taken  to 
France,  and  there  verj'  widely  distributed. 
He  seems  to  have  given  Jiis  name  to  the 
villages  of  St.  Tliibault  (Aube),  St.  Thi^- 
bault  (Haute -Mame),  St.  Thibault- lew - 
Vignes  (Seine^t-Marne),  and  St.  Thibaidt 
(Cote  d'Or),  and  to  a  portion  of  the  village 
of  St.  Satur  (Cher)  known  by  the  same  name. 
He  was  also  venerated  at  Mesgrigny  and 
St.  L<5iger-sous-Brienue,  both  in  the  depart- 
ment of  Aube. 

At  2  S.  xi.  269  Lord  Courtney,  writing 
on  the  subject  of  St.  Tib's  Eve  as  a  synonym 
of  **  never,'*  would  seem  to  refer  to  this 
St.  Theobald  when  he  aays :  "1  find  St. 
Theobald's  Day  is  the  1st  July,  and  appa- 
rently lacks  an  eve."  Is  there  any  evidence 
tliat  any  St.  Theobald's  Day  was  ever 
observed  in  any  English  diocese  on  the  1st 
of  July  or  any  other  day  ?  As  to  saints' 
days  and  eves,  the  present  nde  is  that  only 
"  doubles  "  have  eves.  The  differentiation 
of  days  into  doubles,  semi -doubles,  and 
simples  apparently  dates  from  the  thirteenth 
century  ;  but  it  is  improbable  that  even  in 
earlier'  times  every  samt's  day  had  its  eve. 
St.  Tib's  Eve  has  betm  discussed  in  other 
volumes  of  '  X.  &  Q."  also,  and  I  do  not  wish 
to  revive  the  discussion,  but  merely  desire 
to  point  out  the  imprr»bability  of  the  phrase, 
which  is  an  English  one,  having  any  reference 
to  any  St.  Theobald,  as  suggesteti  by  Lord 
Courtney.     If  the  ordinarily  accept'ed  deri- 

'  vation  from  St.  Xlje's  Eve  be  wrong,  it  is 

,  on  the  whole  far  more  probable  that  the 
phrase  is  connected  with  St.  Tibba,  a  virgin, 

,  wlioae  day  was  obser\'ed  at  Peterborough  on 

1  C  March.

To  return  to  the  subject  of  tlim  note. 
Tiiougli  it  is  probablti  that  the  Went  Hamliatn 
St.  Tlieobald  was  one  of  the  two  already 
mentioned,  it  i»  possible  that  he  was  one 
of  the  saints  of  tliis  nanio  not  formally 
canonized  : — 

3.  St.  Theobald.  Canon  of  the  Collegiate 
Church  of  Le  Dorat  (Haute-Vienne),  died 
on  6  Xovembor,  1070.  His  name  was  first 
inserted  in  the  projwr  of  the  diocese  of 
Liinogej?  in  1669,  when  his  feast  was  trans- 
lerred  to  1 3  September. 

4.  St.  Theobald,  Archbishop  of  Vienne 
(Is^re),  was  alive  at  the  )>eginning  of  the 
eleventh  century,  and  was  great- uncle  to 
St.  Theobald  of  Provins.  His  festival  was 
kept  on  21  May.  The  village  of  St.  Thibault 
(Savoie)  may  possibly  ownTiim  as  its  epony- 
mous hero. 

5.  St.  Theobald  of  Mondovi  was  first  a 
cobbler  there,  and  afterwards  a  portfir  at 
Alba,  in  the  district  of  Monferrato.  in  Pied- 
mont, At  the  close  of  his  hfe  he  was  em- 
ployed in  sweeping  the  catiiedral  and  other 
chiirches  of  Alba.  He  died  on  1  June.  1150, 
on  which  dav  his  feast  was  kept. 

6.  St.  Theobald  of  Marly  was  Abbot  of 
the  Cistercian  Abbey  of  Les  Vau.x  de 
Cemay  (Heine-et-Oise).  He  died  on  8  De- 
cember, 1247,  but  his  festival  was  kept  on 
8  July.  Was  St.  Tliibault  (Oise)  »^amed 
after  this  saint  t 

7.  Who  was  the  St.  Thibaut  whose  chapel 
stands  on  an  isolated  rcK-k  near  Kendeux,  in 
the  Belgian  province  of  Luxembourg  '! 

None  of  these  saints,  ao  far  as  I  know,  are 
coimected  with  Wiltshire,  or  indeed  England, 
in  any  way. 

Theobald.  Archbishop  of  Canterburj'  1 1 39- 
1161,  is  known  to  have  been  in  Wiltshire, 
but  there  is  no  evidence  that  he  was  ever 
reputed  to  be  a  saint,  and  the  action  of 
Boniface  IX.  implies,  if  not  formal  canoniza- 
tion, at  least  long  repute. 

I  have  gone  rather  elaborat-ely  into  details 
because,  in  a  case  like  this,  it  is  iinposdible 
tfO  tell  what  facts  may  provide  a  clue. 

John  B.  Walsewbiokt.

Tlie  narrative  is  accepted  as  truth  by

in  order  to  make  it  better  fit  in  witii  (Jilbert.

that  the  Club  has  leased  one  of  the  fieida

Tepresenting  the  site  of  the  old  castle.  The 
captain  of  the  Club  entertained  his  company 
by  relating  what  Mr.  Lloyd  had  written, 
and  accepted  it  all  as  truth.  The  editor  of 
^he  aforesaid  journal  also  said  "  It  is  made 
still  more  memorable  by  the  fact  that 
Robert  Bruce  was  once  in  hiding  in  thn 
<7hapel "  ;  and  he  concluded  by  asking  if 
the  place  will  *'  now  be  a  pilgrimage  for 
Scots." 

It  appears  to  me  to  be  quite  time  to  rele- 
tgate  this  story  of  Homsey  to  the  realms  of 
folk-lore,  and  to  emphasize  the  fact  that 
the  author  never  represented  it  as  truth, 
although  the  "  historians "  have  made 
plenty  of  copy  out  of  it.  I  do  not  intend  to 
•  enter  into  the  history  of  the  Lodge  here, 
as  I  have  fully  treated  it  in  my  own  '  History 
'of  Homsey.'  Let  me  merely  state  that 
Edward  I.  and  his  Court  were  at  Harengeye 
— on  friendly  terms  with  the  then  rector — 
when  the  alleged  events  are  supposed  to 
have  taken  place,  and  that  it  is  hardly 
possible  to  conceive  that  Robert  Bruce 
would  hide  where  his  enemy  was. 

Miss  Porter's  authority  does  not  mention 
the  name  of  Highgate.  She  depends  upon 
the  account  given  by  Hector  Boece  or 
Boethius  and  Scottish  readers  will  know 
how  little  reliance  can  be  placed  on  any- 
thing he  said.  The  '  D.N.B.^  states  :  "  The 
gravest  charge  against  Boece  is  that  he 
invented  the  authorities  on  whom  he  relies." 
Joseph  Colyer  Marriott.

Free  Society  of  Artists. — In  tlie  follow-

there  are  two  sliglit  inaccuracies  that  might
