"In  the  following  year,  1761,  we  find  two  Exhi- 
bitions. The  artists  had  come  to  lojc^erheads ;  the 
main  Ixxly,  styled  henceforth  the  Society  of  Artists, 

continueti  its  triumjjhant  career The  seceders

So<:iety  of  Artists.  Tliey  continued  to  hold  Exhi-

from  history."' 

Apparently  the  title  of  "  The  Free  Society

Was  the  second  paper  also  by  De  Quincey. 
and  the  passage  quoted  an  editorial  inter- 
polation ?  It  is  possible,  but  perhajjs  not 
probable.  The  second  article  is  partly  a 
notice  of  Dupotet's  '  Introduction  to  Aniinal 
Magnetism,'  and  partly  an  account  of  the 
experiments  and  conclusions  of  Dr.  ElUotson 
and  Prof.  Mayo.  The  aim  of  the  first 
article  was  to  call  the  attention  of  the  pulilic 
to  an  important  document  wliich  up  to  that 
time  had  been  ignortMi  by  British  writers. 
The  report  of  the  commiR.sion  of  inquiry 
appointed  by  the  French  Government  was 
issued  in  1784,  and  ha  called  by  De  Quincey 
"  the  most  meniorable  instance  on  record 
of  violent  prejudice,  and  the  extent  to  which 
it  paralyzes  the  judgment."  It  formed  tlie 
basis  of  most  of  the  English  notices  of  the 
subject. 

But  at  the  end  of  18E5,  after  considerable 
debate,  the  Paris  Academy  of  Sciences 
decided  upon  a  fresh  investigation  ;  and  it 
is  the  result  of  tliis  second  inquiry  tliat 
De  Quincey  has  epitomized  in  his  article, 
which  ha.s  not  yet  been  included  in  any 
collected  edition  of  his  writings. 

William  E.  A.  Axon.

*' Beitzmeb"  =  Irishman. —In  the  Yid- 
dish jargon  of  New  York  and  London  an 
Irishman  is  called  "  Beitzmer."  The  origin 
of  this  name  is  sufficiently  cmious  to  be 
worth  preserving  here.  In  Harkavj'^s  *  Dic- 
tionary of  the  Yiddish  Language,'  1898,  it 
is  printed  in  Hebrew  characters  iyD*^*3. 
but  no  etymology  is  given.  The  word  is- 
reaUy  hybrid.  Beitzim  is  the  Hebrew  for 
"  eggs,"  and  the  termination  -cr  is  German. 
It  would  seera.  that  when  the  German  Jews 
first  heard  the  name  Ireland,  they  cunuected 
it  with  the  German  word  for  *'*eggs,"  as  it' 
it  were  EierA&nd,  "  the  Land  of  Eggs." 
Hence  they  imagined  that  a  fitting  trans- 
lation of  Iriahman  into  jargon  would  be 
*'  Beitzmer,"  i.e.  "  Egg-lander."  This  pecu- 
liar rendering  ia  now  coimnon.  and  is 
universally  understood. 

Jas.  Platt,  Jtm.

f^nt  states,  but  nearly  40,000.  The  ordinarj^

bfjen  given  to  the  world  of  the  destruction 
of  Sodom  and  Ciomorrah.

«pondent'8  list  of  great  earthquakes  must  be

matches  used  with  an  old  black  wooden

servants  ;  and  as  a  child  I  learned  from  them 
a  little  song  of  a  match-maker's  wife  or 
child,  in  which  were  the  words 

'E  splits  'em,  *e  dips  'em,  an'  I  do  the  same. 
I  do  not  think  I  was  old  enough  to  attach 
any  definite  meaning  to  the  words.  Can 
any  one  furnish  the  rest  of  the  song,  or  what- 
ever it  is  ?  I  think  I  have  some  faint 
recollection  of  the  matches  being  bought 
at  the  kitchen  door.  J.  T.  F. 

Winterton,  Doucaster. 

[See  post,  p.  351.] 

*'  Matboss  *' :  "  TOPASS." — ^The  above 
were  words  for  gunners,  or  ratings  of 
gunners,  in  the  old  Indian  Artillery.  The 
former  word  should  be  from  Ger.  Matrose, 
and  is  given  in  Bailey's  '  Diet.'  as  being 
*'  next  below  the  giumers  "  ;  but  I  wish  to 
know  the  status  of  the  "  topass  "  and  the 
etymology  of  the  word.  H.  P.  L.

^r- ■  - 't  riccw>«8arily{uci7ermatchee. 

•ixt'S     always    contained 

Uj.^j.^v;   ^ith  sulphur  to  eatcii  the 


long-sought  spark  on  the  tinder.  The  lucifer 
(phosphortis)  matches,  producing  a  real 
flame  themselves,  were  introduced  aboxit 
1830,  and  were  a  great  advance  in  tJje  art 
of  fire -producing ;  but  surely  the  old 
sulp}»ur  matches  are  as  old  as  the  tinder- 
boxes  themselves,  for  without  tliern  the 
tinder  would  have  been  useleiis, 

J.    FOSTEH    PALrMKB, 
%  Royal  Avenue,  8.W. 

The  matches  here  alluded  to  are  evi- 
dently the  tliin  slips  of  wood  tipped  at  each 
end  with  sulphur  wliich  were  used  with 
tinder-boxes  until  the  latter  went  out  of  use. 
When  a  spark  ignited  the  tinder,  the  sulphur 
match  was  put  on  to  the  little  ring  of  fire, 
and  blown  upon  until  it  caught  and  set 
light  to  the  wood.  The  making  of  these 
matches  was  a  gipsy  industrJ^  CJenuine 
old  ones  are  not  often  met  with  nowadays, 
but  they  are  easy  enough  to  make.  I  have 
often  made  them  to  demonstrate  the  way 
of  using  a  tinder-box.  E.  E.  Street. 

The  old  tinder-box  contained  a  flint,  a 
steel,  a  quantity  of  tinder,  and  some  matches. 
The  matches  were  of  the  same  sliape  antl 
size  as  those  now  in  use,  but  were  tjppe<l 
only  with  stUphur,  and  of  course  did  not 
ignite  by  friction.  By  the  striking  together 
of  the  flint  and  steel,  sparks  fell  on  the 
tinder,  and  where  they  fell  the  tinder 
ignited,  and  was  for  some  minutes  like  the 
red  coals  in  a  grate,  A  match  was  ignited 
by  touching  the  sulphur  tip  on  one  of  these 
red  spots.  Matches  are  referred  to  in  The 
Sftertator,  but  I  cannot  point  out  the  exact 
passage,  as  1  have  not  the  book  at  hand. 

M.  N.  G. 

Although  suggestive  of  an  early  name  for 
friction  matches — namely,  '*  Congreves  "  (so 
termedfrom  thefamous  rocketraanufftcturer). 
the  matche«  referred  to  in  '  Love  for  Love  ' 
wnidd  be  the  ordinary*  ones  made  of  thin 
slipii  of  pinewood  or  i>aBteboard,  dipped  in 
brimstone  at  both  ends,  and  used  yyiih  the 
tinder-box.  Although  mostly  made  at  home 
on  Saturday  evenings,  when  the  week's 
tinder  was  pre|>ared,  they  were  also  manu- 
facturetl  for  sale  and  hawked  about  in 
country  places.  A  perii>atetic  vendor  of 
them,  being  repulsed  by  a  villacer  with  the 
remark,  "No,  we  never  buy  of  strangerH." 
meekly  replied,  *'  Be  not  forgetful  to  enter- 
tain strangers,  for  thereby  some  have  enter- 
tained angels  unawares  !  "  but  was  crushed 
by  the  retort,  "  (^t  out  !  Angels  don't 
come  round  with  brimstone  matchea."

was  not  •*  hamraals,"  but  **  almons  '  or

bairn  its  a^^-muns  ''  (pronounced  in  this 
neighbourhood  like  the  word  "  almonds " 
without  the  d  sound)  was  formerly  common 
all    over   the   nortli    of   England,    and   still 

{jrevails  in  country  districts.  Tt  is  mentioned 
)y  Brand  imder  the  section  in  vol.  ii.  dealing 
with  christening  customs.  Mackenzie,  in 
luB  '  History  of  Northumberland  '  (voL  i. 
p.  205),  writing  in  1825  of  the  manners  and 
customs  of  the  people  of  tliis  county,  said  r 
*'  It  would  be  thought  ver>'  unlucky  to 
send  away  a  cliild  the  first  time  its  niu-se- 
has  brought  it  on  a  \'isit  without  giving  it  an

the  child's  "  awmuns." 

Nowadays  in  this  neighbourhood  the- 
cliild  receives  three  things  in  the  first  three- 
houses  it  enters.  Usually  the  tlu-ee  things- 
are  a  silver  coin  (a  threepemiy  bit  or  a  six- 
rnce),  an  egg,  and  a  piece  of  salt.  The  last,^ 
am  told,  is  considered  an  essential ;  the 
other  two,  so  fai*  as  I  can  leani,  are  occa- 
sionally varied.  In  the  case  of  my  eldest 
sun,  his  mother  tells  me  he  got  the  tliree 
tilings  mentioned,  together  with  a  piece  of 
sugar-  The  "awmuns"  are  placed  on 
the  child's  lap.  and  it  is  thus  made  to  carry 
its  *'  awiuuiis  "  out  of  the  house  itself,  as 
they  are  not  removed  from  its  lap  imtil 
the  house  has  been  left.  This  would  seem 
also  to  be  an  essential  part  of  the  ceremony.

*'  Road  of  wobos  "  (10  S.  vii.  290). — 
I  suppose  this  to  be  merely  an  error  for 
*'  rote  of  words,"  i.e.,  a  set  of  words  said  by 
rote.  The  o  in  roU  was  once  open,  so  that 
it  was  also  spelt  root.  Hence  Nares  has  the 
verb  to  rote  or  to  root,  to  repeat  by  memory  ; 
so  that  rote  could  mean  "repetition  by 
memory."  The  '  Century  Dictionary  *  gives 
■a  similar  example  from  Swift  (no  reference) : 
"  a  rote  of  buffoonery  that  serveth  all 
occasions."  Walter  W.  Skeat.

Munnacus  was  a  shoemaker.  Ereismus 
mistook  the  exact  meaning  of  Stratonicus's 
sarcasm  through  a  confusion  of  a^vpov 
(ankle)  with  a-<t>vpa  (liammer).  The  ed. 
princ.  of  Athenaeus  (Venice,  Aid.,  1514),

jxivvoKov,  prints  quite  clearly  avtaT€pov 
TOV  a-it>vpov  (p.  133, 11.  49,  60). 

Erasmus  repeats  the  error  in  his  '  Apoph- 
thegmata '  (lib.  vi.,  heading  '  Stratonicus,' 
No.  18) :  "  Idem  Minnaco  fabro,  ut  opinor, 
secum  de  musica  disceptanti,  non  ani- 
maduertis,  inquit,  te  supra  malleum  loquir?]" 
(p.  375,  Paris,  1533). 

The  same  saying  of  Stratonicus  is  correctly 
quoted  by  L.  Ca^lius  Rhodiginus  (Richerius) 
in  liis  '  Lectiones  Antiquae  '  (lib.  iv.  cap.  xii. 
nd  fin.  of  tlie  enlarged  edition,  p.  132,  Basel, 
1542).  1  '  . 

In  his  '  Adagia  '  (*«  Festinatio  l^aepropera  ;

Aldus  at  Venice  (September,  1608),  he  was 
indebted  to  the  kindness  of  learned  men  for 
:the  loan  of  many  works  winch  had  not  yet 
appeared  in  print,  among  tliem  Athenseus's 
■*  Deipnosophists.'  So  the  error  may  be  due 
to  a  manuscript.  Perliaps  some  corre- 
spondent could  say  in  which  edition  of  the 
*  Adagia  *  this  story  first  occurs.

The  Town  Gaixant's  Sox«. 
We  are  bom,  then  cr>', 
We  know  not  for  why ; 
And  all  our  lives  long 
8till  but  the  aame  song. 
Our  lives  are  but  short. 
We're  made  Fortune's  siwrt; 
We  spend  them  wilh  care 
In  hunting  the  hare, 
In  tossing  the  pot, 
In  vent'nng  our  lot 
At  dice,  when  we  play 
To  pass  time  away. 
We  dress  ourselves  fine. 
At  noon  we  do  dine ; 
We  walk  then  abroad, 
Or  ride  on  the  road. 
With  women  wo  daily. 
Retreat,  and  rally. 
And  then  in  the  bed 
We  lay  down  our  head. 
And  all  this  and  more 
We  do  o're  and  o're  [«c]. 
Till  at  last  we  all  die, 
And  in  the  oold  grave  lie. 

Then  let  us  be  merry. 
Send  down  to  the  ferry 
A  bottle  for  him. 
Old  Charon  the  grim, 
A  bribe  for  our  stay 
Till  we  must  away. 

Edwabd  Latham. 
Neither  Keat«,  nor  the  Belgian  poet,  nor 
any  one  else,  can  \'ie  with  Shakspeare  in 
poetical  descriptions  of  life  : — 

Life  iH  as  tediouH  as  a  twice-told  tale, 
Vexinf?  the  dull  ear  of  a  drowsy  man. 

To-morrow,  and  to-morrow,  and  to-morrow, 
Creeps  in  this  i>etty  i>ace  from  day  to  day, 
To  the  last  syllable  of  recorded  time ; 
And  all  our  yestei-days  have  lighted  fools 
The  way  to  dusty  death.    Out.  out,  brief  candle  I 
Life 's  but  a  walking  shadow ;  a  \tooT  iilayer, 
That  struts,  and  frets  his  hour  upon  the  stage, 
And  then  is  heard  no  more  :  it  is  a  tale 
Told  by  an  idiot,  full  of  sound  and  fury, 
Signifying  nothing. 

Sliakspeare  may  liave  remembered  Job : 
*'  How  oft  is  the  candle  of  the  wicked  put 
out !  "  This  supposes  that  the  passtige  in 
the  older  translation  of  the  Bible  is  the  same 
as  in  the  Authorized  Version,  whicli  is  later 
than  *  Macbeth.'  Shakspeare  also  may 
have  remembered  the  Bible  in  the  lines  from 
•King  John.'  E.  Yardley.

Life. 
How  vain  is  life ! 
A  lover's  sigh,

Echidna  (10  S.  vi.  490).— ^The 
tremata,  the  lowest  order  of  mami 
which  Ornithorhynchus  and  Echidni 
though  not  outwardly  resembling 
have  notwithstanding  marked  afiSnil 
them  in  the  eyes  of  the  osteolof 
cranium  being  small  and  the  facii 
well  developed.  Hence  the  name 
was  doubtless  given  to  that  curious 
animal  the  porcupine  anteater,  n 
the  Greek  word  «x^va,  but  fi 
mythological  monsterEchidna,  hall 
half  snake,  mentioned  by  Ovid,  wl 
birth  to  Cerberus,  and  the  Nem 
slain  by  Hercules.  Zoologists  have 
a  similar  mode  of  nomenclature  in 
of  other  animals,  such  as  the  Hydra 
Alcyone,  Arachne,  Cydippe*  Hipp 
and  Midas.  I  cannot  find  the  fac 
in  works  on  natural  history,  but  t 
names  all  seemingly  explain  their  tr

Alguer6s.  The  Alguerese  appHed  tlie  ierni

BRITISH    NAMES:    THEIR 
I INTKRPRETATION. 
(S«?a»il<,  p.  101.) 
reialfd  Names. — It  wiU  be  con- 
eor-' <  ••  'u'xt  srome  other  river- 
ifi   t  uitli  those  eonneeted 

'         -  ..da    for    water.    Gaehe 


uUge,  and  Welsh  dobur^  its  earliest  form. 
These,  as  will  be  »»een  below,  I  have  treat-ed 
a»  variant* — that  is.  as  derived  from  the 
Hanie  root.  As  the  frequent  repetition  of 
the  t^anie  river-name  suggests  that  Huch  a 
name  is  an  appellative — generic,  not  specific 
— signifying  water,  and  as  we  found  tliat 
se\'eral  river-names  involve  tlie  root  vad  or 
wat  of  this  signification,  so  we  might  expect 
that  a  large  rmmber  of  river- names  would 
involve  the  root  of  the  Celtic  word  for  water 
(«?>f/e)  ;  and  such  is  the  conclusion  to  which 
an  examination  of  many  river-narnes  leads. 
Tlie  root  appears  in  a  great  variety  of  forms, 
as  in  Sequana,  ancient  name  of  Ihe  Seine  ; 
Eftk,  the  Isca  Siluruni  of  the  Romans  ;  in 
Segontium,  ancient  natne  of  Carnarvon  ; 
Abersoch  (Carnarvonshire)  ;  in  the  river- 
name  iSowe  (Warwickshire  and  elsewhere), 
where  the  g  has  disappeared  ;  in  Esk  in 
Scotland  ;  in  Sena,  ancient  name  of  tlie 
Shamion,  and  Suir  in  Ireland  ;  and  appa- 
rently in  the  river-names  Sabrina  and 
Sombre,  the  former  the  ancient  name  of  the 
Severn.  Further,  it  is,  I  think,  tlie  root 
involvetl  in  tlie  names  Biscaj%  Oascony 
(Vascones  of  the  Romans),  and  Euskarian 
tlie  laijt  the  name  by  wliich  tlie  Basqvie 
people  call  themselves,  given  to  them  pro- 
bably from  without  and  not  indigenous, 
a!>  in  other  cases. 

Now,  by  comparing  the  above  names  with 
each  other,  it  will  be  seen  that  they  can  all 
be  derived  from  a  common  primitive  root 
tfvac^  which  by  traiwposition  of  the  letters 
might  easily  be  transformed  into  some  such 
form  as  vase,  yielding  ultimately  tliat  seen 
in  uisge  (Gaelic  for  water),  iii  Esk,  and 
Biscay  ;  while  by  suppression  of  the  v  in 
itmc  we  get  Segonfciiun.  So  we,  &c.  Cf.  for 
suppression  of  v  Engliali  itfm  with  Greek 
huit/it  and  Latin  fi-lius,  Gaelic  athir  witli 
Latin  quatuor.  Once  more,  by  changing 
the  »  in  our  postulatwl  root  into  h  we  get 
the  form  hvac  or  rnc,  or  by  transposition  acv. 
The  WeJ«Ji  word  for  water  (dobur)  seems  to 
be  thus  derived,  that  is,  from  such  a  form 
as  vac.  do  being  a  very  common  Celtic  prefix, 
r  a  formative  element,  the  guttural  dis- 
appearing, and  the  v  provected  into  a  6. 
The  original  fonn  of  the  word  woidd  there- 
fore be  something  like  do-vacr.  Tlie  ancient 
name  of  Worcester  (Vigorn).  now  repre- 
sented by  the  first  syllable,  seems  to  confirm 
this  ;  and  it  is  in  the  same  way  that  such 
names  as  Varrow,  Aeron  (in  Wales)  and 
Barrow  (in  Ireland  and  Britain),  where  b 
represents  an  original  t%  are  most  easil; 
explained,  that  is,  by  the  loss  of  a  (^  before 
the  r.     In  passing  we  may  note  that  Vigorn,

-wAter-pIoce,  ifl  comparable  with  Engllab

«aine  manner  in  the  naruea  Sena  and  Suir 
■we  have  the  loss  of  the  guttural ;  wliUe  in 
Sombre  and  Sabrina  it  ^lag  apparently 
paaBed  into  a  labial.  A»  to  the  forc«  of  tlie 
r  in  such  riani(»  as  these  and  that  of  the 
Humber,  it  may  denote  plurality  or  fullnees  ; 
while  in  other  caae»,  aa  de-bur,  it  may  be 
for  original  *,  the  sign  of  the  nominative 
case.  The  recurring  fiver-name  Stour  eeems 
to  be  from  a  previouaSour*  pacing  into  Stour, 
*  being  euphonic  ;  or,  less  probably »  the

dour  or  dwT.  As  another  name  from  the

the  Swale  in  Yorkshire^  titanding  for  8wigel. 
Nor  must  we  omit  to  note  that  the  English 
word  sea  may  be  from  this  sajne  root  svac, 
-and  therefor©  alhed  to  Eak»  So  we,  &c.,  and 
1  think  also  to  the  Greek  6k-eanos, 

Lastly,  the  recurring  river-name  Avon 
(Abona  of  the  Romans)  seeraa  to  belong  to 
this  root,  being  from  the  form  acv  above 
mentioned,  yielding  ap  by  the  labialization 
of  the  e.  Cf.  Greek  hippos  and  Latin  equun. 
It  is  in  fact  the  aame  word  as  Latin  aqua, 
Norse  oej,  Sanskrit  apdt  whence  Penjaub, 
the  region  of  the  five  rivers. 

Now  the  question  here  arises^and  I  put 
it  with  some  diffidence — Are  the  two  roots 
vat  and  vac  distinct  from  each  other,  or 
really  only  different  forms  of  the  same  root  ? 
I  am  disposed  to  tliink  that  the  latter  is  tlu- 
caae^  the  change  implied  of  a  dental  for  a 
guttural  being  not  of  infreq\ient  occurrence, 
as  mav  be  seen  by  comjjwing  Welsh  hraUiau 
with  Engliflh  breek^,  \\\  ffrud  with  English 
brook.  But  if  they  are  the  same,  how  are 
w^e  to  account  for  the  coexistence  in  the 
same  language  of  words  derived  from  either 
iorm,  as,  for  example,  Norse  vand  and  oeg^ 
of  the  same  meaning  ?  The  answer  i8» 
By  the  fusion  of  two  peoples,  one  using  the 
one  form  of  the  word,  and  the  other  the 
other  form.  For  instance,  if  in  Norway 
tlere  were  fimt  a  people  who  used  the  form 
•seen  in  oeg,  and  these  were  afterwards  mixed 
with  a  people  who  used  that  seen  in  vofuit 
we  might  tJms  account  for  the  phenomenon 
— that"  is,  by  overlapping. 

The  original  form  of  the  root  for  wat«r 
was  {8)vact,  which  was  assimilated  in  sonae 
-cases  to  vace,  and  in  others  to  vaU.  As  Latin 
^epUm  is  Gaelic  secht,  so  L.  Neptunus  is 
G.  Nechtan,  the  former  part  of  which  is 
«een  in  Norse  Nidd,  in  the  name  Ken-Neth 
(son  of  Neth  or  Nidd),  and  probably  in 
that  of  Macbeth.  This  seems  to  prove  the 
identity   of  vac   and   vat.    Another  corro- 


borative inatanoa  lA,  I  Hunk,  V* 
of  Wight),  which  meant,  therefm 
The  meaning  of  the  root  oocf  was  a 
tairdy  either  smooth  or  soluble,  thi 
of  solid.

uisffe,  Welsh  dwr^  and  Latin 
derived  from  the  same  primitiv^e

RUVIONY. 


The  Gypsy  Lobe  Society. — In  October, 
1887,  Col.  W.  F.  Prideaux  inquired  in 
your  columns  (7  S.  iv.  289)  whether  any 
systematic  attempt  had  been  made  to 
collect  the  songs  and  ballads  current  among 
the  English  Gypsies.  The  late  Mr.  W.  J. 
Ibbetson  replied,  suggesting  that  an  asso- 
ciation should  be  formed  for  the  purpose 
of  publishing  Romani  vocabularies,  ballads, 
&c.  ;  and  the  result  was  the  foundation,  by 
Mr.  Da\'id  MacRitchie,  of  the  Gypsy  Lore 
Society,  which  from  1888  to  1892  issued  its 
valuable  journal.  The  suspension  of  the 
Society,  which  was  cosmopolitan  in  cha- 
racter, was  regretted  no  less  by  tlie  Romani 
Hairt  of  Great  Britain  tlian  by  tlie  large 
body  of  continental  and  American  scholars 
who  were  interested  in  its  aims.  At  the 
Hamburg  Congress  of  Orientalists  in  1902 
a  Gesellscliaft  fiir  Zigeunerforschung  was 
formed,  with  tlie  lat«  Arcliduke  Joseph  of 
Austria  for  President,  and  Prof.  Anton 
Herrmann  as  Secretary.  Tliis  society  issued 
gratis  to  its  members  several  important 
works  at  irregular  intervals,  but  it  publishes 
no  journal,  nor  has  it  attempted  to  replace 
its  predecessor  as  the  central  organization 
for  all  Gvjjsy  students  and  the  rei>ository 
of  miscellaneous  Romani  knowledge.  It 
has  therefore  been  decided  to  revive  the  old 
Ciypsy  Lore  Society,  and  a  large  measure  of 
sujjport,  both  Uterary  and  tinancial,  has 
already  been  secured.  Tlio  first  number  of 
tlie  new  series  of  the  Journal  will  appear 
on  July  1st,  and  I  shall  be  glad  to  send  a 
prosjMJctus  to  any  of  your  readers  who  are 
interested.  R.  A.  Scott  Macfie.

stand how  it  came  to  have  tiKtse  two  differ-

aa  '*  ce  dont  on  se  sert  pour  acheter  ou  pour 
vendre."  The  connexion  between  the  wanyun 
box  and  the  ummun  boat  is  that  both  are 
used  for  trading  purposes.

"  Lead  bis  owx  hoiise." — I  often  hear 
this  saying  in  application  to  home  matters. 
The  head  of  the  house  likea  to  '*  lead  his  own 
horse  "  to  some  extent  in  its  affairs  ;  and 
the  stay-at-liome  man  who  prefers  hia  own 
fireside  to  that  in  the  ale-house  likes  to 
"  lead  his  own  horse  "  =  stay  at  home.  And 
so  on  in  other  things  domestic  which  do  not 
trench  ux>on  tho  woman's  righte  in  the  home 
domain.  Thos.  Ratcljffe.

HoRNSEY    Wood    House  :     Harbinoay 
HorsE  (10  S.  vii.  106.  157,  216,  263.  274).— 
If    I    understand    Prof.   Skeat    aright,    hel 
considers  that  Harringay  and  Homsey  are' 
two  different    names,  the  former  answering 
to    an    A.-S.    Heringa-fg.   or    "isle    of   the 
Herings."  and  the  latter  to  Heringes-eg,  or» 
*'  isle  of  Hering."     The  history  of  the  names, 
wliich  can  be  easily  traced  in  the  Middleeexi 
Feet  of  Fines,  does  not  seem  to  substantiate 
this  view. 

In  my  former  reply  (ante,  p.  216)  I  gave 
instances  of  the  form  "  Harjnigeseye  "  in  the  . 
latter  half  of  the  fourteenth  century.  In  thd  J 
time  of  Henr>^  VIL  we  get  to  the  form 
"  Harnyssay,"  which  runs  side  by  aide  mth 
*'  Haringay  "  until  the  reign  of  Elizabeth. 
In  a  fine  dat«d  4  &  6  Efiz.  ('  Calendar  of 
Feet  of  Fines  for  London  and  Middlesex,* 
ed.  Hardy  and  Page,  I.  ii.  120).  we  findj 
"  Haryngey  otherwise  Harnessey";  and  after 
that  date "  Haringay  disappears  altogether, 
and  only  Harnsey  or  Homsey  occurs  in  the 
fines.  Norden  in  his  '  Si>eculum  BrifcamiiiP,' 
1593,  makes  no  mention  of  Haringay,  but 
in  his  list  of  Middlesex  towns  and  villages 
enters  only  '*  Hamsey,  of  some  Homesey." 
From  this  date  the  old  name  vanishes  in 
favour  of  the  new  one,  just  as  about  the  same 
date  "  Stabonhith  "  or  "  Stcbonlieath  " 
gives  place  to  Stepney,  and  '*  Chelchith  " 
or  "  Chelsith  "  to  Chelsea.  The  modern 
Harringay  is  apparently  a  revival,  possibly 
due  to  the  builder  of  Harringay  House  in  the 
eighteenth  centiu^'.  Tliis  was  the  age  of 
Strawberry  Hill,   Lord  Holland's  buildings

Tlio  picture«<^ue  old  tea-house,  of  which  a

The  meaning  of  the  name  Hornsey  or 
Hamsey  is  very  frequently  discusaed,  and 
Pbof.  Skeat  now  derives  the  final  syllable 
from  rg,  *'  an  island."  But  with  all  defer- 
ence 1  submit  that  no  one  who  knows 
Hornsey  could  accept  that  as  the  etymon. 
The  parish  church,  the  burial-ground,  tJie 
glebe  land,  the  manor  house,  and  the  village 
street  are  clustered  on  the  northern  slopes 
of  a  little  hill,  and  it  is  a  physical  impossi- 
bility for  that  hill  ever  to  have  been  sur- 
rounded by  water.  For  the  east  side  of  this 
particular  hill  shelves  sharply,  and  with  a 
south-easterly  trend,  and  this  is  continued, 
with  but  ver\'  shght  unduJation,  right  awav 
to  the  river  Lea,  which  flows  through  much 
lower  ground  at  a  distance  of  about  two 
miles. 

£  do  not  tliink  there  is  a  better  reason  for 
aa^uming  that  Hornsey  is  identical  in  ety- 
mon  and    formation   with,    say,    Guernsey, 
than    there    would    be    for    ussuming    that 
another    Middlesex    name,    ac.    Stepney,    is 
formed  on  the  same  principle  a»  Aldemey. 
The    former    place   in    Domesday    Book   is 
**  Stibenhede,"    and    about  five-and-twenty 
years    ago    the    "  old    name "    of    Stepney, 
namely.    "  Stebonlieath,"    was    knowTi    to 
copyholders    therein,  and    may,  of    co\u«e, 
be  still  known  to  such  tenants.     Why,  then, 
lould  not  Hornsey  =  Harn*s  Heath  t 
Similarly,  it  is  not  at  all  clear  why  there 
!ithould  be  assumed  to  be  a  verbal  comiexion 
between    Horn-,     Harn-,     and     Harringay.

Some  montlis  ago  a  well-known  personage, 
writing  to  The  Times  on  the  subject,  1  think, 
of  the  destruction  of  Swiss  scenery  by  the 
new  railways,  i&c.,  remarked  that,  English 
visitors  being  on  the  decrease,  British  opinion 
had  no  longer  much  weight  here.  French. 
Germans,  and  Italians,  he  said,  now  visit<«d 
the  country  in  increasing  numbers,  and  were 
regarded  as  of  greater  importance.  In 
support  of  this  view  he  mentioned  that 
puoUc  notices  were  invariably  to  be  seen 
urinted  in  French,  German,  and  Italian. 
Englbli  being  seldom  used.  He  overlooked 
the  fact,  that,  tlie  Confederation  being  com- 
posed of  French-,  German-,  and  Italian- 
speaking  cantons,  these  three  languagi^ 
are  tJie  official  languages  of  the  country, 
and  that  all  official  notices  are  printed  in 
tliese  tongues.  This  will  be  seen  at  once  by  a 
reference  to  a  Swiss  post  card.  The  absence 
of  a  notice  in  English  thereon  has  nothing 
to  do  with  indifference  to  British  interest*. 
and  a  preference  for  those  of  the  three  nations 
whose  languages  are  to  be  seen  on  the  address 
side  of  the  card.  J,  H.  Hi\'ETT-CAR?fAC. 
8ohlosa  Rothl^ertr,  fSwitxerland.

It  is  certainly  curiou»  that,  despite  the 
fact  that  St.  George  was  the  patron  saint  of 
more-or-less  Merry  England,  George  is  not 
before  I  TOO  at  all  a  common  Christian  name. 
One  remembers,  of  courae,  George,  Duke  of 
Clarence,  the  unliappy  brother  of  Ed- 
ward IV.  ;  George  Vilhers,  father  and  son, 
Dukes  of  J3uckingham  ;  and  George,  Prince 
of  Denmark,  consort  of  Queen  Anne.  I  have 
always  supposed  that  the  name  George 
came  into  favour  owing  to  the  accession  of 
the  House  of  Hanover.  George  Lewis 
( 1660-1 727).  »econd  Elector  of  Hanover, 
and,  as  Georgo  I.,  King  ctf  Great  Britain 
and  Ireland,  wa»,  like  George,  Prince  of 
l>enmark,  grandson  of  George,  Duke  of 
Brunswick-  Liineburg. 

Another  common  Christian  name  of  to-day, 
.Arthur,  seema  to  have  been  brought  into 
faftliioii  by  the  fact  that  Arthur  Wellesley, 
Duke  of  ^Wellington,  was  the  victor  of 
Waterloo.'  He  was,  of  course,  godfather 
to  Ilia  queen's  third  son,  Arthur,  Duke  of 
Coimaught.  A.  K.  Bavlky. 

Surely  W.  C.  B.  i*  mistaken  in  stating 
that  George  is  not  at  all  a  common  Christian 
name  prior  to  1700.

George  as  a  Cliristian  name  arose  from  the

the  latter.  The  reason  for  it«  not  having 
iM-en  much  in  use  before  that  time  probably 
lies  in  the  fact  of  it«  being  strictly  a  surname,

land,  thus  corresponding  to  a  fturname 
already  in  uae,  that  of  Farmer.

BD   Steel  (10  S.  vij.  329).— My 

r..,f  .t.iiiide  record"  from  the 

X,    but    it    is    clear 

Jut',  '  of  steel  and  fhnt^by 


the  smoker.  In  every  case  thus  vividly 
recalled,  the  flint,  with  superimposed  match- 
paper  to  catch  the  »park,  was  held  in  the 
left  hand,  and  struck  with  the  steel  in  i>er- 
peiidicular  strokes  rapidly  delivered  from 
tht'  right.  Of  course,  if  the  operator 
J»a]>pened  to  be  left-handed*  the  process  was 
revei-sed  ;  but  the  steel,  which  in  its  most 
developed  form  was  ingeniously  contrived 
to  serve  the  purpose,  was  invariably  the 
constraining  force.  A  good  knife,  especially 
in  the  hands  of  the  precocious  boy,  made  an 
efficient  substitute  for  the  more  dignified 
and  full-dress  steeL  Held  in  an  upright 
position  between  the  thumb  and  tlie  first 
two  fingers  of  the  active  hand,  the  closed  knife 
witli  a  few  deft  strokes  from  the  back  of  its 
blade  quickly  produced  ignition.  In  those 
days  hawkers  at  rural  fairs  used  to  sell 
match-paj>er — ordinarj''  grey  paper  rubbed 
with  Halti>etre  and  i>erhaps  other  ingredients 
— giving  sheets  of  about  two  feet  square  for 
the  modest  sum  of  one  penny.  Boys  could 
develope  their  own  paper  from  domestic 
resources.  Thomas  Bavne. 

As  one  who  remembers  the  "  hungry 
forties,"  those  barley-bread  starvation  days, 
I  tliink  that  the  steel  was  held  in  the  right 
hand.  The  tinder,  or  rather  burnt  rag, 
was  kept  in  the  end  of  a  cow's  horn  (about 
four  or  five  inches  long,  with  a  wooden 
Rtopper),  which  was  held  in  the  left  hand, 
the  flint  being  Jield  on  the  edge  between  tJie 
thumb  and  finger,  and  struck  with  the  steel, 
or,  what  was  ahnost  equally  common,  the 
back  of  the  blade  of  a  closed  pocket-knife. 
The  8t<»cl  was  generally  made  of  an  old  ra«*p, 
a  part  of  wliicli  was  turned  baolc  to  form  the 
handle.  (Rev.)  J,  Brown.

Mr.  T.  C.  Down  writes  on  'Pirate  Trelawny,' 
having  discovered  some  new  letters  and  documents 
which  concern  that  remarkable  man's  behaviour  in 
<Treeoe.  Mr.  Herbert  Paul  gossiiw  iileasantly  about 
'  Idle  Reading.'  The  Rev.  A.  J.  Church,  an  author 
for  forty-six  years,  in  'Authors  and  Publishers' 
declares  emnhatioally  that  **  the  charges  of  rapacity 
■and  hard  dealing  so  freely  brought  against  pub- 
lishers are  unjust.'* 

The  Nationcd  Rttiew.  after  a  summary  of 
*  Episodes  of  the  Mouth,  leads  off  with  a  poem 
Dy  the  Laureate,  'The  I)ee))er  Note.'  The  army 
and  other  matters  of  political  moment  receive 
vigorous  treatment.  Lord  Cranworth  deals  with  an 
important  subject  in  '  Game  Preservation  in  East 
Africa,'  and  points  out  that  two  animals  unaffected 
by  the  rinderpest— the  elephant  and  the  rhinoceros 
—are  threatened  with  extermination  since  the  in- 
troduction of  firearms.    He  is  justified  in  referring 

to  *' the  refining  or  devastating influence  of  the 

white  man."  Mr.  A.  Maurice  Low  in  *  American 
Affairs '  says  that  the  masses  who  believe  in  Mr. 
Roosevelt  are  not  shaken  in  their  belief  by  the  dis- 
closures of  Mr.  Harriman.  *  Literary  Misfits,*  by 
Mr.  W.  Hamilton  Fyfe.  is  a  discussion  of  style. 
His  article  is  an  appeal  against  meaningless  pre- 
ciosity, but  overdoes  that  side  of  the  question. 


Alys  Hallard  has   'Some  Unpi 

Ernest  Renan,'  which  are  attractive,  bringing  out 

clearly  the  fluid  state  of  his  beliefs.  In  this  respect 

Renan's  mind  resembles  a  large  body  of  cultivated 

opinion  which  has,  perhaps,  never  made  itself  felt, 

precisely  Ixicause  it  has  no  firm  basis  of  dogma  or 

conviction. 

The  Cornhill  Magazine  has  a  mixture  of  dialogue 
and  verse  by  Mrs.  Margaret  L.  Woo<l8,  entitled 
♦  The  May  Morning  and  the  Old  Man.'  Mrs.  Woods 
seems  to  us  to  owe  something  in  style  to  Matthew 
Arnold,  and  has  some  pretty  lines  of  her  own. 
Buttercups  figure  prominently,  so  evidently  the 
Ist  of  May  is  not  the  season  figured.  Prof.  G.  H. 
Bryan  discusses  'The  Problem  of  the  Flying 
Machine,'  and  insists  on  the  necessity  of  mathe- 
matical calculations  made  by  a  "stabilimeter." 
Mr.  Horace  Hutcliinson,  wntiiig  on  *Boys  and 
Birds,'  holies  that  "the  Wild  Birds'  Protection 
Act  will  not  rob  boyhood  of  its  inducement  to  the 
study  of  the  birds  and  other  wild  things."  Mr. 
Hartley  Withers  deals  with  'The  Rise  of  Insur- 
"   and  we  wonder  that  he    does   not   quote 


Mr.  Leonard  Huxley,  deals  with  the  career  of  Sir 
Joseph  Hooker,  Darwin's  closest  and  oldest  friend, 
and  the  X  Club,  which  brought  some  famous  men 
of  science  together  in  1864  and  for  many  years 
afterwards.  Mr.  Huxley  claims  for  science  more 
certainty  than  the  new  generation  allows  to  it,  but 
it  is  pleasant  to  find  this  tribute  to  a  grand  old  man 
of  ninety.

From  the  career  of  the  said  Viscount 
(whose  exact  date  of  birth  or  baptism  has 
not  been  ascertained)  it  is  clear  he  must  have

bably wBfl  bom  about  1598  or  1599.  His 
parents  married  in  1595,  and  had  a  son  John 
(who  died  an  infant)  bom  10  Sept.,  1597, 
and  a  daughter  baptized  27  Feb.,  1600/1. 
He  is  identical  (though  such  identity  is  not 
mentioned)  with  the  William  Monson  "put 
forward  in  1618  as  a  rival  to  Buckingham 
in  the  Iving's  favour,"  who  is  called  '*  elder  '* 
son  of  Admiral  Sir  W'iUiam  Monson  in  the

ning's *  Surrey,'  ii.  197),  which  John— who  is 
said  (*  Line.  Peds.')  to  have  been  born  in 
1604 — died  without  male  issue,  before 
1666.  These  tliree  were,  apparently,  the 
only  sons  of  the  Admiral.  G.  E.  C. 


THE    SLOVENISH  LANGUAGE. 

In  the  north-eastern  regions  bordering 
on  the  Adriatic  Sea  an  interesting  and  ancient 
language  is  snoken,  the  old  form  of  wliich 
is  considered  by  some  authorities  (inoluding 
Prof.  Miklosich  and  his  eminent  successor 
Prof.  y.  E.  Jagic)  as  the  Slavonic  Vrspracfm. 
Slovenish  ia  classed  under  the  Bouth-eaj*tem 
branch  of  the  Slavonic  tongues.  The  name 
Slovene  is  traceable  to  alovo,  or  alava,  glory*

whence  also  Slav.*  (The  province  known 
08  Siavonia  bears  but  a  fragmentary  relation 
to  the  vast  amount  of  territory,  extending 
over  many  countries,  occupied  by  the  Slavs 
or  where  they  have  left  traces.)  During  tlie 
Napoleonic  wars  Russian  soldiers  detected 
similarities  between  their  language  and  the 
dialects  of  Styria  (Steirmark,  Stajera), 
Carinthia  (Karntner,  Korosliko),  and  Car- 
niola  (Krain,  Kranjsko).  The  last  of  these 
resembles  the  name  Ukraine,  Russia.  The 
poet  Valentine  Vodnik  (1758-1819).  who 
suffered  severely  for  his  enthusiasm  for 
foreigners,  eulogized  the  Russian  language 
(called  Moskovitarski  by  the  Slovenes)  as 
that  to  wliicli  regenerators  of  Slovene  Tnust 
look.  Writing  in  1799,  he  exclaims  at 
the  si^ht  of  Russian  troops  at  Laibach 
(Ljubljana)  : — 

•' Imjiortant  news  for  us  GAmioUus  (Kraintai). 
The  Russiaiifi,  our  ancient  brethren,  have  come  not 
only  to  visit  U8,  but  to  defend  ub  against  the  enemy. 
Fifteen  hundred  yuara  ago  the  first  Slavs  entered 
our  eoiuitry.  They  were  of  the  race  of  theKuBsians 
and  the  other  SIuvb.  That  is  why  we  so  easily 
understand  the  Rnssian  language  ;  they  are  indeed 
Slavs,  and  tlie  root  whence  our  forefathers  8i)rang. 

Now  we  behold  with  our  owni  eye«  what  niljihty, 

grand  brt?threu  we  have  on  the  earth.  Tbeybave 
keiit  our  language  in  iHjrfeot  imrity.  Wo  should 
make  advances  to  them  when  we  desire  to  purify 
our  language," 

Entiiusiasra  seems  to  have  carried  the  poet 
off  his  feet,  as  the  date  for  the  arrival  of  the 
Slavs  seems  rather  remote,  and  during  the 
eighteenth  centurj''  the  Russian  language, 
far  from  being  niu'e,  had  been  materiallv 
affected,  Srst  by  German,  and  later  by  FrencL 
usage.  In  1821,  at  a  congress  at  Lublin, 
Zhupan  stronglv  affirmed  the  superiority  of 
"  Krainstshina  *'  over  Rusaian.  For  many 
years  Slovene  'writers  occupied  themselves 
with  Russian  literature,  as  though,  to  borrow 
Pushkin's  expression,  this  Slav  stream  was 
to  flow  into  the  Russian  sea.  Some  authors 
are  charged  with  borrowing  Russian  words 
instead  of  using  native  ones,  and  Prof. 
Jagic  gives  a  list  of  Russisms  adopted  by 
Stiftar,  with  their  Slovene  equivalents,  e.g,  :

** After  two  years."  continues  Mr.  Parham,  **I 
rose  to  the  head  of  the  Cantores^  side,  which  jKMsi- 
tion  I  held  for  the  rest  of  my  time  as  a  chorister. 
Noveml>er,  1H97,  I  sang  my  brat  solo ;  and  my  last 
in  March,  liMXI.  There  are  always  two  chief  aolo 
boys,  who  generally  *lead'  the  Decani  and  Cantorea 
reaijectively.  Visitors  w^hohartpen  to  have  attended 
aerrice  at  Macdalen  when  trie  President,  or  Vice- 
President,  or  both  are  present,  may  have  noticed' 
two  boys  quietly  leave  their  places  at  the  begin- 
ning of  the  tirat  lesson  and  walk  down  the  ohapel, 
one  to  the  President's  and  the  other  to  the  \  ice- 
Prosident's  'throne.'  They  are  the  leaders  of  the 
two  flides,  whose  duty  it  is  to  find  the  place  in  the 
anthem-lMxikfl  of  those  dignitaries  and  ixiint  it 
out  to  them.  In  this  manner  I  found  tiie  Vice* 
President's  place  whenever  he  waa  in  cha[«l ;  while 
my  vis-a-vis  u]>ou  the  Decani  fulfilled  the  same 
otnoe  for  the  President. 

*•  I  can  never  remember  beinjf  wearied  of  the 
service*.  The  oonstant  variety,  the  excitement, 
and  the  enthusiasm  for  keeping  up  our  iin'eat  re]ni- 
tation  which  insipired  the  smalleab  of  us  prevented 
any  feeliuK  of  impatience  at  the  daily  routine.  But, 
lookinjj  back  upon  my  time  as  a  chorister,  my 
)»le»UMintoat  memories  are  connected  with  those 
red-letter  dayw  when  we  celebrated  some  annual 
observance.  Some  U  these  survived  oidy  as  a 
name  and  as  a  tradition  in  my  day;  Bome  of  those 
then  existing  bid  fair  Bradually  to  die  out ;  but  the 
life  of  a  chorister  wilTbe  bereft  of  half  its  charm  if 
all  these  picturesque  oererooniea  are  abolislied, 

"One  faat  trace  of  the  old  times  of  M'aitinc  in 
Hall  still  lingered  iii  my  day.  Each  of  the  eiyht 
senior  ohoristei's  in  turn  Ijad  a  week's  duty  a* 
'grace-boy.'  Dinner  at  Ma^alen  takes  place  at 
7  o'clock,  and  at  7..T0  the  'grace-boy' — in  the  cai» 
and  gown  witlioul  whioh  no  chorister  ever  went 
into  oollepe — walked  up  the  H&ll  to  a  stool  in  the 
conier  behind  the  Vioe-Preaident's  chair.  There 
he  sat  until  diimer  was  ended,  when  he  seated  him- 
self  in  the  Vioe-Preaident's  groat  ohair  and  waa 
served  with  hU  meal.  The  reason  for  his  jiresenoe 
was  that  he  had  to  call  for  ^uoe  should  the  high- 
table  happen  to  finish  dinner  before  the  Demiea.  In 
such  a  case  the  Vice-President  would  turn  to  the 
'  grace- boy '  (Aitlariim)  and  aay, '  Grace,  please ' ;  the 
latter  would  walk  across  to  the  head  of  the  Demies' 
table  and  say, '  ( J  race,  iilcasc,'  return  to  tbv  centre  of 
the  dais,  and,  facing  the  Hall,  cry  out,  '<;ratiamm 
actio';  there  i-emainiiiR  while  the  junior  Demy 
recited  a  Latiu  grace.  This  l>etng  over,  the  higli- 
table  filed  out  of  Hall  and  the  Ixjy  had  his  supper 
as  usual.  It  very  seldom  hapjicuea  tliat  the  'grace- 
Itoy'  wa)4  rc^iuired  -inde«l,  in  over  three  yearn  Z 
only  call e<l  two  or  three  times — .The  result  has 
l>een  that  the  '  grace-boy'  was  abolished  a  year  or 
more  ago ;  doubtless  much  to  the  regret  of  those- 
who  remembered  the  joys  of  that  excellent  sui»|icr 
—such  a  rontrAtit  to  the  Sjvartan  diet  of  iSofiool.

Restoration  Day,  I  may  note,  ift  the  annual 
commemoration  on  25  October  of  the  return 
in  1688  of  the  President  and  Fellows  ex- 
truded by  James  II.,  when  the  toast  "  Jus 
suumcuique"  is  drunk.  But  the  Restora^ 
tion  cup,  from  which  this  toast  is  drunk, 
refljly  records  the  earlier  eviction  of  the 
Fellows  in  1648,  and  their  restitution  after 
tlie  Restoration. 

Mr.  Parham,  after  mentioning  that  he 
has  been  present  on  Magdalen  Tower  on  ten 
consecutive  May  Mornings,  says  : — 

"  Some  people  say  tliat  *  this  ceremony*  is  a  relic 
of  Bun-worshiiming  days,  among  them,  apiMirently, 
Mr.  Holman  Hunt,  if  the  venerable  patnaroh  in 
his  famous  picture  is  truly,  as  we  were  always  told, 
a  priest  of  the  sun." 

The  figure  referred  to  is,  I  think,  a  Parsi ; 
and  among  others  represented  in  the  picture 
(1890)  one  may  discern  portraits  of  the 
President,  Dr.  Bloxam — "Awful  John," 
as  he  was  nicknamed  from  the  severity  of 
Iiis  attire — and  the  organist.  A  silver-gilt 
bowl  given  by  Sir  John  Harpur,  a  Gentle- 
man-Commoner of  1697  and  pupil  of  Addi- 
son, is  also  shown. 

"At  4.30  a.m.  on  1  May  the  choristers  and  the 
ohoir  gather  in  their  surjjlices  at  the  foot  of  the 
tower  and  ascend  to  the  top,  where  the  leads  are 
dividefl  by  a  roi)e— one  half  being  reserved  for  the 
choir,  the  other  for  such  visitors  as  have  obtained 
tickets.  At  ten  minutes  to  five  the  sun  rises  in  a 
blaze  of  splendour,  and  as  the  ^reat  l)ells  strike  the 
hour  of  live  a  hush  falls  on  the  tower  and  on  the 
waitin-f  crowjls  IkjIow.  Taking  their  note  from  the 
last  stroke  of  the  liell,  the  choir  sinu;  a  simple  Latin 

hymn  of  rive  verses The  last  chord  lingers  in 

tlie  clear  air,  the  bells  rinn  out  a  May  Day  welcome, 
while  the  great  tower  rocks  with  their  motion,  and 
tlie  famous  ceremony  is  over.  It  is  very  short,  very 
siini)le,  very  dis4i)»itointing  to  those  who  come  ex- 
j>ecting  much,  yet  indescribably  charming  to  those 
to  whom  such  rites  a])i)eal.  Older  and  more 
barbarous  generations  of  choristers  delighted  to 
convey  bad  eggs  to  the  top  of  the  tower,  which

street   below Breakfast    in    Hall    followed   at 

t)  o'clock,  to  which  full  justice  was  d(me  byapi^tites 
sharpetiecl  by  the  keen  air.  After  which,  as  May

ran"  wild  in  the  College  until  chapel  time— the  one 
day  in  the  year  upon  which  we  were  allowed  to 
do  so.

John  Opie.  R.A. — The  recently  passed 
centenary  of  the  death  of  John  Opie  prompts 
me  to  send  to  '  N.  &.  Q.*  a  copy  of  the  in- 
scription on  the  slab  wliich  covers  his 
remains  In  the  crypt  of  St.  Paid's  Cathedral. 
His  grave  is  next  to  that  of  RejTiolds,  in 
the  south-east  or  Painters'  Comer,  and  is 
thus  indicated  : — 

Here  lie  the  Remains  of 

John  Ojiie  Flsquire 

Member  of  and 

Profe^jftor  of  Painting 

to 
the  Royal  Academy 

of 

Painting  Sculpture 

and  Architeoiure. 

Ho  was  Born  May  1761 

at  St,  Afines  in  Cornwell  [-Hie] 

and  Died  at  his  House  in 

Bemcrs  Street  London 

the  29th  of  April  1807. 

The  house  in  which  Opie  was  bom  ia  still 

in   existence.      It    is    known    as    Harmony 

Cot,  and  is  owned  and  occupied  by  one  of 

his  direiTt  desoendants.     A  photo-engraving 

of    the    hoiwe    appeared    in    The    lUvstrated 

Wc8te'n^    Weekly   News   of   4    May.     May   I 

inquire  if  the  house  in  which  he  died  is  still 

intact ;  end,  if  so,  whether  it  is  marked  by  a 

County  Council  plaque  ? 

Is  the  exact  date  of  Opie's  birth  known  ? 
'  Chambers's  Encycloptedia '  and  other  books 
of  reference  I  have  consulted  incorrectly 
state  that  his  death  occurred  on  tlie  9th 
(instead  of  the  29th)  of  April,  1807.

AviONON  Society  of  Illumikati.  (See 
9  S.  vii.  186.) — Continuing  the  researches 
indicated  at  this  reference,  the  Rev.  James 
Hyde  (the  compiler  of  the  *  Bibhography  of 
Swedenborg  *  lately  published)  contributes 
to  The  New-Church  Review  (Philadelphia)  for 
April  an  article  of  -24  pp.  entitled  '  Benedict 
C]iastanier  and  the  lUuminati  of  Avignon.* 
Tlu;  influence  of  this  and  similar  secret 
Hocieties  upon  the  genesis  of  the  French 
Revolution  is  being  recognized — e.g.,  by 
the  ^vTiter  of  articles  in  The  Edinburgh 
Review  for  July  and  September,  1906 — 
and  details  of  their  generally  obscure  history, 
such  as  those  given  by  Mr.  Hyde,  are  in- 
vested with  a  growing  interest.  Students 
of  eigliteonth-century  Freemasonry  and  of 
many  minor  religioiw  movements*  of  that 
time  will  also,  possibly,  be  glad  to  have 
their  attention  thus  called  to  an  American 
publication — wliioh  is  not  widely  known  in 
England.  Charles  *Hio  ham.

Teetotaller. — Erasmus,  in  the  '  Convivium 
Fabulcsum,'  after  describing  the  abstinence 
of  Romulus,  "  who  drank  as  the  dogs 
drink,"  mentions  Johan  Botzcm,  a  canon 
of  Constance,  as  another  example  of  water- 
drinking.  He  was  a  pleasant  and  courteous 
companion.  Teetotallers  were  not  very 
common  in  tlie  fifteenth  century,  though 
many  of  the  mediaeval  saints  and  hermits 
rigidly  abstained  from  intoxicants.  There 
is  a  German  biography  of  Johannes  von 
Botzheim  by  Carl  Walchner,  wliich  was 
published  at  Schaffhausen  in  1836 ;   but  it 


is  not  at  the  moment  aeoesslble. 
his  letters  appeir  in  the  *  Briefe  wM 
derius   Erasmus    von  Rotterdam,'  U 

§)geben  von  tJoaeph  Fontemann  nil 
unther  (Leipug,  Harraasowitz,  19H_

there  is  given  the 

Epitaphicm  Testamest.vbicm. 

T^    Toi^    'ESTHSE    Tov    linBavouc    Eiiitaphittm 
testamentarium  ahrhypa^ov. 
Quie  linnuam,  aut  nihil,  autnihili,  autvtx  sunt  mea. 

Sorties 
Do  Morti :  re<ldo  o«tera»  Christe  I  tibi.    1826. 

This  epitaph  appears  as  a  foot-note  to  the 
'  Lines  suggested  by  tlie  Last  Words  of 
Berengarius  *  in  '  The  Literarj^  Souvenir  ' 
for  1827.  edited  by  Alaric  A.  Watts  (p.  17), 
except  that  *'  hnquam "  is  printed  *'  lin- 
guam,"  and  iTiBavw^  is  the  form  of  the 
Greek  word — a  form  adopted  by  Camp- 
bell in  his  notes,  but  not  in  the  t«xt.  Of 
this  %vord  none  of  the  classical  scholars  con- 
suited  by  Campbell  could  make  anything. 
He  Buggeste  "  worthless  *'  or  "  lacking  "  as 
the  interpretation.  Rouglily,  the  epitaph 
miglil  perhaps  be  rendered  : — 

What  things  I  leave  are  naught,  or  Httle  worth, 

Or  else  can  scarce  belong  \o  me  ; 
To  Death  I  jjive  the  sordid  dust  of  »irth. 

The  rest  give  back,  O  Christ,  to  Thee. 

The  appearance  of  the  '  Epitapliiimi 
Tcstamentarium  '  in  '  The  Literarj*  Souvenir  * 
is  duly  noticed  in  Haney's  *  Coleridge  Biblio- 
graphy.' but  the  reference  to  tlie  page  is 
inaccurate  ;  and  in  Sheplierd-Prideaux'a 
*  Coleridge  Bibliography '  it  is  wrongly 
assigned  to  1829.  Possessors  of  these  useful 
books,  which  I  have  frequently  used  with 
gratitude,  may  like  to  make  a  note. 

William  E.  A.  Axon.

Wheel  Crosses. — In  Cornwall  there  are 
a  large  munber  of  crosses  oaUed  wheel 
crosses.  Tliis  kind  of  cross  consists  of  a 
shaft  with  a  round  beaded  head,  on  each 
side  of  which  is  an  equal-hmbed  crctos  filling 
the  circle.  Are  there  any  of  these  crosses 
in  Brittany,  in  the  eoimties  on  the  Mediter- 
ranean, or  in  the  East  1  If  so,  in  what 
books  are  they  described  ? 

I  shall  be  glad  to  hear  direct.

Authors  of  Quotations  Wanted. — A 
relative  of  the  present  writer  set  out  at  San 
Francisco  in  1891  to  find  the  source  of  three 
quotations.  The  first — "  Friends  such  as 
we  desire  are  dreams  and  fables  " — she  found 
in  Chile,  a  year  or  two  later,  in  a  volume  of 
Emerson.  After  fifteen  years  the  seccnd 
turned  up  in  a  ccpy  of  '  Adam  Bede  '  at 
Lausanne.  It  was  a  saying  of  Mrs.  Poyser  : 
'*  But  wooden  folks  would  need  ha'  wooden 
things  t'  handle." 

The  third  remains  uniound,  and  an 
imploring  voice  from  Iquique  asks  my  help. 
Can  any  reader  say  where  I  shall  find  "  No 
star  ever  rose  and  set  without  influence 
somewhere  '*  ?  M.  H.

took the  ends,  and  overestimated  the 
powers,  of  Government "  ?  I  think  in  one 
of  the  '  Essays.'  N.  W.  H.

Rev,  Osborke    Gokdon's    Obiod?.  —  A 

good  deal  has  been  written  about  this  dis- 
tinguished Oxford  don,  who  died  in  1883. 
ajia  to  whose  care  the  King  was  committed 
at  Christ  Church  over  lialf  a  century  ago  ; 
but  neither  the  notice  in  the  *  D.N.B-'  nor 
the  large  '  Memoir  *  prepared  in  1 885  by 
the  Rev.  George  Marshall  says  anything 
about  his  origin.  I  believe,  however,  I  ara 
right  in  saying  that  his  father  wbjs  George 
Osborne  Gordon,  of  Broselev  (died  1822), 
and  that  his  mother's  maiden  name  was 
Elizabeth  Onions.  His  grandfather  was 
Alexander  Gordon,  of  Charterhouse  Square, 
London  (1742-1823),  who  either  founded  or 
was  connected  with  the  famous  gin  distillery 
in  Goswell  Road,  now  conducted  by  Tan- 
queray,  Gordon  &  Co.  There  is  a  family 
tradition  that  these  Gordons  are  cadeta  of 
the  famdy  who  own  the  beautiful  estate  of 
Abergeldie,  Aberdeenshire,  now  tenanted 
by  the  Prince  of  Wales,  as  it  was  by  King 
Edward  himself  for  a  great  many  years. 
Alexander  Gordon  aforesaid  is  stat^-d  to 
have  married  hia  cousin,  Susanna  Osborne, 
daughter  of  WUUam  Osborne  and  Hannah 
Herbert.  Any  information  about  the  family 
will  be  welcome.  What  was  the  name  of 
the  Rev.  Osborne  Gordon's  brother  who  was 
killed  in  a  carriage  accident  shortly  after  I 
Osborne's  own  death  ^  J,  M.  Bulloch. 
118,  Pall  PoU.

abnormal.

I  doubt  whether  any  poet's  rimes  prove 
anything  in  this  case.  1  know,  indeea,  one 
rime  to  ivound  as  now  pronounced — the 
Salopian  river-name  Cound  ;  but  the  plenti- 
ful lack  of  rimes  ine-vitably  leads  to  tJie  use 
of  ej'e-rimes  only  when  the  word  occurs  at 
the  end  of  a  line.  Of  course  your  corre- 
spondent knows  that  in  se\'"eral  of  our 
dialects  toound  is  pronounced  so  as  to  rime 
with  sound.  I  never  heard  it  otherwise 
pronounced,  for  instance,  in  the  name 
*  Wound-ill  Spring  "  (Wound-heal  Spring), 
a  spring  rising  in  the  parish  of  Upper  J 
Broughton,  Notts,  which  used  to  be  credited] 
with  wonderful  healing  virtue.       C.  C.  B. 

In  my  young  days  the  parson  in  the 
pulpit,  his  clerk  below,  the  master  in  the 
school,  and  all  the  folks  made  wound  rime 
with  ^ound.  It  was  like  the  word  iix  '*  I  'vo  , 
wound  up  t'  clock."        Thos.  R.\tcliffe.

The  Newes  was  issued  on  Thursdays, 
No.  1  appearing  on  3  Sept.,  and  the  number- 
ing continued  until  No-  18,  31  Dec.,  1663. 
The  following  issue  waa  called  No.  2*  and 
appeared  on  Thursday,  7  Jan.,  1663/4, 
being  succeeded  by  No.  4,  on  Thursday, 
14  Jan.  The  paper  continued  xmtil  No.  93, 
16  Nov..  1665. 

The  first  issue  of  The  Jntelligencer  was 
called  No.  2,  and  appeared  on  Monday, 
7  Sept.,  the  numbering  continuing  until 
No.  18.  28  Dec.,  1663.  The  succeeding  issue 
was  on  Monday,  4  Jan.,  1663/4,  and  called 
No.  1.  No.  3  appeared  11  Jan.,  and  so  on 
until  2  Dec..  1665. 

There  may  have  been  later  issues  of  The 
Newts  and  the  I  nieiligencer.

William Talman,  Architect:  Hamptox 

CorniT  Palack  (10  S.  vii.  288).— I  t  unk 
there  is  verj^  little  doubt  that  Wdham 
Talrnan  acted  as  Wren's  representative  (or. 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES. 


as  "we  should  now  call  it,  &^  archit€»ct'« 
clerk  of  the  works)  at  Hampton  Court 
Palace.  The  position  was  a  responsible 
onot  and  Tahnan  was  well  suited  to  fill  it. 
I  am  not  aware  that  there  are  any  drawings 
in  existence  of  the  old  palace  aa  it  was 
before  Wren's  alterations,  and  it  is  probable 
that  no  such  drawings  were  made  by 
Wren.  Architectural  draughtsmanship  in 
the  seventeenth  eenturj',  and  even  in  the 
eighteenth,  was  of  a  rudimentary  kind,  as 
may  be  seen  by  the  volume  of  drawingH  in 
the  Soane  Muaeum  attributed  to  Thorpe, 
and  Sir  Joim  Soane's  own  working  drawings 
at  the  same  place.  Architects  formerly 
troubled  themselves  bxit  little  about  the 
plans  of  existing  buildings  to  which  they 
made  additions.  Johk  H£B9. 

Brighton. 

Wabam  Talman  (fl.  1670-1700)  was 
appointed  Comptroller  of  the  Works  to 
William  IH.,  and  in  that  capacity  was 
responsible  for  the  carrying  out  of  the 
extensive  additions  and  alterations  to 
Hampton  Court  Palace,  begun  in  1690  from 
the  designs  of  Sir  Christopher  Wren,  witli 
whose  opinion  Tahnan  appears  to  have 
frequently  disagreed. 

A  folio  volume  of  Talman's  drawings  is 
preserved  at  the  Royal  Institute  of  British 
Architects  ;  but  not  having  referred  t<t  this 
volume,  I  do  not  know  if  it  contains  any 
designs  of  Hampton  Coiu"t  Palace. 

Alfred  Sydney  Lewts.

UUB  (10  S.  vii.  U9,  214,  276).— The

One  means  of  tiscertaining  the  number 
of  windmills  in  Sussex  would  be  to  count 
them  from  the  Ordnance  map,  in  which  the 
position  of  everj'  mill  is  indicated  by  a 
niiniature  representation  of  one  ;  but  taere 
is  nothing  to  show  whether  the  miJl  is  still 
in  use,  or  itd  condition  (very  many  mills 
are  saU-less) ;  neither  are  smock-miUs  and 
post- mills  differentiated.  Supplementary  to 
tliis  your  correspondent,  if  his  ultimate  object 
justifies  the  trouble,  might  advertise  for 
information  in  a  county  paper.  There  are 
probably — if  they  could  be  found — half  a 
dozen  men  in  Sussex  who  could  name 
between  them  every  mill  left  standing  in  the 
county,  and  a  good  manj'  of  those  that 
have   disappeared.  Perceval  Lucas. 

TUttkehain,  Pulborough- 

Windmills  with  five  sails,  formerly  known 
as  "  five- wand  "  mills,  are  not  so  uncommon 
as  Mr.  Curtis's  informant  believes.  Her© 
in  Newcastle  we  have  a  very  conspicuous 
example  standing  between  tlie  Castle  Leazes 
and  the  Town  Moor  ;  and  I  fancy  tliere  is 
one  on  the  east  side  of  the  Great'Northem 
RaUwav,  which  I  see  merrily  revolving  when 
I  travel  by  dayhght  to  London. 

The  name  of  another  i.-i  preserved  in  tho 
*'  Five- Wand  Mill  "'  Im^  Bensham  Road, 
Gateshead  ;  but  the  mill  itself,  alas  !  no 
longer  keeps  company  with  the  hostelry

The  old  notion  that  the  Hocktide 
festivities  commemorated  tiie  massacre 
of  tlie  Dane«  in  W<)Q  seems  to  have  been 
abandoned.  It  lias  been  pointed  out*  as 
perhaps  significant  that  the  season  of  tl»© 
celebration,  *.e.,  the  Tuesdaj'  following  the 
second  Sunday  after  Easter,  coincided  with 
that  of  the  opening  of  the  High  Courts  of 
Justice.  At  Hungerford,  we  are  told,  the 
borough  officers — constable,  bailiff,  port- 
reeve, &c. — were  elected  at  Hocktide,  and 
sworn  in  at  the  Court  Baron  on  the  Friday 
of  that  week.  Was  it  as  a  sort  of  burlesque 
of  the  polling  practice  of  **  chairing  "  the 
candidate  (so  vividly  illustrated  in  Hogarth's 
series  of  electioneering  i>ictures  at  the  Soane 
Museum)  that  at  Hungerford  the  Tithing 
men  on  Hock  Monday  were  wont  to  carry 
through  the  streets  a  gaily  bedecked  chair, 
in  which  they  '"hocked  "  {Le.,  uplifted)  any 
woman  who.  on  being  captured  by  them, 
refused  to  give  a  penny  for  her  release  ? 

I  am  under  the  impression  that  the  t«rms 
'*  hock  day,"  "  hock  money."  are  not  to  be 
met  with  in  the  same  accounts  with  those 
of  '*  Hoggeners,"  '*  Hogen  store";  nor,  I 
fanC}-,  in  the  same  counties  :  Somerset  and 
Devon  jield  examples  of  the  latter  words. 

In  the  South  Ta^^ton  accounts  I  find 
among  receipts  in  1526  "  vli,  xvcf,,  de 
custod'  S'uisie "  (ale-wardeas) :  in  1526-0 
about  the  same  sum  "  rec.  de. ..  .custod' 
de  le  hoggenre  store  "  ;  in  1626-7.  *'  xjli. 
iiijd.  de.,.. custod  S'uisie,"  and  so  on; 
the  word  "  hoggenro  "  not  occurring  again  ; 
while  later  under  the  accounts  of  the  warden 
uf  tlie  "  Young  men,"  comes  such  an  entry 
as  that  of  1664.  "We  made  of  our  Alle 
and  gathering  xl/t.  viijs.  viijd." 

The  terra  *' Ho^gener  "  or  "  Hoggler," 
which  the  '  N.E.D,  states  to  be  of  obscure 
origin,  has  been  much  discussed — moat 
thorouglily,  perhajis,  by  the  late  Bishop 
Hobhouse,  from  whose  notes  on  some 
*  Somerset  Churchwardens'  Accounts  'f  it 
appears  that  at  Crosoombe,  Somerset,  the 
*'  Hogglera "  were  a  guild  who  paid  con- 
tributions to  the  head  churchwarden.     At

Banwell  the  church  derived  ita  larsest  in- 
come from  the  gifts  of  "  Hogglers/*  the  item 
constantly  recurring  "  Venditio  et  incro- 
mentum  forinsecum  de  la  hogelin^."  The 
"  Hogeling  "  in  that  parish  was  divided  into 
the  ^'Upland"  and  "Marshland."  The 
words  "  Venditio  ad  incrementum  "  bid  us 
believe,  says  the  editor,  that  there  was  a 
common  stock  running  on  common  lands, 
on  the  hill  and  in  the  moor,  in  which  the 
Church  had  rights  ;  and  that  the  stock  was 
husbanded  and  the  rights  made  productive 
by  a  band  of  working-men,  who  thus  made 
a  contribution  to  tne  church  funds.  In 
another  note  Bishop  Hobhouse  states  that 
the  "  Hog^lers  "  were  the  lowest  order  of 
labourer  with  spade  or  pick,  in  tillage  or  in 
minerals ;  but  the  earliest  evidence  that 
he  adduces  for  this  use  is  from  a  speech  by 
the  sister  of  Hannah  More.  In  local  idiom 
he  finds  that  the  word  "hoggle"  has  a 
contemptuous  application  ;  e.^.,  *  You  might 
Jioggle  them  potatoes — ^but  you  can't  dig 
them  ! " 

In  the  churchwardens'  accounts  of  Min- 
chinhampton  '*  Hogling  money "  is  a  fre- 
quent source  of  revenue.  The  editor,  John 
Bruce,  F.S.A.,  takes  this  to  have  been  a 
customary  payment  made  by  the  sheep- 
farmers  of  the  parish  for  their  "  hoglings  " 
-'  "  hoggets  "  (that  is,  he  says,  their  sheep 


or 


of  the  ftecond  year). 

According  to  the  '  N.E.D.,'  "  hog,"  a  word 
of  uncertain  origin,  was  applied  both  to 
pig  and  to  lamb,  the  sense  of  yearling  appear- 
ing to  prevail. 

In  conjunction  with  the  foregoing  sugges- 
tions I  venture  to  submit  to  philologists  an 
idea  of  my  own  :  tliat  the  root-notion  of  all 
tlie  terms  under  consideration  is  hight  and 
that  the  *'  Hoggenera  "  were  originally  as  a 
class  hill-men,  whether  as  herdsmen  or 
miners,  or  both.  Dufresne  and  otlier  dic- 
tionaries give  "  Hoga,  a  hill  "  (cf.  '*  Hoga  de 
Cosdone  '^  in  the  Dartmoor  Forest  Per- 
ambulations). In  HaUiwell  I  find  "  Hoggan- 
bag — a  miner's  bag,  wherein  he  carries  his 
iwovisions  (Comw. )."  Wright's  *  Prov.  Diet.' 
gives  "  Hoggle,  to  take  up  from  the  ground, 
Dke  potatoes"  (wliich  might  perhaps  ori- 
ginally have  referred  to  work  with  a  pick). 
The  '  N.E.D.'  states  that  the  conjecture 
that  M.E.  hog  represented  Cornish  hoch, 
Welsh  huch,  swine,  is  improbable  on  plionetic 
and  other  grounds.  Is  it  possible  that  it 
might  have  been  derived  from  hoga,  hill, 
and  have  designated  animals  that  were 
allowed  to  roam  freely  on  the  hills,  like  the 
Dartmoor  cattle  and  ponies  ? 

Ethel  Leoa-Weekes.

Jw/i&oori.— ^The   inodo  of  playing   this 

!  is  given  in  Stnitt's  '  Sporte  and  Pas- 


times.' At  oae  end  of  the  board  tliere  was. 
a  line  drawn  across  parallel  with  the  edge, 
about  four  inches  from  it.  The  object  of  Uie 
players  was  to  sliove  or  propel  from  the  palm 
of  tlie  hand  smooth  coins,  or  discs  of  metaU 
beyond  the  line  as  near  the  edee  of  tlie  table 
as  possible.  The  coin  that  feS  off  the  table 
was  lost ;  if  it  hunc  on  the  edge,  it  counted 
3  ;  if  between  the  line  and  the  edge,  2  :  if 
on  the  line,  1.  At  Chartlev  Hall,  Stafiford- 
sliire,  the  table  was  31  ft.  long ;  and  there 
was  one  in  London  39  ft.  in  length.  Htnitt,. 
in  the  Introduction  mentions  that  Lord 
Mount  joy,  Regent  of  Ireland  in  1599^ 
*'  delighted  in  plapng  at  shovel-board "  :. 
and  tlxe  game  was  so  popular  in  England 
that  there  was  scarcely  a  gentleman's  house 
in  the  country  in  which  this  piece  of  furniture 
was  not  a  conspicuous  object. 

The  game  was  know^i  by  various  names : 
shovelboard,  shovegroat,  shoveboard,  shuffle- 
board.  In  *  Merrj'  Wives  of  Windsor,'  I.  i., 
Falstaff  asks  :  "  Pistol,  did  you  pick  Master 
Slender's  purse  ?  ''  to  wliioh  Slender  repHes  : 
"  Ay. . .  ,of  seven  groats  in  mill-sixpences, 
and  two  Edward  eliovelboards,  that  cost 
me  two  sliilUng  and  two  pence  a  piece." 
Slender  refers  to  the  broad  shillings  of 
Edward  VI.  commonly  used  in  plaving.  In 
*  2  Henry  IV\,'  II.  iv.,  Falstaff  says  •  "  Quoit 
liim  down,  Bardolph,  like  a  shovegroat 
shilling."  Ben  Jons  on  in  '  Everj'  Man  in 
his  Humour,'  III.  ii.  (acted  in  1698),  usee 
the  expression,  *'  And  made  it  run  as  smooth 
off  the  tongue  as  a  shovegroat  shilling." 
John  Taylor,  the  Water  Poet  (1630),  calls 
the  game  shoveboard,  and  makes  one  of  these 
slullinga  complain  of  being  so  used  : — 

You  see  my  face  in  heardlefiB,  antooth,  and  plaine.

With  me  the  unthrifte  every  day,

"Prince**  Boothby.  (See  9  S.  v.  127. 
236).— At  the  first  of  these  references  H.  T.  B, 
inquires  as  to  the  identity  of  *'  Prince  " 
Boothby,  referred  to  in  the  letters  of  Horace 
Walpole  and  George  Selwjm.  asking  ( 1 )  w)«y 
he  was  called  "  Wince  "  :  (2)  whether  he 
belonged  to  the  Ashbourne  family  ;  (3)< 
whom  he  married  ;  and  (4)  when  he  died. 
xAs  no  one  seems  to  have  answered  these 
questions,  it  may  be  as  well  to  put  the 
desired  information  on  record  in  '  X.  &  Q.' 

Thomas  Boothby  (1677-1752),  of  Tooley 
Park,  Leicestershire,  one  of  tlie  fathers  of 
foxhunting,  married  for  his  first  wife 
Ehzabeth,  eldest  daughter  and  coheir  of 
Sir  Charles  Skrymsher.  of  Norbury,  Staffs, 
and  had  by  her  an  eldest  son,  Thomas 
Boothby  (1699-1751),  who  assumed  the 
additional  name  of  Skrymsher  under  }jis 
grandfather's  will.  Thomas  Boothby 
Skrymsher,  who  was  M.P.  for  Leicester^ 
1726-7,  and  *'  register  general  of  all  trading 
ships  belonging  to  Great  Britain,"  married 
in  January,  1720/21,  Anne,  eldest  daughter 
of  Sir  Hugli  Cloptou,  ot  ^«vf  P\swi%;f^'«^vS.*2j^^-

Admiral  Bedford. — Can  any  of  your 
readers  inform  me  wliether  Gilbert,  in  the 
'Historical  Survey  of  Cornwall,'  1820.  was 
correct  in  saying  that  Admiral  William 
Bedford,  who  died  1827,  was  the  son  of  the 
Rev.  William  Bedford,  and  Dorothea  Kemp 
and  thus  of  the  Cornish  clerical  family  of 
Bedfords  ?  The  '  D.N.B.'  queries  the  date 
of  his  birth,  and  omits  the  name  of  his  father. 
If  Gilbert  was  right,  it  would  have  been 
easy  for  the  editor  to  find  the  records  of  the 
Admiral's  baptism  and  the  name  of  his 
parents.  My  reason  for  asking  is  that  I 
Jiave  information  which  points  to  his  father 
having  been  of  Yorkshire  and  Hertfordshire, 
and  not  the  Rev.  W.  Bedford.  Were  there 
two  Capt.  W.  Bedfords  in  the  n^xy  in  1804  7

Cowper's  John  Gilpin. — The  identity  of 
*'  John  Gilpin  *'  has  been  the  subject  of 
man),  and  lengthy  contributions  to  '  N.  &  Q.* 
It  is,  however,  doubtful  if  it  has  been  made 
clear,  while  in  several  instances  the  name 
itself  has  been  supposed  factitious ;  the 
latter  idea  must  of  course  be  discarded,  the 
surname  being  fairly  common.  .Any  add!' 
tional  bits  of  information  bearing  on  the 
subject  will,  I  doubt  not,  be  acceptable,  even 
if  they  load  not  to  an  absolute  solution  of 
the  question.  The  statement  that  manv 
cavalry  men  have  ecurned  the  *'  nickname 
of  **  Johnny  Gilpin  "  seems  valueless,  unless 
it  be  that  a  bad  horseman  was  designated  a 
"  Gilpin  "  from  Cowper's  GUnin. 

We  have  accounts  of  the  death  of  several 
individuals  who  were  stated  t^  have  been 
the  original  Gilpin  of  Cowper's  lijwsft*.  csttfii 
who  died  at  BatVv  m  \'\'^^,\<s««^^%'^^i^^^'^*

to  his  daughter ;  another  named  Beyer, 
a  linendraper  of  London,  who  died  in  1791  ; 
and  a  third,  named  Jonathan  Gilpin,  who 
deceased  in  Bath,  1790.  The  last  was 
mentioned  in  1878  by  the  London  papers 
at  the  time  of  the  rebuilding  of  "  The  Bell  " 
at  Edmonton.  It  was  then  stated  that  he 
was  the  original  of  a  *'  train-band  captain." 

It  will  be  noticed  that  there  is  a  similarity 
between  this  Gilpin,  as  regards  the  date  of 
his  death,  and  the  one  who  left  the  20,000Z. 
Now  it  appeeurs  to  me  that  the  dates  of  the 
deatli  of  these  so-called  originals  of  Cowper's 
Gilpin  put  the  whole  of  them  "  out  of  court." 
Lady  Austin  told  Cowper  a  story  which  she 
had  treiisured  in  her  memory  from  her 
childliood.  This  lady,  at  the  period,  was  at 
least  fifty  years  of  age,  and  it  is  improbable 
that  she  would  have  heard  tlie  story  in  her 
childhood  as  told  of  persons  who  were  con- 
temporaneous or  nearly  so. 

The  bedlcul  was  written  in  1782,  but  was 
not  generally  made  puVlic  till  two  or  three 

{rears  after.  On  tlie  authority  of  Southey's 
ife  and  works  of  Cowper,  we  learn  that 
Mr.  Unwin,  to  whom  Cowper  sent  *  John 
Gilpin,'  wrote  tliat  it  "  made  him  laugh 
tears."  Cowper  replied  "  that  a  laugh 
tliat  hurts  nobody  has  at  least  the  grace  of 
novelty  to  recommend  it." 

Clearly  tlie  gentle  and  timid  vouth ;  the 
modest  and  sliy  young  man  ;  the  constitu- 
tionally melancholy  elderly  man,  whose 
public  exliibition  of  liimself  was  as  *'  mortal 
l)oison "  ;  the  man  who  loved  the  poor  ; 
the  man  with  whom  Mr.  Newton  lived  in 
close  communion  for  twelve  years,  confessing 
that  for  the  first  half  lie  daily  admired  and 
endeavoured  to  imitate  Cowper,  and  saying, 
"  The  last  six  I  walked  ])ensively  with  liim 
in  the  valley  of  the  shadow  of  death,"  was 
not  the  man  likely  to  bring  a  living  person 
into  such  world-wide  ridicule  as  he  did 
"  John  Gilpin."  Mrs.  Unwin,  Cowper's 
amiable  friend,  was  the  sister  of  John 
Unwin,  at  whose  residence  she  said  she  met 
the  Kev.  \Vm.  Gilj>in.  There  are  several 
Unwins  who  lie  in  St.  John's  Churchyard, 
Croydon. 

The  hoa<*e  in  which  John  Gilj>in  lived  at 
Thornton  Heath,  in  the  county  borough  of 
Croydon,  was  standing  about  eight  years 
ago.  An  inscription  on  it  recorded  that  it 
was  *'  Gilpin's,"  and  the  picture  of  the  old 
house  ha«  been  reproduced  on  post  cards. 

In  the  register  of  Croydon  Parish  Church 
we  find  that  a  '*  Luke  Gylpin  "  was  christened 
in  1621.  There  is  also  in  Croydon  Parish 
Church  a  chaUce,  whicli  I  saw  a  few  days 
ago,   with   an   inscription   on   the   bottom. 


to  the  effect  that  it  wu  the  gift  of  '*Mr. 
John  Gilpin,  of  Croydon."  It  was,  I  bdient 
this  John  Gilpin,  or  one  of  his  f  amUv,  ito 
figured  in  Cowper's  liofiB,  and  who  bodhl 
or  owned  the  estate  of  "  Collier's  Watet 

In  another  record  I  find  the  wtn, 
"Payde  for  the  Lorde  quit  rent  to  K 
Gilpin."     This  was  in  1636. 

With  these  facts  before  us,  is  it  not  mort 
probable  that  John  Gilpin  of  Croydon  wj 
the  hero  of  Lady  Austin's  story,  which  hid 
been  handed  down  from  one  generation  to 
another,  and  which  she  remenabered  "frott 
her  childhood,"  and  related  to  Cowper,  *h» 
founded  his  ballad  upon  it  ?  ^^ 

It  is  perhaps  remarkable  that  for  the  wood 
generally  '  John  Gilpin '  won,  if  it  does  not 
retain,  affection  over  any  other  of  the  sun* 
author's  poems.  In  1785  *  A  Second  Holidtf 
for  John  Gilpin'  was  published,  certainly

Sir  Thos.  Bloodworth,  Lord  Mayor 
1665-0. — I  should  be  grateful  ior  positiv'e 
information   as  to   the  plaoe  of   interment 


410 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES,     iw  &  til  may  %w. 


of  Sir  Thomas  Bloodworth,  or  Bludworth, 
Lord  Mayor  during  the  period  of  the  Great 
Fire.  Hw  wiU,  dated  24  Nov.,  1680,  and 
proved  10  July,  1682,  does  not  name  his 
desired    burying-place,    cmd    none    of    the 

fenealogical  writers  seem  to  be  aware  of  it. 
do  not  know  the  exact  date  of  his  death 
in  ICay,  1682,  but  his  funeral  sermon  was 
reached  on  the  24th  of  the  month  by  Samuel 
Freeman,  rector  of  St.  Anne's,  Alderssate. 
He  can  hardly  have  been  buried  there,  how- 
ever, as  there  is  no  entry  of  the  event  in  the 
register. 

Sir  Thomas  was  a  parishioner  of  St.  John 
Zachary,  in  the  records  of  which  his  signature 
is  very  numerously  extant.  The  parish 
register  dates  from  1693  only,  and  it  is  not 
quite  dear  whether  a  separate  register  was 
kept  from  1670  (the  year  m  which  the  parish 
was  united  to  St.  Anne's  above)  or  not.  Of 
course  Freeman  was  rector  of  both  parishes, 
and  may  have  officiated  at  his  interment 
in  the  St.  John's  side  of  the  church,  no  record 
of  the  burial,  however,  remaining  (presuming 
a  separate  register  to  have  been  kept  and 
since  lost  or  destroyed).  Against  this 
theory,  however,  must  be  set  the  fact  that 
the  event  is  not  referred  to  in  the  wardens' 
accounts  of  St.  John's,  which  are  etill  in 
existence.  Wiluah  McMurbay. 


THE  PAGE  FAJHILY  AND  THEIR 

MIDDLESEX    ESTATES. 

(10  S.  vii.  322.) 

The  name  of  Page  is  an  honoured  one  at 
Harrow  School.  On  19  Feb.,  1571,  six 
original  governors  were  appointed,  the  tliird 
and  fourth  names  being  John  Page  of 
Wembley  and  Thomas  Page  of  Sudbury 
Court,  which  Thomas  rented  of  the  Norths. 
John's  daughter  Katlierine  by  her  meuTiage 
with  Richard  Brownlow  (1553-1638),  eliief 
prothonotary  of  the  Court  of  Common  Pleas, 
became  ancestress  of  the  Earls  Brownlow 
(v.  'D.N.B.,'  vii.  82).  On  20  Feb.,  1624, 
John  of  Wembley  was  succeeded  as  a 
governor  of  the  school  by  his  son  John 
Page,  who  was,  in  his  turn,  succeeded  in  this 
office  by  Jolm  Page  of  Uxendon  on  15  Dec., 
1654.  The  last  was  followed  on  27  Sept., 
1667,  by  Thomas  Smitli.  Thomas  Page  of 
Sudbury  was  executor  to  Lady  Lyon's 
will  (proved  in  1569  ?).  She  was  wife  of 
Sir  John  Lyon,  Kt.,  of  Alperton,  viz.  Twy- 
ford  Abbey,  Lord  Mayor  of  London  1554, 


and  apparently  first  comin  to  the  founder 
of  the  school,  John  Lyon  of  Preston,  flii 
3  Jan.,  1586,  Henry  Page,  son  of  Thomatf 
Sudbunr,  then  stated  to  be  recently  deceaed^ 
is  the  fourth  governor ;  and  Thomas  ftp 
appears  as  such  in  1615.  On  1^  Oct.,  YMt 
John  Page  ol  Harrow  occupies  the  flam 

5ost :  he  is  followed  on  29  Nov.,  1715,  ly 
ohn  Page  of  Wembley,  who,  in  his  ton* 
is  succeeded  on  16  Jan.,  1727,  by  ThomH 
Graham.  On  31  Jan.,  1669,  William  Page 
of  Uxendon  is  appointed  second  governor; 
on  7  April,  169(1,  Richard  Page  is  i^ 
pointed,  but,  declining,  is  foUowed  on 
7  Aug.  by  Edward  Waldo.  On  27  June  ,177i 
Richard  Page  becomes  second  governor; 
and  is  f oUowed  by  William  Page  on  26  Dm^

">Utross'*:  "Topass"  (10  S.  -vtI. 
348), — Every  one  who  cares  about  Anglo- 
Indian  terms  should,  without  hesitation, 
acquire  a  copy  of  Yule's  '  Hobson-Jobson,' 
being  '*  a  Glossary  of  Anglo- Indian  Words 
and  Phrases,"  now  in  the  second  edition. 
**  Matrosa  "  is  explained  fully,  with  four 
quotations  ;  "  topass  "  likewise,  with  eigh- 
teen quotations.  It  is  almost  a  pity  to 
mutilate  Yule's  article ;  but  I  give  liia 
definition  of  *'  topass  "  : — - 

"A  name  used  in  the  17th  and  18th  oeuturies  for 
dark-skinned  or  half-ca«te  claimants  of  Portutnieso 
d€»8cent,  and  ChriBtian  |jrofes.4ion.  It^^  ai>iilication 
is  generally,  tJiough  not  universally,  to  eokiiers  of 
this  olaea." 

There  are  some  books  of  reference  so 
extremely  good  that  they  requiro  no  recom- 
mendation beyond  a  mere  statement  of 
their  existence.  Yule's  book  is  one  of  them. 
The  etymology  of  "  topics  "  is  uncertain  ; 
but  Yule  gives  all  that  can  well  be  said 
about  it,  Walter  W.  Skeat. 

The  etymology  of  "topass,"  an  old  nam© 
for  half-casto  soldiers,  is  somewhat  doubtful. 
It  is  possible  that  it  may  be  a  corruption  of 
Turkish  top-ji,  an  artiflerjTnan,  a  gunner. 
Having  regard,  however,  to  the  fact  that 
it  wa.s  applied  to  Eurasians,  it  seems  more 
likely  to  be  from  the  Tamil  and  Malayalam 
word  tupdshi^  or  topdehi,  wliich  means  an 
interpreter,  literally  a  man  of  two  tongues. 
i.e..  speaking  both  Portuguese  and  the  ver- 
nacular. Some  authors  call  these  half- 
oastea  *'  Black  Portuguese.'* 

Jas.  Platt,  Jun. 

The    use    of    "  matroas "    in    India    was 
probably    due    to    the    Dutcht    in    whose 
language  it  meant  a  sailor.     In   the  Com- 
pany's  early  days   the  terminology  of   the 
artiiler>'  was  naval.     The  officer  commanding 
the   artillery   at   Fort   St.    George  was  the 
Gunner  or  the  Chief  Gunner ;    other  officera 
were  gunner's   mates  ;     the   common  room  i 
was  the  gun-room  ;    the  men  were  termed 
'*  Matross."     After   1700,  when  drafts  from 
the  R.A.  were  sent  to  the  coast,  the  nomen- 
clature  underwent   a  change  ;     there   were  i 
sergeants,  corporals,  bombardiers,  gunners^ 
and   matrosses   (Duncan's  *  History   of  the 
R.A.'),    the    matrosses    being    the    general 
assistants    in    working    a    gun — apprentices, 
without  skilled  knowledge,  but  learning  toi 
be  gunners.

"Moke,"  a  Donxey:  Nicknames  or 
THE  .Army  Service  Cokps  (10  S.  \ni.  68, 
115,  257).— I  belonged  to  the  L.TX\  in 
1855  ;  it  was  then  known  as  the  "  London 
Thie\Hng  Company."  From  the  Land  Trans- 
port Corps  it  was  changed  to  the  Military 
Train  depot,  Horfield  Barracks,  Bristol,  on 
1  Jan.,  1857.  It  was  then  turned  into 
'' Miu-dering  Thieves"  and  "Muck  Tum- 
blers." Afterwards  it  became  the  Array 
Ser\'ice  Corps.  Can  Mr.  Pierpoint  tell  me 
of  the  nickname  of  tliis  corps,  as  I  have 
failed  to  obtain  it  ?  Every  regiment  has  its 
nickname.  1  first  wrote  to  you  on  this  subject 
thirty  or  forty  years  ago. 

"  Moke,"  for  donkey,  was  known  in 
London  in  1851,  when  1  first  went  there 
to  the  Exliibition  in  Hyde  Park.

ISHAM  Family  (10  8.  ^-ii.  265)— The 
curious  contradiction  of  the  ruinour  with 
regard  to  Sir  Edmund  laham's  supposed 
marriage — the  raore  curious  as  made  by 
himself  to  his  own  wife — is  witness  to  the 
oarofuhieas  witli  which  his  family  set  down 
in  black  and  white  all  that  concerned  its 
history.  The  volume  entitled  *  Northamp- 
tonshire Families  '  in  the  '  Victoria  History  ' 
bears  out  this  point  very  strongly.  The 
pedigree  of  the  Ishams  is  given  therein 
moie  fully  aiid  accurately  than  ever  before. 
Among  the  family  documents  and  records 
there  is  a  traditional  pedigree  dra\\'n  out 
in  the  handwriting  of  Sir  Justinian  Isliam, 
the  fifth  baronet.  All  the  steps  in  it  about 
wliich  there  was  the  least  uncertainty  are 
represented  by  dotted  lines.  Not  till  very 
shortly  before  the  publication  of  the  above 
history  did  e\'idence  turn  up  which  exactly 
testified  that  the  family  tradition  was 
actual  fact,  and  carried  the  history  of  the 
family  back  at  least  tliree  generations  ^^-ith 
oeitalnty.  It  would  be  cf  stiU  greater 
interest,  and  surelv  within  the  bounds  of 
posaibihty,  to  establish  the  exact  relation- 
sliip  of  the  Ishams  of  Pj-tcliley  >»ith  the 
older  manorial  house  of  laham  of  Isham, 
and  to  verify  the  traditional  pedigree  in  its 
earUer  portion.  H.  IsaAJt  Longden.

Cathat  (10  S.  vii.  168,  235).— May  not 
the  Bristol  street-name  "  Cat-hay "  and

and  a  survival  of  Norman-French  t  In 
Cardiff  there  is  a  suburb  called  Cathays, 
and  a  district  in  the  centre  of  tlie  city  called 
"  The  Hayes."  There  is  also  the  Uttle 
border  town  of  Hay.  partly  in  Brecknock- 
shire and  partly  in  Herefordsliire,  which  in 
former  times  was  usually  called  "  The  Hay." 
Near  by,  in  Kadnorshire,  is  Heyop  ;  and  the 
family  name  Delahaye,  once  of  territorial 
sig^ficance,  still  survives  in  the  same  district. 
Are  not  all  these  derived  from  the  French 
"  La  Haye,"  an  enclosed  space,  an  enclosure, 
a  park  ?  Bb.

abbatis  ex  dono  dni  Thome  vicarii  de
