Brigam's  instruction  to  his  eldest  son. 
No.  514  is  described  as  '  The  Gran  Contro- 
versy of  the  Eucharist,'  a  seventeenth- 
century  MS.  No.  711  is  a  volume  of  the 
writings  of  Gervais  Melkel,  and  was  given 
to  tJ^e  English  College  at  Douai  by  Alban 
Butler.  Nos.  734  and  735  are  miscellaneous 
collections,  including  poem.^  of  Drj'den, 
Milton,  and  Addison.  No.  109  ia  a  thir- 
teenth-century '  Vita '  of  St.  Thomas  a 
Becket  by  John  of  Salisbury.  **  Ce  manu- 
Bcrit,"  says  M.  Duthilloeul, 

"|kro\ient  sans  doute  de  la  hibliothe^jue  6odl<^nnc 
d'Oxfort,  car  on  trouve,  sur  line  note  nreaque

manha,'  an  eighteenth -century  *  Homo

Mabveij.'8  PoEMa«  168L  —  Everybodr 
interested  in  the  bibliography  of  Marvell 
knows  that  the  collation  of  the  '  JVIiscellaneous 
Poems  *  of  1681  presents  certain  irregularities 
not  hitherto  accounted  for.  Besides  the 
portrait,  there  are  two  leaves  of  signatures 
A  (unmarked),  B,  and  c,  and  four  of  signa- 
tures D  to  Q  inclusiv-e.  There  is  only  one 
leaf  of  B ;  the  next  leaf,  which  has  but  six 
lines  of  text  upon  it,  is  marked  s  (pp.  115 
and  116 — up  to  tJiis  point  tlie  pagination  is 
correct),  and  the  next  is  t2  |p.  131).  After 
t3  and  t4  comes  one  leaf  of  r,  and  the  book 
ends  on  the  recto  of  x  (p.  139).  The 
catchwords  and  figures  (with  the  exception 
of  a  palpable  nusprint,  p.  82)  correspond 
to  the  text  throughout. 

I  have  just  had  the  great  good  fortune 
to  obtain  from  Mr.  Dobell,  whose  faculty 
for  making  discoveries  seems  to  descend 
upon  his  friends,  a  copy  of  this  book 
which,  though  unliappily  imperfect  at  the 
end,  explains  the  irregularities  noted  abo\'e. 

The  signatures  (a,  b,  and  c,  2  leaves  only) 
and  pagination  of  my  copy  up  to  the  point 
where  it  ends  on  u4  (p.  144)  are  regulac.

Chief  Justice  Taney  and  the  Dhed- 
Scott  Case. — By  vay  of  doing  justice  to  an 
eminent  American  jurist,  I  make  this  note. 
Roger  Brooke  Taney  (1777-18G4)  became 
Chief  Justice  of  the  Supreme  Court  of  the 
U.S.,  and  in  that  capacity,  fifty  years  ago, 
jjronotmced  the  opinion  of  the  Court  in  the 
case  of  Drcd  Scott,  a  negro  captured  tmder 
the  Fugitive  Slave  Law.  The  judge's 
enemies  {>er3istently  represented  him  as 
lajTng  it  down  that  a  negro  had  no  rights 
wiiich  a  white  man  was  boimd  to  respect. 
The  case  is  one  of  historic  importance. 
What  he  really  said  was  this  : — 

*'  It  19  difficult  at  this  day  to  rejilize  the  state  of 
lanblic  opix\ioii  in  relation  to  that  unfortunate!  raoe 
which  iirevailwl  in  the  civilised  and  enlightened 
portions  of  the  world  at  the  time  of  the  Declaration. 

of    Lide])endence But    the    publicf    history    of 

every  European  nation  difsnlays  it  in  a  manner  too- 
plain  to  be  mistaken.  Tfiey  had  for  more  than  a. 
century  lieforv  been  refsardefl  as  beinits  of  an  in- 
ferior order,  and  altogether  unfit  to  associate  with 
the  white  race,  either  in  social  or  nolitioal  rohi- 
tions :  and  «o  far  inferior  that  they  nad  no  riuht« 
whioh  a  white  man  was  btjund  to  rem^ect,  and  that 
the  negro  ntiuht  justly  and  lawfully  be  reduced  to- 

slavery   for  his   nenefit This   upinion    was    re* 

warded  as  on  axiom  in  morals  a«  well  as  in  iH>litios,. 
Avhioh  no  one  thou(;ht  of  di.«)[>utinK,  or  8Up)>o»ed  to 
be  open  to  dispute ;  and  men  in  every  urade  audi 
]>ogition  in  society  daily  and  habitually  acted 
upon  it." 

The  case  is  to  be  found  in  vol.  3dx.  of  Howard's 
*  Reports. *  I  may  add  that  the  name 
Taney  is  pronotinced  Tawney. 

Richard  H.  Thornton.

Some  of  the  men  did  not  come  very  regularly, 
•as  haymakmg  -waa  on;  and  some  were 
occupied  at  home  during  the  morning.  A 
diagram  was  given  against  each  man's 
name  :  thus  D*  a  square,  represented  a  full 
day's  work.  He  b^an  from  the  east,  so  | 
meant  work  in  the  early  morning,  and  J  up 
to  midday.  The  addition  of  a  stroke  to  the 
west  U  mdicated  the  hours  after  noon; 
and  the  sq\iare  enclosed  D>  as  i^eady  shown, 
indicated  that  the  man  had  worked  up  to 
seven  o'clock,  and  was  entitled  to  a  full 
'day's  wage.  J.  H.  Rivbtt-Cabnao. 

Sohloss  Rothberg,  Switzerland. 

Good  Kino  Wencesiaus. — The  hero  of 
this  venerable  carol  resembles  Santa  Claus, 
and  in  pictures  he  is  usueJly  represented 
with  a  long  white  beard  like  the  other 
Christmas  worthv.  I  do  not  know  whether 
any  tradition  of  his  benevolent  act  is  at- 
tached to  the  real  St.  Vaclav  (Qerman 
Wenzel,  latinized  Wenceslaus),  to  whom 
jthe  Bohemian  hymn  is  addressed  "  Svaty 
Vaclave,  pomiluji  nas."  According  to  the 
•'  Story  of  Prague,'  by  Count  Liitzow, 
D.Litt.(Oxon),  Prince  (Icnez :  not  king, 
kral)  Wenceslaus  received  the  tonsure  in  a 
•church  built  by  the  first  Christian  ruler, 
Borivoj.  Having  received  from  the  German 
king  Henry  I.  the  arm  of  the  youthful 
St.  Vitus,  he  proceeded  to  found  a  church 
on  the  Hradcany,  where  the  present  cathe- 
dral of  St.  Vitus  stands,  the  resting-place  of 
the  Bohemian  kings.  (The  possible  con- 
nexion of  St.  Vit  with  the  ancient  Slav  deity 
Svantovit  has  been  discussed  by  my  erudite 
friend  Prof.  L.  Leger,  a  first-rate  authority 
on  Slav  antiquities.)  In  935  Prince  Wences- 
laus was  murdered  by  his  heathen  younger 
brother  Boleslav  at  Stara  Boleslav  (Alt 
Bunzlau),  and  in  939  his  remains  were 
brought  to  the  church  on  the  Hradcany. 
On  the  gates  of  the  present  Wenceslaus 
Chapel  of  St.  Vitus's  Cathedral  is  the  ring 
to  which  the  saint  is  said  to  have  clung 
when  murdered.  Illustrations  of  the  murder 
which  I  have  seen  show  a  man  in  his  prime, 
not  the  venerable  crowned  figure  of  good 
Iving  Wenceslaus." 

Last  Christmas  my  friend  Prof.  V. 
Zeitharamer,  of  Kutna  Hora,  an  excellent 
master  of  English,  translated  our  carol  into 
Cech,  and,  thanks  to  assistance  from  a  local 
choirmaster,  his  version,  set  to  the  traditional 
txm.e,  became  very  popular  among  students 
and  schoolchildren.  Kutna  Hora,  or  Kut- 
tenberg,  is  the  ancient  mint  and  mining 
town,  not  far  from  the  battle&eld  oi  Kolin, 
«riier©  the  "  decreee  of  Ki^tna,  Uqic^;'  ^«aV 


ing  the  supremacy  of  the  BoheoCiian 
at  Prague  University,  were  signed  h 
Vaclav  IV.  in  1409.  Not  many  yeai 
wards  the  grim  Zizka  won  victories 
town  over  the  troopa  of  ihk  Empen 
mund.

"Dump." — In  remote  villages  in 
this  name  is  given  to  flat  pieces  < 
which  little  children  push  about  i 
play.  Halliwell  attachea  several  m 
to  the  word,  and  appends  to  No.  2 
list:  "A  clumsy  medal  of  lead  < 
moist  sand  (East)."

School  for  the  Ixdioent  Blixd. — 
Unfortunately,  the  earUer  records  of  this 
Charity^  late  of  St.  CJeorge's  Circus,  South- 
wark,*  liave  been  lost.  The  School  was 
established  in  1799,  and  remained  in  South- 
wark  till  1902,  when  it  moved  to  Leather- 
head,  wliere  it  now  flourishes,  with  some 
215  male  and  female  rodent  pupils.  The 
history  of  the  School  is  now  being  compiled, 
but  tliere  appears  to  be  a  hiatus  in  informa- 
tion as  to  its  site,  which  others  and  myself 
have  been  unable  to  fill. 

The  point  at  issue  is  the  following.  At 
Midsummer,  1811,  the  School  left  its  original 
premises  at  St.  George's  Spa,  St.  George's 
Fields,  Southwark,  and  moved  into  tem- 
porary ones  in  St.  George's  Circus  {vide, 
toUowing  minute}  until  1814,  when  it  occu- 
pied a  schoolhouso  erected  therct  remaining 
in  that  building  until  1902. 

I  shall  be  glad  of  any  information  defining 
the  premises  occupied  by  the  School  from 
Midsummer,  1811,  until  1814.  when  the 
schoolhouse  was  built  and  prepared  for 
reception  of  the  pupils,  &.c. 

The  foUowing  is  a  copy  of  the  minute  from 
the  School  records  : — 

"8AuK.,  I81L 

The  .SecretAn'  rejiorted  that  on  Tuestlfly.  the 
*23  July,  nil  the  ^miiils  Rtid  all  the  drtick'S  lielongiiig 
to  iht*  institution  had  been  removed  Irora  tho 
«ld  Bchool  to  the  new  premises  in  the  Circas, 
St.  Geonce's  Fields." 

All  I  can  say  is  that  the  premises  miL«»t  have 
been  large  and  important  to  accommodate 
resident  pupils  who  numbered  at  least  TU  odd.

"Salutation  Ta^tsbn,"  Billingsgate. — 
Where  waa  the  site  of  this  tavern  ?  It  ia 
recorded  by  Pepys  in  his  diary,  under  date 
1659,  that  he  visited  it  and  dined  there. 
As  it  is  called  by  tliia  name  in  a  will  dateid 
1C8I,  it  existed  after  the  Fire  of  London. 
In  the  diary  of  the  Rev.  Rowland  Davies 
tlie  diarist  states,  circ.  1689,  that  he  dined 
there  upon  fish  in  company  with  the  Earl 
of  Orrerj'  and  others  :  the  diary  is  pub- 
lished by  the  Camden  Society.  In  Boyne'a

^OALi^RY"  (10  S.  vii.  888). — Db* 
iT  invites  some  rational  suggestion

therefore  be  pardonable  to  indulge  in  con- 
jecture. Our  Northern  seaport  in  the  Tyne 
is  typical  of  conditions  existing  in  almost 
every  port  and  harbour,  and  one  of  its 
features  in  thus  described  : — 

"  Scsaroe  a  boune  on  the  rivyr  side  of  the  street 
but  had  ita  ruinous  whiirF,  aupixirtcd  on  haU-a- 
dozeii  green  weedgrown  piles,  or  its  tumble-down, 
ffaudy-painted  balcony."  —  G.  H.  Haswell,  *  The 
Maister  :  a  Century  of  Tyneaide  Life,'  1895,  p.  34. 
In  nautical  terms  such  a  balcony  is  called 
a  "  gallexy  "  :  for  example,  *'  Oalleryf  a 
balcony  projecting  from  the  admiral's  or 
captain's  cabin "  (Smj^h,  '  Sailor's  Word- 
Book,'  1867,  8.V.).  Outjutting  encroach- 
ments on  the  foreshore  in  constructing 
riverside  galleries,  or  balconies,  oceiu*  as 
frequent  causes  of  litigation.  Most  of  the 
pubhc-houses  on  the  shore  were  furnished 
with  an  annexe  of  tliis  kind,  a  favourite 
lounging-place,  where  frequenters  were  in 
full  \-iew  of  the  passing  river  traffic.  This 
common  adjunct  of  the  pothouse  may  well 
have  been  known  by  seafarers  as  a  *'  pot- 
gallery,"  and  its  undue  projection  would 
lonn  the  subject  of  such  injunctions  as 
those  cited  by  Da.  Murray.

Newcastle-upon-Tyne. 

The  contrivance  for  restraining  the  liberty 
of  a  river  whicli  is  known  on  the  Thames 
between  Windsor  and  Oxford  as  a  "lock" 
is  called  on  the  Itchen  a  "  pot,"  derived,  I 
suppose,  from  puUiii,  like  pozo  in  Castlhan, 
pozzo  in  Italian,  puita  in  French.  When 
the  gates  of  the  pot,  or  lock,  are  closed,  there 
is  on  the  top  of  them  a  nsmrow  bridge  of 
planks  which  gives  passage  over  the  river, 
and  it  often  has  railings.  Such  a  bridge 
miglit  possibly  be  kno^^-n  as  a  "  pot-gallery." 
E,  S.  DoDOsor. 

Db.  Mubbay  invites  explanation  or 
suggestion  as  to  this  word.  May  1  make 
my  suggestion  in  the  form  of  historical 
narration  ? 

Formerly  the  Thames  was  vnde  and 
shallow.  At  low  water  there  was  a  narrow 
channel  and  a  wide  expanse  of  uncovered 
shore.  Fishing  was  an  unportant  industry, 
both  by  nets  and  kiddles.  Fishermen  liNong 
on  the'bank  ran  a  stage  or  fence  riglit  down 
the  sloping  bank  to  near  the  navigable 
channel.  To  judge  from  the  passage  in 
Stow,  they  went,  indeed,  beyond  it.  This 
stage  the  fisherman  equipped  with  fish- 
kiddles  and  eel-pots.  To  get  at  them  at 
high  water  or  on  the  dry  he  added  a  foot- 
plank  to  his  stage,  and  thua  made  a  '*  gallery' ' 
of  it.  When  liis  eel-pots,  &c.,  were  not  va. 
us©  they  were\ha\3\ed  m?^  ••  ^xift  <ia».  ^«fe  \>B«nx

Worksop. 

The  arrangement  in  the  house  in  wMdi 
I  was  brought  up  was,  to  the  best  of  my 
recoUectiont  as  follows.  At  the  back  of  the 
fire-place,  both  in  kitchen  and  brewhoi^, 
there  was  an  upright  iron  fixture  with  ft 
swinging  arm  terminating  in  a  horizontal 
hook.  This  was  called  the  gate.  From  it 
there  depended  the  pot-hook,  a  flat  piece  of 
iron  wJuch  turned  up  hook-wise,  and  waft 
pierced  with  holes»  by  mean^  of  which  it 
could  be  hung  from  the  gate-arm  at  ftor 
desired  height.  Tlie^  hanger  was  a  mum 
piece  of  iron  hook^  at  both  ends,  and  waft 
used  to  lengthen  the  "pot-hook"  b* 
required. 

Pot-hooks  in  wTiting  are  strokes  "  hooked  " 
at  top  or  bottom  onlj',  like  the  first  stroke 
of  n  or  the  last  stroke  of  u  ;  hangers  are 
'*  hooked  "  at  both  ends,  like  the  last  stroke 
of  *(,  They  tlius  resemble  respectively  the 
pot-Jiook  and  Jianger  in  domestic  use* 

C.  C  B. 

The  pot-hook,  a  kitchen  fire  utensil,  is 
also  called  a  "  hook-and-hanger,'"  becau.* 
its  uj>per  end  hooka  on  to  the  '*  crane," 
whilst  its  lower  extremity  is  used  as  a  hanger 
for  pot  or  kettle.  It  is  a  single  object.  The 
form  of  the  heading  is  misled ing,  inasmuch 
as  it  suggests  two  different  things  :  whereas 
the  phrasal  term  merely  d&wribes  two 
functions  of  one  and  the  same  object.  Tlje 
printed  setting,  therefore,  would  be  cleaner 
if  it  were  "  pot  hook-and-hanger,"  instead 
of  **  pot-hooks  and  hangers," 

TJie  strokes  and  tuma,  in  the  Isrge-hanti 
practised  bj-  tlie  schoolboy,  have  their 
names  from  this  domestic  implement.  I'p* 
stroke  and  *'  down-turn  "  (the  first  element 
of  the  letter  n)  sliow  the  '*hook"  ;  dowB- 
stoke  and  *"  up-turn  "  (the  second  element 
of  the  letter  u)  resemble  the  "hanjeer"! 
and  **  double-turn  "  (the  last  stroke  in  th* 
letter  m)  combines  both  features  in  it^^* 
completing  its  similitudo  to  a  "hook^^od-

it  bemg  then  the  time  when  the  Civil  \^'a^ 
was  at  its  height,  Lord  Hartington  had  the 
bad  taste  to  appear  at  some  pubUc  asMembly 
at  the  national  CBpital  wearing  a  Confede-

and,  when  Lord  Hartington  was  preHcnted. 
he  feigned  not  to  have  caught  the  name,  and 
said,  in  an  inquiring  tone,  *'  All  !  Mr. 
Partington  ?  " 

I  do  not  vouch  for  the  truth  of  the  story 
— very  likely  it  is  a  fable  ;  but  that  is  the 
story.  It  has  the  advantage  over  the  other 
version  in  tl>at  it  is  witty  ;  that  it  implies, 
if  the  story  is  true,  a  weli-deserved  rebuke  ; 
and  that  it  is  characteristic  of  Lincoln; 
whereas  the  version  of  M.  N.  G.  represents^ 
nothing  but  simple  boorislmesa,  is  without 
])oint,  and  without  sufficient  verisimilitude 
ever  to  liave  became  popular. 

Isaac  Hull  Platt, 
WallinKford,  Pa. 

TJie  Marquis  of  Hartington  was  at  a 
masked  ball  in  New  York,  where  an  unknoM-n 
lady  came  up  to  him  and  pimied  a  secession 
badge  on  his  coat.  The  Marquis  did  not 
know  w^at  it  was,  and  allowed  it  to  remain  ; 
but  very  soon  a  man  told  him  roughly  to 
take  it  off.  The  Marquis  of  cout^^  paid  no 
attention,  and  a  disturbance  seemed  to  bo 
near,  when  a  gentleman  wliisjiered  to  the 
Marquis  \vhat  the  badge  was.  The  Marquis 
at  once  removed  it.  This  account  of  tho 
badge  incident  differs  from  that  quoted  from 
LoweU  at  the  last  reference  :  but  every  one 
must  believe  as  he  thinks  proper.

Musical  Genius  :    is  it  Hebeditahy  ? 

(10  S.  xi\.  170,  236.)— St.  Swithin's  refer- 
ence to  the  Scarlattis  induces  mention  of 
another  master  of  church  music,  Antonio 
Lotti,  who  was  the  son  of  a  Hanoverian 
chapel-master,  flourished  from  1667  to  174U, 
and  becanie  the  founder  of  tlie  Venetian 
school.  The  great  Braham,  "  who  sang 
like  an  angel,  came  of  a  musical  family, 
and  began  aa  a  chorister  in  the  Catherlral 
SjTiagogue  in  Duke's  Place,  handing  down 
his  gifts  to  his  daughters,  one  of  whom  was 
a  fine  actress,  and  of  such  surpassing  beauty 
that  she  attracted  the  notice  of  the  then 
Duke  of  St.  Albans,  who  fell  in  love  with 
and  married  her.  If  we  may  give  hving 
examples  of  hereditary  genius,  we  ne<*d 
only  cite  the  Harnb*.m-g  family,  Mark. 
Horis.  and  Jan,  trained  by  their  father. 
Prof.  Hambourg.

saying  is  given  in  Gardiner's  *  Cix-il  War,' 
chap.  lii.  Referring  to  a  visit  of  Belhe\Te,

•'  It  was  doubtle«s  on  this  occasion  that  BelliAvre, 
apparently  after  sounding  Cromwell  as  to  his  am- 
bitious aiiij.%  received  the  memorable  reply:  *  No 
one  rises  so  high  as  he  who  knows  not  whither  he 
is  going.' " 

In  a  note  Gardiner  gives  as  his  authority 
De  Retz  ('Meraoires.'  ed.  1859.  iii.  242), 
"  who  heard  this  from  BeUievTO  "  ;  he  also 
discusses  the  date,  and  concludes  that  the 
date  of  the  visit  is  by  far  the  most  hkelv

events  it  cannot  have  taken  place  earlier 
than  d  July  or  later  than  October  in  1647.

Heraldic  :  Cross  Clech^ie  (10  S.  v.  190  ; 
vi.  135). — Sadi  asks  to  whom  belongs  the 
arms  "  Gules,  a  cross  clech^e  or." 

Although  I  am  an  old  student  of  heraldry, 
my  later  and  more  utihtarian  hfe  has  caused 
me  to  forget  many  things  which  I  had  leamt 
in  my  earlier  days,  and  so,  as  tlie  epithet 
clechie  seemed  strange  to  me,  I  turned  to 
what  few  heraldic  authorities  I  have  with 
me  here  for  information. 

Boutell  ( 'Heraldry,  Historical  and  Popular  ' 
1864)  is  silent  as  to  its  meaning,  though 
Aveling,  in  his  treatise  founded  on  Boutell 
(pubHshed  in  1891),  gives  it  in  liis  Glossary 
(p.  126)  as  "  pierced  so  that  only  the  rim 
or  outer  edge  remains,"  as  distinguished, 
perhaps,  from  voided,  when  only  some  part 
of  the  charge  (not  practically  the  whole)  is 
removed.  * 

The  later  well-known  lieraldic  authority 
Dr.  Woodward,  however,  in  his  glossary  of 
the  French  terms  of  blazon  in  '  Heraldry, 
British  and  Foreign'  (1896),  vol.  i.  p.  462. 
seems  to  apply  tJie  term  clechie  only  to  a 
cross  "  the  arms  of  wliich  are  shaped  like 
the  handle  of  an  ancient  key.  The  cross 
of  Toulouse  is  a  cross- chchee.^'  And  on 
l)late  XV.  fig.  10  in  tlie  same  volume  ho  gives 
an  illustration  of  tliis  cross  of  Toulouse 
(St.  Gilles)  whicli  reads  *'  Gules,  a  cross 
olech^e  or  " —  the  very  arms  your  corre- 
spondent seems  to  bo  in  searcli  of. 

J.  S.  Udal,  F.S.A. 

Antigua,  W.I.

There  appeared  in  the  Weekly  Journal,  or

'' Mist's  "—for  16  May,  1719,  the  following 
paragraph  : — 

"  l^st  Week  a  Tryal  was  Lrought  at  Ouildhall, 
lrt?fnttf  the  I^rd  Chief  Justice  Pratt,  l)et ween  two 
Coinitanies  of  Cricket  I'layers,  the  Mfu  of  Kent, 
riaiiitifla,  and  the  Men  of  London,  Defendant's, 
for  Sixty  Pounds  jdayed  for  at  Cricket,  and  after  A 
long  Hearing,  and  near  2ll0/.  exi»ended  in  the  Cause, 
niy  Lord,  not  understandinn  the  Came,  ordoreti 
them  to  play  it  over  again ;  and  tliey  met 
aocoi-dinply  on  Monday  last  in  Laiul/s-Conduit- 
Fields,  but  one  of  the  Playern  Wnng  taken  ill,  it 
was  deferred  till  another  Opportunity." 

And  the  same  newspaper  of  4  July  gave  the 
following  report  of  the  conclusion  of  the 
contest : — 

"  The  great  Cricket-Match  betwixt  the  Londoners 
and  the  Kentisli  Men,  for  which  there  has  been  n. 
farnouH  Trial  at  Law ;  and  by  a  Rule  of^  Court  was 
ordered  to  be  jilay'd  out,  in  which  the  Kentish  Men 
liad  four  Men  to  play,  and  to  Lfet.'Wto  eome  up  with 
the  Londoners,  was  nlay'd  in  White-Conduit  Field, 
near  Islington ;  the  Kentish  Men  were  l^owled  out 
after  they  had  tjot  nine,  and  last  the  Match.  'Tia 
reckoned  the  Law-8uit  will  amount  to  200/,  The 
>Iatch  was  piay'd  for  a  Guinea  a  Man  on  eaoh  Side." 

The  Lord  Chief  JiLstice  of  the  Iving's 
Bench  here  referred  to  was,  by  the  way.  Sir 
John  Pratt,  and  is  not  to  be  confounded  with 
Sir  Charles  Pratt,  subsequently  Lord  Cliief 
Justice  of  the  Common  Pleas,  and  later 
Lord  Chancellor  of  Great  Britain  ;  and  it  is 
of  some  interest  that  the  present  holder  of 
tlie  position  of  the  former,  w^lio  anticipated 
the  Heathen  Chinee's  historic  remark  con- 
cerning **  a  game  he  did  not  understand/' 
is  Lord  Alverstone,  who  at  this  moment  is 
Prt^fiident  of  the  Surrey  County  Cricket  Club.

TooKE  AND  Halle V  Families. — Dr.  Ed- 
mond  Halley  is  said  to  have  married  Miss- 
Mary  Tooke,  in  the  churcli  of  St.  Marj^'s, 
Islington,  circa  Januar>',  1682.  The  bride's 
father  is  described  as  *'  Mr.  Tooke,  Auditor 
of  the  Exchequer."  but  no  person  of  that 
name  ha.*  l>een  found  in  any  printed  list  of 

•  dlioial^.  He  was  probably  descended  from 
tlie  family  of  Tooke  of  Norfolk.  Hib  widow, 
Margaret*  Tooke  (born  Kinder),  made  her 
will  as  of  London  (?  parish  of  AWL<iTvt\axw- 
bury).  la  Oct.,  \-V^V^^vix^e).\i^t.  ^>  V->^*^-

NOTES  AND  QUERIES,      [lo  &  vn.  j™  %,m. 


P.C.C,  Aston,  250),  and  bequeathed  to  her 
grandson  Edmund  Halley,  jun.  (later  suroeon 
R.N.)»  certain  lands  in  Up  well,  &c.,  which 
■are  identically  described  in  the  will  of  Mrs. 
•Catherine  Price  (see  10  S.  iii.  6).  This  con- 
clusive evidence  indicates  that  Surgeon 
Halley  did  not  leave  any  issue  surviving 
him,  and  that  his  wife  Sybilla  must  have 
been  previously  married,  for  she  in  her  will 
names  two  granddaughters  (see  ante^  p.  80). 
The  documentary  proof  of  relationship 
between  the  Halley  and  Pyke  families  is, 
therefore,  at  present,  confined  to  the  will  of 
Francis  Halley  (see  anU,  p.  263).  These 
new  data  have  been  supplied  by  Mr.  R.  J. 
Beevor.  Euoexe  F.  McPike.

"  Breese  "  IN  *  HuDiBBAS.' — There  is  a 
curious  blunder  as  to  the  meaning  of  tliis 
word   in  the   edition  of   *  Hudibras '   pub- 
lished in  1732,  with  woodcuts  by  Hogarth. 
Part  III.  canto  ii.  opens  with  the  lines  : — 
The  Learned  write.  An  InMect  Brenne 
Ih  but  the  nuiiigrel  Prinoe  of  Bee8, 
That  falls  l>efore  a  Stomi,  on  Cowr, 
And  fltingH  thu  FuunderH  of  Iiis  House. 
Breese   here   is   so   ob\'iously   the   gadfly 
{A.-S.  britnsa)  that  it  is  difficult  to  see  how 
it  could  be  misunderstood.     Yet  the  "  anno- 
tation "  supplied  in  this  edition,  p.  290,  is 
as  follows  : — 

•*  An  Iiistct  linjif  :  Breezes  of  ton  bring  along  with 
tlieni  great  C^nantitieH  of  InNeets,  whicli  some  are 
of  Opinion,  are  generated  from  viscous  Exhalations 
in  the  Air." 

This  note  is  roi)roduc'od  in  Zacliarj-  Grey's 
edition,  17-14  (rei)rint,  I860,  p.  248),  witli 
tl»e  additional  comment :  '*  See  an  account 
of  blasts.  Lord  Bacon's  '  Natural  History,' 
<>ent.  vii.  §  696.  p.  143  "  !  The  word  must 
have  been  then  quite  obsolete,  one  would 
imagine,  to  be  tlius  mistaken  for  "  breeze," 
a  current  of  air.  A.  Smythe  Palmer.

KiKKSTEAD  Chapel,  Lincs. — A  few  miles 
from  Woodlmll  Spa,  on  the  east  coast  of 
Lincolnshire,  is  a  beautiful  little  Early 
English  chai)cl,  formerly  tJie  parisli  clmrch 
of  Kirkst«ad,  but  now  disusecl.  Tlie  build- 
ing adjoins  tlie  site  of  Kirkst(>ad  Abbey,  but, 
to  judge  from  the  block  i>lan  of  tlieicono- 
grajiJiy  of  tlie  abbey  in  Stukeley's  '  Itine- 
rarium  Curiosum,'  it  did  not  form  part  of  the 
monastery,  but  was  probably  formerly  a 
wayside  cliapel. 

Tlie  recent  history  of  the  chapel  is  curious, 
and  is  tlius  d(?scribed  by  Allen  : —

"  LvLVG  Bishop.** — This  name  is  given  to

been  told  tliat  a  milestone  is  said  to  be 
'*  bishojjed  "'  if  corrected  in  tliia  manner.

tion of  the  name?  In  the  *N.E.D.  '*  to 
btsliop  "  seems  to  be  applied  only  to  liorse*.

Buhton's  *  Scented  Gabden.' — In  the 
late  Lady  Burton's  '  Life  *  of  her  husband 
there  is  a  defence  by  her  of  her  action  in 
destroJ^ng  the  MS.  of  '  The  Scented  Garden.' 
on  wliich  her  husband  was  engaged,  she 
states,  up  to  the  time  of  his  death.  Lady 
Burton  also  says  that  the  MS.  was  unlinished, 
and  that  tliere  is  no  authentic  copy  in 
existence.  Now,  in  the  list  of  works  by 
Burton  appended  to  the  hfe  of  Burton

oedinK  day,  in  the  Slnt  year  of  her  age.  Mm.  Elisa- 
beth Best,  widow  and  reliot  of  the  Rev.  Edward 
Best,  olerk,  heretofore  Vicar  of  Wednesbury, 
mother  of  Edward  Best^  clerk,  the  present  Vicar 
of  Wednesbury  and  Minister  of  Bilston.  She  was 
the  daughter  of  John  Jevon,  heretofore  of  Tipton 
iii  the  Uounty  of  Ktaflbrd,  gent.,  by  Mary  his  wife, 
the  daughter  of  John  Harris,  heretofore  of  Brad- 
ford in  the  County  of  Worcester,  gent.  She  was 
interred  on  the  12th  inst.  near  the  remains  of  her 
late  husband  in  a  vault  in  the  South  Clianoel  of 
Wednesbury  Church.  Her  said  late  husband  was 
the  son  of  the  Rev.  Edward  Best,  clerk,  hereto- 
fore Rector  of  Elmley  Lovett  in  the  County  of 
Worcester,  and  descended  by  a  regular  and  con- 
tinued succession  of  Clergymen  from  the  Right 
Revd.  John  Best,  who  was  consecrated  Bishoj)  of 
Carlisle  ujton  the  restoration  of  the  Protestant 
Religion  in  the  beginning  of  the  reign  of  Queen 
Elizabeth." 

Edward  Best,  the  husband  of  Elizabeth 
Jevon,  was  buried  12  Dec.,  1718.  He  was 
the  son  of  Edward  Best,  rector  of  Elmley 
Lovett,   who   died   17   March,    1707 ;     and 

grandson  of  Edward  Best,  rector  of  Elmley 
ovett,  who  was  bom  circa  1580,  and  di^ 
26  Jan.,  1662,  a^  eighty-two.  The  pedi- 
gree of  this  family  is  given  in  MisceUanea 
Genealogica  et  Heraldicay  New  Series,  vol.  iv. 
p.  234. 

I  should  be  glad  to  know  liow  this  Edward 
Best  (1S80  to  1662)  was  related  to  Bisliop 
John  Best  of  Cariisle  (who  died  in  1570). 
If  he  were  really  a  descendant  of  tlie  Bishop, 
as  stated  in  the  register  of  Bilston,  lie  must 
liave  been  his  grandson.

Kichard  Ames's  incumbency  are  full  of  most 
interesting  entries,  such  as  the  one  given 
above,  and  deserve  to  be  printed  by  the 
Parisli  Register  Society  or  the  Staffordshire 
Parish  Register  Society.

SuupHUB  Matches  :  Match  -  Makeb's 
SoKO  (10  S.  vii.  348,  396).— I  well  remember 
an  old  Brighton  character,  wiiose  portrait 
is  in  the  Pavilion  and  one  in  my  possession, 
whose  song  was 

Come,  buy  my  good  matohe«:  oome,  buy  them  uf  me ; 
Thev  are  the  liest  matohes  Inat  ever  you  i^.*e. 
For  lij^hting  your  candle,  or  kindling  your  fire, 
They  are  the  best  matches  that  you  can  desire. 

There  wa8  an  old  wonian  in  Rosemary  l-j<i.ne; 
She  cuti*  "em  and  dips  cm,  and  I  do  tne  Hame. 
For  linhtinK  your  candle,  Ac. 

He  also  sold  pennv  almanacs,  and  eaug 
"  Here's  yer  new  book  almanac  for  the  Kew 
Year :  only  one  penny."  But  in  time  the 
almanac  doubled  in  price,  and  then  his  lay 
was  altered  to  '*  Here's  yer  new  book 
almanac  for  the  New  Year :  only  one 
tupijonee." 

The  uno  of  flint,  steel  and  tinder  was 
universal  in  those  days,  R.  P.  H. 

I,  too,  well  remember  seeing  matclies  of 
tlie  kind  described  by  J.  T.  F.  (unsplit  at 
one  end),  and  am  .surprised  that  nobody 
has  referred  to  them  before  in  this  corre- 
spondence. I  do  not  remember  them  in 
use,  but  we  had  some  in  tJie  house  m  the 
early  fifties  of  laet  century.  Mr.  Peacock's 
amusing  anecdote  reminds  one  of  Haw- 
thorne's note  :  '*  Brimstone  and  wood  ;  a 
scold  and  a  blockhead  :   a  good  match  I  ^'  ^ 

C.  C.  B. 

"  Matches  "  ts  Congre\*e  (10  S.  vii. 
269,  351,  397).— A  small  bundle  of  the  old 
"  ttinder-box  *'  matches  which  I  have  are 
tipped  at  both  ends  with  sulphur.  Tliey 
are  those  mentioned  by  J.  T.  F.,  and  both 
ends  are  for  *'  business."  The  first  matches 
I  remember  used  were  **  Congreves,"  and 
they  were  thrice  as  thick,  and  twice  as  long, 
as  modern  wooden  matches.  The  big  red 
tips  were  the  dehght  of  boys,  for  a  match 
screwed  into  a  shght  hollow  in  a  atone  wall 
would  emit  from  half  a  dozen  to  a  dozen 
loud  cracks,  besides  sparks,  before  the 
"  brimstone  '  was  spent.  The  sparks  sent 
out  were  capable  of  biuTiing  the  finger* 
badly,  and  that,  jierhaps,  was  why  **  lucifer  " 
became  one  of  the  matcli-names,  I  remember 
three  early  kinds  of  match-boxes:  one 
made  oval  in  sliape  with  a  lid.  another 
round  with  lid  and  of  stronger  wood,  and 
the  big  slide  box — all  to  hold  the  big  matches. 

ThOS.    liATCLirFE. 

The  matclies  mentioned  in  Congreve  were 
undoubtedly  the  old-fa»ihioned  brimstone 
matches  w'hich  always  accompanied  the 
tinder-box  with  its  flint  and  steel,  because 
it  was  imposavbVe,  ot  eX  \««tsX  Ners  <ibSftR.\iiX> 


452 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES.       [w  ».  va  J^Btl^ 


to  light  a  tire  or  a  candle  directly  by  the 
ignited  tinder,  which  usually  consisted  of 
charred  linen  or  cotton  rags,  and  was  kept 
in  a  tin  box  provided  with  »  closely  Htting 
cover.     The  latter  was  the  tinder-box. 

In  early  times  the  matches  aeeni  to  have 
been  coarse  strings  of  tow,  hemp,  cotton,  or 
some  such  material,  dipped  in  melted 
sulphur ;  but  all  tliese  gave  way  to  the 
simple  wooden  splint,  one  end  of  which 
was  cut  to  a  fine  point,  while  the  other  was 
left  thick,  and  both  ends  were  coated  with 
sulphur  or  brimstone.  The  word  *'  match  *' 
i3  derived  from  an  old  word  signifying  the 
wick  or  snuflE  of  a  candle,  but,  like  many 
other  technical  terms,  it  has  changed  its 
meaning.  The  first  matches,  however,  were 
probably  made  of  candle- wicking. 

The  matches  made  of  wood,  the  ends 
bein^  coated  with  sulphur,  were  in  general 
use  in  ray  boyhood  (seventy  years  ago), 
and  I  have  mode  hundreds  of  them,  perhaps 
tliQusaads.  They  were  the  only  kind  in 
common  use.  and  most  families  made  their 
o\ni,  the  job  being  generally  assigned  to 
the  boys.  The  pointed  end  was  used  for 
getting  a  light  from  ignited  tinder,  which 
would  not  set  fire  to  any  considerable  mass 
eitlior  of  sulphur  or  any  other  material: 
the  thick  end  was  used  for  obtaining  a  light 
from  burning  coals  which  it  might  be 
dilficult  to  fan  into  a  flame. 

The  process  of  lighting  a  candle  was  as 
follows.  By  striking  a  piece  of  properly 
tempered  steel  witli  a  sharp-edged  flint, 
giving  the  latter  a  scraping  motion,  a  shower 
of  sparks  was  thrown  down  on  the  tinder 
and  set  it  on  fire :  then  it  was  easy  to  li^t 
the  sharp  end  of  tlie  brimstone  match, 
and  with  this  to  liglit  the  candle.  In 
dexterous  hands,  with  good  flint,  steel,  and 
tinder,  the  process  did  not  take  long  j  I 
have  lighte<i  a  candle  in  tliis  way  in  forty 
seconds. 

Gulliver  in  his  *  Vovage  to  Laputa  '  tells 
us  that  when  he  reached  one  of  the  desert 
isles  he  had  "about  him  his  flint,  steel, 
match,  and  burning-glass. "  The  match 
hero  mentioned  may  have  been  rags,  pai>er, 
or  candle-wicking  'soaked  in  nitre.  This 
was  wT-itten  prior  to  1727  (tlie  date  of 
publication),  and  long  before  friction  or, 
as  they  are  sometimes  called,  lucifer  matches 
Jiad  been  thought  of.  Phosphorus  had 
been  discovered  in  1009,  and  had  been  used 
in  connexion  with  the  old  brimstone  match  ; 
but  in  this  case  it  served  merely  as  a  sub- 
stitute for  the  flint,  steel,  and  tinder. 

Johx^Phin. 
Paterson,  N.J.

Seine,  River  and  Saint  (10  8.  vii.  348). 
— There  is  no  connexion  whatsoever  between 
the  two,  beyond  tliat  of  locality.  The 
source  of  the  Seine  is  near  Chanceaux, 
several  miles  from  St.  Seine,  and  in  another 
township,  altogether.  St.  Seine,  the  town, 
takes  \t-%  name  from  St.  Sejanus,  and  is 
always  spoken  of  as  St.  Seine-l'Abbaye. 
Not  only  is  it  in  another  township,  but  on 
the  other  side  of  the  waterslied,  its  medicinal 
.sj (Tings  feeding  the  Tille,  a  tributary  of  the 
Saone.  The  water  of  tJie  source  of  the 
Seine  finds  its  way  in  wet  weatlier  to 
the  Channel;  tJiat  *  of  St.  Seine  to  the 
Mediterranean  —  in  wet  weatlier  only,  for 
in  the  siunmer  lieats  tJie  true  source  of 
the  Seine  is  at  Chatillon-sur-Seine.  many 
miles  below.  The  Municipality  of  Paris, 
in  the  early  days  of  last  century,  had  the 
Seine  traced  to  its  source,  and  erected  an 
allegorical  figure  there,  known  to  the  whole 
country-side  as  '*  la  Dame  au  Bain  de 
Pieds.''  When  the  foundations  were  being 
dug.  it  was  found  that  the  Romans  had 
been  there  already,  and  had  done  something 
of  the  same  kind.  At  St.  Seine-rAbbaye 
the  beautiful  cliureh  is  still  in  good  condition. 
H.  Hallidav  Sparling. 

1.1,  Villa  Dav'oust,  Asniere**  (Seine). 

The  town  of  St.  Seine-l'Abbaye  is  four  or 
five  miles  from  the  source  of  the  river. 
The  abbey  was  founded  by  St.  Seine,  who 
was  *'  the"  son  of  a  pagua  of  M^mont,"  and 
lived  in  the  sixth  centur>'.  One  of  the 
legends  of  tJie  saint  is  that  he  walked 
across  France,  and,  wherever  he  was  well 
received,  lie  asked  his  hosts  what  they 
would  like  in  return,  and  the  general  request 
was  for  drinking  water,  for  there  were  no 
streams  and  few  wells  in  that  i>art  of  the 
country.  On  his  return  to  the  abbey  he 
struck' the  rook,  and  sent  forth  the  river» 
giving  it  instructions  that  it  was  to  flow 
by  the  cottages  of  those  wiio  had  entertained 
him,  and  dodge  the  habitations  of  the  in- 
hospitable. This  accounts  for  tlie  verj- 
serpentine  course  taken  by  the  river.  In- 
cidents in  the  saint's  career  are  re]ire9ented 
'  in  a  series  of  fifteentli-centurj'  paintings  on 
'  the  back  of  tlie  chancel  screen  in  the  present 
1  abbey,  which  is  a  very  ^iv<i  «»Vi   ^vax<5«ev —

I  do  not  think  tlie  name  George  is  so  un- 
con^mon  before  1700  as  some  of  your  corre- 
sijondents  assume.  Looking  through  some 
Lancashire  pedigrees  and  lists  in  *  Gregaon'a 
Portfolio.'  I  find  the  following  early  instances: 

Stanley  family. — George,'  Lord  Strange, 
son  of  the  second  Lord  Stanley,  died  1497. 
Sir  George  Stanley,  Kt.,  of  Crosshall,  Lanes, 
son  of  Sir  James  Stanley,  died  1570. 

Lydiates  of  Lydiate. — In  the  pedigree  is 
given  the  achievement  of  CJeorge  Ireland, 
born  14(37. 

Masey  or  Massey  of  Rixton. — George 
Mass'*y  had  grant  of  lands  in  Cheshire  for 
life  from  his  father,  9  Jan.,  1436. 

Grrell  of  Orrell. — George  Orrell,  living  in 
15i»6. 

Longworth  of  Longworth. — George,  son 
and  heir  of  Thomajs  Longworth,  who  was 
twenty-one  in  1448-  Anoth  t  George  Long- 
worth,  living  in  15<n,  married  Margaret 
Trafford,  of  co.  Chester. 

Pilkington  pedigree. — A  George  is  men- 
tioned in  a  Rivington  deed  in  1478.  though 
it  is  not  known  if  he  was  a  Pilkington. 

In  the  musters  of  soldiers  in  the  county 
of  Lancaster  in  1553  and  li>74  the  name 
George  occurs  18  times,        A.  H.  Arkle.

de  Foulis.*'     Alexander  11.  reigned  1214-49.

"The  Old  Highlander"  (10  S.  vii.  47» 
92,  115,  137). — A  Highlander  of  oak  stands 
in  the  doorway  of  one  of  the  shops  in  St. 
Heliers,  Jersey,  It  is  stated  to  be  a  magni- 
ficent specimen  of  a  full-sized  42nd  Black 
Watch  Hi^ljJander.  Tradition  has  it  that 
it  was  originally  the  figurehead  of  a  man-o'- 
war  wrecked  off  the  island  of  Alderney. 
It  has  been  in  the  possession  of  a  stories  of 
tobacconists  for  seventy  odd  years,  and  is 
now,  or  was  until  recently,  OHTied  by  Mr. 
J.  F.  Belford,  of  St.  Helicrs.  An  illustra- 
tion of  this  "  dummy  Highlander  **  appeared 
in  The  Daily  Graphic  of  22  Sept.,  1905. 

On  31  Dec  1720,  was  oi>ened  a  shop  at 
20,  Coventry  Street,  London,  at  the  sign  of 
''  The  Highlander,  Thistle,  and  Crown,  by 
David  Wishart.  This  shop  was  a  favourite 
resort  of  the  Jacobites,  and  is  thought  to  be 
the  first  whereat  a  figure  of  a  Higlilander  waa 
placed.  The  figures  have  now  become  rare, 
but  there  was  a  very  fijie  example  in  Bridge 
Street,  Westminster,  in  1899,  See  Tm 
Builder^  22  July  of  that  year. 

The  Tottenham  Coiu*t  Road  figure  was 
illustrated  in  The-  Daily  Graphic  of  8  Sept., 
1905,  and  in  Lloyd's  Weekly  News  of  li  Jan. 
layt.  Chas.  Hall  Cbodch.

would  point  out  that  they  are  at  all  times  prepared  to  buy  for  cash 
rge  or  small  collections  of  Books  {as  well  as  Prints  and  Autograph 
SS.),  or  to  value  the  same  for  Probate,  or  for  Fire  Insurance  if,  as  is 
]en  the  case,  the  Library  has  not  yet  been  insured. 

It  often  happens  that  in  old  country  houses  there  are  Books  which 
ve  lain  for  years  unused  and  unappreciated,  but  which  might  yet  be — 
nn  a  commercial  point  of  view — of  value  and  importance, 

Messrs.  Sotheran  are  ready  to  send  experienced  buyers  to  all  parts 
the  country  to  view  such  Books,  and  to  give  the  best  advice  respecting 
em;  and  any  purchases  they  might  make  would  be  removed  without  any 
mile  or  expense  to  the  sellers. 

They  will  also  be  glad  to  be  consulted  with  regard  to  the  re-binding  or 
pairing  of  such  Books  in  the  Library  as  may  now  be  in  a  dilapidated 
Ue  through  age,  and  to  furnish  estimates  for  such  work.  Indeed  in 
nnexion  with  all  matters  pertaining  to  a  Private  Library  they  will  feel 
a  privilege  to  be  referred  to,

Coming  to  the  question  of  the  Strawberry 
Hill  sale  catalogue,  your  contributor  writes : 
"  Of  the  Catalogue  itself  there  are  two 
editions."  He  states  that  the  first  edition 
contained  so  many  blunders  that  it  was 
decided  to  cancel  the  portion  of  the  iraprea- 
Rion  which  had  not  been  distributed  and  to 
i-mploy  aJi  expert  to  recataloguo  :  also  that

while  in  the  corrected  reissue,  which  "happens 
to  extend  to  exactly  the  same  number  of

days'  sale  is  oniitted,  the  papi\&tvo\vT>M«ck.\\^

John  Russell  lived  for  some  time  in 
Windsor  View,  off  Lodge  Lane,  a  spot  in 
those  daj's  quite  out  of  the  town.  He  and 
liis  family  are  well  ren^embered  by  relatives 
of  my  ©u-n  who  lived  in  Lodge  Lane  over 
sixty  years  ago.  In  1849  he  was  living  at 
Manor  View,  Claughton.  on  the  Cheshire 
side  of  the  Mersey,  and  the  inscription  above 
suggests  that  he  liad  removed  there  in 
1847  or  earlier.  He  had  a  third  son,  Fred 
Russell,  born  about  1827,  remembered  as  an 
expert  tlirower  of  stones,  who  died  while 
at  school  in  Germany- 

The  beginnings  oi  men  who  achieve 
eminence  are  so  often  misrepresented  that 
it  is  well  to  demonstrate  Sir  William  Howard 
Russell's  very  respectable  middle-class  deri- 
vation. The  biographers  of  the  future  may 
thuH  know  that  the  most  distinguished  of 
British  war  correspondents  neither  was 
born  to  the  advantages  of  wealth  and 
pcxsition,  nor  had  to  combat  the  disad- 
vantages of  poverty  and  lowly  birth.

The  Keeper  of  New*:; ate. — In  none  of 
the  standard  works  upon  the  recenth 
demolished  Newgate  Prison  is  there  a  com- 
plete list  of  its  Governors  or  Keepers. 
During  the  greater  part  of  the  eighteenth 
century  the  two  Akermans — father  and  son 
— held*  the  office.  According  to  Ivnapp  and 
Baldwin's  '  Newgate  Calendar  '  (1824).  vol.  i. 
p.  88t  the  elder  Akerraan  occupied  the 
position  in  1729,  and  he  was  succeeded  by 
his  son,  who  remained  the  Governor  of  the 
prison  until  liis  death  on  19  Nov.,  1792 
{Oe^H,  Mag.,  vol.  Isdi.  pt.  ii.  pp.  1062.  1150). 
This  is  the  Mr,  Akerman  whose  praises  are 
clu-onicled  by  Boswell,  and  whose  kindness 
and  humanity  to  the  prisoners  imder  his 
care  are  often  spoken  of  in  the  UtevoA-va^  <i^ 
crime. 


466 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES,     [lo  &  vn.  jfne  15,  m 


The  next  Keeper  of  Newgate  wa«  John 
Kirby,  who  held  the  office  until  his  death 
on  30  Aug.,  1804.  A  Mr.  Newman  was 
Governor  of  the  prison  in  1814;  and  he 
was  succeeded,  about  1817,  by  a  Mr.  Brown. 
John  Wontner,  a  former  Marshal  of  the 
City  of  London,  who  lost  a  leg  in  a  horse 
accident  on  16  Nov.,  1821,  was  the  successor 
of  Mr.  Brown,  and  remained  Governor  for 
eleven  years,  until  his  death  on  7  Nov.,  1833 
{Qent.  Mag.,  vol.  ciii.  pt.  ii.  p.  476). 

A  full  list  of  these  functionaries  would 
no  doubt  prove  of  value  to  students,  and 
along  with  the  catalogue  of  the  Kings  of 
Bath  and  the  Ordinaries  of  Newcate,  which 
have  been  given  previously  in  N.  &  Q.,' 
might  well  be  added  to  future  editions  of 
•  Haydn's  Book  of  Dignities.*

'  Cartularium  Saxonicu.m.'  (See  10  S. 
vii.  185,  287.) — Prof.  Skeat's  note  is  very 
welcome.  The  projHT  way  to  deal  with 
place-names  is  to  trace  them  historically. 
I  found  that  tlie  '  Cartularium  '  had  abun- 
dant material  for  a  basis  of  work,  and  pre- 
sently set  about  a  topographical  index  to 
tlje  entire  collection.  About  half  of  the 
charters  are  done,  and  the  hopes  of  its  being 
completed  are  much  raised  by  the  opinion 
of  Prof.  Skeat  that  the  thing  will  be  a 
public  service.  I  have  occasionally  glanced 
at  Kemble's  index  to  the  '  C.  D.,'  and  with 
unsatisfactory  results ;  but  in  the  end  I 
shall  perhaps  collate  my  work  with  his 
before  considering  it  fit  for  imblication. 
Thorpe' 8   index    to   liis   *  DiplomaTiunv '    is 


worse  than  uselese,  being  full  of  mere  ety- 
mological speculations.  The  work  is  vsj 
arduous — much  more  so  than  might  hne 
been  expected — and  requires  what  m«]>b« 
called  a  topograi>hical  torn  of  mind  nte 
than  a  disp»oeition  for  speculative  (t^ 
mologies.  With  reference  to  Pagham,  I W 
not  give  the  county  in  such  case  becasse 
of  an  assumption  that  the  student  vooU 
see  from  the  context  to  what  coonty  it 
belongs. 

Here  are  three  further  interesting  item*: 

340.  Dunhamstyde  is  probably  Dunham- 
stead,  near  Oddingley,  Worcestershire,  and 
certainly  not  *'  Hempstead  in  Gloucester." 

477.  Wenbeorh,  Wenbeorgen,  has  nothint 
to  do  with  Hinton  Ampner  ;  it  is  now  callea 
Wanborough,  but  according  to  the  charttf 
was  at  one  time  Hvnyton.  The  adjacnt 
village  of  Little  Hinton  presupposes  a 
greater  Hinton,  which  is  the  place  in  question. 

628  (also  290,  312,  409).  Dommuc  is  no! 
Dimwich.  The  seat  of  the  bishops  of  Eat* 
Anglia  was  at  Dommuc  or  Dommoc,  a  town 
near  or  on  the  site  of  Felixstow  that  has  beea 
washed  away  by  the  sea  (9  S.  x.  312).

Chantries  and  Chiirch  Stores. — In 
preparing  for  publication  some  old  church- 
wardens' accounts  I  meet  with  allusions  to 
r re- Reformation  usages  of  which  I  feel  that 
ought  to  obtain  a  fuller  understanding 
before  completing  my  remarks  thereon.  To 
this  end  I  venture  to  solicit  authoritative 
answers  to  any  of  the  following  questions. 

In  many  churches  we  find  that  there 
existed  an  altar  at  the  east  end  of  each  aisle. 
Does  the  erection  of  a  side  altar  imply  the 
endow^ment  in  perpetuity  of  a  prie«t  distinct 
from  the  incumbent  of  the  parish  1  or  might 
an  altar  be  installed  without  any  such 
provision  for  it«  .services,  the  expense  being 
met  by  single  or  combined  voluntary  effort 
on  the  part  of  parishioners  ? 

Bequests  for  the  "  foundation  of 
chantry  "  {a  very  loosely  ajiplied  term)  ot, 
"for  the  maintenance  of  a  chaplain"  are 
frequently  so  meagre  as  to  have  been  quite 
inadequate  to  the  entire  subsistence  of  a 
man  for  the  space  of  time  covered  by  them. 
This  and  the  fact  that  they  were  often  only 
for  a  few  anniversary  obit«  or  a  few  "  month  s 
minds  "  imply  that  ( 1 )  a  priest  and  (2)  an 
altAr,  not  already  monopolized  or  fully 
engaged,  were  available.  Would  an  altar 
or  a  priest  endowed  by  the  founder  of  a 
church,  or  by  a  lay  patron,  or  by  a  guild 
(let  us  say  for  the  celebration  of  a  daily

I  have  read  that  by  Canon  Law  it  was  not 
lawful  at  the  same  altar  to  say  two  masses 
on  the  same  day  (Rev.  O.  T.  Reichel,  *  On 
Canon  Law/  vol.  i.  p.  98) ;  also  that  chantry 
priests  (who  in  all  cases  were  bound  to  be 
at  the  vicar's  conmiand  on  **  high "  or 
*'  double "  feasts)  might  assist  in  solenm 
masses  in  the  capacity  of  deacon  or  sub- 
deacon»  but  must  not  receive  the  Eucharist 
more  than  once  in  the  same  day  (*  Gent. 
Mag.  Lib.  of  Ecclesiology/  p.  69). 

How  can  these  two  prohibitions  be  recon- 
ciled with  the  above  considerations,  and  with 
the  fact  that  in  some  chiut;hes  the  number 
of  chantry  foundations  was  greatly  in  excess 
of  the  nimiber  of  altars,  e.g.,  in  the  old  church 
of  St.  Paul,  47  chantries,  14  altars  (Fuller, 
*  Ch.  Hist.,'  p.  360)  ? 

Might  the  incumbent  of  the  parish  say 
**  private  "  masses  (by  which  I  mean  masses 
for  private  persons)  by  bequest  or  scUeuy  1 
and,  if  so,  might  he  say  them  at  the  high 
altar,  or  must  a  side  altar  alone  be  used  for 
such  a  purpose  ? 

Does  the  reference  to  several  **  stores " 
in  the  parish  accounts,  such  as  the  **  Store 
of  St.  Katherine,"  the  '*  Store  of  St.  George," 
&c.,  amounting  perhaps  to  seven  or  nine 
(each  with  its  pair  of  "  wardens  "  accounting 
to  the  head  warden),  imply  the  existence  of 
as  many  local  gilds  ?  or  might  several  such 
stores  be  supported  by  one  gild,  or  merely 
by  casual  contributions  ? 

What  were  tlie  practical  manifestations  of 
devotion  to  tlie  patron  saint  of  a  gild,  short 
of  founding  a  chantry  altar  to  his  or  her 
honour,  or  of  paying  a  chantry  clerk  to  say 
a  mass  on  that  saint's  feast  day  ?  Was  there 
necessarily,  in  the  clmrch,  an  effigy  or  picture 
of  each  of  the  saints  whose  names  were 
borne  by  the  several  stores  ?  and  did  members 
of  the  gilds  maintaining  such  stores  sit  near 
the  images  of  their  titular  saints  at  church 
services  ? 

In  the  case  of  a  chapel  being  a  distinct 
building,  standing  in  a  "  borough  "  half  a 
mile  from  the  church,  might  this  be  pro- 
j:»erly  described  as — at  one  and  the  same 
time — a  "  chantry  chapel  "  and  a  "  chapel  of 
ease  "  ? 

Of  what  nature,  or  on  what  grounds  (in 
cither  or  both  the  above-named  capacities), 
were  payments  received  from  its  wardens 
by  the  head  warden  of  the  parish  church 
(both  before  and  after  the  Keformation)  ? 
Could  it  have  belonged  ab  ori^ino,  as  it 
appears  to  have  done  in  modern  days,  to 
the  borough  corporation  ?  It  is  not  named 
in  the  official  list  of  suppressed  chantries, 
1549,     Could  it,  if  a  chantry  foundation, 


have  been  exempted  under  clause  xv.  ol 
the  Act  of  1  Ed.  Vl.,  w^xiv.  t

beneficed  with  cure  of  souls,  may  takeaf

Sir  Robert  W*alpole's  remark  to  Mr. 
Leveaon,  as  quoted  by  Mr.  Latham,  was 
recorded  by  Dr.  King,  who  adds  that  Sir 
Robert  lived  long  enough  to  know  that  ray 
Lord  Gower  had  his  price  as  well  as  the  rest 
(King's  '  Anecdotes/  p.  44). 

The  saying  was  quoted  in  a  different  fonii 
by  Richardson  the  painter,  namely,  "  There 
was  not  one,   patriot  howsoever  he  might 
seem,  of  whom  he  did  not  know  the  price." 
(*  Richardsoniana,'  1776,  p.  178).

'*  RAJiftAMMY  **  (10  S.  vii.  407).— It  is 
curious  tliat  this  word  slioald  h«ve  aoqaififd 
Ll>e  sense  of  a  dnaaktti  spree.  Of  conMtm  tt 
ta  very  well  known  as  a  slang  term  appUed 
by  Europeans  to  Hindus,  much  as  wa  eisll  a 
ScotcJiman  Sandy  or  Savmey.     tt  is  derived 


474 


NOTES  AND  QUERIES,     ao  8.  vn.  Ju«  w,  m 


from  the  common  Hindu  personal  name 
Kamsammy,  more  correctly  Ramaswamy» 
**  devotee  of  the  god  Rama."  There  are 
other  names  of  the  same  termination^  such 
as  Krishnaswamy,  "  devotee  of  the  god 
Krishna.*'  Indian  Mohammedans  do  not 
use  these  names,  but  have  a  similar  class 
formed  with  the  prefix  **  Ghulam."  Among 
my  correspondents  I  count  a  Ghulam 
Kasull  ("servant  of  the  Prophet")  and 
a  Ghulam  Mohi-ud-din  ("  servant  of  the 
saint  Mohi-ud-din"). 

Jas.  Platt,  Jun.

*'  I  'm  purty  necu:  mazed,  vur  thews  yer

"  O  DEAR,  WHAT  CAN  THE  MATTER  BE  ?  " 

(10  S.  vi.  29,  57,  73,  92,  116,  152,  198,  464, 
.•ilS  ;  vii.  255,  -315.) — It  may  interest  Mr. 
Page  to  learn  that  the  above  line  was 
parodied  much  earlier  than  W.  B.  H.  inti- 
mates, viz.,  '  Pa  Mulligan's  Courtship,'  a 
rollicking  song  which  was  issued  in  a  series 
of  similar  things  between  1832  and  1847. 
They  were  jmblished  as  a  collection  in  2  vols, 
at  Glasgow  in  1853.  A  new  edition  was 
afterwards  published  in  the  same  place  in 
1878,  with  biographical  sketches. 

The  .song  appears  on  pp.  212-13  of  vol.  i. 
of  this  issue,  as  follows  : — 

O  <lear  !  dear  !  what  can  the  matter  he  ? 

Och,  lK)t))eration  now  !  what  can  the  matter  be  ? 

Thunder  and  turf  !  why,  what  can  tlie  matter  be  ? 
How  Cui>id  my  jKwr  heart  doth  flail. 

Then  tlear,  &c 

Pewter  and  i>ots, 

Cupid 

Well !  well  I  now  nought  can  tiie  matter  Ih)  ! 

Honey  and  sugar  now, 

I'igH  and  iiraties  since 

Paddy  no  longer  need  wail.

^'iven.  K.  Simms.

Tb  trace  the  various  occupants  of  GiUitig  Oastle 
down  to  the  heginnJi^t  of  the  nineteenth  centuiy 
wookl  occupy  more  qiace  than  we  can  afford.  The 
castle,  however,  claims  attention  as  a  record  of  past 
times,  althou^  nothing  remainB  above  ground  of 
an  earlier  date  than  the  second  half  of  the  four* 
teantii  century.  Itislikeaped-tower.bntof  much 
larger  dimensions  than  any  other  existing  example. 
Many  additions  have  been  made  from  Tudor  times 
down  to  almost  the  present  day,  but  its  outward 
appearance  has  been  little  damaoed.  The  great 
chamber,  as  it  was  called,  now  tiie  £ning<room,  was 
built  by  Sir  William  Fairfax,  and  completed  in 
166&  It  is  probaUe  that  there  is  no  finer  example 
of  a  state  apurtment  which  has  survived  the  de- 
struction of  three  centuries.  The  sight  of  the 
painted  glass  alone  is  worth  a  Ions  pilgrimaKe.  It 
18  not  only  very  fine  as  to  form  and  oolour,  but  also 
must  have  jfreat  interest  for  every  student  of  the 
heraldry  and  genealosy  of  Yorkshire,  as  it  is  nearly 
all  devoted  to  heralaio  display.  The  shields  with 
their  acoomi>anying  insorijitions  are  treated  of  at 
length  by  the  writer,  who  also  ^ves  an  account  of 
the  elaborate  i  tainted  frieze  which  runs  round  the 
room,  setting  forth  the  arms  of  the  {(entry  of  York- 
shire with  whom  Sir  William  Fairiax  was  con- 
temporary. This  was  always  a  very  uncommon 
form  of  ornament,  but  at  one  time  there  were 
parallels  to  be  found,  though  we  are  not  aware  that 
there  are  any  others  now  in  existence. 

Mr.  F.  Royston  Fairbank's  account  of  *  The  Last 
Earl  of  Warren  and  Surre:^and  the  Distribution 
of  his  Poesessioiis'  is  a  highly  imixtrtant  essay 
alike  for  tbe  historian  and  the  local  antiauary. 
Much  of  the  information  the  author  has  oolleoteil 
has  remained  unknown  until  now.  The  details  of 
the  long  struggle  regarding  the  divorce  which  the 
Earl  endeavoured  to  force  the  authorities,  eccle- 
siastical and  lay,  to  provide  for  him  will  be  new  to 
nearly  every  one.  They  are  highly  curious  when 
studied  in  the  light  of  the  canon  law.  Mr.  Fair- 
bank  takes  a  more  lenient  view  of  the  Earl's 
character  than  we  can  consent  to  do.  '*  He  was 
T)robably,"  we  are  told,  "  not  one  whit  worse  than 
the  great  majority  in  his  own  station."  This  is 
surely  a  view  which  is  not  borne  out  by  what  we 
know  of  the  lives  of  his  contemporaries.  William 
de  Anne,  a  Y<Mrk8hire  squire,  is  twice  mentioned  by 
Mr.  Fairbank.  The  "de"  attached  to  hU  name u 
evidence  that  this  old  YorkBhite  UMne  -w«a  isot,  sa 


has  been  hastily  assumed  hf  sonenarsoo 
of  the  origin  of  surnames^  a  famab  nao 
hereditary.  Its  origin  was  evidently  1

This  is  a  reprint  of  the  lecture  delivered 
by  the  vicar  of  St.  Helen's,  Bishopsgatc,  on 
5  Aug.,  1842,  to  the  Crosby  Hall  Literary 
and  Scientific  Institution. 

Architectural  Aiitiquitieg  and  Prewnt  Stat«  of 
Crosby  Plnce,  with  ftii  Historical  and  Deseriijtive 
Account.  By  H.  J.  Hammon.— p|i.  16,  ]:>  plated 
4to,  cloth,  18+4-    Also  issutid  on  largu  iiafwr. 

A  Memoir  of  Crosby  Place.  By  the  Rev.  Thomas 
Huco.  M.  A.,  F.S.  A.  Read  at  Crosby  Hall, :»  Jan., 
1856.— Included  in  Traunaclioivt  of  ike-  London  uuU 
MitUUci'iex  ArchitofogiccU  Socitfy,  vol,  i,  p.  35.  Also 
issued  as  a  itaitiphlet. 

Dr.  J.  E.  Cox  in  his  well-known  work  '  The 
Annals  of  St.  Helen's,  Bishopsgate '  (1875), 
provides  a  great  deal  of  useful  information 
respecting  trosby  Place,  derived,  as  he 
acknowledges,  from  the  collections  of  Mr. 
H.  K.  Williams^  who  was  a  member  of  the 
firm  of  wine  merchants  for  many  years 
tenants  of  the  crypt  and  vault.  "Alejih  '* 
(Dr,  Harvey)  in  *  London  Scenes  and  London 
People  '  refers  to  this  collection,  and  de- 
scribes his  visit  to  the  Hall  in  1863. 

Tlie  first  appeal  for  its  preservation,  sent 
out  on  1  Dec.,  1832,  refers  in  a  foot-note  to 
"  Historical  and  Antiquarian  Notices  of 
Crosby  Hall,  by  E.  I.  Carlos,"  one  of  the 
committee.  I  have  not  seen  a  copy  of  this 
pamphlet. 

On  its  being  converted  to  a  restaurant  in 
1868  (?)  the  proprietors,  Messrs.  A.  Gordon 
&  Co.,  published  a  pamphlet,  '  Crosby  Hall, 
the  Ancient  City  Palace  and  Great  Ban- 
queting Hall :    its  Hiatorj'  and  Restoration.' 

In  addition  to  the  majority  of  the  preced- 
ing pamplilets  and  books  the  following 
circulars,  &c.,  are  before  me  : — 

The  appeal  issued  5  June,  1833. 

Prospectus  of  the  Crosby  Hall  Literary 
and  Scientific  Institution  '(dat^d  "  1842  ^* 
by  Sir  Henry  Ellis). 

Invitation  to  a  soiree  held  27  July  (1842  ?), 
This  has  a  very  pretty  engraving  of  the  Hall, 
showing  the  minstrels'  gallery. 

Programme  of  a  concert  and  entertain- 
ment by  Miss  Clara  Seyton,  February,  1846. 

These  dates  correct  some  of  those  given 
by  the  Rev.  J.  E.  Cox.  Miss  Maria 
Hacketfs  correspondence  with  John  Nichols 
Sl  Son,  her  publishers,  resi>ecting  the  several 
editions  of  her  '  History^  and  Description 
of  St.  Paul's  Cathedral,*  contains  many 
references  to  her  work  in  connexion  with 
the  preservation  of  Crosby  Hall  ;  but  there 
is  nothing  of  sufficient  importance  to  b© 
worth  transcribing  for  these  pages.

ROOD-LOFTS. 

In  the  parish  church  of  Moreton  Hamp- 
stead,  Devon,  I  have  noticed  a  very  curiouB 
contrivance  for  ascending  to  the  leads  of 
the  south  aisle.  In  many  churches  where 
an  aisle  roof  is  on  a  different  level  from 
that  of  the  nave  it  is  made  accessible — pre- 
sumably for  the  purpose  of  "ridding  the 
gutter,  so  familiar  an  item  in  old  church- 
wardens' accounts — by  a  "  vise,"  or  winding 
stone  staircase,  in  a  special  turret  apart 
from  the  belfry  or  tower.  At  South  Tawton 
there  is  a  stair  in  such  a  turret,  outside  the 
south-west  angle  of  the  church,  for  the  south 
aisle,  and  another  stair,  within  the  thick- 
ness of  the  walling,  at  the  north-west  comer, 
for  the  north  aisle.  Can  any  one  tell  me 
which  is  the  earlier  type  of  construction  ? 

At  Moreton  Hampstead  the  turret  is 
attached  externally  to  the  south  side  of  the 
chancel,  though  it  does  not  rise  from  the 
ground,  but  is  dorbelled  out  from  the  face 
of  the  wall,  its  base  being  at  about  half  the 
height  from  the  ground  to  the  eaves.  On 
examining  the  interior  of  the  chancel  I  found 
a  doorway  high  up  in  the  south  wall,  opposite 
to  the  upper  doorway  of  the  disused  rood- 
loft  stairs  in  the  wall  of  the  north  aisle. 
Evidently,  in  this  case,  the  southern  leads 
were  attained  by  crossing  the  rood-loft  and 
mounting  the  short  flight  of  steps  in  the 
turret. 

Abbot  Gaaquot  in  his  '  Parish  Life  in 
Mediaeval  England,'  p.  56,  says  that  rood- 
lofts  were  sometimea  entered  from  without 
the  cliurch,  and  \\e  instances  an  outside 
entrance  to  a  rood-loft  at  St.  John's,  Win- 
chester. May  it  be  tliat  the  stairs  in  this 
or  other  instances  originallv  reached  higher 
than  the  loft,  having  exits  both  on  tlie  loft 
and  on  the  leads  ?  Mr.  K.  H.  Murray,  in  a 
very  instructive  j)aper  on  '  The  Evolution 
of  Church  Chancels '  in  the  ArcJicroIogical 
Transactions  of  the  Birmingham  and  Midland 
Institute,  gives  instances  of  external  entrance 
to  the  loft,  from  the  churchyard,  at  Dunston 
in  Somersetshire,  at  Watchett,  and  at 
Minehead.  At  the  last  there  was  also  a 
stair  inside  the  church,  down  from  the 
southern  end  of  the  loft.  In  an  art<icle  in 
The  Gentleman  8  Magazine  sitrned  G.  C. 
("G.  M.  Lib.,"  •  Ecclesiology,'  ed.  G.  L. 
Gomme,'  p.  85)  we  are  told  that  in  St. 
Michael's,  Beccles  (between  Decorated  and 
Peri>endicular  periods),  the  priest's  door 
has  a  low,  very  small,  flat-topped  porch, 
over  which  is  a  doorway  (without  chamfer, 
dripstone,  or  moulding)  on  the  probable 
level   of  the   rood-loft,  its  sill  being   9  ft. 


from  the  ground.  **  What,"  he  asks,  "  was 
its  purpose  ? "  The  rood-staira,  he  adds, 
are  in  the  north  aisle.  Several  other  of  iui 
questions  on  the  subject  of  rood-lofts  m^ 
have  eUcited  interesting  answers. 

My  conjectural  explanation  of  the  oalnb 
entrance  to  the  loft  is  that  it  was  far&» 
use  of  lay  musicians,  vocal  or  (qoey) 
instrumental,  who,  it  appears,  were  ofta 
itinerants  engaged  for  special  oocaaooi, 
such  as  the  Easter  or  Christmas  celebrations, 
and  were  sometimes,  as  at  Chagf  ord,  members 
of  the  local  "Young  Men's  Guild."  An 
outside  entrance  would  obviate  disturbance, 
and  profanation,  of  the  chancel  by  lay 
intruaers. 

A  variety  of  different  purposes  has  ben 
assigned  to  rood-lofts ;  probably  a  chrono- 
logical distinction  between  these  uses  might 
be  established.  According  to  Mr.  Mumy 
and  other  writers,  the  rood-loft  was  no(t 
feature  of  EngHsh  church-interiors  until 
the  fourteenth  century.  Before  this  if 
had  only  the  rood-  or  "  candle-beam." 
stretching  from  wall  to  wall  of  the  chanceL 
above  the  altar ;  and  wherever  Xonmn. 
Early  English,  or  Decorated  stonevoik 
contains  rood-loft  stairs,  these  are,  3Ir. 
Murray  considers,  after-insertions  (chancel 

fiers  are  often  pierced  for  such)  of  th» 
'erpendicular  period. 
At  Lydford,  where  they  are  in  the  angle 
of  the  chancel  and  south  aisle,  and  are 
evidently  an  afterthought,  being  extn- 
ordinarily  contrtujted,  and  faultily  close 
to  a  "  squint,"  the  doorway  is  so  hirf» 
in  the  wall  that  the  lowest  step  is  only 
about  on  a  level  with  the  floor  of  the 
"  squint,"     or    the    shoulder    of    a    sitting 

{)erson.  I  have  noticed  the  same  peco- 
iarity  in  some  other  churches,  and  fas'* 
wondered  whether  the  steps  in  the  ^ 
were  supplemented  by  portable  ones.  B* 
what  are  we  to  think  when  we  find  the  staff* 
so  nan*ow  that  it  must  have  been  perilous. 
if  not  impossible,  for  a  stout  man  to  ascend 
them  ?  May  one  suppose  that  the  earlier* 
form  of  loft  (whether  attached  to  a  scre« 
or  not  is  a  secondary  question)  was  but  • 
slight  gallerj'  suitable  for  the  slender  acol.W 
to  walk  along,  for  the  purpose  of  dusting  <"■ 
draping  the  high  cross  and  images  tW 
stood  on  its  front  rail,  and  of  lighting  th* 
candles  ranged  (in  sconces  perhaps,  or  o^ 
brackets)  "before "  these  ?  The  accom- 
modation of  musicians  in  the  loft  n*.'' 
j  perhaps  have  been  a  later  developrnrtt- 
!  And  if  the  \ncar  with  deacon  and  sub-deecoft

Mr.  Murray  remarks  that  in  many  churches 
in  the  Westem  counties  the  screen  stood, 
not  across  the  chancel  arch,  but  at  one-tJiird 
the  distance  from  that  to  the  west  end  of  the 
nave,  and  that  a  papal  decision  assigned  the 
chancel  to  the  regular  clergy,  and  one-tliird 
of  the  nave  to  the  secular  clergy,  the  rest  to 
the  laity.  But  in  the  case  of  a  small  parish 
church  where  there  were  no  regulars,  and 
certainly  not  a  sufficient  number  of  seculars 
to  fill  the  chancel,  but  only  a  vicar,  a  chantry 
priest,  and  a  parish  clerk,  how  are  we  to 
account  for  the  triple  division  of  the  interior  ? 
At  South  Tawton  and  some  neighbouring 
churches  the  chancel,  occupying  the  first 
bay,  was  of  the  whole  width  of  the  church 
(nave  and  aisles),  but  the  sacrarium  or 
presbyterj',  which  jutted  eastward,  was  only 
the  same  width  as  the  nave. 

Should  it  be  answered  that  the  chancel 
was  occupied  by  the  choir,  two  objections 
might  be  raised  :  first,  that,  according  to 
Micklethwaite  and  others,  musical  accom- 
paniments to  services  were  of  very  rare 
occunence  in  small  or  rural  parishes  ;  and, 
second,  that  if,  as  we  are  told,  the  rood-loft 
was  for  the  "  prick-song  singers,"  that  space 
would,  presumably,  have  sufficed. 

It  is  said  that  *'  the  organs  "  sometimes 
stood  on  the  rood-loft ;  but  surely  the 
typical  fifteenth-century  wooden  screens 
(such  as  we  find  at  Bovey  Tracy,  at  Ex- 
bourne,  at  Lew  Trenchard,  Ac,),  whose 
covings  sujjported  a  gallery  apparently  not 
more  than  4  ft.  or  5  ft.  wide,  would  not  have 
been  adapted  to  such  a  superstructure. 

I  shall  bo  grateful  if  readers  of  *  N.  &  Q.' 
will  communicate  with  me  direct,  and  aa 
early  as  possible. 

(Miss)  Ethel  Leoa-Weekes. 

Sunny  Nook,  Rugby  Mansions,  West  Kensington.

lh>v..  ...  ..^..-:,  unveil  in  their -t.  tatiies. 

There  seems  to  be  a  pretty  general  consensus 
of  opinion  that  the  last  line,  as  we  at  present 
have  it,  is  not  exactly  aa  Shakespeare  left  it ; 
that  "cradles,*  which  leaves  the  metre  in- 
complete, is  in  all  probability  a  miatSLke  at  ^ 
printer  or  copyist ;    \.\vft\,  \>rta  wvfevws\  -^k**

NOTES  AND  QUERIES,     [lo  8.  vn.  ju^b  a,  m 


■something  very  di£Ferent,  less  familiar  and 
homely,  more  rarely  conceived,  and  more 
finely  expressed.  To  "unveil  thoughts" 
we  do  not  so  much  want  to  know  about  the 
cradle  which  contained  them  as  to  learn 
something  about  the  thoughts  themselves, 
to  discover  their  features,  the  flash-lights 
which  proceed  from  them,  the  notes,  signs, 
subtle  characters,  by  which  alone  the  provi- 
dential, watchful  ^e  can  hope  to  decipher 
and  read  them.  Just  as  by  watching  the 
signs  in  the  great  heaven  above  us  we  may 
forecast  the  weather  that  is  to  be,  so  by 
■careful  observation  of  the  lofty  heights  of 
the  thought-sphere  we  may  form  a  shrewd 
guess  of  what  is  passing  there.  Now  it  is 
evident  that  any  word  which  we  propose 
to  substitute  for  "  cradles "  should  bear 
some  resemblance  to  it ;  should  be  Shake- 
spearian in  style,  and,  if  possible,  rest  to  a 
-certain  extent  on  Shakespearian  authority ; 
it  should  satisfy  both  the  sense  and  the 
scansion,  and  should  be  a  fitting  finale  to  an 
•exceptionally  fine  passage.  Now  I  venture 
to  think  that  we  have  such  a  word  in 
"  heraldry,"  pronounced,  we  may  be  sure, 
hy  many  in  that  day,  as  it  would  be  by  not  a 
few  in  the  present  day,  '*  eraldry,"  and  so 
written — cf.  Italian  aralfio.  Now  suppose 
-that  c  was  set  down  for  an  e  (the  two  letters 
are  frequently  confounded),  and  that  d 
and  I  changed  places  (a  common  printer's 
-error),  and  the  hard  word  "  eraldry " 
might  readily  pass  for  the  easy  word 
"'  cradles." 

In  confirmation  of  this  conjecture  I  shall 
now  quote  a  passage  from  '  The  Rape  of 
Lucrece,'  I.  64,  where  Shakespeare,  por- 
traying the  shifting  flushes  in  Lucrece's 
face,  says  : — 

This  heroJdrtj  in  Lucrece'  face  was  seen, 
ArfTued  by  t>eaiity's  red  and  virtue's  white  ; 
and  in  the  very  first  line  of  the  next  stanza, 
repeating  the  thought  in  shghtly  different 
language,  lie  says  : — 

This  Hthnt  war  of  lilies  and  of  roses. 
Here  we  have  "  heraldry  "   applied  to  the 
comings  and  goings  in  Lucrece  s  face,  which 
the  author  explains  as  a  silent  war — in  other 
words,  "  dumb  heraldry." 

Philip  Pebkino. 

'  Macbeth  ' :  the  Three  Witches. — In

Tliough  a  resort  to  textual  emendation,.j 
however  slight,  is  generally  to  be  deprecate 
I  think  the  present  crux  can  onlv  be  sur-^ 
mounted  by  such  a  method  ;  anti  I  would 
therefore  propose  to  read  *'  a  Uttle  change  " 
in  both  cases  where  "  a  little  charge  "  occurs,, 
inasmuch  as  the  verj--  essence  of  the 
context  prescribes  it.  Glendower's  remark 
which  follows,  "  I  will  not  have  it  alt^rd,' 
will,  I  trust,  be  deemed  sufficient  cause  to 
justify  this  interpretation.  As  Hotspur, 
Glendower,  and  Mortimer  were  debating 
tlie  partition  of  the  country  by  the  aid  of  ai 
map  displayed  before  them,  the  proposed 
alteration  would  naturally  appear  to  their 
unscientific  eyes  only  trifling  upon  the  map  ; 
while  in  no  circumstances  whatever  could  a 
work  of  such  magnitude  be  regarded  by 
them  as  involving  only  "  a  little  charge." 

The  difficulty  surrounding  the  word 
"  advantage  "  in  the  above  passage  can  be 
got  rid  of.  I  believe,  by  construing  it  as 
^'  continuation  "  {like  Fr.  davantagc]^  as  it 
really  signifies  here  dimdvantage,  which  is 
evident  from  the  mamier  in  which  Mortimer 
proceeds  to  put  forward  his  claim.

AoLAUS  says  that  the  Helios  myths 
explain  perfectly  the  epithet  "pitiful- 
hearted."  But  on  his  own  acceptance  of 
Theobald  there  is  no  pitiful-hearted  Helios 
or  Titan  in  the  case ;  the  butter  claims  the 
epithet.  His  remark  fits  only  the  common 
form.  Db.  Kbueoeb  says  that  "  melted  " 
is  unobjectionable,  because  it  refers  to 
**  the  special  case  witnessed  in  the  past." 
What  special  case  ?  When  was  there  a 
special  case  of  butter  melting  in  the  sun, 
and  so  w6ll  known  that  The  Prince's  com- 
panions would  comprehend  the  allusion  at 
once  ?  The  preterite  is  not  possible  in  such 
a  connebcion.  Is  it  Db.  Kbueoeb's  sup- 
position that  Prince  Hal  had  seen  that 
phenomenon  only  once,  and  was  referring 
to  his  memory  of  the  startling  event  ?  He 
was  no  adult  biped  if  he  had  not  seen  it  a 
hundred  times. 

But  ia  Titan  the  sun  ?  Certainly,  if  so, 
the  common  form  is  hopelessly  out  of  court, 
for  Shakespeare  could  never  have  written, 
"  Pitiful-hearted  sun,  that  melted  at  the 
sweet  tale  of  the  sun  !  "  That  is  a  negative 
argument  for  Theobald,  I  admit ;  but  that 
preterite  will  not  go  down,  and  still  convinces 
me  that  the  reference  is  te  some  cla^sicfJ 
story.  That  is  why,  despite  its  plausibility, 
I  cannot  accept  this  meaning  for  Titan,  who 
certainly  never  melted  at  liis  own  sweet 
tale.  Whether  my  suggestion  is  right  or 
not,  the  other  three  fonns  seem  to  me 
assuredly  wrong.  And  I  wish  Aqlaus 
would  tell  me  where  he  finds  that  Titan- 
Helios  was  noted  for  tender-heartedness : 
I  confess  inability  to  find  it  in  anything 
Shakespeare  was  likely  to  have  known,  or 
indeed  anywhere  else. 

Forrest  Morgan. 
Hartford,  Conn.

Botha:  the  Name. — ^Now  that  the 
Colonial  Premiers  have  come  and  gone,  it 
may  be  interesting;  to  many  recwlers  te  inquire 
what  is  the  origin  of  the  name  Botiia.  I 
take  it  to  be  a  variant  of  the  not  uncommoa 
Duteh  surname  Bote  or  Botto,  which  is 
derived  from  the  old  Low  German  and 
Frisian  personal  name  Botho.  Another 
Duteh  family  name,  Botenga  or  Bottengo, 
is  derived  from  the  same  root,  by  means  of 
the  patronymic  sufiSbc  -tng,  and  means  "  the 
son  of  Botho."  Presuxnably  the  English 
surname  Bott  and  Botting  are  from  the 
same  source.  Jas.  Platt,  Jun.

George  Romney's  House  in  Cavendish 
Square. — In  the  fine  work  on  George 
Romney  by  Messrs.  T.  Humphry  Ward  and 
W.  Roberts,  and  in  an  article  by  Mr.  C. 
Lewis  Hind  recently  published  in  The 
Daily  Chronicle^  and  entitled  '  A  House  in 
Cavendish  Square,'  the  number  of  Romney's 
house  in  the  square  is  stated  to  have  been 
32.  But,  in  fact,  the  number  was  24. 
I  have  some  personal  interest  in  this  ques- j 
tion  ;  for  at  No.  23,  next  door  to  Roniney,'| 
lived  my  great-great-grandfather,  James ' 
Clayton,  and  his  wife  Hatmah  (my  '*  step  ''- 
great-great-grandmother),  who  was  a  grand- 
daughter of  William  Penn,  the  founder  of 
Pennsylvania.  The  remainder  of  the  lease 
of  this  town  house  (23)  had  been  left  to  her 
by  her  father,  Richard  Penn,  who  had  a 
house  also  at  Sunbury,  Middlesex. 

In  P.  Boyle's  *  Fashionable  Court  Guide ' 
for  1792,  first  edition,  these  entries  appear  : 

(alphabetical  section)  "  Rumney, ,  Esq.. 

24,      Cavendish      Square "  ;       and      (under 
'Cavendish   Square')    "23,   Mrs.    Clayton; 

24,  Rumney  ;    25,  Sir  John  Thorold." 

In  the  second  edition  (1792)  the  same 
numbers  are  given,  and  the  artist's  Christian 
name  is  inserted.  And  in  Boyle's  *  Guide 
for  1796  Romney 's  number  is  still  24, 
while  the  spelling  of  his  name  is  corrected. 
Further,  these  books  indicate  that  there 
were  only  twent5*-eight  houses  in  the  square. 
No.  24  still  exists,  apparently  very  little 
altered.  It  is  a  substantial  eiglitoenth- 
century  mansion.  A  Romney  plaque  should 
be  inserted  in  the  front  of  the  house. 

Edvvy  G.  Clayton. 

10,  Old  Palace  Lane,  Richmond,  Surrey. 

Uoo  FoscoLO  IN  London.  (See  9  S.  vi. 
326.) — The  house  in  Handel  Street  (formerly^ 
Henrietta  Street)  at  the  corner  of  Kenton! 
Street,  St.  Pancras,  where  Ugo  Foscolo  hid 
himself  from  his  creditors  at  a  crisis  in  his 
career,  is  about  to  be  demolished.  The 
house  has  no  interest  beyond  its  temporary 
association  with  Foscolo,  and  its  demolition 
is  no  cause  for  regret.  John  Hebb.

*  LoBNA  DooNK.* — I  have  midfirtakea  to 
edit  and  illustrate  a  new  issue  of  'Loroa 
Doone,*  wbioh  is  to  be  published  in  the

1.  The  historical  facts  rejgiarding  the 
Doones.  The  principal  theories  are:  (a) 
that  th^  are  mstorted  memories  of  Danes, 
of  the  tune  of  Alfred  the  Great ;  (6)  that 
th^  were  outlaws  from  the  coast  of  Wales, 
aoroes  the. &istol  Channel;  (c)  that  they 
were  a  miscellaneous  crowd  of  outlaws 
(Western  men  and  men  from  further  up 
country),  of  whom  the  remnants  died  at  the 
beginning  of  the  last  century ;  and  (d)  that 
tbsiy  were  a  Scottish  party,  of  the  time  of 
Chwles  I.,  who  remained  in  Devon  for  more 
than  half  a  century,  and  then  returned  to 
their  own  land.  It  is  possible  that  there  is 
some  truth  in  more  than  one  of  these  theories. 

2.  The  Doone  legends  earlier  than  the 
publication  of  '  Loma  Doone.' — The  stories 
that  can  be  clearly  established  seem  to 
be  of  outrages  :  (a)  near  MinehecKl,  a  house 
sacked,  but  no  person  harmed  at  the  time, 
though  in  consequence  of  the  death  of  a 
young  Doone,  who  had  been  wounded  at  the 
house,  his  comrades  returned  and  slew 
every  one  in  the  house.  (6)  At  Exford, 
where  the  Doones  killed  (and  in  one  version 
ate)  a  child,  saying  : — 

If  any  one  asks  who  kill^  thee, 
Tell  'em  'twas  the  Doones  of  Badgeworthy. 
Blackmore  lays  the  scene  of  this  at  Martin- 
hoe,  and  adds  the  abduction  of  Chris. 
Badcock's  wife,  (c)  Parsonage  Farm  (?), 
Oare,  Amaid  (or  the  mother)  hastily  hid, 
leaving  a  child.  The  Doones  said,  **  Prick 
the  c«df,  and  the  old  cow '11  bellow,"  and 
pricked  the  child  with  their  swords  until 
it  died,  but  the  woman  did  not  appear. 
{d)  Yenworthy,  a  farm  near  Oare,  was 
attacked,  when  a  woman  fired  a  **  long  gun," 
wounding  one  of  the  Doones  so  that  his 
blood  was  tracked  in  the  snow  for  miles. 

So  far  as  I  know,  (c)  is  unpublished, 
except  by  myself.  It  is  a  genuinely  old 
Btory,  gathered  from  the  old  f ^  and  seems

Wooden  Cups  in  East  Anolia. — I  have 
tliree    wooden    cups — two    of    them    nine 
inches   high,    the   third   a  little   taller   and 
bigger.     They  were  given  me  by  a  native  of 
BIythbui'gh,   Suffolk,  and  came  out  of  the 
eliiirch     there     during     its     "  restoration " 
some   time    diiring   the   last   century.     The 
cups  are  of  brown  varnished  wood,  and  do 
not  look  very  old.     Can  any  of  youtr  corre- 
spondents tell  me  what  piirpose  they  served  ? 
In  this  part  of  East  Anglia  the  device  of 
'*  Tlire©  Cups  *'  is  common  as  a  sign  :    there 
are     "Tliree    Cups"     inns    at    Colchester, 
Chelmsford,  and  Harwich,  and  a  beerhouse 
at  Ipswich.     Moreover,  the  tlu-ee  cups  occur 
on  the  font  at  Nacton  (or  Le\'ington)  and 
on   the   west   front   of   St.    Marj's   Church, 
Trimley.     These  are  villages   between   Ips- 
wich and  Harwich.     But,  most  curious  of 
all,  the  tlu"ee  cups  occur  on  the  notice-board 
of  the  church  at  HarvWch. 

No  one  geenis  able  to  explain  what  they 

mean.     I  think  that  my  wooden  cups  from 

Blythburgh  and  all  these  other  instances  of 

the  same  device  must  have  a  common  origin. 

Aubrey  Stewart.

Francis  Kendaix,  citizen  and  Merchant 
Taylor  of  London,  married,  at  St.  Helen's 
Church,  Bishopsgate,  29  Dec.,  1670,  Sarah, 
elder  daughter  of  John  Cope,  citizen  and 
Haberdasher  of  London  {Misc.  Oen.  et  Her.^ 
l^iird  Series,  iv.  212).  Francis  Kendall,  in 
his  will,  dated  1  Oct.,  1673  (proved,  with  a 
codicil  dated  2  Feb.,  1673/4,  by  the  testator's 
father  John  Kendall,  16  Feb.,  1673/4, 
P.C.C.  26  Bunce),  directs  his  burial,  with  his 
wife  and  child,  in  a  vault  in  the  church  of 
St.  Mary-at-Hill,  Eastcheap,  City  of  London. 
He  possessed  land  in  Aythorp,  Essex,  and 
refers  to  the  children  of  his  uncles  Bromidge 
(one  of  the  witnesses  to  his  will  was  George 
Bromwich)  and  Tissard,  to  a  cousin  John 
Bennet,  and  to  Kendall  Heron  and  William 
Heron.  Who  was  Edward  Augustus  Ken- 
dall, who  in  1796  married  Maria  Webb  ? 
What  was  the  link,  if  any,  between  this 
Maria  Webb  and  Charles  Newdigate  Webb 
who  was  living  24  Jan.,  1812  ;  and  also  with 
Benjamin  Webb,  wlio  married,  in  1767,  a 
Miss  Newdigate,  of  Clapliam,  Surrey  (8  S.  xii. 
87)? 

I  shall  be  much  indebted  for  information 
— as  full  as  possible — with  regard  to  the 
above.     Please  reply  direct. 

Reginald  Stewart  Boddington.
