CJemetery  Consecration. — In  a  popular 
reference  book  it  is  stated  that  the  first 
cemetery  consecrated  in  England  was  Kensal 


Green,  2  Nov.,  1832.  But  this  is  nearly  four 
years  later  than  the  oonsecration  of  St 
James's  Cemetery,  Livearpool,  an  old  quarry, 
converted  into  a  public  graveyard,  axid  coo- 
secrated  12  Jan.,  1829,  the  first  intennnt 
taking  place  five  months  alter.  It  is  the 
site  of  the  new  cathedral,  now  fast  appnidh 
ing  completion. 

Is  any  earUer  consecration  known  than 
1829  ?  William  Jaooabd.

the  Hertalet  family.

Hocac  t  Hoo :  Hooa  (10  S,  vH.  401).— 
This  article  is  prooisely  of  such  a  character 
as  to  fill  a  philologist  with  despair.  Thar© 
is  no  more  deplorable  method  of  arriving 
at  truth  than  to  toss  together  a  quantity 
of  words  which  have  no  relation  to  each 
other,  merely  becanae  they  seem  to  have 
some  superficial  similarity.  The  unreason- 
ablene«8  of  such  a  method  cannot  be  made 
clear,  simply  because  it  would  oceupy  very 
many  pages  to  disentangle  such  a  tangled 
mass. 

A  little  investigation,  or  a  mere  reference 
to  th©  '  N.E.D.,'  would  have  extricated 
some  at  least  of  these  unrelated  words  from 
the  heap.  Take,  for  example,  fiGUf^-hock^ 
which  we  are  told  means  "  the  tall  flowering 
plant,"  merely  for  the  «ake  of  connecting  it 
with  the  German  hoch  I  But  it  meanB 
**  holy  mallow."  and  the  hock  in  it  is  allied 
to  the  Welsh  hocya,  mallows,  from  the  early 
A--S.  hocCt  a  mallow, 

Next,  take  the  verb  to  hock.  This  is 
allied  to  the  A.-S.  hoh,  a  heel  ;  see  hough  in 
the*N.E.D.' 

Next  take  the  sb.  hog.  Since  th©  article 
in  *  N.E.D. '  was  printed  the  A*-8.  form  has 
been  found  ;  it  was  originally  hocg,  a  strong 
masculine. 

I  decline  to  go  through  this  tiresome 
jumble.  Let  it  suiBce  to  say  that  hoeh,  a 
mallow,  hough,  a  hock  of  an  animal,  and 
hog,  a  pig,  are  all  totally  distinct  words; 
aiul  none  of  them  has  any  oonnexjon  what- 
ever With  "  the  root-notion  of  AtgA.'*    Why 


w©  should  be  expected  to  explain 
things  over  again,  I  do  not  know. 

The  point  is,  of  course,  that  *' 
notion  of  high  "  ought  mot  to  be  < 
by  euch  a  form  as  an  imaginary 
hock,  when  we  know  ell  the  while 
A.-S,  form  was  Ada  A,  from  a  Teutt 
*hauh-0£  {again  see  'N.E.D.'). 
mere  modern  Gerinan ;  and  Old 
(let  me  say  just  onc«  more)  is  no 
from  modem  German.  The  hoga 
we  are  referred  ie  noUm^  but  a

Mirage  (10  S.  vii.  390,  453).— The 
phenomenon  described  by  Pbof.  Skeat 
is  not  infrequent — in  fact,  it  ia  almost  usual 
on  the  French  and  Italian  Riviera  di  Ponente, 
t-c,  east  of  Genoa. 

In  certain  states  of  the  atmosphere,  and 
generally  in  the  early  morning  or  evening, 
the  island  of  Corsica  is  seen  well  up  above 
the  horizon,  with  the  mount^ns  of  the 
interior  towering  above  the  coast- line,  and 
appearing  to  be  at  a  distance  of  25  or  30 
miles.  The  nearest  point  of  Corsica  to  this 
part  of  the  mainland  is  about  100  miles, 
%vhiie  the  mountains  are  perhaps  10  or 
15  miles  further  ofiF.  The  oonx-exity  of  the 
earth  prevents  the  possibility  of  directly 
seeing  any  such  distance,  even  from  the  hiUa 
lying  behind  Mentone,  San  Remo,  or 
AJassio  ;  but  on  several  occasions  I  have  seen 
Corsica  with  great  plainness  while  standing 
on  the  shore  at  one  or  other  of  those  places. 
Allowing  a  height  for  human  eyes  of,  say 
6  feet  from  the  ground,  I  think  the  '*  offing  ' 
is  not  more  than  a  few  miles  off,  though  of 
course  the  horizon  extends  largely  with 
every  foot  of  rise.  VV.  C  J. 

A  description  of  a  mira£;e  seen  on  the 
Thames  was  given  by  Dr.  Wollaston  (Secre- 
tary of  the  Royal  Society)  in  1797.  Abroad, 
the  mirages  seen  by  Ki^ber's  army  in  Egj'pt 
in  1798,  and  by  the  French  army  in  Syria 
in  1799,  are  well  known  ;  and  another  re- 
markable one  is  described  by  Lord  Roberts 
in  '  Forty-One  Years  in  India.'  vol.  i.  p.  393. 

^.  ^, 

Upton.

Sprino-heeled  Jack  (10  8,  vii.  206.  256, 
394). — More  than  thirty  yoars  ftgo  juinping 
pranks  were  played  many  nights  on  th«* 
gentry  over  the  magazine  by  the  carml  near 
the  South  Camp  at  Aldersliot.  It  was  a 
lonely  post  at  some  distance  from  tJie  giiard- 
room.  Jack  used  to  spring  across  the  c«nal 
while  the  sentry,  pacing  liis  be^t,  was  walk- 
ing away  from  it,  and  then  on  to  tlie 
man's  shoulders,  sorely  frightening  liim,  aiid 
usually  disarming  him  by  carrying  off  his 
rifle.  The  pranks  were  popularly  attri- 
buted to  a  lively  oJBioer  of  Rifles ;  he 
certainly  was  not  con\^ct«d  of  them,  and 
I  do  n<jt  know  that  lie  ever  acknowledged 
himself  to  be  spring-heeled  Jack, 

Alfred  C.  E.  Welby. 

"  Lying  Bishop  "  :  Miles  or  Vauyino 
Length  UO  S.  vii.  44$*). — I  cannot  answer 
Hr.  J.  W.  Brown's  query,  but  I  wish  to 
ask  another  question  on  tlie  same  subject.. 
Seeing  that  the  two  supposed  errors  on  the 
milestone  in  question  are  very  nearly  exactly 
proportionate  to  one  another,  I  desire  to 
seek  information  whether  the  fact  may  not 
be  that  the  milestone  indicates  miles  of  a 
different  length — viz.,  about  2,640  ^yards^ 
from  that  of  the  standard  English  mile. 

This  leads  me  to  ask  a  further  question. 
Do  any  of  your  readers  know  of  the  exist- 
ence of  any  stones — I  cannot  aay  milestones 
— which  still  continue  to  record  obsolete 
measures  of  length  ? 

Wlien  motoring  last  year  along  the  high 
road  between  Madrid  and  Toledo,  I  noticed 
that  there  were  still  several  atones  left  which 
gave  distances  not  in  kilometers,  but  in 
leagues.  This  is  an  interesting  link  with  the 
past ;  but  I  have  myself  not  noticed  any- 
thing of  the  sort  elsewhere. 

R.  Johnson  Walker. 

Little  Holland  House,. Kenaington,  W. 

Though  not  directly  relevant  to  Mr. 
Brown's  query,  there  is  one  detail  of  his 
communication  to  which  I  should  like  to 
refer.  His  citation  of  the  old  and  the  new 
mileage  eomputations,  whereby  16  miles 
have  been  corrected  to  23,  and  10  miles  to 
1  5,  would  lead  the  ordinaiy  reader  to  suppose 
that  the  original  inscription  blundered 
grossly.  If,  however  {as  I  assume  to  be  the 
case),  the  latter  was  a  century  or  so  old, 
the  discrepancy  is  open  to  logical  explana- 
tion. I  think  such  as  have  any  experience 
of  old-time  mileage  computations  will  bear 
roe  out  that  Engli.sh  milos  used  to  be  much 
longer  than  at  present— I  believe  about  half 
A3    long    again — and    that    this    variation

*'DuMP"  (10  S.  vii.  426).— As  the  illufl- 
trations  brought  together  by  my  friend 
Mb.  Pickeord  conveniently  show,  the  term 
**  dump "  has  two  separate  purposes  to 
serve,  and  it  is  important  to  remember  that 
it  represents  two  distinct  words.  There  is 
the  one  that  is  used  in  connexion  with  value 
— ^for  the  counter  employed  in  the  game  of 
chuck-farthing,  the  amoimt  paid  for  a  glass 
of  rum  in  the  Austrahan  bush,  and  so  forth 
— and  there  is  the  other  that  is  associated 
with  music  and  the  throes  of  a  mental 
dilemma.  "  Dump "  in  the  sense  of  a 
medal  may  be  connected  with  Icel.  dumpa 
to  thump,  whence  also  comes  the  term, 
"  dumpinsr.*'  familiar  in  discussions  on  Free 
Trade ;  while  the  other  word  is  manifestly 
allied  to  "  damp,"  which  is  a  very  significant 
monosyllable  when  applied  with  reference 
to  a  reduction  ot  spiritual  exuberance.  In 
Scotland,  at  the  present  time,  when  a  man 
is  said  to  have  '.ta'en  the  dumps,"  it  is 
known  that  he  is  out  of  temper  and  had 
better  be  temporarily  left  to  his  own  reflec- 
tions. The  musical  dump  and  the  doleful 
dumps  are  associated  in  an  interesting  and 
suggestive  fashion  in  the  last  scene  of  the 
fourth  act  of  *  Romeo  and  Juliet.'  When 
Peter,  with  his  heart  full  of  woe,  pleads  with 
the  musicians  to  play  him  *  Heart's  Ease ' 
or  "  some  merry  dump  "  whereby  he  may 
be  comforted,  the  second  player  snappishly 
responds,  "  Not  a  dump  we  ;  'tis  no  time 
to  play  now."  This  leads  to  some  swift, 
incisive  \^Tangling,  which  induces  in  Peter 
the  determination  to  dry-beat  his  antagonists 
with  iron  wit,  and  he  calls  upon  them  to 
answer  liim  like  men  as  he  rehearses  thus  : — 

When  uriniiijj  j;riof  the  heiirt  cloth  M^ouiid, 
And  doleful  duniiw  the  mind  oppress, 

Then  music  with  her  silver  sound 
With  speedy  help  doth  lend  redress. 

Thomas  Bayxe. 

"  Taping  shoos  "  (10  S.  vii.  206,  259). — 
A  very  old  Launceston  shoemaker  t^lls  me 
that  while  for  certainly  soventj'-five  years 
he  can  recall  the  use  of  the  words  soleing 
and  tapping  in  regard  to  boots  and  shoes, 
he  always  understood  them  to  mean  the 
same  thing  ;  but  the  better  class  would  use 
the  first,  and  the  working  class  the  second. 

DUNHEVED. 

The  Rev.  J.  Trounsell  Mugford,  \ncar  of 
Treleigli,  Cornwall,  in  an  interesting  article 
in  the  February  issue  of  The  Church  in  the 
West,  entitled  *  Life  in  a  Cornish  Parish 
100  Years  Ago,'  referred  to  *'  the  old  parish 
chest  with  its  three  locks."  Upon  reading 
YoBEC's    statement    that    no    such    chest 


existed  there,  I  wrote  to  the  reverend 
gentleman  in  question,  and  he  tells  me  in 
reply  that  he  described  not  his  present  churd^ 
but  his  former  one  at  Lanteglos  (ooir 
Fowey,  in  the  same  county). 

My  corrector  is  right  in  adding  thit  tli0 
parish  of  Treleigh  does  not  possess  a  "fif- 
teenth-century church  "  ;  but  before  irfa- 
ring  to  it  as  such,  I  consulted  Kelly's  comnl 
issue  of  the  *  Cornish  Director^'-,*  whcnin 
the  edifice  is  distinctly  defined  as  "Per* 
pendicular."  The  two  definitions  being 
accepted  as  sjmonymous,  I  trust  I  may  be 
excused  for  perpetuating  an  undoobted 
inaccuracy — one  which  I  am  grateful  to 
YoBEC  for  pointing  out.      Habby  Heus. 

Fair  Park,  Exeter.

JOSEPH   KNIGHT. 

\  Ox   Sunday  last,  after  some  weeks  of 
'  ness,  Joseph  Ivnight  passed  to  a  peace- 
'  end  in  his  seventy-ninth  year. 
I  Since    1883    our  venerable    and    well- 
\red    Editor    had    been    looking    after 
dc  Q.'     All  tho8e  who  have  had  any 
jminunication    with    him,    either    per- 
ly    or    by    letter,    will    realize    how 
►  x»  the  loss  that  we  feel  to-day,  when 
ave    to   work    without   the   charm 
at  gracious  personality  to  aid  and 
jicourage  us,  deprived  of  that  ripe  and 
pacious  outlook  over  the  world  of   men 
letters  which  was  a  marvel  ahke  in 
length    and    ita    brightness.     Tliose 
^o  have  been  closely  associated  with  our 
iitor  cannot  as  yet  appreciate  their  loaa. 
le^     the     youngest     of     veterans,    the  i 
pightest    of    scholars,    the    readiest    of  ' 
umers    when    he    did    not    know,    the 
ndliest   of   teachers,   seemed  privileged 
live  for  ever.     Hia  many  excellences, 
ciaUy  his  zeal  for  scholarehip  as  the 
attest     thing    in    the    world,  and    his 
onderful      endowments      of      physical 
rength  and  high  spirits,  suggested  other 
jnturies  than  those  in  which  we  live. 
|e  was  an  example  to  himself,  and  did 
seem  subject  to  the  ordinary  rules 
life.     Men    certainly    of    no    greater 
owers    than    he    liave    obtained    much 
-ider  recognition,  but  he  has  the  rarest 
claims  to  regard.     He  made  innumer- 
b\e    fellow-creatures    the    brighter    for 
acquaintance.     He    was    happy    in 
life,    and    a    cause   of    happiness    in 
liers.     He  had  a  genius  for  friendship, 
a  delicate  regard  for  others  which  led 
to  forget  hunself.     He  never  adver- 
",  never  pushed  himself  forward. 
What  our  late  Editor  frequently  im- 
l  on  rae  was  that  Westland  Marston 
__;  the  one  man  who  was  an  inspiration 
lum  and  who  led  him  to  do  his  best 
rork.     Marston' s  parties  formed  a  centre 
^(  good  talk  and  cultivated  Bohemianism 
lied  by   the  presence  of  many 
men    ol   letters.     Dr.    Sebastian 
the    accomplished    scholar    who 
dated  tlie  *  History  of  the  Holy  Graal,' 
wiother  friend  singled  out  in  former 
ftys    for     special     intimacy.     But     the 
ndlees  procession  of  friends  who  rejoiced 
thf  radiance  of  Joseph  Knight  cannot 
lie  roxuit^d.     No  man,  I   think,  was  so 
! lived;    and  not  even  his  extra* 
memorv  could  hold  the  names 


of  all  who  claimed  his  acquaintance 
everywhere,  well  knowing  the  pleasant- 
ness of  that  privilege.  Joseph  Knight 
was  never  old  in  spirit,  and  his  friend- 
ships with  men  younger  than  himself 
were  a  striking  feature  of  his  life.  During 
his  last  years  the  writer  of  this  notice 
sat  daily  beside  him,  and  was  proud 
to  receive  as  much  of  his  confidence  as 
any  one.  That  handsome  fa^e  and  noble 
brow  could  not  fail  to  express  distinction 
and  intellect ;  but  day  after  day,  as  lie 
unfolded  fresh  stores  of  knowledge, 
*'  the  wonder  grew  "  that  even  that  big 
head  "  could  carry  all  he  knew." 

He  was  probably  as  complete  a  man 
as  wiU  be  seen  for  many  a  day — a  success- 
ful amateur  of  life  as  of  books.  His  remi- 
niscences would  have  made  a  most  enter- 
taining volume,  but  he  was  too  kindly,  too 
great  a  gentleman,  to  run  the  risk  of 
offending  the  sensibilities  of  others  by 
his  records  of  the  past.  Not  that  there 
was  really  any  danger  of  such  intimate 
revelations  on  his  part.  He  was  no 
De  Quincey,  to  lay  bare  with  venomous 
veracity  the  frailties  of  his  fellows  : 
when  he  had  occasion  to  touch  on  such 
faults,  it  was  done  with  a  delicacy  that 
few  could  equal.  He  was,  as  might  have 
been  expected,  the  recipient  of  many 
confidences  in  letters  from  famous  men.' 
,  He  came  to  London  rather  late, 
having  been  employed  in  his  father's 
business  as  a  cloth-worker ;  but  he  had 
been  from  his  earliest  days  imbued  with 
hterature  be>^nd  hia  years.  When  he 
was  elected  Dux  of  his  school— a  position, 
no  doubt,  obviously  his  alike  from  liis 
alertness  of  wit  and  personal  charm — 
the  only  question  as  to  his  promotion 
(settled  by  his  fellows)  was  wliother  a 
boy  who  had  the  disabihty  of  knowing 
Pope's  and  Byron's  works  could  be 
elected ;  but  his  popularity  won  tlie 
day.  He  was  somewhat  kept  back  by 
ill-health  before  this  period,  and,  being 
allowed  to  run  free  as  he  liked,  set  him- 
self to  reading  widely.  Consequently, 
when  he  went  to  school  he  had  a  good 
deal  of  undigest-ed  knowledge  of  his  own 
acquiring,  and  his  natural  abihty  sent 
hira  up  quickly  through  form  after  fonn, 
wnthout  that  pounding  in  grammar 
and  such  sohdities  which  clever  boys 
I  usually  get  for  their  partial  delay  and 
subsequent  advantage. 
I  He  always  regretted  that  he  did  xvcl 
know  Latin  -weU  ;   V)ul  \v^  TcxaAfe  Vx'kvs/^

ft  good  French  BohdUur,  writing  rwviewi 
in  that  langaage  for  Le  Livre,  and  under- 
stood  both  Itafian  and  Spanish.  Throo^- 
out  his  life  he  was  a  lesohite  bibliophile^ 
if  not  a  bibUomaniao,  and  his  q>Iendid 
library  (whioh  had  to  be  extended  to  a 
room  in  the  next  house  to  his  own  ter 
the  taking  down  of  the  dividing  wall) 
inoloded  many  rare  poets.  There  were 
bargains  to  be  had  then.  Heonoebon^t 
an  edition  of  Wither  for  a  halfpenny 
m^ioh  afterwards  sold,  I  believe^  for  some 
twenty  poonds.  He  had  at  different  times 
in  his  fife  three  book-plates. 

Mr.  John  Morley,  then  of  The  PaR 
Mali  OassetU,  was  the  oocation  of  his 
entering  dramatic  criticism,  and  during 
l^oi^t^s  first  years  in  London  the  two 
in  conjmiction  are  credited  with  writing 
most  of  The  LUeratty  GaxeUe.  The  Athe- 
ntBum  shared  for  many  years  with 
'N.  St  Q.'  the  better  pwt  of  his  Hterary 
activities,  and  to  the  last  he  wrote 
important  reviews,  by  no  means  con- 
flnied  to  the  drama,  in  both  papers. 
Further  work  on  the  stage  was  contri- 
bated  to  The  Sunday  Timee  {where  his 
articles  were  always  eagerly  foUowed), 
The  Gkhe,  and  The  Daily  Graphic.  Bus 
latest  effort  in  the  book  way  was  a  little 
introduction  to  Sheridan*s  plays  for 
Mr.  Frowde's  "Oxford  Edition.'^  Pre- 
viously he  had  written  a  life  of  *  David 
Garrick/  and  a  record  of  D.  6.  Rossetti 
(1887)  for  the  "Great  Writers"  Series, 
the  merits  of  which  have  lopg  been  recog- 
nized by  the  judicious.  He  also  edited 
tiie  rare  *  Roscius  Anglicanus '  (1886), 
and  reprinted  with  Lawrence  &  Bullen  a 
volume  of  his  Athenceum  notices  of  plays 
from  1874  to  1879,  which  contains  an 
excellent  portrait  of  him  surrounded  by 
his  books.  His  lives  of  actors  and  others 
in  the  *  Dictionary  of  National  Biography  * 
represent  a  good  deal  of  careful  research 
in  a  compact  form.  He  wrote  for  some 
years  a  monthly  record  as  "  Sylvanus 
Urban"  in  Ttie  Gentleman* a  Magazine^ 
and  his  style  was  one  which  showed  to 
great  advantage  in  a  "  causerie."  His 
interests  were  many;  in  his  earlier  da3rs 
lie  played  chess,  billiards,  and  the  piano, 
and  to  the  end  of  his  life  he  was  a  con- 
stant card  player. 

In  his  favourite  clubs,  the  Garriok 
and  the  Beefsteak,  he  enjoyed  imequalled 
popularity,  beiiu;  always  a  mine  of  good 
nature  and  ^ood  talk.  He  was  an  artist 
in  speech  as  m  writing,  mmb\e-w\t\«d  and 


qniok  at  repartee,  with  a  GaQio  neatness 
at  expression.  GhanoeDor  Cbristie  once 
saadt  when  he  was  asked  wlnr  he  did  not 
go  into  literary  oiiolei,  that  he  prefefxed 
his  own  society.  "I^HOorel'*  was 
Knight*s  concise  and  oomplimentaiy 
retort.  EJndly  as  he  was,  he  preserved 
his  independence^  and  those  who  ventured 
on  mulne  liberties,  whether  they  wert 
lords  or  commoners,  foimd  tiiemselvei 
stemW  rebnked.  Ha  waa  once  aAed 
to  take  the  place  of  an  art  critic  on  a 
daily  paper.  **Yoa  know  more  then 
he  did,"  said  the  questioner.  "Tei, 
bat—"  was  the  reply,  and  the  eca^ 
Boienoe  of  ih»  expert  m  tiiat  "bat"  m 
a  great  thmg.  to  his  feUowHsritiei^ 
when  their  interests  combated  his,  hs 
showed,  as  I  happen  to  know,  lUMxanqM 
generosily.  WhaX  he  qtoka  d  with  moit 
pride  was  his  part  aa  the  leading  apirit 
m  getting  the  i^ole  of  the  (SoaM» 
VnEgtaae  over  to  this  country  to  act  in 
1871.  Here,  he  contended,  he  had  nuMto 
history,  a  precedent  which  waa  frnitinl 
of  good  for  both  nationa. 

He  was  always  a  kyver  of  poetry,  of 
which  he  had  great  knowledge.  Shske- 
speare  and  Milton  were^  I 'Uiibik,  most 
in  his  mind ;  he  qaoted  constantly  from 
both  with  equal  felicity.  He  did  some 
excellent  sonnets  and  translations  him- 
self. The  Athenceum  for  Jan.  13th,  1906, 
has  a  nest  rendering  by  him  of  the 
*  Sonnet  d'Arvers.*  His  extraordinary 
keenness  and  freshness  in  the  apprecis- 
tion  of  fine  letters  suggested  a  scholar 
of  the  Renaissance  rather  than  of  our 
present  day.  He  bewaUed  the  loss  of 
public  taste,  the  prevalence  of  snippets, 
the  commercialism  of  all  forms  of  writing. 
But  he  held  with  unabated  optimism 
that  scholarship  was  to  come  again,  and 
to  be  held  in  due  regard  as  the  fins 
flower  of  the  human  mind. 

If  there  were  more  like  him  \iriio  were 
eminent  both  as  scholars  and  men  of  the 
world,  the  leaven  of  the  honourable 
minority  would  work  the  quicker.  But, 
alas !  he  is  dead,  and  we  are  not  likely 
to  see  his  equsL  His  life  was  so  full 
and  various  that  it  is  difficult  even  to 
smnmarize  its  main  points.  That  I  have 
given  any  adequate  idea  of  his  vivid 
charm  and  personality  is  hardly  to  be 
hoped.  But  I  have  reoHsDed,  to  the  best  of 
my  power,  the  points  which  he  wished  to 
be  emphasized  m  any  account  of  him.

EveI\Ti  again  returned  to  Venice,  but,

artistftrum  at  Padua  University,  he  hastened

Abdy.  In  October,  1667,  Evelyn  made  a 
present  to  the  Royal  Society  of  the

transferred  in  1780  to  the  British  Musemu 
C  Diary,'  ed.  Wheatley,  1879,  i.  p,  xxi).

Mr.  Van  Noorden  and  I  have  discovered 
the  identity  of  many  more  of  these  portraits  ; 
and  of  the  286  male  personages  whose  pictures 
are  given  in  the  magazine,  only  26  remain 
unnamed.  It  would  be  unfair,  however^ 
for  me  to  print  a  revised  Ust,  as  Mr.  Van 
Noorden  is  preparing  a  book  upon  the  subject^ 
which  will  oe  published  in  due  course.  Its 
documentation  should  prove  of  the  greatest 
service  to  students  of  the  eighteenth  century. 
Horace  Bleackley.

"  Dovetailing  "  :  "  Chigaoo."  —  Sixty 
years  ago,  in  what  were  the  frontier  settle- 
ments of  the  U.S.,  it  was  customary  in  stage- 
coaches for  the  passengers  to  make  room  opr 
putting  their  knees  between  those  of  their 
opposites  :  and  this  was  called  *'  dovetailing.'  * 
It  is  recorded  that  once  a  Lake  captain  sat 
opposite  a  fat  old  lady,  and,  finding  the 
accommodation  scanty,  said :  "  I  gues6» 
marm,  it's  got  to  be  done  anyhow,  sooner 
or  later,  so  you  and  I  must  jist  dovetail.'* 
She  replied  :  **  Must  what,  sir-r  ?  "  "  Dove- 
tail, marm  ;  you  and  I  have  got  to  dovetail^ 
and  no  two  ways  about  it."  "  Dovetail  me,. 
you  inhuman  savage  I  '*  she  roared  out, 
shaking  her  fist :  "  dovetail  a  lone  woman 
in  a  Christian  coimtry  I  If  there's  law  on 
airth,  sir-r,  and  in  the  State  of  lUinoy,  I  '11 
have  you  hanged "  (George  F.  Ruxton'a 
'  Adventures  in  Mexico,'  &c,,  Murray,  1847, 
pp.  328-9).  On  the  latter  page  the  writer 
alludes  to  '*  Chigago." 

Richard  H.  Thornton.

**  Early  in  the  Spring,  when  little  or  no  Mp  had 
«•  yet  entered  theplant.  Dr.  Hope  made  a  number 
of  luoiiions  of  different  altitodes  into  the  root  and

every  other  part."

Epigram  on  Ferdinand  I.,  Kino  of  the 
Two  SiciiJES,   1761-1825. — Ferdinand,  the 
father  of  Bomba,  King  of  Naples,  succeeded 
his  father  Charles  ni.   as  Ferdinand  IV., 
King  of  Naples  ;  he  afterwards  assumed  the 
title  of  Ferdinand  III.,  King  of  Naples  and 
Sicily ;    and  ultimately  called  himself  by 
royal  decree,  in  1816,  Ferdinand  I.,  King  « 
the  Two  Sicilies.     These  changes  of  nan* 
occasioned  the  following  epigram : — 
Era  quarto,  e  poi  f d  terzo, 
E  divenne  poi  primiero, 
E  se  oontinuA  lo  scherco 
Fineri  per  esser*  cera 
This  may  be  roughly  translated : — 

He  first  was  fourth  and  then  was  third, 
And  thon  advanced  to  first,  our  hero ; 
If  it  goes  on,  this  game  absurd 
Wni  end  in  his  beooming  zero. 
The    epigrammist    was    very    nearly  an 
actuaJ  prophet :   Ferdinand  I.  did  not,  it  is 
true,  fall  to  zero,  but  Francis  II.,  of  infamoitf 
memory,  did  when,  in  1861,  Gaeta  fell,  and 
with  it  the  Bourbon  dynasty. 

John  Hebb.

LOKDE8." — In  an  inquisition  of  Edward  HI.

not   to  have   ofFerinffS,   "  thiggyng,"    **  ml-    M 
cenale,"  nor  any   other  profit,  saving  only    f 
"  putiu*a  '*  of  those  tenements  called  '  ware-

*'  Putura  "  is  lodging  and  refreshment  for  ^

from  the  inhabitants.  In  a  forest  plea  of

Dublin  MS.— I  should  be  glad  of  any 
information  about  the  writer  and  object  of 
the  following  MS.  It  was  lent  to  me  by 
Mr.  Elliott,  of  Cumljerwell,  and  ia  an  item^ 
of  his  large  collection  of  an^lnvological  and 
Hterarj^  curiosities.  The  MS.  is  bound  as  a 
I2mo  volume  in  old  calf-covered  boards. 
The  caUigraphy  looks  Like  a  seventeenth- 
century  running  hand,  with  long  s's.  It  is 
in  excellent  condition,  apparently  perfect, 
and  is  labelled  '*  Elliott  Col.  No.  4.^' 

The  first  8  pp.  are  unpaged  ;  then  follows 
aj)agination  of  7  to  363  pp.,  then  3  pp.  blank. 

On  p.  1  is  the  autograph  of  **  Timothie 
Taylor.  1674/'  who  was,  I  conclude,  the 
writer  of  the  MS.,  the  signature  being  in  * 
similar  style  to  that  of  the  MS. 

Tlie  contents  give  the  impression  that  the 
writer  was  a  well-educated  merchant  or 
business  man  of  some  sort,  residing,  it  seemSt 
in  Dublin.  He  often  speaks  of  the  packet 
bringing  political  and  other  news  from  Eng- 
land. Many  of  the  obser\*ations  seem  traO" 
scribed  from  the  Oazetle  of  the  period.  He 
writes  as  if  he  were  a  liberaJ-miiided  Non- 
conformist, frequently  alludes  to  the  per- 
secution of  Nonconformists,  and  was  evi- 
dently a  man  of  strong  religious  feeliBg» 
averse  to  war. 

On  p.  3  are  **  Some  considerations  relatiag 
to  Spain  and  ye  EropjTe.  1673.*'  He 
frequently  mentions  the  '*  t'aball." 

The  earliest  date  is  1673,  on  p.  3,  end 
18  Aug.,  1673,  on  p.  7.  On  p.  13  ho  says 
"  Mrs.  Deane  told  mo  *'  pfrhaps  the  wife  of 
the    Dean    of    St.     '  And    Ottiel 

Churchyard  is  ment .  rhatw  refegring 

to  Christ  Church  C«lht>dral.  boto  buildinfs 
being  in  Dublin.  The  latest  date  is  23  May, 
1676^  on  p.  363.

p.  346  It  is  said  that  there  ia  to  tie  a  meMMve.

The  aocounto  there  given  by  various  con-

Ralph,  Lord  Camoys  (son  of  Thomas, 
Lord  Camoys»  who  died  in  1371),  married 
a  daughter  of  Hugh  le  Despencer,  Earl  of 
Winchester.  Of  which  Hugh  le  Despencer  7 
Thomas,  Lord  Camoys,  K.G.,  who  died 
in  1421,  married  two  wives:  (1)  Elizabeth, 
dai:^hter  and  heiress  of  William  Louches, 
and  (2)  Elizabeth,  daughter  of  Edmund 
Mortimer,  third  Earl  of  March,  and  widow 
of  Henry  Percy,  "Hotspur."  Which  of 
these  wives  was  the  motlier  of  his 
children  T  Authorities  differ ;  the  *  Vis. 
Sussex  '  states  that  Elizabeth  Louches  was 
their  mother,  and  does  not  even  mention 
the  second  wife  ;  whilst  Joseph  Foster  (*  Our 
Noble  and  Gentle  Families  of  Royal  Descent,' 
Wolseley  pedigree)  and  other  writers  make 
Elizabeth  Mortimer  (Percy)  to  be  their 
mother. 

How  many  children  had  Tliomas,  Lord 
Camoys,  K.G.  (died  1421)  ?  Two  are 
generally  given:  (1)  Sir  Richard  Camovs, 
who  died  in  his  fatlier's  lifetime,  leaving 
by  his  wife  Joan  Poynings  two  daughters 
and  eventual  coheirs ;  and  (2)  Alice,  wife 
of  Sir  Leonard  Hafltinfja  (who  died  in  1455). 
Sir  Leonard  Hastings  and  Alieo  Camoys 
were  the  parents  of  Sir  William  de  Hastings 
wlio  was  created  in  1461  Lord  Hastings  of 
Ashby  do  la  Zouch. 

I  am  anxioLLS  to  know  what  children,  if 
any,  Elizabeth  PercN'  (Hotspiur'a  widow) 
had  by  lier  second  Imsband,  Thomas,  Lord 
Camoys,  K.G. 

W.  G.  D.  Fletcher,  F.S.A.

11  March,  1712,  where  Clarinda,  a  maiden

Huatle-cctp. — ^In  the  eleventh  chapter  of 

*  The  Fortunes  of  Nigel '  Sir  Walter  Scott 
writes : — 

"  *  He  is  by  this  time/  said  Lord  Balftamo,  'play- 
ing at  hustle-oap  and  ohuok-farthing  with  the  most 
blackguard  imp  upon  the  wharf,  unless  he  has 
forgotten  the  old  customs.' " 

Smollett  (1751)  in  the  fifteenth  chapter  of 

*  Peregrine  Pickle  *  speaks  of  Tom  Pipes 

"  as  master  of  the  revels  in  the  whole  school ;  he 
regulated  their  motions  by  his  whistle ;  instructed 
the  young  boys  in  the  games  of  hustle-cap  and 
chuck-farthing." 

The  Parson  has  loat  his  Cloak. — ^There  is  a 
reference  to  this  game  in  No.  268  of  The 
Spectator,  7  Jan.,  1712,  contributed  by  Steele, 
where  some  correspondent  writes  : — 

"  I  desire  to  know  in  your  next  if  the  merry  game 
of  *  The  Parson  has  lost  his  Cloak '  is  not  mightily 
in  vogue  amongst  the  fine  ladies  this  Christmas, 
because  I  see  they  wear  Hoods  of  all  colours,  which 
I  supixjse  is  for  that  i»uri>ose.  If  it  is,  I  will  carry 
some  of  these  Hoods  with  me  to  our  ladies  in 
Yorkshire,  because  they  enjoined  me  to  bring  them 
something  from  London  that  was  very  new." 

Wild-goose  chase. — In  '  Romeo  and  Juliet,' 
II.  iv.,  Mercutio  says  to  Romeo,  "If  thy 
wits  run  the  wild-goose  chase,  I  have  done  ; 
for  thou  hast  more  of  the  wild  goose  in  one 
of  thy  wits  than,  I  am  sure,  I  have  in  my 
whole  five."  Burton  in  his  '  Anatomy  of 
Melancholy,'  part  ii.  sec.  ii.  memb.  iv., 
writes  (a.d.  1621)  of  the  many 

"common  recreations  of  the  country  folk  :  riding  of 
great  horses,  running  at  rings,  tilts  and  tourna- 
ments, horse-races,  wild  -  goose  -  chases,  which  are 
the  disports  of  greater  men  and  good  in  themselves, 
though  many  gentlemen  by  that  means  gallop  quite 
out  of  their  fortunes." 

Drake  in  'Shakespeare  and  his  Times,' 
published  in  1817,  believes  this  diversion 
entirely  obsolete,"  and  adds  that 

"this  barbarous  sjiecies  of  horse-race  has  been 
named  from  its  resemblance  to  the  flight  of  wild 
geese.  This  elegant  amusement  consisted  in  two 
horses  starting  together,  and  he  who  proved  the 
hindmost  rider  was  obliged  to  follow  the  foremost 
over  whatever  ground  he  chose  to  carry  him,  that 
horse  which  could  distance  the  other  winning  the 
race."

►ROE :  George  as  a  CmtxsTtAN 
S.  vii.  308,  375,  465).— It  will  be 
'  3  see  if  England  can  produce  an 
W  than  Ge«:>rge  de  Cantilupe, 
'~    Eujs  states  to  have  been 

1  other  instances  do  occur,  the 

of    the    name,    even    long 

ate,  is  aliown  by  the  record  of 

of  the  Garter.     The  order 

l^il,  but  it  waft  not  till 

eorge,  Duke  of  Clarence, 

ui  name  upon  its  roll. 

knight,  and  was  bom  in 

Joy,  Lord  Strange,  bom 


c,  1460,  comes  245th ;  and  George  Talbot, 
4th  Earl  of  Slirewsbury,  born  1408,  cornea 
247ti». 

In  the  first  150  years  263  knights  were 
appointed,  and  the  name  "  George  "  appears 
on  only  these  three  occasions. 

But  the  curious  thing  is  that,  long  before 
that  time,  this  name,  considered  so  typicallv 
English,  was  qiiite  common  in  Scotland. 
One  would  like  to  know  the  authority  for 
"  George  Munro  do  Foulis  "  in  the  time  of 
Alexander  II.  ;  but  later,  besides  George^ 
Earl  of  Dunbar,  bom  before  1340,  we  have 
George  Douglas,  Earl  of  Angus,  bom  before 
1378,  and  George,  1st  Lord  Seton,  bom 
before  1429. 

On  the  Border  the  name  was  quite  common 
at  the  commencement  of  the  fifteenth  oenturj'. 
To  give  one  instance.  To  a  Swinton  charter, 
dated  1433,  thirteen  minor  landowners  are 
witnesses.  Tliere  are  two  Richeu'ds  and 
two  Thorns,  a  Philip,  a  John,  a  David,  a  Col, 
a  Hud,  and  a  William.  But  there  are  also 
a  George  of  Ellem,  a  George  of  Redpath,  and 
a  George  Ramsay.  G.  S-  C.  S. 

Those  who,  like  myself,  have  had  experi- 
ence of  parish  registers,  will,  I  tliink,  agree 
that  George  as  a  Christian  name  is  com- 
paratively uncommon  in  England  before 
1700.  "  Comparatively,"  because  the  saint's 
name  was  thrust  into  such  prominence  before 
the  people  in  almost  everj'  parish  in  £lngland 
that  we  should  naturally  expect  to  find  it 
preponderating  at  the  font  above  ail  others. 
But  it  is  so  low  as  eighth  on  the  list  in 
Oxford  Hist.  Soc.,  vol.  xiv.,  with  a  total  of 
647,  a  tiny  fraction  of  the  30,000  on  the 
register.  Moreover,  some  of  these  probably 
came  from  countries  other  than  England. 
Thus  Buchanan  and  Mackenzie  were  Scots  ; 
and  none  of  the  Georges  mentioned  antfi. 
p.  375,  are  of  first-rate  importance. 

The  truth  seems  to  be  that  the  popularity 
of  St.  George  was  somewhat  factitious ;  he 
was  a  foreigner,  if  he  was  not  mythical, 
and  he  did  not  appeal  much  to  the  imagina- 
tion of  our  forefathers.  W.  C.  B.

Haybiarket,  Westminster  (10  S.  vii. 
270,  370). — ^The  hay-c€uis  were  probably 
loaded  with  hay  from  the  south-western 
suburbs,  and  were  going  by  Westminster 
Bridge  to  Cumberland  Market.  The  wide 
thoroughfare  of  the  Havmarket  was  found 
a  convenient  "  pull-up  for  about  half  an 
hour,  free  from  police  interference,  where 
tlie  men  could  regale  themselves  on  some- 
thing toothsome  in  the  8hai)e  of  stewed 
eels  and  whelks,  or  cheap  oysters  (then  three 
a  penny),  to  be  had  at  street  stalls  in  the 
neighbourhood.  These  hay-cart  men  were 
of  a  gregarious  nature,  and  it  was  a  common 
occurrence  in  those  days  to  see  tliree  or 
four  loaded  hay-carts  standing  in  a  line, 
in  the  middle  of  a  broad  thoroughfare, 
en  route  for  the  market,  particularly  in  the 
vicinity  of  cheap  refreshment  stalls. 

Charles  Shelley.

"Rime"  v.  *' Rhv\le  '  (10  S.  v.  469» 
5U;  vi.  52,  90,  132,  192.  233,  332.  391).— 
I  believe  it  is  a  rule  of  procedure,  not  only 
of  the  House  of  Commons,  but  also  of  all 
other  distJussive  and  discursive  bodies,  that 
no  one  should  be  allowed  to  apeak  twice 
to  the  same  motion  or  amendment.  But 
surely,  if  it  should  happen  that  what  a 
person  has  said  on  the  hrst  occasion  should 
have  been  absolutely  forgotten  by  sub- 
sequent speakers,  it  would  be  justifiable 
for  the  first  speaker  again  to  intervene. 

And  so  in  the  above  discussion  upon 
"  Rime  "  v.  *'  Rhyme."  Only  a  very  few 
yeai's  ago  I  ventured  to  raise  an  objection 
to  the  alteration,  by  the  Editor  of  '  N.  &  Q.,* 
of  m,y  spelling  of  the  word  rhyinc  to  rime, 
when  Prof.  Skeat  and  early  literature,  as 
here,  were  brought  in  to  crush  me.  And, 
apparently,  so  effectually  was  this  done  that 
neither  Senex,  nor  anybody  else,  seems  to 
be  aware  that  this  question  has  been  raised 
before.  I  would  €wk  the  Editor  kindly  to 
supply  the  reference,  as  all  my  earlier  volumes 
of  *  N.  &  Q.*  have  already  been  sent  to 
England  for  safety's  sake  ;  and  then,  if  I 
might  be  allowed  in  these  circumstances  to 
have  a  second  small  say,  I  should  like  to 
offer  a  tremulous  support  to  the  position 
taken  up  by  Senex,  though  I  have  no 
intention  of  getting  between  the  upper  and 
the  nether  millstone  in  a  controversy  so 
much  beyond  me. 

But  is' the  reference  (in  an  editorial  note 
at  the  first  reference)  to  CoFeridge  as  an 
authority  against  that  position  altogether 
a  happy  one  ?  Sui-ely,  if  that  autJior'a 
rendering  of  the  word  rime  be  accepted,  why 
should  we  not  accept  the  rest  of  liis  spelling, 
and  call  it  "The  Rime  of  the  Aimcyent 
Marinere,'  as  I  think  it  will  be  found  to  be 
in  the  earliest  editions  of  that  poem  ?  But 
to  my  boyish  fancy — so  strange  did  the 
rendering  appear — it  has  always  seemed 
that  Coleridge  intended  by  it  a  reference  to, 
or  a  play  upon,  the  surroimdings  of  the 
"  bright-eyed  mariner  "  : — 

The  ioe  was  hero,  the  ice  was  there. 
The  ice  was  all  around. 

And  in  no  other  connexion  can  /  ever  think 
of  rime  than  as  hoar-frost. 

But  my  object  was  more,  when  I  first 
wrote  on  this  subject — and  it  is  so  solely 
on  this  occasion — to  protest  against  the 
tjTanny  of  Mr.  Editor,  m  an  old-established 
periodical  like  '  N.  &  Q.' — **  a  medium  of 
mtercommunication  for  literary  men  and 
general  readers,"  in  which  we  all  feel  as  if  we 
had  a  vested  interest — seekiu^,  vix  «.  \y!^afb 


518 


NOTES  AND  QUERIEa    tio  ti.  vil  jtx»:  29, m. 


spirit  of  philology  (as  I  conceive  it),  to 
reduce  all  our  spelling  to  the  particular 
canons  of  which  he  may  happen  to  approve. 
I  trust  that  it  will  be  long  before  President 
Roosevelt  assumes  the  editorial  chair  ! 

Can  any  one  imagine  that  the  speUing 
rime,  that  we  now  see  so  frequently  used  by 
BO  many  contributors  to  '  N.  &  Q,\  is  put 
there  by  themselves  !  If  I  spell  badly  in 
the  ordinary  sense,  the  compositisr  or  proof- 
reader will,  I  hope,  soon  put  me  straight  ; 
but  if  I  spell  a  word  in  the  way  in  which 
it  has  commonly  been  spelt  by  the  best 
wTiters  and  authors  for  well  nigh  upon  350 
years,  then  I  would  respectfully  ask  that 
Air.  Editor  should  not  correct  it  against  my 
wish.  It  seems  a  pity  that  anvthing 
should  tend  to  destroy  that  incUviduality 
which  is  so  marked  a  feature  amongst  the 
contributors  of,  I  sujjpose,  the  moat  original 
of  all  our  journals.        J.  S.  Udax,  f'.S.A. 

Antigua,  W.I.

"  Ulidia  *•  (10  S.  viL  289.  35C).— The  Ret.

NOTES  AND  QUERIES,     no  &  m  j;in  9 


•eveiry  one  who  is  interested  in  the  development 
of  mankind  in  abnormal  surroundings.  Though, 
judged  by  the  standards  and  by  the  narrow  sym- 
pathies of  Europeans,  the  little  people  of  the  far 
North  are  wallowing  in  grossness,  it  appears  that, 
when  unoontaminatod  by  the  brutality  of  white 
men,  they  are  gentle,  affiectionate.  gay,  and  happy. 
"  Theft  is  almost  unknown,  though  it  must  be  said 
that  opportunities  of  theft  are  equally  abseotu 
Adultery  (in  the  Eskimo  sense  of  the  wora)  is  like- 
wise unknown.  Moreover,  as  within  a  olan,  there 
is   no   blood-feud,  even  when   homicide   is  oom- 

mitted Evil  magio  is  of  course  condemned,  and 

punished,  even  by  death,"  which  may  lead  to 
tragedy  when  the  angakokt  or  wizard,  who  investi- 
gates the  case,  makes  a  mistake,  with  the  result 
that  there  is  a  miscarriage  of  justice. 

Jamaican  Sona  and  Story^  collected  and  edited 
by  Walter  Jekyll,  is  one  of  the  publications  of  the 
folk-lore  Society,  and  well  deserves  to  be  given  to 
the  world.  Mr.  Jekvll  has  evidently  an  affbction 
for  the  merry-hearted  if  irresponsible  negroes  whose 
tales  and  tunes  he  now  hands  over  to  the  erucUte 
•oommentary  of  grave  and  anxious  civilization. 
*' The  book  as  a  whole,"  he  says,  "is  a  tribute  to 
my  love  for  Jamaica  and  its  dusky  inhabitants, 
with  their  winning  ways  and  their  many  good 
•qualities,  among  which  is  to  be  reckoned  that 
■supreme  virtue,  cheer/ulnMs," 

First  in  the  collection  stand  the  Annancy  stories. 
"Mr.  Annancy"  is  properly  a  spider,  that  is,  he 
was  one  originally  in  his  old  Gold  Coast  home,  but 
in  Jamaica  he  has  become  a  strange  legendary 
being,  whose  chief  characteristics  are  trickery, 
laziness,  thievish  voracity,  and  callousness.  Like 
Reynard  the  Fox,  and  the  wily  "Thiefie"  who 
was  once  evolved  by  the  mischief-loving  inhabitants 
•of  a  certain  strictly  governed  English  nursery,  he 
has  a  8Ui)erficial  camaraderie,  and  quickness  of  wit 
which  help  him  to  attain  his  ends ;  for  he  is  one  uf 
those  heroes,  familiarly  known  wherever  physical 
power  is  regarded  as  oppressive,  whose  readiness  of 
thought  enables  him  to  get  the  better  of  clumsv 
strength.  The  dialect  in  which  his  adventures  and 
those  of  his  fellow-tricksters  are  related  is  some- 
times very  quaint,  even  when  the  stories  are  mani- 
festly of  Euroi)eAn  origin  ;  and  there  is  a  childlike 
simplicity  about  the  tales  which  makes  it  easy  to 
accept  the  belief  that  negro  brains  scarcely  ever 
reach  the  full  development  normal  among  white 
men. 

After  the  Annancy  stories  come  the  digging  songs, 
in  which  the  incidents  of  daily  life  or  of  local  eossip 
are  set  to  a  tune  and  chanted  to  enliven  lield-labour. 
These  are  followed  by  the  ring  tunes  and  words 
used  in  the  informal  dancing  which  originated  in 
the  ring  games  of  English  cnildren.  Finally,  we 
are  given  the  dancing  tunes  proper,  with  their 
ajipropriate  words.  Jamaican  music  seems,  as  was 
to  be  expected,  reminiscent  of  European  models, 
but  we  are  told  that  the  decided  tendency  to  short 
refrains  may  be  African  in  origin.  Mr,  Jekyll's 
book  certainly  shows  us  the  amiable  side  of  a  light- 
hearted  peoiile  untroubled  by  what  has  been  called 
"  the  malady  of  thought."
